Since 2002, German troops formed part of the [[International Security Assistance Force]] in the [[War in Afghanistan (2001–2021)|War in Afghanistan]], resulting in the first German [[German Armed Forces casualties in Afghanistan|casualties]] in combat missions since World War II.
In light of the worldwide [[Great Recession]] that began in 2008, Germany did not experience as much economic hardship as other European nations. {{Citation needed|date=October 2025}} Germany later sponsored a massive financial rescue in the wake of the [[European debt crisis|Eurozone crisis]] which affected the German economy.
Following the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|2011 earthquake and tsunami]] in Japan, which led to the [[Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster|Fukushima nuclear disaster]], German public opinion turned sharply against [[nuclear power in Germany]], which at the time produced a fourth of the electricity supply. In response Merkel announced plans to close down the nuclear power plants over the following decade, and a commitment to rely more heavily on wind and other alternative energy sources, in addition to coal and natural gas.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Mufson |first=Steven |date=30 May 2011 |title=Germany to close all of its nuclear plants by 2022 |newspaper=The Washington Post|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/business/economy/germany-to-close-all-of-its-nuclear-plants-by-2022/2011/05/30/AG0op1EH_story.html}}</ref>
Germany was affected by the [[European migrant crisis]] in 2015 as it became the final destination of choice for many asylum seekers from [[Africa]] and the [[Middle East]] entering the EU. The country took in over a million refugees and migrants and developed a quota system which redistributed migrants around its federal states based on their tax income and existing population density.<ref>{{Cite news |date=28 January 2016 |title=Migrant crisis: Migration to Europe explained in seven charts |publisher=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-europe-34131911 |access-date=31 January 2016}}</ref> The decision by Merkel to authorize unrestricted entry led to heavy criticism in Germany as well as within Europe.<ref>{{Cite news |date=19 January 2016 |title=Chancellor Running Out of Time on Refugee Issue |work=Spiegel Online |url=http://www.spiegel.de/international/germany/critique-of-merkel-on-refugee-issue-deepens-a-1072549.html |access-date=7 June 2017}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=9 August 2016 |title=Merkel Critic Says Chancellor's Refugee Policy Is a 'Time Bomb' |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2016-08-09/merkel-critic-says-chancellor-s-refugee-policy-is-a-time-bomb |access-date=7 June 2017 |publisher=[[Bloomberg News]]}}</ref> This was a major factor in the rise of the far-right party [[Alternative for Germany]] which entered the Bundestag in the [[2017 German federal election|2017 federal election]].<ref>{{Cite news |last=Welle (www.dw.com) |first=Deutsche |title=Far-right AfD enters German parliament: What it means for German politics {{!}} DW {{!}} 24.09.2017 |work=DW.COM |url=https://www.dw.com/en/far-right-afd-enters-german-parliament-what-it-means-for-german-politics/a-40664281}}</ref>
===German government response to the COVID-19 pandemic (2020-22)=== {{Further|German government response to the COVID-19 pandemic}} In January 2020, Germany confirmed the first case of [[COVID-19 pandemic in Germany|novel coronavirus]], originating from Wuhan, China. In March 2020, Germany went on national lockdown, which greatly impacted the German economy, healthcare system, and society, and was also commended for being an effective model for instituting methods of curbing infections and deaths, but lost this status by the end of the year due to rising number of cases, hospitalizations, and deaths. In December 2020, COVID-19 vaccines began to be administered in Germany. Unfortunately, from June 2021 to the end of March 2022, Germany saw a new surge of huge COVID-19 infection wave, fueled by the highly transmissible [[SARS-CoV-2 Delta variant|Delta]][[SARS-CoV-2 Omicron variant|cron]] hybrid variant, which is combined of Delta and Omicron mutations. However, Germany has suffered from a recombination event of Deltacron, which was caused by vaccine shortages in the first quarter. As of May 2022, Germany has reported 140,292 COVID-19-related deaths, the fifth highest mortality toll (behind Russia, the United Kingdom, [[Italy]], and [[France]]), out of 2 million deaths in Europe.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Germany COVID – Coronavirus Statistics – Worldometer |url=https://www.worldometers.info/coronavirus/country/germany |website=worldometers.info |language=en}}</ref>
COVID-19 measures in Germany were relaxed in March 2022. Mask obligation has been lifted, except for facilities such as hospitals, nursing homes, and public vehicles.<ref>{{Cite web |title=COVID-19 measures lifted in most European countries |url=https://www.aa.com.tr/en/europe/covid-19-measures-lifted-in-most-european-countries/2570613 |access-date=2025-07-08 |website=www.aa.com.tr}}</ref>
===Post-COVID period (Since 2022)=== On 8 December 2021 just three months after Germany's centre-left Social Democrats (SPD) narrowly won the federal [[2021 German federal election|election]], ending 16 years of conservative-led rule under Angela Merkel, Social Democrat [[Olaf Scholz]] was sworn in as Germany's new chancellor. He formed a coalition government with the Green Party and the liberal Free Democrats.<ref>{{Cite news |date=27 September 2021 |title=Germany elections: Centre-left claim narrow win over Merkel's party |publisher=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-europe-58698806}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |last=Crowcroft |first=Orlando |date=8 December 2021 |title='New era': Scholz becomes Germany's new chancellor as Merkel bows out |language=en |work=euronews |url=https://www.euronews.com/2021/12/08/olaf-scholz-to-become-germany-s-new-chancellor-as-merkel-bows-out}}</ref>
In February 2022, [[Frank-Walter Steinmeier]] was elected for a second five-year term as Germany's president. Although largely ceremonial post{{Clarify|reason=Poor English|date=October 2025}}, he has been seen as a symbol of consensus and continuity.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Welle (www.dw.com) |first=Deutsche |title=Frank-Walter Steinmeier elected to second term as German president {{!}} DW {{!}} 13.02.2022 |url=https://www.dw.com/en/frank-walter-steinmeier-elected-to-second-term-as-german-president/a-60760871}}</ref>
After [[2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine|Russia's Feb. 24 invasion of Ukraine]] in 2022, Germany's previous foreign policy towards Russia (traditional Ostpolitik) has been severely criticized for having been too credulous and soft.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The ruins of Ostpolitik |url=https://www.eurozine.com/german-ostpolitik-in-the-shadow-of-russias-imperial-revenge |website=eurozine.com|date=23 February 2022 }}</ref> Following concerns from the [[2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine]], Germany announced a major shift in policy, pledging a €100 billion special fund for the Bundeswehr{{Snd}}to remedy years of underinvestment{{Snd}}along with raising the budget to above 2% [[GDP]].<ref>{{Cite news |last1=Sheahan |first1=Maria |last2=Marsh |first2=Sarah |last3=Sheahan |first3=Maria |last4=Marsh |first4=Sarah |date=27 February 2022 |title=Germany to increase defence spending in response to 'Putin's war' – Scholz |language=en |work=Reuters|url=https://www.reuters.com/business/aerospace-defense/germany-hike-defense-spending-scholz-says-further-policy-shift-2022-02-27/}}</ref> As of April 2023, over 1.06 million refugees from Ukraine were recorded in Germany.<ref>{{cite web |title=Ukrainian refugees by country 2023 |url=https://www.statista.com/statistics/1312584/ukrainian-refugees-by-country/ |website=Statista |language=en}}</ref>
As of December 2023, Germany is the fourth largest economy in the world after the United States, China and Japan and the largest economy in Europe. It is the third largest export nation in the world.<ref>{{Cite web |date=5 December 2023 |title=Economic Key Facts Germany |url=https://kpmg.com/de/en/home/insights/overview/economic-key-facts-germany.html}}</ref>
In February 2025, CDU/CSU, the conservatives, won Germany's 2025 federal [[2025 German federal election|election]], becoming the biggest group in the parliament. However, far-right Alternative for Germany, AfD, doubled its support to became the second biggest political party in parliament with 20.8% of the vote. SPD, the Social Democrats, had its worst performance in decades with 16.4% of the vote.<ref>{{cite news |title=Five key takeaways from the German election |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cx29wlje6dno |work=www.bbc.com |date=24 February 2025}}</ref>
On 6 May 2025, [[Friedrich Merz]] was sworn in as Germany's next chancellor by President Frank-Walter Steinmeier. Merz formed a coalition with his Christian Democrats, its sister party the Christian Social Union, and the Social Democrats.<ref>{{cite news |title=Germany's Merz becomes chancellor after surviving historic vote failure |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cvgp22zlrgko |work=www.bbc.com |date=6 May 2025}}</ref>

===Since 1945=== {{Refbegin|30em|indent=yes}} * {{Cite book |last1=Bark |first1=Dennis L. |title=A History of West Germany |last2=Gress |first2=David R. |date=1992 |publisher=Blackwell |isbn=978-0-6311-6787-7 |volume=1: From Shadow to Substance, 1945–1963 |ol=2058977M}} * {{Cite book |last1=Bark |first1=Dennis L. |title=A History of West Germany |last2=Gress |first2=David R. |date=1992 |publisher=Blackwell |isbn=978-1-5578-6322-5 |volume=2: Democracy and Its Discontents 1963–1991 |ol=8602440M |author-mask=1 |author-mask2=1}} * Berghahn, Volker Rolf. (1987) ''Modern Germany: Society, Economy, and Politics in the Twentieth Century'' [http://hdl.handle.net/2027/heb.01673 ACLS E-book online] * [[Andreas Daum|Daum, Andreas]]. (2008) ''Kennedy in Berlin''. New York: Cambridge University Press, {{ISBN|978-0-5218-5824-3}}. * Gehler, Michael. (2013) ''Three Germanies: West Germany, East Germany and the Berlin Republic'' (Reaktion Books). * Hanrieder, Wolfram F. (1989) ''Germany, America, Europe: Forty Years of German Foreign Policy'' {{ISBN|0-3000-4022-9}} * [[Ulrich Herbert|Herbert, Ulrich.]] (2019) ''A History of Twentieth-Century Germany'' * Jähner, Harald. (2022) ''Aftermath: Life in the Fallout of the Third Reich, 1945-1955'' * Jarausch, Konrad H. (2008) ''After Hitler: Recivilizing Germans, 1945–1995'' * Junker, Detlef, ed. (2004) ''The United States and Germany in the Era of the Cold War'', 150 short essays by scholars covering 1945–1990 * Main, Steven J. (2014) "The Soviet Occupation of Germany. Hunger, Mass Violence and the Struggle for Peace, 1945–1947". ''Europe-Asia Studies'' 66#8 pp: 1380–1382. * Schwarz, Hans-Peter. (1995) ''Konrad Adenauer: A German Politician and Statesman in a Period of War, Revolution and Reconstruction''[https://books.google.com/books?id=T4vQw1RNkQ8C excerpt and text search vol 2] * Smith, Gordon, ed, (1992) ''Developments in German Politics'' {{ISBN|0-8223-1266-2}}, broad survey of reunified nation * {{Cite book |title=Documents on Germany under Occupation, 1945–1954 |date=1955 |publisher=Oxford University Press |editor-last=Von Oppen |editor-first=Beate Ruhm |oclc=1980291 |ol=7206115W}} * Weber, Jurgen. (2004) ''Germany, 1945–1990'' (Central European University Press) {{Refend}}
===GDR=== {{Refbegin|30em|indent=yes}} * Dennis, Mike, and Norman LaPorte. (2011) ''State and Minorities in Communist East Germany'' (Berghahn Books) scholarly analysis of treatment of Jehovah's Witnesses, Jews, guest workers from Vietnam and Mozambique, football fans and others. * Fulbrook, Mary. (1998) ''Anatomy of a Dictatorship: Inside the GDR, 1949–1989'' * Fulbrook, Mary. (2008) ''The People's State: East German Society from Hitler to Honecker'' * Harsch, Donna. (2008) ''Revenge of the Domestic: Women, the Family, and Communism in the German Democratic Republic'' * Jarausch, Konrad H.. and Eve Duffy. (1999) ''Dictatorship As Experience: Towards a Socio-Cultural History of the GDR'' * Jarausch, Konrad H., and Volker Gransow, eds. (1994) ''Uniting Germany: Documents and Debates, 1944–1993'', primary sources on reunification * McAdams, A. James. (1992 and 1993) "Germany Divided: From the Wall to Reunification". Princeton University Press * Pence, Katherine, and Paul Betts, eds. (2008) ''Socialist Modern: East German Everyday Culture and Politics'' * Pritchard, Gareth. (2004) ''The Making of the GDR, 1945–53'' * Ross, Corey. (2002) ''The East German Dictatorship: Problems and Perspectives in the Interpretation of the GDR'' * Saxonberg, Steven. (2013) ''The fall: A comparative study of the end of Communism in Czechoslovakia, East Germany, Hungary and Poland'' (Routledge). * Steiner, André. (2010) ''The Plans That Failed: An Economic History of East Germany, 1945–1989'' {{Refend}}
===Historiography=== {{Refbegin|30em|indent=yes}} * Berghahn, Volker R., and Simone Lassig, eds. (2008) ''Biography between Structure and Agency: Central European Lives in International Historiography'' * Chickering, Roger, ed. (1996) ''Imperial Germany: A Historiographical Companion''; 18 essays by specialists; * Evans, Richard J. (1997) ''Rereading German History: From Unification to Reunification, 1800–1996'' * Hagemann, Karen, and Jean H. Quataert, eds. (2008) ''Gendering Modern German History: Rewriting Historiography'' * {{Cite journal |last=Hagemann |first=Karen |date=2007 |title=From the Margins to the Mainstream? Women's and Gender History in Germany |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-womens-history_spring-2007_19_1/page/193 |journal=Journal of Women's History |volume=19 |issue=1 |pages=193–199 |doi=10.1353/jowh.2007.0014 |s2cid=143068850}} * Hagen, William W. (2012) ''German History in Modern Times: Four Lives of the Nation'' * Jarausch, Konrad H., and Michael Geyer, eds. (2003) ''Shattered Past: Reconstructing German Histories'' * Klessmann, Christoph. (2001) ''The Divided Past: Rewriting Post-War German History'' * Lehmann, Hartmut, and James Van Horn Melton, eds. (2003) ''Paths of Continuity: Central European Historiography from the 1930s to the 1950s'' * Perkins, J. A. (April 1986) "Dualism in German Agrarian Historiography, ''Comparative Studies in Society and History,'' Vol. 28 Issue 2, pp 287–330, * Rüger, Jan, and Nikolaus Wachsmann, eds. (2015) ''Rewriting German history: New Perspectives on Modern Germany'' (Palgrave Macmillan). * Stuchtey, Benedikt, and Peter Wende, eds. (2000) ''British and German Historiography, 1750–1950: Traditions, Perceptions, and Transfers'' {{Refend}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|none}} {{Very long|date=June 2023|words=24,000}} {{multiple image | perrow = 2 | total_width = 340 | image1 = María Ruiz de Burton.jpg | image2 = Initial Monument Marking the United States - Mexico Border.png | image3 = ‘Dead Mexican Bandits’.jpg | image4 = Lynching of Francisco Arias and José Chamales.jpg | image5 =Democratic Convention in New York City, July 14, 1976. Cesar Chavez at podium, nominating Gov. Brown.tif | image6 = Mexican Repatriation, 1931.jpg | image7 = May Day Immigration March LA74.jpg | footer = first female Mexican American author in English [[María Ruiz de Burton]], 1887 picture of the initial boundary marking the [[U.S.-Mexico border]], Texas Rangers during the 1910-1920 [[La Matanza (1910–1920)|La Matanza]], 1877 [[lynching]] of two Mexican-American men in California, civil rights leader [[Cesar Chavez]], the [[Mexican Repatriation]], the [[Great American Boycott]] }} {{Chicano and Mexican American history sidebar|state=collapsed}} {{Hispanic and Latino Americans|right}} {{History of the United States|expanded=topics}}
'''[[Mexican American]] history''', or the history of American residents of Mexican descent, largely begins after the [[Mexican Cession|annexation of Northern Mexico]] in 1848, when the nearly 80,000 Mexican citizens of California, Nevada, Utah, Arizona, Colorado, and New Mexico became U.S. citizens.<ref>{{cite web |title=Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo |url=https://www.digitalhistory.uh.edu/disp_textbook.cfm?smtID=3&psid=1141 |website=University of Houston, Digital History |publisher=University of Houston |access-date=18 August 2020}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Baca |first1=Herman |title=The little-known treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo and 5 years of conquest. |url=https://library.ucsd.edu/dc/object/bb9250521w/_1.pdf |website=The San Diego Union-Tribune |publisher=University of California, San Diego |access-date=18 August 2020}}</ref> Large-scale migration increased the U.S.' Mexican population during the 1910s, as refugees fled the economic devastation and violence of Mexico's high-casualty [[Mexican Revolution|revolution]] and [[Cristero War|civil war]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Steinhauer |first1=Jason |title=The History of Mexican Immigration to the U.S. in the Early 20th Century |url=https://blogs.loc.gov/kluge/2015/03/the-history-of-mexican-immigration-to-the-u-s-in-the-early-20th-century/ |website=Insights: Scholarly Works at the John W. Kluge Center |date=11 March 2015 |publisher=Library of Congress |access-date=18 August 2020}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=The Mexican Revolution 1910-1917 |url=http://www.laits.utexas.edu/jaime/cwp5/cig/text/extramexrev.html |website=UT Liberal Arts Instruction Technology Services |publisher=University of Texas, Austin |access-date=18 August 2020}}</ref> Until the mid-20th century, most Mexican Americans lived within a few hundred miles of the border, although some resettled along rail lines from the Southwest into the Midwest.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Garcilazo |first1=Jeffrey Marcos |title=Traqueros: Mexican Railroad Workers in the United States, 1870 to 1930 |date=February 16, 2016 |publisher=University of North Texas Press |isbn=978-1574416275 |edition=2 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=aAdOBTaj7T0C |access-date=18 August 2020}}</ref>
With the border being established many Mexicans began to find more creative ways to get across. In the article ''Artificial Intelligence and Predicting Illegal Immigration to the USA'' the statistic that "more than half of undocumented immigrants in the USA enter the USA legally and overstay their visas" (Yektansani). This happened all throughout the timeline.
In the second half of the 20th century, Mexican Americans diffused throughout the U.S., especially into the Midwest and Southeast,<ref>{{cite web |last1=Anderson |first1=Lydia R. |title=Hispanics in the Midwest |url=https://www.bgsu.edu/content/dam/BGSU/college-of-arts-and-sciences/center-for-family-and-demographic-research/documents/OPN/Ohio-Population-News-2016-Hispanics-in-the-Midwest.pdf |website=Center for Family and Demographic Research |publisher=Bowling Green State University |access-date=18 August 2020}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Kochhar |first1=Rakesh |last2=Suro |first2=Roberto |last3=Tafoya |first3=Sonya |title=The New Latino South: The Context and Consequences of Rapid Population Growth |url=https://www.pewresearch.org/hispanic/2005/07/26/the-new-latino-south/ |website=Pew Research Center: Hispanic Trends |date=26 July 2005 |publisher=Pew Research Center |access-date=18 August 2020}}</ref> though the groups' largest population centers remain in California and Texas.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Lopez |first1=Mark Hugo |title=Ranking the Latino population in the states |url=https://www.pewresearch.org/hispanic/2016/09/08/4-ranking-the-latino-population-in-the-states/ |website=Pew Research Center |date=8 September 2016 |access-date=18 August 2020}}</ref> During this period, Mexican-Americans campaigned for voting rights, educational and employment equity, ethnic equality, and economic and social advancement.<ref>{{cite web |title=Chicano/Latino Movements History and Geography |url=https://depts.washington.edu/moves/Chicano_intro.shtml |website=Mapping American Social Movements |publisher=University of Washington |access-date=18 August 2020}}</ref>
==Spanish period== [[File:Mission Santa Barbara by Carleton Watkins, 1876.jpg|thumb|right|The [[Mission Santa Barbara|Santa Barbara Mission]], established in 1786]]
[[Spanish colonization of the Americas|Spanish entry]] into what is now the United States southwest began in 1540, when [[Francisco Vázquez de Coronado]], his 230 Spanish soldiers, 800 Indigenous Mexicans, and three women marched into the [[Lower Rio Grande Valley|Rio Grande valley]].<ref>{{cite book |last1=de Otermín |first1=Antonio |title=A Forgotten Kingdom: The Spanish Frontier in Colorado and New Mexico, 1540-1821 |date=20 November 2008 |publisher=National Park Service |url=https://www.nps.gov/parkhistory/online_books/blm/ut/29/chap1.htm |access-date=18 August 2020}}</ref> Soon after, [[Juan Rodríguez Cabrillo|Juan Rodriguez Cabrillo]] led the first expedition into [[Alta California]] in 1542, when he landed in modern-day Santa Barbara.<ref>{{cite web |title=Spanish California |url=https://www.loc.gov/collections/california-first-person-narratives/articles-and-essays/early-california-history/spanish-california/ |website=California as I Saw It: First-Person Narratives of California's Early Years, 1849 to 1900 |publisher=Library of Congress |access-date=18 August 2020}}</ref> There are reports that the explorer [[Marcos de Niza]] entered Arizona in 1539, but scholars have cast doubt on his fabled exploration for the [[Seven Cities of Gold]].<ref name="Myth and the History of the Hispanic Southwest">{{cite book |last1=Weber |first1=David |title=Myth and the History of the Hispanic Southwest |date=January 1, 1990 |publisher=University of New Mexico Press |isbn=978-0826311948 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=DN7xQW3aIbMC |access-date=18 August 2020}}</ref>
Full-scale Spanish colonization of the Southwest did not begin until 1598, when the Spanish government, under pressure from the Catholic Church to Christianize the [[Coahuiltecan]] peoples of the Rio Grande Valley, selected [[Juan de Oñate|Juan Oñate]] to cross the Rio Grande and establish a permanent settlement in San Juan Pueblo, near present-day [[Española, New Mexico|Espanola]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Colonial New Mexico |url=https://www.cabq.gov/parksandrecreation/parks/veterans-memorial-park/documents/COLONIALNEWMEXICO_Final.pdf |publisher=City of Albuquerque |access-date=18 August 2020}}</ref> Rumors of hidden gold and silver ores in New Mexico circulated in the lead-up to the Oñate settlement, but none were ever found in the region.<ref>{{cite web |title=Spanish New Mexico |url=https://americanhistory.si.edu/many-voices-exhibition/spanish-new-mexico |website=National Museum of American History |date=12 June 2017 |publisher=Smithsonian Institution |access-date=18 August 2020}}</ref> The focus remained on religious conversion.
The first [[Spanish missions in the Americas|Spanish missions]] in [[Spanish Texas|Texas]] were colonized in the 1680s around present-day [[San Angelo, Texas|San Angelo]], [[El Paso, Texas|El Paso]] and [[Presidio, Texas|Presidio]], near the New Mexico settlements. In the early-1680s, however, [[Pueblo Revolt|conflict]] emerged in New Mexico, as the [[Puebloans|Pueblo people]] rebelled against the Spanish colonization.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Gutiérrez |first1=Ramón A. |title=When Jesus Came, the Corn Mothers Went Away |date=January 1, 1991 |publisher=Stanford University Press |location=Palo Alto |isbn=978-0804718325 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=IeqvKX44GhsC |access-date=19 August 2020}}</ref> Spanish colonization nevertheless persisted, and in 1690, new missions were built in East Texas by [[Alonso de León]] after the Spanish discovered the [[French colonization of Texas|French]] had been encroaching into the territory.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Whitehurst |first1=Katie |title=Spanish Colonial |url=https://texasourtexas.texaspbs.org/the-eras-of-texas/spanish-colonial/ |website=Texas PBS |publisher=PBS |access-date=18 August 2020}}</ref> In [[Pimería Alta|Arizona]], the first Spanish settlements were founded in 1691 by the Italian Jesuit missionary Father [[Eusebio Kino|Eusebio Francisco Kino]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Conquistadors and Spanish Colonial Period |url=https://dema.az.gov/sites/default/files/Conquistadors%20and%20Spanish%20Colonial%20Period.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161228123424/https://www.dema.az.gov/sites/default/files/Conquistadors%20and%20Spanish%20Colonial%20Period.pdf |url-status=dead |archive-date=December 28, 2016 |website=Department of Emergency and Military Affairs |publisher=Government of Arizona |access-date=18 August 2020}}</ref> [[Alta California|California]]'s first permanent Spanish settlement wasn't established until 1769, when the [[Presidio of San Diego]] was founded by Father [[Junípero Serra|Junipero Serra]] and his accompanying Spanish soldiers.<ref>{{cite web |title=San Diego Mission Church (San Diego de Alcala) |url=https://www.nps.gov/nr/travel/ca/ca3.htm |archive-url=https://archive.today/20121214131447/http://www.nps.gov/nr/travel/ca/ca3.htm |url-status=dead |archive-date=December 14, 2012 |website=Early History of the California Coast |publisher=National Park Service |access-date=18 August 2020}}</ref> This marked the beginning of the [[Spanish missions in California|Mission system]], an era infamous for its brutality toward Indigenous peoples.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Castañeda |first1=Antonia I. |title=Sexual Violence in the Politics and Policies of Conquest: Amerindian Women and the Spanish Conquest of Alta California |url=https://www.law.berkeley.edu/php-programs/centers/crrj/zotero/loadfile.php?entity_key=FJSI8D4W |website=UC Berkeley Law School |publisher=University of California, Berkeley |access-date=18 August 2020}}</ref>
The Spanish period ended in 1821 with the signing of the [[Treaty of Córdoba]], which officially ended the [[Mexican War of Independence]].<ref>{{cite book |last1=Miguel-Stearns |first1=Teresa M. |title=Sources of State Practice in International Law |date=June 6, 2014 |publisher=Brill - Nijhoff |isbn=978-9004178861 |page=371 |edition=2 |url=https://digitalcommons.law.yale.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?referer=https://www.google.com/&httpsredir=1&article=1036&context=ylss |access-date=18 August 2020}}</ref>
==Mexican period== [[File:José María Estudillo.jpg|thumb|upright|left|Don [[José María Estudillo]], patriarch of the [[Estudillo family of California]], served twice as commandant of the [[Presidio of San Diego]].]]
The Mexican period of the U.S. southwest lasted from 1821 until 1848. The [[First Mexican Republic]] (1824-1835) had difficulties maintaining control over the region.<ref name="Mexican California">{{cite web |title=Mexican California |url=https://www.loc.gov/collections/california-first-person-narratives/articles-and-essays/early-california-history/mexican-california/ |website=California as I Saw It: First-Person Narratives of California's Early Years, 1849 to 1900 |publisher=Library of Congress |access-date=19 August 2020}}</ref> Sparsely populated and far from the economic and political center in Mexico City, the northern territories of [[Alta California]], [[Santa Fe de Nuevo México|Santa Fe de Nuevo Mexico]], and [[Coahuila y Tejas|Tejas]] were now free to engage in economic exchange with American traders, a practice which had been barred under the [[New Spain|Spanish Empire]]. This newfound freedom resulted in the development of strong economic and social ties between the economic elites of Tejas, Alta California, and the United States.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Camarillo |first1=Albert |title=Chicanos in a Changing Society: from Mexican Pueblos to American Barrios in Santa Barbara and Southern California, 1848-1930 |date=February 1, 1996 |publisher=Harvard University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=978-0674113978 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4z0cAQAAIAAJ |access-date=19 August 2020}}</ref>
Mexico's [[1824 Constitution of Mexico|Constitution of 1824]] guaranteed the equality of all Mexicans regardless of race. This had significant consequences in Alta California. In 1824, the [[Chumash people|Chumash]] of Santa Barbara coordinated a rebellion against the [[Spanish missions in California|Mission system]], protesting the inhumane conditions they continued to experience under the Jesuits.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Sandos |first1=James A |title=Levantamiento!: The 1824 Chumash Uprising Reconsidered |journal=Southern California Quarterly |date=July 1985 |volume=67 |issue=2 |pages=109–133 |doi=10.2307/41171145 |jstor=41171145 }}</ref> Simultaneously, the elites of the state, including the Vallejos, Alvarados, and Peraltas, urged for the total [[secularization]] of Mission lands. These agricultural families understood that if the Missions were secularized, the churches' large land-holdings would be distributed through land-grants by the regional government.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Hollenbaugh Aviña |first1=Rose |title=Spanish and Mexican Land Grants in California |date=November 1, 1976 |publisher=Ayer Company Publishing |isbn=978-0405094835 }}</ref> The Mexican government eventually acquiesced, and the Mission system was abolished through the [[Mexican secularization act of 1833|Secularization Act of 1833]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Manuel P. |first1=Servín |title=The Secularization of the California Missions: A Reappraisal |journal=Southern California Quarterly |date=June 1965 |volume=47 |issue=2 |pages=133–149 |doi=10.2307/41169919 |jstor=41169919 }}</ref> As a result, the large land-holdings of the Missions were distributed through grants to the state's wealthiest families, including the Vallejos, Alvarados, Peraltas, Carillos, de la Guerras, and Picos.<ref>{{cite web |title=Secularization and the Rancho Era, 1834-1846 |url=https://www.nps.gov/goga/learn/historyculture/upload/Chapter-8.pdf |website=Ohlone/Costanoan Indians of the San Francisco Peninsula and their Neighbors, Yesterday and Today |publisher=National Park Service |access-date=19 August 2020}}</ref> The California neophytes, rather than being freed, became laborers on the [[Ranchos of California|Ranchos]] the Californios created. These ranchos were compared to [[Plantation complexes in the Southern United States|Plantations]], and the indigenous laborers were often "treated worse than slaves".<ref>{{cite book |last1=Almaguer |first1=Tomás |title=Racial Fault Lines: The Historical Origins of White Supremacy in California |date=December 30, 2008 |publisher=University of California Press |location=Berkeley |isbn=978-0520257863 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=32zxH7dvAWcC |access-date=19 August 2020}}</ref>
[[File:Pío de Jesus Pico 1858.jpg|thumb|upright|[[Pío Pico]], a Californio ranchero and the last Mexican governor of Alta California]]
During this period, California and Texas were flooded by Anglo American businessmen.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Hackel |first1=Steven W. |title=Land, and, Labor, and Production: The Colonial Economy of Spanish and Mexican California |publisher=University of California Press}}</ref> These migrants were welcomed into the region, and intermarriage between U.S. men and Mexican women was common practice, as it was a way to secure business loyalties through familial bonds.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Casas |first1=Maria Raquel |title=Married to A Daughter of the Land: Spanish-Mexican Women and Interethnic Marriage in California, 1820-1880 |date=March 28, 2009 |publisher=University of Nevada Press |isbn=978-0874177787 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=oCuVDwAAQBAJ |access-date=19 August 2020}}</ref> Yet the continual flood of Americans into the Northern territories grew into an ever-larger issue for the Mexican government. In 1835, less than 14 years after Mexico's independence from Spain, American ranchers in Tejas [[Texas Revolution|revolted]] against Mexico and declared themselves the [[Republic of Texas]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Texas Declaration of Independence |url=https://www.tsl.texas.gov/treasures/republic/declare-01.html |website=The Republic of Texas - The Texas Revolution |publisher=Texas State Library and Archives Commission |access-date=19 August 2020}}</ref> Mexico's President [[Antonio López de Santa Anna|Santa Anna]] led an army to put down the ''[[Filibuster (military)|filibusteros]]'', but after initial victories at [[Battle of the Alamo|The Alamo]] and [[Battle of Goliad|Goliad]], Santa Anna's army surrendered defeat on April 21, 1836.<ref>{{cite web |title=The Republic of Texas - The Texas Revolution |url=https://www.tsl.texas.gov/treasures/republic/san-jacinto.html |website=The Battle of San Jacinto |publisher=Texas State Library and Archives Commission |access-date=19 August 2020}}</ref> The Republic of Texas was never recognized as a sovereign state by the government of Mexico, which refused to recognize the [[Treaties of Velasco|treaties]] signed by Santa Anna, as he was a hostage when he signed them.<ref>{{cite web |title=The Treaties of Velasco |url=https://www.tsl.texas.gov/treasures/republic/velasco-01.html |website=The Republic of Texas - The Texas Revolution |publisher=Texas State Library and Archives Commission |access-date=19 August 2020}}</ref> In the new Republic of Texas, [[Tejanos]] faced severe educational and economic discrimination.<ref>{{cite web |title=Tejano Voices and the Demand for Inclusion |url=https://www.tsl.texas.gov/lobbyexhibits/struggles-tejano |website=Texans' Struggle for Freedom and Equality |publisher=Texas State Library and Archives Commission |access-date=19 August 2020}}</ref> Meanwhile, Mexico struggled to maintain its hold on political stability following the rebellion in Texas. In fact, the nation went through eighteen [[List of heads of state of Mexico#Centralist Republic (1835–1846)|Presidential administrations]] from 1836 to 1845.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Costeloe |first1=Michael P. |title=The Central Republic in Mexico, 1835-1846: 'Hombres de Bien' in the Age of Santa Anna |date=August 21, 2008 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=London |isbn=978-0521530644 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=iI9xFYwnTt0C |access-date=19 August 2020}}</ref>
In 1845, newly elected U.S. President [[James K. Polk]], aware of the lingering instability in Mexico and eager to [[Manifest destiny|expand]] the United States to the Pacific Ocean, propositioned Mexico to purchase Alta California and Santa Fe de Nuevo México.<ref>{{cite web |title=The Mexican American War |url=https://www.pbs.org/wgbh/americanexperience/features/grant-mexican-american-war/ |website=American Experience |publisher=PBS |access-date=19 August 2020}}</ref> The offer was flatly rejected by the Mexican government. Polk responded by moving U.S. troops, led by [[Zachary Taylor]], into the [[Nueces Strip]] to provoke the Mexican Army into attacking the U.S. in order to get Congress to declare war.<ref>{{cite web |title=Mexican American War |url=https://www.pbs.org/wgbh/americanexperience/features/grant-mexican-american-war/ |website=American Experience |publisher=PBS |access-date=19 August 2020}}</ref> Taylor set up camp in a disputed border territory and refused to leave, even after repeated warnings from the Mexican government.<ref>{{cite web |title=Zachary Taylor |url=https://library.uta.edu/usmexicowar/item?bio_id=17&nation=US |website=A Continent Divided: The U.S.-Mexico War |publisher=The University of Texas Arlington |access-date=19 August 2020}}</ref> After several skirmishes in the disputed zone, the U.S. Congress declared war on May 13, 1846.<ref>{{cite web |title=The Senate Votes for War against Mexico |url=https://www.senate.gov/artandhistory/history/minute/Senate_Votes_for_War_against_Mexico.htm |website=United States Senate |access-date=19 August 2020}}</ref>
===Mexican–American War=== {{Main|Mexican–American War|Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo}}
====Conflict and battles==== [[File:Daguerreotype of the Mexican American War ca 1847 1848-restored.jpg|thumb|upright|left|U.S. battalion in [[Saltillo]]]]
The [[Mexican–American War]] of 1846–48 would prove one of the most consequential events for Mexican Americans in United States history. In 1846, U.S. general [[Stephen W. Kearny|Stephen W. Kearney]] marched into New Mexico, where he faced little resistance from the Mexican residents of Santa Fe.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hollon |first1=Cory S. |title='A Leap in the Dark': The Campaign to Conquer New Mexico and California, 1846–1847 |journal=Army History |date=Winter 2015 |volume=94 |issue=94 |pages=6–26 |jstor=26300360 }}</ref> He installed local elite nuevomexicanos as the head of the [[U.S. provisional government of New Mexico|provisional military government]], which largely placated the residents of the territory.<ref name="American Language">{{cite book |last1=Lozano |first1=Rosina |title=An American Language: The History of Spanish in the United States |date=April 24, 2018 |publisher=University of California Press |location=Berkeley |isbn=978-0520297074 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=mzZODwAAQBAJ |access-date=20 August 2020}}</ref> In his first public speech to residents, he proclaimed the forthcoming equality of an American regime, claiming that, "''El fuerte, y el debil; el rico y el pobre; son iguales ante la ley ... protegeré los derechos de todos con igualdad''" ("both the strong and the weak, the rich and the poor ... everybody is equal before the law and will be protected by the same equal rights").<ref name="American Language"/>
New Mexico at first accepted the United States' military occupation without resistance, but within a year of Kearney's annexation of the territory, there was a widescale uprising.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Gonzalez |first1=Albert |title=The 1847 Revolt: The Beginning of Modern Taos |date=2 November 2015 |url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=i0Puc58XP4k |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/varchive/youtube/20211212/i0Puc58XP4k| archive-date=2021-12-12 |url-status=live|publisher=University of New Mexico, Taos |access-date=20 August 2020}}{{cbignore}}</ref> Nuevomexicano [[Pablo Montoya]] and Taos Puebloan [[Tomás Romero (revolutionary)|Tomás Romero]] together led the 1847 [[Taos Revolt]], which resulted in the execution of [[Charles Bent]], Taos sheriff Stephen Lee, Judge Cornelio Vigil, Bent's brother-in-law Pablo Jaramillo, the attorney J. W. Leal, and a young boy named Narciso Beaubien.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Beyreis |first1=David C. |title=Dangerous Alliances in the New Mexico Borderlands: Charles Bent and the Limits of Family Networks |journal=Journal of the Early Republic |date=Spring 2019 |volume=39 |issue=1 |pages=57–80 |doi=10.1353/jer.2019.0005 |s2cid=151104726 }}</ref> The U.S. military moved quickly to quash the revolt, and the fighting ended in New Mexico after the Nuevomexicanos' subsequent defeats in the [[Red River Canyon affair|Battle of Red River Canyon]], the [[Las Vegas affair|Battle of Las Vegas]], and the [[Cienega affair|Battle of Cienega Creek]].
[[File:Pedro Vander Linden.JPG|thumb|upright|Battlefield during the U.S.-Mexico War (April 18, 1847)]]
In California, residents also fought the American army. In 1847, Californios staged battles throughout Southern California against the American [[Conquest of California|conquest]], including the [[Siege of Los Angeles|Battle of Los Angeles]] and the [[Battle of San Pasqual]] (present-day San Diego). The United States Navy, believing that cutting off supplies to the Californios would ensure their defeat, implemented blockades along the Pacific Coast and Gulf of Mexico. As a result of these actions, the Navy's [[Pacific Squadron#Mexican–American War|Pacific Squadron]] subsequently conquered Monterey, San Francisco, and San Diego, virtually guaranteeing victory for the U.S. in California.<ref>{{cite web |title=Mexican-American War |url=https://www.history.navy.mil/browse-by-topic/wars-conflicts-and-operations/mexican-american-war.html |website=Naval History and Command Center |publisher=United States Navy |access-date=20 August 2020}}</ref> The war ended on September 8, 1847, when [[Winfield Scott]] took control over Mexico City in the [[Battle for Mexico City]]. The U.S. and Mexico soon after entered negotiations for conditions of surrender.<ref>{{cite web |title=Mexican American War: Timeline of Major Events |url=http://umich.edu/~ac213/student_projects06/magsylje/timeline.html |publisher=University of Michigan |access-date=20 August 2020}}</ref>
====Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo==== [[File:Mexican Cession.png|thumb|left|Lands ceded to the U.S. through the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo]]
On July 4, 1848, the United States and Mexico ratified the [[Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo]], which formally ended the war.<ref>{{cite web |title=Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo |url=https://avalon.law.yale.edu/19th_century/guadhida.asp |website=The Avalon Project: Documents in Law, History, and Diplomacy |publisher=Yale Law School |access-date=20 August 2020}}</ref> Under the conditions of defeat, Mexico also ceded more 525,000 square miles of territory.<ref>{{cite web |title=The Annexation of Texas, the Mexican-American War, and the Treaty of Guadalupe-Hidalgo, 1845–1848 |url=https://history.state.gov/milestones/1830-1860/texas-annexation |website=Office of the Historian |publisher=United States Department of State |access-date=20 August 2020}}</ref> The treaty guaranteed full [[Citizenship of the United States|citizenship]] for all former-Mexican citizens who requested it, as well as formal U.S. citizenship to all who remained in the U.S. territories for one full year after the signing of the treaty.<ref>{{cite web |title=Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo |url=https://www.digitalhistory.uh.edu/disp_textbook.cfm?smtID=3&psid=1141 |website=Digital History |publisher=University of Houston |access-date=20 August 2020}}</ref> The full text of the treaty also included Article X, a provision which would have guaranteed rights to all holders of Mexican land-grants.<ref>{{cite web |title=The Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo |url=https://www.loc.gov/rr/hispanic/ghtreaty/ |website=Hispanic Reading Room |publisher=Library of Congress |access-date=20 August 2020}}</ref> Fearing that Article X would give Tejanos too much protection over their land, the [[30th United States Congress|U.S. Congress]] quietly removed it from the final version, claiming that it was "redundant".<ref name="American Language"/> The law of the treaty guaranteed the "treaty citizens" full rights to their land if they were able to prove ownership, but the ability to prove ownership proved difficult.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hornbeck |first1=David |title=The Patenting of California's Private Land Claims, 1851-1885 |journal=Geographical Review |date=October 1979 |volume=69 |issue=4 |pages=434–448 |doi=10.2307/214806 |jstor=214806 |bibcode=1979GeoRv..69..434H |url=https://digitalcommons.csumb.edu/hornbeck_usa_4_b/1/ |access-date=20 August 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref>
Furthermore, the treaty was signed without any formal legal guarantees that all Mexican citizens would be treated as citizens under U.S. law. Under the Constitution of 1824, all Mexicans, regardless of their race, had gained recognition as citizens, yet the United States, under the [[Naturalization Act of 1790]], only recognized "white persons" as eligible for citizenship through [[naturalization]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Smith |first1=Marian L. |title=Race, Nationality, and Reality |url=https://www.archives.gov/publications/prologue/2002/summer/immigration-law-1.html |website=[[Prologue (magazine)|Prologue]] |date=15 August 2016 |publisher=National Archives |access-date=20 August 2020}}</ref> The treaty thus legally classified Mexicans as "white" in order to allow them U.S. citizenship, yet this was applied only to the Spanish Mexican elite, mestizos, and assimilated Indigenous peoples.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Montoya |first1=Margaret E. |title=Latinos and the Law |url=https://www.nps.gov/articles/latinothemestudylaw.htm |website=American Latino Theme Study: Law |publisher=National Park Service |access-date=20 August 2020}}</ref> Without explicit legal protection, all unassimilated and autonomous [[Indigenous peoples of the North American Southwest|Native Americans]] in the new territories were thereby subjected to prior United States' [[Outline of United States federal Indian law and policy|case law]] relating to "Amerindian" tribes.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Klein |first1=Christine A. |title=Treaties of Conquest: Property Rights, Indian Treaties, and the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo |journal=New Mexico Law Review |date=Spring 1996 |volume=26 |issue=2 |url=https://digitalrepository.unm.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?referer=https://www.google.com/&httpsredir=1&article=1854&context=nmlr |access-date=20 August 2020}}</ref>
==Early-American period== [[File:JosFChaves.jpg|thumb|upright|[[José Francisco Chaves]], territorial representative for the [[New Mexico Territory]]]] [[File:Angustias_de_la_Guerra_Ord_(cropped).jpg|thumb|upright|[[Angustias de la Guerra]], a [[Californio]] author who played an important role in defending [[women's property rights]] in the [[California Constitution]].]] The early-American period in the U.S. southwest was a period marked by [[Anti-Mexican sentiment#1840s to 1890s|violence]] and land loss. Under the terms of the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo, all Mexicans were granted formal citizenship rights as American citizens, yet widespread dissatisfaction emerged amongst the Mexican Americans.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Weber |first1=David |title=Foreigners in Their Native Land: Historical Roots of the Mexican Americans |date=March 1, 2004 |publisher=University of New Mexico Press |location=Albuquerque |isbn=978-0826335104 |edition=2 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nozzhdtLVeYC |access-date=25 August 2020}}</ref> Despite the treaty pledges of full and equal citizenship, rampant discrimination and violence were immediate and widespread.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Gómez |first1=Laura E. |title=Off-White in an Age of White Supremacy: Mexican Elites and the Rights of Indians and Blacks in Nineteenth-Century New Mexico |url=https://www.law.uh.edu/hernandez50/LauraGomez.pdf |website=Law Center |publisher=University of Houston |access-date=25 August 2020}}</ref>
Realizing the potential dissatisfaction which the former Mexicans would face as American citizens, Mexico's president [[José Joaquín de Herrera]] issued a recolonization plan in August 1848, which promised economic resources and land for any former-Mexican who returned to Mexico.<ref name="The Decree of 19 August 1848">{{cite web |last1=Hernandez |first1=Jose Angel |title=The Decree of 19 August 1848: The First Repatriation Commissions and Postwar Settlements along the US-Mexico Borderlands |url=https://www.uh.edu/class/cmas/publications/Working%20Papers/The%20Decree%20of%2019%20August%201848%20The%20First%20Repatriation%20Commissions%20and%20Postwar%20Settlements%20along%20the%20US-Mexico%20Borderlands |website=The Center for Mexican American Studies |publisher=University of Houston |access-date=25 August 2020}}</ref> The commission hired three commissioners to recruit repatriates. Father [[Ramón Ortiz y Miera]], the New Mexico commissioner, encouraged resettlement by criticizing the inferior status of the "treaty citizens".<ref name="The Decree of 19 August 1848"/> Such arguments had a strong resonance for the former Mexicans, as twenty-five percent of the country's Mexican American population repatriated after the war.<ref name="The Decree of 19 August 1848"/>
Yet, the United States, despite guaranteeing the rights of former Mexicans to return to Mexico, developed legal arguments in order to institute formal barriers against this resettlement movement.<ref name="The Decree of 19 August 1848"/> The U.S. Secretary of War [[George W. Crawford]] even claimed that repatriation was prohibited. Because New Mexico served as the primary buffer between American settlers and indigenous groups, the U.S. believed it was in their best interest if the treaty citizens remained in the U.S. to maintain a "civilized" presence in the region and protect against Native encroachment.<ref name="American Language"/>
===Society=== [[File:Mexican Caballero. San Antonio, Texas.jpg|thumb|left|A vaquero in [[San Antonio]], Texas]] In 1850, the [[1850 United States census|United States census]] counted approximately 80,000 Mexican treaty citizens living across California, Texas, and New Mexico.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Nostrand |first1=Richard L. |title=Mexican Americans Circa 1850 |journal=Annals of the Association of American Geographers |date=September 1975 |volume=65 |issue=3 |pages=378–390 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-8306.1975.tb01046.x |jstor=2561888 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/2561888 |access-date=25 August 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref> New Mexico was the largest United States territory at the time, with around 61,547 inhabitants, about 95% of whom were former Mexican citizens.<ref name="New Mexico Census">{{cite web |title=New Mexico |url=https://www2.census.gov/library/publications/decennial/1940/population-volume-1/33973538v1ch07.pdf |website=U.S. Census Library|publisher=United States Census |access-date=25 August 2020}}</ref> The majority of the Nuevomexicanos lived in rural communities with populations of fewer than 1,000 people. According to the 1850 census, the three most common occupations held by Nuevomexicanos were farmer, laborer, and servant.<ref name="New Mexico Census"/>
In [[South Texas]], Tejanos lived in a three-tiered society. At the top were the landed elite, who owned huge ranchos, many of which had been granted by the Spanish colonial empire and turned into [[hacienda]]s.<ref name="Gone to Texas"/> The elite retained their economic dominance through cattle ranching.<ref name="Gone to Texas">{{cite book |last1=Campbell |first1=Randolph B. |title=Gone to Texas: A History of the Lone Star State |date=March 15, 2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=978-0190642396 |pages=190–191 |edition=3 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hYUrXmQgmV0C |access-date=24 August 2020}}</ref> Small landowners occupied the second rung of the South Texas economic and social ladder.<ref name="Gone to Texas"/> These landowners lived in one-room [[adobe|adobe houses]] and spent most of their time caring for their horses and cattle.<ref name="Gone to Texas"/> Finally, South Texas had a third lower class composed primarily of [[peon|peóns]], [[vaquero]]s, and cartmen. Peóns had a status above slaves in antebellum Texas but below that of free men.<ref name="Gone to Texas"/> Peóns worked at the direction of the patróns—planting and harvesting crops, herding goats, digging wells, and doing any sort of manual labor necessary. They lived in tiny one-room [[jacal]]es, huts with walls of mud or any other material available and thatched roofs.<ref name="Gone to Texas"/> Anglo migrants to Texas believed the jacales were evidence of the Tejanos' "sub-human" and "primitive" nature.<ref>{{cite book |last1=De León |first1=Arnoldo |title=They Called Them Greasers: Anglo Attitudes toward Mexicans in Texas, 1821–1900 |date=January 1, 1983 |publisher=University of Texas Press |isbn=978-0292780545 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BJkGwn6H14IC |access-date=24 August 2020}}</ref>
[[File:At the Ranchero, Mendocino County, California.jpg|thumb|left|A [[Pomo]] woman at the [[Rancho Yokaya|Mendocino Rancho]] in California]]
In California, native-born californios mostly lived in small farming and ranching communities in the south. The two largest cities in 1850 were [[Pueblo de Los Ángeles|Los Ángeles]], with a population of 3,500, and [[Santa Barbara, California|Santa Barbara]], where 1,185 people lived.<ref>{{cite web |title=California |url=https://www2.census.gov/library/publications/decennial/1850/1850a/1850a-47.pdf |website=Census of 1850: California |publisher=United States Census |access-date=26 August 2020}}</ref> While elite Californios, such as [[Pablo de la Guerra]] and [[Luis María Peralta]], held political and economic power in the state, they represented only 3 percent of the population in 1850.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Monroy |first1=Douglas |title=Thrown Among Strangers: The Making of Mexican Culture in Frontier California |date=May 25, 1993 |publisher=University of California Press |location=Berkeley |isbn=978-0520082755 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=kgqegBKDclgC |access-date=24 August 2020}}</ref> The vast majority of landed Californios were subsistence farmers who based their livelihood on their small plots of land. In the southern coastal regions, business-ownership and manual labor were also common occupations for general Californios. For the [[Indigenous peoples of California]], the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo's failure to ensure full citizenship and protections had dire consequences. They were subjected to a systematic [[genocide]], funded by the state of California.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Field |first1=Margaret A. |title=Genocide and the Indians of California, 1769-1873 |url=https://scholarworks.umb.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1142&context=masters_theses |website=Scholarworks |publisher=University of Massachusetts, Boston |access-date=25 August 2020}}</ref> The [[California genocide|California Genocide]] killed around 90% of California's Native population during the early-American period, clearing the way for full-scale Anglo colonization.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Madley |first1=Benjamin |title=An American Genocide: The United States and the California Indian Catastrophe, 1846-1873 |date=May 24, 2016 |publisher=Yale University Press |location=New Haven |isbn=978-0300181364 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sycVDAAAQBAJ |access-date=25 August 2020}}</ref>
===Government=== [[File:José Manuel Gallegos.jpg|thumb|right|[[José Manuel Gallegos]], delegate from the [[New Mexico Territory|Territory of New Mexico]] to the U.S. House of Representatives]]
Over time, the social, economic, and legal position of the Spanish Mexicans diminished, largely through political disenfranchisement and large-scale land loss. These two processes were facilitated through the elimination of political, linguistic, and property rights. In two decades, Americans seized complete control over the apparatuses of political power across the U.S. Southwest.
[[José Manuel Gallegos]] was sworn into [[United States House of Representatives|Congress]] in 1853 as the first nuevomexicano territorial representative to Congress. He spoke only Spanish, which was not a problem for his first two terms. After he successfully ran for reelection in 1856, however, his bilingual opponent [[Miguel Antonio Otero (born 1829)|Miguel A. Otero]], contested the election results.<ref>{{cite web |title=Miguel Antonio Otero |url=https://history.house.gov/People/Detail/19218?ret=True |website=History, Art, and Archives |publisher=United States House of Representatives |access-date=26 August 2020}}</ref> Otero claimed Gallegos' inability to speak English disqualified him. Gallego made an impassioned self-defense in Spanish on the House floor, where he protested the "disappointment" he felt from the "sneers" of his colleagues.<ref>{{cite web |title=José Manuel Gallegos |url=https://history.house.gov/People/Detail/13591 |website=History, Art, and Archives |publisher=United States House of Representatives |access-date=26 August 2020}}</ref> Nevertheless, Otero's bid was successful, and he replaced Gallego as the territorial representative of New Mexico.<ref>{{cite web |title=José Manuel Gallegos |url=https://www.loc.gov/rr/hispanic/congress/gallegos.html |website=Hispanic Americans in Congress, 1822-1995 |publisher=Library of Congress |access-date=26 August 2020}}</ref>
California's first U.S. senator, [[John C. Frémont]], introduced legislation for the federal government to arbitrate land claim settlements.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Gates |first1=Paul W. |title=The Frémont-Jones Scramble for California Land Claims |journal=Southern California Quarterly |date=Spring 1974 |volume=56 |issue=1 |pages=13–44 |doi=10.2307/41170514 |jstor=41170514 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/41170514 |access-date=26 August 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref> After the removal of Article X from the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo, treaty citizens were stripped of any formal protection of their land rights.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Rodríguez |first1=Roberto |last2=Gonzales |first2=Patrisia |title=The Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo Still Relevant Today |url=http://umich.edu/~ac213/student_projects06/magsylje/outcome.html |website=DePaul University Libraries |publisher=DePaul University |access-date=26 August 2020}}</ref> After gold was discovered at [[Sutter's Mill]] in [[Coloma, California]] in 1848, a massive migration flooded the state, sparking the [[California Gold Rush|Gold Rush]]. By 1852, the population of California had grown from 8,000 in 1848 to 260,000.<ref>{{cite web |title=California Population by Ethnic Groups, 1790-1880 |url=http://explore.museumca.org/goldrush/curriculum/1stcalifornians/resourcesix.htm |website=Myth and Reality: The California Gold Rush and Its Legacy |publisher=Museum of California |access-date=26 August 2020}}</ref> These gold miners were largely landless and asserted ownership over California lands. The [[California Land Act of 1851]], also known as the Gwin Act, after California senator [[William M. Gwin]], created a presidentially appointed commission to settle disputed claims between the landholders and Anglo miners.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Gates |first1=Paul |title=California Land Act of 1851 |url=https://online.ucpress.edu/ch/article/50/4/395/30778/The-California-Land-Act-of-1851 |journal=California Historical Quarterly |date=December 1971 |volume=50 |issue=4 |pages=395–430 |publisher=University of California Press |doi=10.2307/25157352 |jstor=25157352 |access-date=26 August 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref>
===Land=== [[File:Historic American Buildings Survey Robert W. Kerrigan, Photographer Original- May 1936 VIEW FROM NORTHWEST - Francisco Sanchez Adobe, Linda Mar Boulevard and Adobe Drive, Pacifica HABS CAL,41- ,1-3.tif|thumb|left|The [[Sánchez Adobe Park|Sanchez Adobe]], part of the [[Rancho San Pedro (Sánchez)|Rancho San Pedro]], purchased by U.S. General Edward Kirkpatrick.]]
In California's post-war years, land proved to be the most contentious and sought-after commodity. The [[California Land Act of 1851]] established a commission to determine the validity of Spanish and Mexican land grants.<ref>{{cite web |title=California Land Act of 1851 |url=https://www.loc.gov/law/help/statutes-at-large/31st-congress/session-2/c31s2ch41.pdf |website=31st Congress of the United States |publisher=Library of Congress |access-date=26 August 2020}}</ref> In order to prove ownership over the property, landowners needed to both provide evidence of the initial grant, as well as submit proof they had made "structural and pastoral" improvements to the land.<ref name="American Language"/> If they could not, Anglo [[Squatting|squatters]] were free to claim ownership if they had "improved the land", a contentious claim which was often difficult to disprove.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Pisani |first1=Donald J. |title=Squatter Law in California, 1850-1858 |journal=Western Historical Quarterly |date=Autumn 1994 |volume=25 |issue=3 |pages=277–310 |doi=10.2307/971109 |jstor=971109 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/971109 |access-date=26 August 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Additionally, because many of the initial Spanish and Mexican ''diseños''" grants were vague, merely describing the natural boundaries of the property, contestations over the boundaries of ranchos were difficult for the Californios to prove.<ref name="A Tale of Three Land Grants">{{cite journal |last1=Vaught |first1=David |title=A Tale of Three Land Grants on the Northern California Borderlands |journal=Agricultural History |date=Spring 2004 |volume=78 |issue=2 |pages=140–154 |doi=10.1525/ah.2004.78.2.140 |jstor=3744897 |s2cid=143254378 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/3744897 |access-date=26 August 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref> All documents submitted in support of a claim also needed to be translated into English. Some firms, like [[Halleck, Peachy & Billings]], gained popular reputations as "friends to the Mexicans" for helping the Californios navigate the new American court system, but most land lawyers used the situation to their advantage, drawing out the cases and charging exorbitant fees for their services.<ref name="Decline of the Californios">{{cite book |last1=Pitt |first1=Leonard |last2=Gutierrez |first2=Ramon A. |title=Decline of the Californios: A Social History of the Spanish-Speaking Californias, 1846-1890 |date=1999 |publisher=University of California Press |location=Berkeley |isbn=978-0520219588 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ZRnjfZeIt7UC |access-date=25 August 2020}}</ref>
[[File:North wing, oblique also showing the courtyard at left and the north elevation at right. (recreation of HABS no. CA-38-P152-6) - Vallejo Adobe, Adobe Road at Casa Grande, HABS CAL,49-PET.V,1-25.tif|thumb|right|[[Rancho Petaluma]], which was subdivided and sold by [[Mariano Guadalupe Vallejo|Mariano G. Vallejo]] to pay for his attorneys' fees.]]
In most instances, land claim cases often proved simply too expensive for most Californios to litigate. While the majority of cases were ultimately ruled in favor of the Californios, the average wait-time for a case to be resolved was seventeen years.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hornbeck |first1=David |title=The Patenting of California's Private Land Claims, 1851-1885 |journal=Geographical Review |date=October 1979 |volume=69 |issue=4 |pages=434–448 |doi=10.2307/214806 |jstor=214806 |bibcode=1979GeoRv..69..434H |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/214806 |access-date=26 August 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref> During that time, most Californio families were forced to sell portions of their property to pay their attorneys' fees.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Lopes |first1=Adrian |last2=Libecap |first2=Gary B. |last3=Leuck |first3=Dean |title=A Legacy of History: 19th Century Land Demarcation and Agriculture in California |url=https://www.econ.pitt.edu/sites/default/files/Lueck.dean.15.pdf |website=Department of Economics |publisher=University of Pittsburgh |access-date=26 August 2020}}</ref> In addition, all land commission hearings were held in San Francisco, which created an additional and expensive barrier for Southern California landowners.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Olmsted |first1=Nancy J. |title=Mexican Land Claims—The U.S. Land Commission and The Burden of Proof, 1851-1854 |url=https://www.foundsf.org/index.php?title=Mexican_Land_Claims—The_U.S._Land_Commission_and_The_Burden_of_Proof,_1851-1854 |website=San Francisco's Digital Archive |publisher=Found SF |access-date=26 August 2020}}</ref> Mexican American landowners, in general, faced often insurmountable odds in proving ownership of their lands, which some argue was the intent of the convoluted system.<ref name="Decline of the Californios"/> [[Mariano Guadalupe Vallejo]] wrote, "It requires a lot of work and money that I don't have to locate [possible witnesses], and afterwards to pay for notarized affidavits and English translations for each."<ref name="American Language"/>
Some Californios, however, attempted to use their positions of influence and power to fight against the legal discrimination. [[Pablo de la Guerra]], a Santa Barbara landowner, asserted his political influence as a state senator and then [[Lieutenant Governor of California|lieutenant governor]] to vocally critique the American legal system, which treated Mexicans as a "conquered and inferior race".<ref name="American Language"/> De la Guerra complained that the testimony of white people was taken more seriously in the court system than that of Mexicans; he said, "A disgraceful distinction between white testimony and ours was indelicately paraded."<ref name="American Language"/> De la Guerra would have to fight even to maintain his right to hold political office; the landmark case ''People v. de la Guerra'' decided that despite charges otherwise, De la Guerra could hold political office in the United States.<ref>{{cite web |title=The Public Land Commission |url=http://www.digitalhistory.uh.edu/disp_textbook.cfm?smtID=3&psid=572 |website=Digital History |publisher=University of Houston |access-date=26 August 2020}}</ref> Nevertheless, Anglos came to dominate the political and economic landscape of California, as not even one Mexican family retained their wealth in the early-American period.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Suárez |first1=Camille Alexandrite |title=How California Was Won: Race, Citizenship, And The Colonial Roots Of California, 1846 – 1879 |url=https://repository.upenn.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=5277&context=edissertations |website=Scholarly Commons |publisher=University of Pennsylvania |access-date=26 August 2020}}</ref>
[[File:Historic American Buildings Survey Willis Foster, Photographer Photo Taken- February 1940 View of end - Fulgencio Higuera Adobe, Fremont, Alameda County, CA HABS CAL,1-WARM,2-2.tif|thumb|right|[[Rancho Agua Caliente (Alameda County)|Rancho Agua Caliente]] in [[Fremont, California]], which was subdivided and purchased by [[Leland Stanford]].]]
In [[History of Texas (1845–1860)|Texas]], land grants were never subject to a federally legislated commission. Because Texas had attained statehood in 1845, it retained jurisdiction over the entirety of its border regions and thus claimed exemption from the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Richerson |first1=Rose |title=Spanish and Mexican Land Grants and Heirs' Rights to Unclaimed Mineral Estates in Texas |journal=Texas A&M Journal of Property Law |date=2014 |volume=2 |issue=1 |url=https://scholarship.law.tamu.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1025&context=journal-of-property-law |access-date=26 August 2020}}</ref> The Texas state government thus took the matter of land grants into its own hands, when governor [[Peter Hansborough Bell|Peter H. Bell]] appointed [[William H. Bourland]] and James Miller to determine the validity of Spanish and Mexican land holdings in the state.<ref name="The Bourland and Miller Commission">{{cite journal |last1=de la Teja |first1=Jesús F. |last2=Greaser |first2=Galen D. |title=Quieting Title to Spanish and Mexican Land Grants in the Trans-Nueces: The Bourland and Miller Commission, 1850-1852 |journal=The Southwestern Historical Quarterly |date=April 1992 |volume=95 |issue=4 |pages=445–464 |jstor=30242000 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30242000 |access-date=26 August 2020}}</ref> At its first hearing in [[Webb County, Texas|Webb County]], the Bourland-Miller Commission faced significant opposition from the local Mexican American landowners, who claimed that the commission had been established in order to seize the property of Tejanos and take away their full rights.<ref name="Anglos and Mexicans in the Making of Texas">{{cite book |last1=Montejano |first1=David |title=[[Anglos and Mexicans in the Making of Texas, 1836-1986]] |date=September 1, 1987 |publisher=[[University of Texas Press]] |isbn=978-0292775961 |edition=1}} - [https://books.google.com/books?id=83xKDQAAQBAJ Read online at] [[Google Books]]. Access date: August 26, 2020</ref> Miller and Bourland were able to win over the landowning elite of the [[Laredo, Texas|Laredo]] area, however, by conducting an "impartial" proceeding, which resulted in all the Tejano families retaining their landholdings.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Alonzo |first1=Armando C. |title=Mexican-American Land Grant Adjudication |url=https://www.tshaonline.org/handbook/entries/mexican-american-land-grant-adjudication |publisher=Texas State Historical Association |access-date=26 August 2020}}</ref> In the rest of the state, however, the commission was less favorable to the land-owning claims of the Tejanos. In areas of Southwest Texas, fewer than half of all land grants were recognized as legitimate by the commission, and many of the ones which were recognized as legitimate were already owned by Anglo Texans.<ref name="Anglos and Mexicans in the Making of Texas"/>
===Violence=== {{Seealso|Lynching of Hispanic and Latino Americans}} [[File:Lynching of Francisco Arias and José Chamales.jpg|thumb|left|150px|Two Mexican American men lynched in [[Santa Cruz, California|Santa Cruz]], California]]
In addition to using legalistic maneuvers to seize economic and political control, American settlers also used physical violence as a tactic to control the conquered Mexican American population. In California, Mexican Americans were driven out of their homes, forced out of mining camps in gold-rich areas, barred from testifying in court, and gradually segregated into [[barrio]]s.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Camarillo |first1=Albert |title=Chicanos in California: A History of Mexican Americans in California |date=January 1, 1984 |publisher=Boyd & Fraser Publishing Co |isbn=978-0878351282 |edition=1 |url=https://archive.org/details/chicanosincalifo00albe |access-date=26 August 2020}}</ref> There was resistance to this violence, as men like [[Tiburcio Vásquez]] turned to banditry to resist the domination of the Anglos.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Boessenecker |first1=John |title=Bandido: The Life and Times of Tiburcio Vasquez |date=July 17, 2014 |publisher=University of Oklahoma Press |isbn=978-0806146812 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7Nw9o6dpn4YC |access-date=26 August 2020}}</ref> As a method to keep Mexicans in their place, the American settlers lynched Mexicans. Between 1848 and 1860, at least 163 Mexicans were lynched in California alone.<ref name="The Lynching of Persons of Mexican Origin or Descent in the United States, 1848 to 1928">{{cite journal |last1=Carrigan |first1=William D. |last2=Webb |first2=Clive |title=The Lynching of Persons of Mexican Origin or Descent in the United States, 1848 to 1928 |journal=Journal of Social History |date=Winter 2003 |volume=37 |issue=2 |pages=411–438 |doi=10.1353/jsh.2003.0169 |jstor=3790404 |s2cid=143851795 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/3790404 |access-date=25 August 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref>
Between 1848 and 1879, Mexican Americans across the United States were lynched at an unprecedented rate of 473 per 100,000 of population. Most of these lynchings were not instances of "[[frontier justice]]"— out of 597 total victims, only 64 were lynched in areas which lacked a formal judicial system.<ref name="The Lynching of Persons of Mexican Origin or Descent in the United States, 1848 to 1928"/> The majority of lynching victims were denied access to a trial while others were convicted in unfair trials. Mexican Americans had no avenues for justice in the early-American period. As a result, many of the [[folk hero]]es of this period were considered to be outlaws: robbers, [[Social banditry|social bandits]], and freedom fighters.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Paredes |first1=Américo |title='With His Pistol in His Hand:' A Border Ballad and Its Hero |date=June 1, 1958 |publisher=University of Texas Press |isbn=978-0292701281 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zQxbHl8xGJAC |access-date=25 August 2020}}</ref>
In Texas, Mexican Americans also resisted the violence of the U.S. settlers. [[Juan Cortina]] began the [[Cortina Troubles|First Cortina War]] in 1859 when he shot the [[Brownsville, Texas|Brownsville]] town Marshall, Robert Shears, for brutalizing Cortina's former employees.<ref>{{cite web |title=Latinos: Juan Cortina |url=https://repository.si.edu/bitstream/handle/10088/19510/cfch_Deutsch_GregorioCortez.pdf |website=Smithsonian Repository |publisher=Smithsonian Institution |access-date=26 August 2020}}</ref> Cortina raided and occupied the town with a squad of armed men. They held the city for several months, until they were attacked by a joint effort between the [[Texas Ranger Division|Texas Rangers]] and U.S. Army, led by [[John Salmon Ford|John Ford]] and [[Samuel P. Heintzelman|Samuel Heintzelman]]. The final battle was fought in March 1860, when Cortina was defeated.<ref>{{cite web |title=Juan Nepomuceno Cortina and the 'First Cortina War' |url=https://www.utrgv.edu/civilwar-trail/civil-war-trail/cortina-first-war/index.htm |website=Rio Grande Valley Civil War Trail |publisher=University of Texas, Rio Grande Valley |access-date=26 August 2020}}</ref>
==Late-19th century==
===Civil War=== {{See also|Hispanics and Latinos in the American Civil War}} [[File:Rafael Chacon.jpg|thumb|right|upright|[[Captain (United States O-3)|Cpt.]] Rafael Chacón of the Union New Mexico Volunteers]]
Mexican Americans played a major role in the [[American Civil War]] (1861-1865). [[Texas in the American Civil War|Texas]], which was home to a significant portion of the nation's Mexican American population, seceded from the [[Union (American Civil War)|Union]] and joined the [[Confederate States of America]] in February 1861. In the [[Arizona Territory|Arizona]] and [[New Mexico Territory|New Mexico]] territories, many elite Mexican American families held views sympathetic to the Confederacy.<ref name="Hispanics and the Civil War">{{cite web |title=Hispanics and the Civil War |url=https://www.nps.gov/articles/hispanics-and-the-civil-war.htm |website=National Park Service |publisher=U.S. Department of the Interior |access-date=3 September 2020}}</ref> In New Mexico, wealthy Mexican American crop-farm families openly supported the [[Slavery in the United States|slave-owners]] of the South, perhaps due to their own reliance on the [[History of slavery in New Mexico|forced labor]] of Native Americans.<ref name="Hispanics and the Civil War"/>
Across the country, Mexican Americans felt resentment toward the U.S. because of the ethnic discrimination they experienced after the Mexican American War. The result was a mixed dispersion of support and opposition toward the United States. In [[New Mexico Territory in the American Civil War|New Mexico]] and [[California in the American Civil War|California]], support among Mexican Americans was split.<ref name="Hispanics and the Civil War"/> Many wealthy landowners in southern New Mexico supported the Confederacy, while most northern New Mexicans fought for the [[Union Army]].<ref name="Hispanics and the Civil War"/> In California, Union support tended to be stronger in [[Northern California]], while many Mexican Americans in [[Southern California]] leaned toward the Confederacy. Nevertheless, California remained in the Union.<ref name="Hispanics and the Civil War"/>
[[File:Cipriano Andrade of U.S. Navy in uniform CYMERA 20160308 152507 (cropped).jpg|thumb|left|upright|Rear Admiral [[Cipriano Andrade]], a [[third engineer]] who served the Union]]
The Confederates, however, believed that opening a route to California would aid their cause. In summer 1861, [[John R. Baylor]] led the Confederates into [[Mesilla, New Mexico|Mesilla]] and declared the southern portion of New Mexico as the [[Confederate Arizona|Confederate Territory of Arizona]].<ref name="Arizona in Exile">{{cite journal |last1=Finch |first1=L. Boyd |title=Arizona in Exile: Confederate Schemes to Recapture the Far Southwest |journal=The Journal of Arizona History |date=Spring 1992 |volume=33 |issue=1 |pages=57–84 |jstor=41695920 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/41695920 |access-date=3 September 2020}}</ref> He then marched into [[Tucson, Arizona|Tucson]] and declared Southern Arizona the second district of the Arizona Territory.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Finch |first1=Boyd |title=Sherod Hunter and the Confederates in Arizona |journal=The Journal of Arizona History |date=Autumn 1969 |volume=10 |issue=3 |pages=137–206 |jstor=41695524 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/41695524 |access-date=3 September 2020}}</ref> In response to this aggression, President [[Abraham Lincoln]] appointed [[Henry Connelly]] - an Anglo politician who married into a Mexican American family – as the territorial governor of New Mexico.<ref name="Establishment of New Mexico's American Identity: A Survey of Governance and Events highlighting The Kearny Code, the first American Rule of Law and the Civil War Battles in New Mexico">{{cite web |last1=Dziurzynski Cox |first1=Karen |title=Establishment of New Mexico's American Identity: A Survey of Governance and Events highlighting The Kearny Code, the first American Rule of Law and the Civil War Battles in New Mexico |url=https://www.jamesmadison.gov/pdf/Madison%20Fellows/KarenDzCox.pdf |website=James Madison Memorial Fellowship Foundation |publisher=Ashland University |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190119220134/https://www.jamesmadison.gov/pdf/Madison%20Fellows/KarenDzCox.pdf |access-date=3 September 2020|archive-date=2019-01-19 }}</ref> Inspiring confidence amongst the Nuevomexicanos, the Union army was soon filled by Mexican Americans. The [[List of New Mexico Territory Civil War units|New Mexico units]], known as the New Mexico Volunteers, were led by Brigadier General [[Diego Archuleta]], Lt. Col. [[Manuel Antonio Chaves|Manuel Chávez]], Lt. Col. [[Francisco Perea]], Col. [[José Guadalupe Gallegos]], [[José Francisco Chaves|J. Francisco Chaves]], and Cpt. Rafael Chacón.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Hannigan |first1=Isabel |title='Overrun All This Country...' Two New Mexican Lives Through the Nineteenth Century |url=https://etd.ohiolink.edu/apexprod/rws_etd/send_file/send?accession=oberlin1525431471822028&disposition=inline |website=OhioLINK |publisher=Oberlin College |access-date=3 September 2020 |archive-date=7 October 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211007231909/https://etd.ohiolink.edu/apexprod/rws_etd/send_file/send?accession=oberlin1525431471822028&disposition=inline |url-status=dead }}</ref> This massive Mexican American army was able to destroy the Confederate hold on New Mexico by March 28, 1862, when Lt. Col. [[Manuel Antonio Chaves|Manuel Chávez]] and his troops destroyed the Confederate supply train on [[Glorieta Pass]] and forced the Confederate soldiers to abandon the field.<ref>{{cite web |title=The Gettysburg of the West: The Battle of Glorieta Pass (1862) |url=https://usacac.army.mil/node/1957 |website=United States Army Combined Arms Center |publisher=United States Army |access-date=3 September 2020}}{{dead link|date=April 2025|bot=medic}}{{cbignore|bot=medic}}</ref> Often called the "Gettysburg of the West", the [[Battle of Glorieta Pass]] effectively ended the Confederates attempts to take over the Western United States.<ref>{{cite web |title=Battle of Glorieta Pass: Action at Apache Canyon |url=https://www.nationalguard.mil/Resources/Image-Gallery/Historical-Paintings/Heritage-Series/Glorieta/ |publisher=United States National Guard |access-date=3 September 2020}}</ref> With the Confederates' surrender of the Territory, Mexican Americans from California were responsible for clearing out all Confederates supporters, including [[Second French intervention in Mexico|French imperialists]] who entered the U.S. during [[Maximilian I of Mexico|Maximilian]]'s rule in [[Second Mexican Empire|Mexico]].<ref name="Hispanics and the Civil War"/>
[[File:Tejano unionist.jpg|thumb|upright|A Tejano Union soldier]]
As the last Confederate stronghold of the Southwest, Texas played a major role in Civil War battles. Wealthy Tejano ranchers, such as [[Santos Benavides]], were the strongest Texas supporters of the Confederacy.<ref>{{cite web |title=Col.
