Santos Benavides |url=https://www.utrgv.edu/civilwar-trail/civil-war-trail/webb-county/santos-benavides/index.htm |publisher=University of Texas, Rio Grande Valley |access-date=3 September 2020}}</ref> Nevertheless, many working class Tejanos fought for the Union army, as they had no interest in living in a social system predicated on [[History of slavery in Texas|unfree labor]].<ref name="Hispanics and the Civil War"/>
Some Tejanos, such as Antonio Ochoa, had fought against the Texas Confederates from the time of secession. In 1861, Ochoa and a group of 40 men marched to the [[Zapata County, Texas|Zapata County]] courthouse and sought to prevent the town officials from swearing their allegiance to the Confederacy.<ref>{{cite web |title=Confrontation at Carrizo |url=https://www.utrgv.edu/civilwar-trail/civil-war-trail/zapata-county/confrontation-at-carrizo/index.htm |publisher=University of Texas, Rio Grande Valley |access-date=3 September 2020}}</ref> Ochoa and his men were immediately attacked by Confederate troops and forced to flee into Mexico. There they met and recruited [[Juan Cortina]], who'd been forced out of Texas at the end of the [[Cortina Troubles|First Cortina War]].<ref name="Hispanics and the Civil War"/> Ochoa and Cortina together launched multiple military and economic attacks in [[South Texas]], targeting supply lines, and even assassinating a Confederate county judge.<ref>{{cite web |title=Second Battle of El Clareño and Hanging of Zapata County Judge |url=https://www.utrgv.edu/civilwar-trail/civil-war-trail/zapata-county/second-battle-el%20clareno/index.htm |publisher=University of Texas, Rio Grande Valley |access-date=3 September 2020}}</ref> After each attack, they fled back to the safety of Mexico, waited for a short time, and then moved back into Texas for their next attack. This continued until Ochoa was executed by the brother of Santos Benavides.<ref name="Hispanics and the Civil War"/>
The final [[Battle of Palmito Ranch|battle]] of the U.S. Civil War was fought in Texas. One month after Robert E. Lee's surrender at [[Battle of Appomattox Court House|Appomattox]] in April 1865, Union forces marched toward [[Brownsville, Texas|Brownsville]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Stephen |first1=J. |title=The Battle of Palmito Ranch (May 12-13, 1865) |url=https://web.nmsu.edu/~stephenj/ICT460/lesson/ranch.php |publisher=New Mexico State University |access-date=3 September 2020}}</ref> Tejano Confederates responded near the mouth of the [[Rio Grande]] and attacked the Union soldiers.<ref>{{cite web |title=The Battle of Palmito Ranch |url=https://historyengine.richmond.edu/episodes/view/722 |website=The History Engine |publisher=University of Richmond |access-date=3 September 2020}}</ref> While the Confederates won this final victory, they were the ultimate losers of the war. All told, an estimated 20,000 [[Hispanics and Latinos in the American Civil War|Latino soldiers]] fought during the American Civil War.<ref>{{cite web |title=Civil War |url=https://www.army.mil/hispanics/history.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150918191912/http://www.army.mil/hispanics/history.html |url-status=dead |archive-date=September 18, 2015 |website=Hispanics in the U.S. Army |publisher=United States Army |access-date=3 September 2020}}</ref>
===Reconstruction era=== [[File:School, Church of San Miguel, Santa Fe 1873 New Mexico - NARA - 519749.jpg|thumb|left|[[San Miguel, New Mexico]] in 1873]]
Despite the significant contributions of Mexican Americans in the American Civil War, the community faced a resurgence of discrimination in the [[Reconstruction era]]. In the 1870s, the New Mexico Territory saw a massive influx of Anglo-American settlers and land [[Speculation|speculators]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Correia |first1=David |title=Land Grant Speculation in New Mexico During the Territorial Period |journal=Natural Resources Journal |date=Winter 2008 |volume=48 |issue=4 |url=https://digitalrepository.unm.edu/nrj/vol48/iss4/4 |access-date=12 September 2020}}</ref> The Territory legislature, fearing a Gold Rush-style land grab, petitioned Congress for protections.<ref name="American Language"/> In their 1872 [[Wikt:memorial#Noun|memorial]] to [[42nd United States Congress|Congress]], the New Mexico legislators argued for a change in federal land laws, which stipulated that in the case of a dispute, the owners of the land must present evidence of their original land grant in both English and Spanish. The legislators argued this provided an undue burden to Nuevomexicanos, since in the territory, "very few ... understand the English language."<ref name="American Language"/> As migration increased over the course of the decade, the legislature issued more memorials, stressing the need for a Board of Commissioners to settle disputed land claims.<ref name="American Language"/> [[File:Romualdo Pacheco - Brady-Handy.jpg|thumb|upright|[[Romualdo Pacheco]] is the only Mexican to serve as [[Governor of California]] since the [[Conquest of California|United States conquest of California]].]] In Texas, disputes between Tejanos and white Americans resulted in open [[Ethnic conflict|racial conflict]]. The Skinning Wars, also known as the [[Cortina Troubles#Second Cortina War|Second Cortina War]], erupted in the 1870s.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Collins |first1=Michael L. |title=A Crooked River: Rustlers, Rangers, and Regulars on the Lower Rio Grande, 1861–1877 |date=April 12, 2018 |publisher=University of Oklahoma Press |isbn=978-0806160085 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hl9TDwAAQBAJ |access-date=12 September 2020}}</ref> After the Civil War, Texas ranchers found themselves with a massive [[Excess supply|surplus]] of cattle, and this resulted in a precipitous drop in the price of beef. The cost of [[cowhide]]s, however, remained relatively high. Because of the high price of hides, disputes soon emerged over [[Maverick (animal)|mavericks]], which, in this period, were often left to grange on the [[open range]]. These disputes resulted in "skinning raids", where young Mexican men would round up disputed herds of cattle and [[Skinning|skin]] them all at once. In retaliation, white Americans in South Texas organized "[[vigilance committee]]s", which quickly gained notoriety for their violent tactics.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Jennings |first1=N. A. |title=A Little Standing Army in Himself |url=http://historymatters.gmu.edu/d/6534/ |website=History Matters |publisher=George Mason University |access-date=12 September 2020}}</ref> In [[Corpus Christi, Texas|Corpus Christi]], the Anglo vigilance committee raided Tejano ranches, where they would kill every Mexican male, burn down all their buildings, and force any survivors across the border to Mexico.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Weber |first1=John William |title=The Shadow of the Revolution: South Texas, the Mexican Revolution, and the Evolution of Modern American Labor Relations |url=https://scholarworks.wm.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=3326&context=etd |website=Theses, Dissertations, & Master Projects |publisher=College of William & Mary |access-date=12 September 2020}}</ref> Texas Ranger [[Leander H. McNelly]], a former Confederate, imposed punishments against the Tejanos he believed were responsible for the "raids", formally ending the race war.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Callahan |first1=Manuel |title=Mexican Border Troubles: Social War, Settler Colonialism and the Production of Frontier Discourses, 1848-1880 |url=https://repositories.lib.utexas.edu/bitstream/handle/2152/480/callahanm036.txt |publisher=The University of Texas at Austin August |access-date=12 September 2020}}</ref>
[[File:José Mauro Luján (1840-1916).jpg|thumb|right|upright|José Mauro Luján, a San Elizario resident and participant in the [[San Elizario Salt War]] of 1877]]
In [[West Texas]], violent ethnoracial tensions exploded by 1877. In September of that year, [[San Elizario, Texas|San Elizario]] District Judge Charles Howard sought to charge collection fees from Mexicans, Tejanos, and [[Ysleta del Sur Pueblo|Tiguas]] when they harvested from local [[Salt pan (geology)|salt beds]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Comar |first1=Scott C. |title=The Tigua Indians of Ysleta del Sur: A Borderlands Community |url=https://scholarworks.utep.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=2022&context=open_etd |website=Open Access Theses & Dissertations |publisher=University of Texas at El Paso |access-date=12 September 2020}}</ref> The residents were outraged by the fees, as the salt beds had been considered a public resource for many generations.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Stuart |first1=Andrew |title=Tracing the Salt Road, Where West Texas Geology, History Meet |url=https://marfapublicradio.org/blog/nature-notes/tracing-the-salt-road-where-west-texas-geology-history-meet/ |website=Nature Notes |publisher=Marfa Public Radio |access-date=12 September 2020}}</ref> After Howard arrested two residents who tried to collect salt without paying, the residents revolted against Howard. Known as the [[San Elizario Salt War]], this revolt resulted in the death of Howard and four other white Americans.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Sonnichsen |first1=C.L. |title=Salt War of San Elizario |url=https://www.tshaonline.org/handbook/entries/salt-war-of-san-elizario |website=Handbook of Texas |publisher=Texas State Historical Association |access-date=12 September 2020}}</ref> In response, the white residents of San Elizario called upon the Texas Rangers, who, along with the U.S. Army, suppressed the rebellion and reasserted Anglo power in the region.<ref name="The El Paso Salt War">{{cite web |title=The El Paso Salt War |url=https://www.nps.gov/gumo/learn/historyculture/saltwar.htm |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150304032647/http://www.nps.gov/gumo/learn/historyculture/saltwar.htm |url-status=dead |archive-date=March 4, 2015 |website=Guadalupe Mountains National Park |publisher=National Park Service |access-date=12 September 2020}}</ref>
Further south, [[Richard King (entrepreneur)|Richard King]] continued to actively consolidate the [[King Ranch]] in the late-1870s, usually through violent and coercive tactics directed against his Tejano ranchero neighbors.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Matthews |first1=Matt M. |title=The US Army on the Mexican Border: A Historical Perspective |date=2007 |publisher=Combat Studies Institute Press |location=Fort Leavenworth |isbn=978-0-16-078903-8 |page=60 |edition=1 |url=https://apps.dtic.mil/dtic/tr/fulltext/u2/a472386.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201115141331/https://apps.dtic.mil/dtic/tr/fulltext/u2/a472386.pdf |url-status=live |archive-date=November 15, 2020 |access-date=12 September 2020}}</ref> In 1878, one newspaper commentator complained that King's neighbors "mysteriously vanish whilst his territory extends over entire countries".<ref>{{cite book |last1=Davis |first1=Steven L. |title=J. Frank Dobie |date=October 15, 2009 |publisher=University of Texas Press |isbn=978-0292721142 |page=13 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=EUwgldrFU80C&q=mysteriously+vanish+whilst+his+territory+extends+over+entire+countries.&pg=PA13 |access-date=12 September 2020}}</ref> King, however, did not work alone. As his wealth grew, so did his political influence, and the territorial consolidation of Texas ranch land was made possible through the Texas Rangers. The Rangers in this period took violent measures against Tejano ranch owners to scare them into selling their land. In fact, the Rangers were known popularly in the late-1870s as ''los riches de la Kineña'', an allusion to the belief they acted as King's private security force.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Solis |first1=Gabriel Daniel |title=El Corrido de Ricardo Aldape Guerra: Form, History, and Resistance |journal=Cultural Dynamics |date=January 18, 2012 |volume=23 |issue=3 |pages=173–196 |doi= 10.1177/0921374011430567|s2cid=145344043 |url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0921374011430567 |access-date=12 September 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref>
===[[Gilded Age]]===
[[File:Band of Apache Indian prisoners at rest stop beside Southern Pacific Railway, near Nueces River, Tex., September 10, 188 - NARA - 523549.jpg|thumb|left|[[Apache]] men on the [[Southern Pacific Transportation Company|Southern Pacific Railway]] near the [[Nueces River]], Texas]]
The 1880s for Mexicans Americans was a period of substantial change, marked especially by the emergence of the [[History of the Southern Pacific|Southern Pacific Railway]]. In [[El Paso, Texas|El Paso]], the Southern Pacific reached the city in 1881, at which point it birthed an immediate economic and industrial revolution, as new industries emerged in mining, [[smelting]], and construction.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Agredano |first1=Hector |title=Rails to Revolution: Railroads, Railroad Workers and the Geographies of the Mexican Revolution |url=https://academicworks.cuny.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=4411&context=gc_etds |website=El Paso and Ciudad Juárez: Border Boomtowns |publisher=City University of New York |access-date=21 September 2020}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Labodda |first1=Marsha |title=Southern Pacific Company (Rio Grande Division) |url=https://scholarworks.utep.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1000&context=finding_aid |website=Historical Sketch |publisher=University of Texas at El Paso |access-date=21 September 2020}}</ref> The economic boom was felt throughout the U.S. Southwest and [[Northern Mexico]], and it brought new national and transnational migrants into the region.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Kupsky |first1=Gregory |title=Responses to Immigration |url=https://ehistory.osu.edu/videos/responses-to-immigration |website=ehistory |publisher=Ohio State University |access-date=21 September 2020}}</ref> In addition to Mexicans entering the U.S. from Mexico, Chinese laborers came from [[History of San Francisco#Paris of the West|San Francisco]], African Americans fled from the [[Jim Crow laws|Jim Crow]] South, and European Americans came from the [[East Coast of the United States|East Coast]]. The influx of new capital and immigrant labor into the region helped transform Texas from a [[Barren vegetation|barren terrain]] into a hub of international commerce, and El Paso emerged as the region's primary economic hub and an international commercial depot.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Hodges |first1=Gladys Arlene |title=El Paso, Texas and Ciudad Juarez, Chihuahua, 1880-1930 |url=https://scholarworks.utep.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=3504&context=open_etd |publisher=University of Texas at El Paso |access-date=21 September 2020}}</ref> Nevertheless, racial violence continued.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Carrigan |first1=William D. |last2=Webb |first2=Clive |title=Forgotten Dead: Mob Violence against Mexicans in the United States, 1848-1928 |date=May 21, 2013 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=New York |isbn=978-0195320350 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=mJNoAgAAQBAJ |access-date=21 September 2020}}</ref> Mary Jaques, a British tourist who spent two years in [[Central Texas]] in the 1880s, wrote that the murder of Tejanos "carried a sort of immunity with it", as Mexicans appeared to be "treated like a dog, or, perhaps, not so well".<ref>{{cite book |last1=Jaques |first1=Mary J. |title=Texas Ranch Life |date=1890 |publisher=Independent Publishers|isbn=978-1519055460}}</ref>
[[File:Initial Monument Marking the United States - Mexico Border.png|thumb|right|Initial monument marking the [[Mexico–United States border]]]]
Migration into the United States in this period was also soon complicated by racial restrictions. For the first time in its history, the U.S. barred an entire national-origin group from immigrating when it passed the [[Chinese Exclusion Act]] in 1882.<ref>{{cite web |title=Chinese Exclusion Act (1882) |url=https://www.ourdocuments.gov/doc.php?flash=false&doc=47 |website=Our Documents |publisher=The U.S. National Archives and Records Administration |access-date=21 September 2020}}</ref> This caused difficulties at the [[Mexico–United States border]], since the Act officially excluded [[Chinese immigration to Mexico|Mexicans of Chinese descent]] from entering the U.S. as well.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Chao Romero |first1=Robert |title=The Chinese in Mexico, 1882-1940 |date=January 1, 2012 |publisher=University of Arizona Press |location=Tucson |isbn=9780816514601 |edition=2 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=U3GQ7o3HV1cC |access-date=21 September 2020}}</ref> "Chinese Inspectors" were hired by the [[United States Customs Service]] to inspect immigrants at ports of entry in the Southwest.<ref>{{cite web |title=Chinese Immigration and the Chinese in the United States |url=https://www.archives.gov/research/chinese-americans/guide |website=National Archives: Chinese Heritage |date=15 August 2016 |publisher=The U.S. National Archives and Records Administration |access-date=21 September 2020}}</ref> While official U.S. policy was to deport all ethnic Chinese immigrants to [[Timeline of Chinese history#19th century|China]], migration policies were slightly revised for Chinese Mexicans, who were deported to Mexico instead, if they held Mexican citizenship, had lived most of their lives in Mexico, or were married to Mexican nationals.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Delgado |first1=Grace |title=Making the Chinese Mexican: Global Migration, Localism, and Exclusion in the U.S.-Mexico Borderlands |date=March 7, 2012 |publisher=Stanford University Press |location=Palo Alto |isbn=978-0816514601 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=IuTtxo2Za_UC |access-date=21 September 2020}}</ref> This "Chinese inspection" force would grow into the [[United States Border Patrol|U.S. Border Patrol]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Border Patrol History |url=https://www.cbp.gov/border-security/along-us-borders/history |website=U.S. Customs and Border Protection |publisher=U.S. Department of Homeland Security |access-date=12 December 2020}}</ref>
[[File:María Ruiz de Burton.jpg|thumb|upright|left|[[María Ruiz de Burton]], a Mexican American author]]
These continued indignities suffered by Mexicans and Mexican Americans did not go completely ignored, however. In 1885, [[María Ruiz de Burton]], a Californian Mexican-American, published ''The Squatter and the Don'', a novel set in 1870s [[San Diego County, California|San Diego County]], where the fictional Amaro family clashed with ''esquatas'', Anglo Americans who "improved" the Amaro family ranch in order to lay legal claim to the land.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Railton |first1=Ben |title=Why We Should All Read The Squatter and the Don |date=15 August 2017 |url=https://americanwritersmuseum.org/why-we-should-all-read-the-squatter-and-the-don/ |publisher=American Writers Museum |access-date=21 September 2020}}</ref> Considered the United States' first female Mexican-American author, Maria Ruiz de Burton had been politicized through her personal experiences in California after the Mexican American War.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Contreras |first1=Alicia |title=María Amparo Ruiz de Burton |url=https://www.oxfordbibliographies.com/view/document/obo-9780199827251/obo-9780199827251-0191.xml |website=Oxford Bibliographies |publisher=Oxford University Press |access-date=21 September 2020}}</ref> Before the publication of her novel, she wrote to her cousin, "It cannot be denied that the Californians have reason to complain. The Americans must know it; their boasted liberty and equality of rights seem to stop when it meets a Californian ... And now we have to beg for what we had the right to demand."<ref name="Nuestra América">{{cite journal |last1=Ruiz |first1=Vicki L. |title=Nuestra América: Latino History as United States History |journal=The Journal of American History |date=December 2006 |page=660 |url=https://www.swarthmore.edu/sites/default/files/assets/documents/black-studies-program/ruiz_lecture.pdf |access-date=21 September 2020}}</ref> The publication of Burton's novel coincided with several other important developments in California for Mexican Americans. The decade witnessed the official dismantling of Spanish usage in official government documents,<ref>{{cite web |last1=Baron |first1=Dennis |title=California must ban language discrimination |url=https://blogs.illinois.edu/view/25/8038 |website=The Web of Language |publisher=University of Illinois |access-date=21 September 2020}}</ref> around the same time the [[Gilded Age]] practice of [[voter suppression]] emerged to [[Disenfranchisement after the Reconstruction era|disenfranchise]] African American peoples from having a say in the functions of government.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Perman |first1=Michael |title=Struggle for Mastery: Disfranchisement in the South, 1888-1908 |date=2001 |publisher=University of North Carolina Press |location=Raleigh |isbn=9780807849095 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KZP4AAAAQBAJ |access-date=21 September 2020}}</ref> For Mexican Americans, in general, the Gilded Age was a period of abrupt economic change, political disenfranchisement, and demographic displacement. While there was significant immigrant labor entering the Southwest from Mexico during this period through the railroads, it was dwarfed by the tidal wave of Anglos moving West from [[Ellis Island|New York]] and other ports of entry. While the 1880s brought major changes, the coming years would emerge as a retrenchment of racial animosity.
===Rise of Juan Crow===
[[File:Group on plaza, Mesilla, New Mexico.jpg|thumb|right|Group of Mexican Americans on the plaza in [[Mesilla, New Mexico]], c. 1890s]]
If the 1880s represented a period of massive cultural and economic change for Mexican Americans in the U.S. Southwest, the 1890s were marked by renewed racial conflict and social unrest. In [[El Paso, Texas|El Paso]], which experienced a massive influx of white American migrants to the region after the completion of the Southern Pacific railway line, there was a widespread retrenchment of racial animosity.<ref name="Porous Borders">{{cite book |last1=Lim |first1=Julian |title=Porous Borders: Multiracial Migrations and the Law in the U.S.-Mexico Borderlands |date=December 18, 2017 |publisher=University of North Carolina Press |location=Chapel Hill |isbn=978-1469635491 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=iDI5DwAAQBAJ |access-date=3 October 2020}}</ref> Interracial marriage, which had theretofore been tacitly allowed in some parts of Texas, became a crime increasingly targeted by Texan officials.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Robinson II |first1=Charles |title=Legislated Love in the Lone Star State: Texas and Miscegenation |journal=The Southwestern Historical Quarterly |date=July 2004 |volume=108 |issue=1 |pages=65–87 |jstor=30239495 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30239495 |access-date=3 October 2020}}</ref> In El Paso, Mexicans Americans were demonized as ignorant, and therefore both ineligible and unworthy of the vote. White Texans also frequently characterized them as "foreigners", despite their U.S. citizenship.<ref name="Porous Borders"/> One El Paso newspaper claimed Mexicans were "foreigners who claim American citizenship but who are as ignorant of things American as the mule."<ref name="Porous Borders"/>
[[File:Lugo family members at their home in Bell Gardens-2.jpg|thumb|left|The Lugo family in [[Bell Gardens, California]], c. 1890s]]
In the 1890s, the railroad and mining industries continued to expand. These industries needed massive pools of labor to fill new jobs, many of which were filled by immigrants from Mexico.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Garcilazo |first1=Jeffrey Marcos |title=Traqueros: Mexican Railroad Workers in the United States, 1870-1930 |date=February 16, 2016 |publisher=University of North Texas Press |isbn=978-1574416275 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=aAdOBTaj7T0C |access-date=3 October 2020}}</ref> Immigrants entering the United States came from remote regions of Mexico, seeking relief from the increasingly brutal [[Porfiriato]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Spener |first1=David |title=Mexican Migration to the United States, 1882-1992: A Long Twentieth Century of Coyotaje |url=https://ccis.ucsd.edu/_files/wp124.pdf |website=The Center for Comparative Immigration Studies |publisher=University of California, San Diego |access-date=3 October 2020}}</ref> The [[Texas Mexican Railway]] had connected the U.S. and Mexico in 1883, and this railway allowed for an increasingly integrated transnational transportation system between the two countries.<ref name="Mexican Emigration to the United States">{{cite book |last1=Cardoso |first1=Lawrence A. |title=Mexican Emigration to the United States, 1897-1931: Socio-Economic Patterns |date=June 1, 1980 |publisher=The University of Arizona Press |location=Tucson |isbn=978-0816506781 |edition=1 |url=https://repository.arizona.edu/handle/10150/632295 |access-date=3 October 2020}}</ref> Meanwhile, for elite Mexican American families who continued to undergo litigation involving disputed land claims, the federal government finally offered them some relief in 1891. The [[United States Court of Private Land Claims|Court of Private Land Claims]] was created to settle disputes, providing a federally funded interpreter for the court.<ref>{{cite web |title=Court of Private Land Claims, 1891-1904 |url=https://www.fjc.gov/history/courts/court-private-land-claims-1891-1904 |website=History of the Federal Judiciary |publisher=Federal Judicial Center |access-date=3 October 2020}}</ref> The New Mexico Territory, which had been urging such a commission for years, fell under the jurisdiction of the court, along with Colorado and Arizona. And though the Court did confirm some Nuevomexicano land grants, many were left unconfirmed.<ref name="American Language"/>
[[File:Catarino Erasmo Garza.png|thumb|right|[[Catarino Garza]], Texan revolutionary.]]
In Texas, the massive economic and political inequalities occurring sparked a resurgence of racial warfare. In September 1891, [[Catarino Garza|Catarino Erasmo Garza]] led an army of hundreds of Tejanos back and forth across the [[Rio Grande]] in a revolt against both Mexico and the United States, known as the [[Garza Revolution]].<ref name="Catarino Garza's Revolution">{{cite book |last1=Young |first1=Elliott |title=Catarino Garza's Revolution on the Texas-Mexico Border |date=July 26, 2004 |publisher=Duke University Press |isbn=978-0822333203 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=mbQkuolSwGIC |access-date=3 October 2020}}</ref> Adopting the slogan "''libres fronterizos''" which were stitched onto their hats, the army, known as the Garzistas, was a multiclass movement, consisting of lower-middle-class professionals, poor farmers, landless ranchers, and wealthy landowners, with both Mexicans and Mexican Americans (as well as a few Anglo Americans who had married into Mexican families).<ref name="Catarino Garza's Revolution"/> The military response to the Garza Revolution was extremely bloody, and set precedent for both Texas police, as well as the U.S. Army for domestic warfare.<ref name="Catarino Garza's Revolution"/> Leading the suppression was U.S. Army captain [[John Gregory Bourke]], who said, "The cheapest thing to do is to shoot them down wherever [they are] found skulking about with arms in their hands, and to burn down some of the ranchos which gave them shelter."<ref name="The Injustice Never Leaves You">{{cite book |last1=Muñoz Martinez |first1=Monica |title=The Injustice Never Leaves You: Anti-Mexican Violence in Texas |date=September 3, 2018 |publisher=Harvard University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=978-0674976436 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VPFoDwAAQBAJ |access-date=3 October 2020}}</ref> Bourke, who had fifteen years experience in Arizona during the [[Apache Wars]], led his armies to destroy all Tejano communities believed to support Garza.<ref name="The Injustice Never Leaves You"/> The U.S. Army burned down ranches, threatened families with lynching, searched without warrants, and stole guns, horses, and money from Tejano families.<ref name="The Injustice Never Leaves You"/> Complaints were filed with state and federal officials, but the atrocities were ignored. The Garza Revolution was suppressed by 1893, at which point Catarino Garza was forced into exile, along with the last of his scattered supporters.<ref name="The Injustice Never Leaves You"/>
[[File:Juan José, Pablo, and Nicanor Herrera.png|thumb|left|The Herrera brothers in New Mexico]]
In New Mexico, there was also significant ethnic violence in the 1890s. It began when the [[Santa Fe Ring]], a group of powerful Anglo lawyers and land [[Speculation|speculators]], succeeded in dispossessing thousands of Mexican Americans of lands they used for [[Community-supported agriculture|community farming]].<ref name="Chasing the Santa Fe Ring">{{cite book |last1=Caffey |first1=David L. |title=Chasing the Santa Fe Ring: Power and Privilege in Territorial New Mexico |date=March 15, 2014 |publisher=University of New Mexico Press |location=Santa Fe |isbn=978-0826354426 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NMalAgAAQBAJ |access-date=3 October 2020}}</ref> The Ring moved quickly to fence the lands to prevent anyone from accessing the areas. In response, a group called [[Las Gorras Blancas]] initiated a lawsuit against the Ring arguing for community access to the Las Vegas Land Grant, but the judge sided with the Santa Fe Ring.<ref name="Retribution Will Be Their Reward">{{cite journal |last1=Correia |first1=David |title='Retribution Will Be Their Reward': New Mexico's Las Gorras Blancas and the Fight for the Las Vegas Land Grant Commons |journal=Radical History Review |date=Fall 2010 |volume=108 |issue=1 |pages=49–72 |doi=10.1215/01636545-2010-003 |url=https://www.unm.edu/~dcorreia/David_Correia/Research_files/Correia_GorrasBlancas_RHR.pdf |access-date=3 October 2020}}</ref> The Gorras Blancas, outraged at the injustice, promised military retribution. In 1890, they began their campaign of vigilante justice, cutting down the new fences, destroying farm equipment, and burning buildings throughout [[San Miguel County, New Mexico|San Miguel]], [[Santa Fe County, New Mexico|Santa Fe]], and [[Mora County, New Mexico|Mora]] counties.<ref name="The White Caps of New Mexico">{{cite journal |last1=Larson |first1=Robert W. |title=The White Caps of New Mexico: A Study of Ethnic Militancy in the Southwest |journal=Pacific Historical Review |date=May 1975 |volume=44 |issue=2 |pages=171–185 |doi=10.2307/3638001 |jstor=3638001 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/3638001 |access-date=3 October 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Believed to have been led by the three Herrera brothers, Juan José (known as El Capitan), Pablo, and Nicanor, the Gorras Blancas held between 700 and 1,500 members at its height.<ref name="Gorras Blancas"/> In 1890, the Blancas declared themselves the defenders of "the rights of all people in general, and especially the rights of poor people".<ref name="Gorras Blancas">{{cite web|author1-link=David J. Wishart |last1=Wishart |first1=David J. |title=Gorras Blancas |url=http://plainshumanities.unl.edu/encyclopedia/doc/egp.ha.016 |website=Encyclopedia of the Great Plains |publisher=University of Nebraska - Lincoln |access-date=3 October 2020}}</ref> The territorial Governor [[L. Bradford Prince|Lebaron Bradford Prince]] responded by declaring them a violent "mob",<ref name="Gorras Blancas"/> and organized officials to clamp down on the group. Four dozen Gorras Blancas were indicted, but none were convicted.<ref>{{cite web |title=Las Gorras Blancas: Millitant Resistance |url=https://mytext.cnm.edu/lesson/las-gorras-blancas-millitant-resistance/ |publisher=Central New Mexico Community College |access-date=3 October 2020}}</ref> Finally, in Arizona, racial violence also erupted in 1896 with the [[Yaqui Uprising]], when a group of [[Yaqui]], [[Pima people|Pima]], and Mexican revolutionaries fought against the U.S. and Mexican armies in an early attempt to overthrow the increasingly despotic President [[Porfirio Díaz]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Herrera |first1=Jose Maria |title=Aguirre, Lauro (1857–1925) |url=https://www.tshaonline.org/handbook/entries/aguirre-lauro |publisher=Texas State Historical Association |access-date=3 October 2020}}</ref>
==1900s==
[[File:George Frank Le Breton (1892-1974).jpg|thumb|right|upright|A Tejano youth, c. 1900]]
In the first decade of the 20th century, the population of Mexicans in the United States grew significantly. The first railroad connecting [[Mexico City]] to the Mexico-United States border was completed, which allowed for greater ease of movement from the interior of Mexico to the United States.<ref name="Rails to Revolution">{{cite book |last1=Agredano |first1=Hector |title=Rails to Revolution: Railroads, Railroad Workers and the Geographies of the Mexican Revolution |date=September 2019 |publisher=City University of New York |location=New York, NY |edition=Dissertation |url=https://academicworks.cuny.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=4411&context=gc_etds |access-date=21 November 2020}}</ref> Migration increased especially after a severe [[recession]] hit Mexico in 1906 and then a [[Economic depression|depression]] from 1908 to 1909.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Anderson |first1=Rodney D. |title=Mexican Workers and the Politics of Revolution, 1906-1911 |journal=Hispanic American Historical Review |date=1 February 1974 |volume=54 |issue=1 |pages=94–113 |doi=10.1215/00182168-54.1.94 |url=https://read.dukeupress.edu/hahr/article/54/1/94/151376/Mexican-Workers-and-the-Politics-of-Revolution |access-date=21 November 2020|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Cahill |first1=Kevin J. |title=The U.S. Bank Panic of 1907 and the Mexican Depression of 1908-1909 |journal=The Historian |date=Summer 1998 |volume=60 |issue=4 |pages=795–812 |doi=10.1111/j.1540-6563.1998.tb01416.x |jstor=24452185 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/24452185 |access-date=21 November 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref> The [[Mestizo]] migrants were met with animosity in the United States, as Anglo Americans in the Southwest began warning about the dangers of non-white immigration.<ref name="Porous Borders"/> As the number of Mexican immigrants increased, [[Nativism (politics)|nativist]] broadsides emerged in the [[Progressive Era]] which asserted the poor living conditions of the immigrants – such as substandard housing, high infant mortality, lack of sanitation, and high crime rates – were indicative of flaws within the Mexican character, rather than a failure on the part of the United States to assist the immigrants.<ref name="Porous Borders"/> In [[Chihuahuita, Texas]], complaints about the "hordes of Mexicans" pouring over the border were widespread; however, the need for Mexican [[traquero]] labor, farmworkers, and miners meant that enforcement against Mexican laborers remained lax.<ref name="Porous Borders"/>
===Domestic politics===
[[File:Food Adm. sugar, unloading beets from wagons, Oxnard, (California), factory (cropped).jpg|thumb|left|Beet farm in [[Oxnard, California]]]]
For U.S.-born Mexican-Americans, the first decade of the 20th century was defined largely by legalistic discrimination, including the creation of [[School segregation in the United States|segregated schools]] for Mexican American children (where they were severely underserved and mistreated),<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Powers |first1=Jeanne M. |title=Forgotten History: Mexican American School Segregation in Arizona from 1900–1951 |journal=Equity & Excellence in Education |date=Oct 2008 |volume=41 |issue=4 |pages=467–481 |doi=10.1080/10665680802400253 |s2cid=143690066 |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/ref/10.1080/10665680802400253?scroll=top |access-date=10 October 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Gonzalez |first1=Gilbert G. |title=Chicano Education in the Era of Segregation |date=April 5, 2013 |publisher=University of North Texas Press |location=Denton |isbn=978-1574415018 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KjA6Ngt3TQYC |access-date=12 December 2020}}</ref> mysterious and unexplained "jail suicides", and a significant number of lynchings.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Villanueva Jr. |first1=Nicholas |title=The Lynching of Mexicans in the Texas Borderlands |date=June 15, 2017 |publisher=University of New Mexico Press |location=Santa Fe |isbn=978-0826358387 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=5nqgDgAAQBAJ |access-date=21 November 2020}}</ref> Some Mexican Americans, however, took a stand against these violent conditions. In 1903 near [[Oxnard, California]], a group of Mexican American beet farmworkers teamed up with their Japanese-American coworkers to demand better wages and working conditions.<ref name="The Oxnard Strike of 1903">{{cite web |last1=Kim |first1=Eugene |title=The Oxnard Strike of 1903 |url=https://www.dartmouth.edu/~hist32/History/S03%20-%20The%201903%20Oxnard%20Strike.htm |website=History 32 |publisher=Dartmouth University |access-date=20 November 2020}}</ref> The [[Oxnard strike of 1903]] is one of the first recorded instances of an organized strike by Mexican Americans in United States history.<ref name="The Oxnard Strike of 1903"/> The Mexican and Japanese American strikers raised the ire of the surrounding white American community. While picketing, one laborer, Luis Vasquez, was shot and killed, and four others were wounded.<ref>{{cite web |title=A History of Mexican Americans in California |url=https://www.nps.gov/parkhistory/online_books/5views/5views5h21.htm |website=Five Views: An Ethnic Historic Site Survey for California |publisher=National Park Service |access-date=21 November 2020}}</ref> While the strikers were able to leverage the press from the shooting into securing some of their demands, the success of the strike proved short-lived after [[Samuel Gompers]] and the [[American Federation of Labor]] refused to grant the strikers an official union charter due to the racial makeup of their group.<ref name="The Oxnard Sugar Beet Strike of 1903">{{cite web |last1=Ramirez |first1=Raymond |title=The Oxnard Sugar Beet Strike of 1903 |url=http://www.sscnet.ucla.edu/aasc/classweb/winter02/aas197a/apaplabor_fp.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20021107124833/http://www.sscnet.ucla.edu/aasc/classweb/winter02/aas197a/apaplabor_fp.html |url-status=dead |archive-date=November 7, 2002 |website=Asian Pacific American Labor History |publisher=University of California, Los Angeles |access-date=21 November 2020}}</ref> Incidents of racial conflict also flared up in areas such as [[Clifton, Arizona]], where, in 1904, mobs of Anglo men raided the homes of Mexican American families to kidnap their children, so as to save the children from the "suffering" of being raised in Mexican homes.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Gordon |first1=Linda |title=The Great Arizona Orphan Abduction |date=2001 |publisher=Harvard University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=978-0674005358 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=UJKyxTxkFlQC |access-date=21 November 2020}}</ref> In 1906, the [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]] refused to hear what became known as the "Arizona Orphan Abduction" case, thereby tacitly allowing the children to be legally adopted and raised by their kidnappers.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Brophy |first1=Anthony Blake |title=Foundlings on the Frontier: Racial and Religious Conflict in Arizona Territory, 1904-1905 |date=1972 |publisher=University of Arizona Press |location=Tucson |isbn=978-0816503193 |edition=1}}</ref>
During the 1900s-1930s, Mexican Americans utilized the court systems to assert and defend their rights as citizens in various ways. Mexican Americans faced challenges regarding land ownership and property rights due to discriminatory practices and racial prejudice. They often turned to the courts to fight against land dispossession, unlawful evictions, and discriminatory property laws.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Mitchell |first=Pablo |title=West of sex: making Mexican America; 1900 - 1930 |date=2012 |publisher=Univ. of Chicago Press |isbn=978-0-226-53269-1 |series=American history |location=Chicago}}</ref> Through legal proceedings, they sought to assert their ownership rights and protect their property interests.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Estrada |first1=Leobardo F. |last2=García |first2=F. Chris |last3=Macías |first3=Reynaldo Flores |last4=Maldonado |first4=Lionel |date=1981 |title=Chicanos in the United States: A History of Exploitation and Resistance |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/20024726 |journal=Daedalus |volume=110 |issue=2 |pages=103–131 |jstor=20024726 |issn=0011-5266}}</ref> Mexican Americans also engaged in legal battles to challenge racial discrimination and assert their civil rights. They filed lawsuits against discriminatory practices in public accommodations, transportation, and voting rights.<ref name=":0" /> These legal efforts played a role in advancing civil rights for [[Chicano|Mexican Americans]] and challenging systemic racism. In "''West of Sex: Making Mexican America, 1900-1930,''" author Pablo Mitchell provides insights into how Mexican Americans engaged with the legal system to recognize and assert their rights as citizens.<ref name=":0" />
===International relations=== [[File:Yaqui Indians lynched by Mexicans LCCN2014688840 (cropped).tif|thumb|[[Yaqui]] men lynched by the [[Porfiriato]].]] In these years around the turn of the century, Mexico intensified its campaign against the [[Yaqui]] of [[Sonora]], who were fighting against the [[forced assimilation]] programs of the Porfirio government.<ref name="Yaqui Resistance and Survival"/> The federal Mexican government initiated a program of [[Population transfer|forced resettlement]], deporting Yaqui rebels to work as slave laborers on the [[Henequen industry in Yucatán|henequen plantations]] in [[Yucatán]].<ref name="Yaqui Resistance and Survival">{{cite book |last1=Hu-DeHart |first1=Evelyn |title=Yaqui Resistance and Survival: The Struggle for Land and Autonomy, 1821–1910 |date=November 1, 2016 |publisher=University of Wisconsin Press |location=Madison |isbn=978-0299311049 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ea-hDgAAQBAJ |access-date=21 November 2020}}</ref> In response, many Mexican Yaquis fled to the United States. The U.S. Army, led by Captain [[Harry C. Wheeler]], negotiated with the Mexican government to deport any Yaqui found in Arizona back to Mexico.<ref name="Porous Borders"/> The [[United States Department of Commerce and Labor|U.S. Department of Commerce and Labor]] went a step further and ordered the [[Immigration detention#Americas|detention]] and [[deportation]] of all Yaquis who entered the United States without documentation. Somewhere between 10,000 and 15,000 Yaquis were deported to Mexico, where they were met with murder, lynching, and enslavement by the [[Porfiriato]].<ref name="Yaqui Resistance and Survival"/>
[[File:Jew Sing.png|thumb|left|upright|Jew Sing, from Mexico, deported from the U.S. for having Chinese ancestry]]
In the latter half of the decade, U.S. immigration officials worked to step up regulations at the Mexico-United States border. As immigration from Mexico to the United States increased around the turn of the century, nativists pushed to increase public health and [[Public charge rule|public charge]] restrictions against potential migrants.<ref name="Porous Borders"/> Widespread [[anti-Chinese sentiment]] also created a sense of hysteria in U.S. border towns. Unable to differentiate between [[Indigenous peoples of Mexico|Indigenous]] and [[Mestizos in Mexico|Mestizo Mexicans]] and [[Chinese immigration to Mexico|Mexicans of Chinese descent]], U.S. immigration officials became increasingly concerned about Chinese Mexicans who entered the United States by wearing "traditional" Mexican clothes and speaking Spanish.<ref name="Enforcing the Borders">{{cite journal |last1=Lee |first1=Erika |title=Enforcing the Borders: Chinese Exclusion along the U.S. Borders with Canada and Mexico, 1882-1924 |journal=The Journal of American History |date=June 2002 |volume=89 |issue=1 |pages=54–86 |doi=10.2307/2700784 |jstor=2700784 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/2700784 |access-date=20 November 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref> In 1905, T. F. Schmucker, Chief of the Immigration Bureau in [[El Paso, Texas|El Paso]], asserted that between 150 and 200 Chinese Mexicans were being held in detention in [[Ciudad Juárez|Juarez]].<ref name="The Future Immense">{{cite book |last1=Lim |first1=Julian |title=The Future Immense: Race and Immigration in the Multiracial U.S.-Mexico Border, 1880-1936 |date=August 2013 |publisher=Graduate School of Cornell University |location=Ithaca, NY |edition=Dissertation |url=https://ecommons.cornell.edu/bitstream/handle/1813/34278/jsl66.pdf?sequence%3D1%26isAllowed%3Dy&usg=AOvVaw0uSG7eNz9kLvTU62KI5DUt |access-date=20 November 2020}}</ref> By 1907, U.S. immigration officials adopted even more stringent policies in order to prevent Chinese Mexicans from entering the United States; however, one immigration inspector named Marcus Braun, wrote, "How exceedingly difficult it is to positively state whether these are ... Chinamen or Mexicans."<ref name="Porous Borders"/> In the lead-up to the [[Mexican Revolution]], the number of immigrants entering the U.S. from Mexico increased dramatically, including the number of Chinese Mexicans. In 1909, the ''[[El Paso Times]]'' wrote, "There are a hundred 'Celestials' where there was one twenty years before ... every train brings a dozen or two to Juarez, all intent on getting across the border, and in the course of a few years hundreds of them have been taken off trains on the American side."<ref name="Porous Borders"/>
==1910s==
===Mexican Revolution=== {{Main|Mexican Revolution}} [[File:Mexican refugees going to Marfa (LOC).jpg|thumb|right|Refugees fleeing the Mexican Revolution, heading to [[Marfa, Texas]]]]
The [[Mexican Revolution]] (1910-1920) resulted from the increasing unpopularity of the 31-year-long brutal dictatorship of [[Porfirio Díaz]]. Growing resistance to Diaz resulted in a power struggle among competing elites, which created the opportunity for agrarian insurrection.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Tutino |first1=John |title=From Insurrection to Revolution: Social Bases of Agrarian Violence, 1750–1940 |date=1986 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-0691022949 |page=327 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=g_9ZDwAAQBAJ |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref> Wealthy landowner [[Francisco I. Madero]] challenged Díaz in the 1910 presidential election, and following the rigged results, revolted under the [[Plan of San Luis Potosí]].<ref>Friedrich Katz, ''The Secret War in Mexico: Europe, the United States and the Mexican Revolution''. Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 1981, p. 35.</ref>
Armed conflict broke out in northern Mexico, led by Madero, [[Pascual Orozco]], and [[Pancho Villa]], and with support from portions of the middle class, the peasantry, and organized labor,<ref>Katz, ''The Secret War in Mexico'' p. 35.</ref> Díaz was forced out. In the [[Treaty of Ciudad Juárez]], Díaz resigned and went into exile. New elections were held in 1911, and Madero was elected, taking office in November. Opposition to his regime then grew from both the conservatives, who saw him as too weak, and from former revolutionary fighters, who saw him as too conservative.
In a period in February 1913, known as the [[Ten Tragic Days]], Madero and his vice president Pino Suárez were forced to resign and then assassinated. The counter-revolutionary regime of General [[Victoriano Huerta]] came to power, backed by the [[United States involvement in the Mexican Revolution|United States]] and its ambassador [[Henry Lane Wilson]],<ref name="Tuñon Pablos p. 855">Tuñon Pablos, "Mexican Revolution: February 1913 – October 1915", p. 855</ref> business interests, and supporters of the old order. Huerta remained in power until July 1914, when he was forced out by a coalition of different regional revolutionary forces, including the forces of Pancho Villa and [[Emiliano Zapata]]. Wealthy landowner [[Venustiano Carranza]] formed the "Constitutionalist" political faction, and with military forces under the leadership of [[Álvaro Obregón]], played an important part in defeating Huerta.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|title=Villa and Zapata: A History of the Mexican Revolution|last=McLynn|first=Frank|publisher=Carroll & Graf Publishers|year=2001|isbn=0-7867-1088-8|location=United States|chapter=The Revolt Against Huerta}}</ref> When the revolutionaries' attempt to reach political agreement failed, Mexico plunged into a civil war (1914–15). Carranza emerged as the victor in 1915, defeating the Villistas and forcing Zapata back to [[guerrilla warfare]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=Villa and Zapata: A History of the Mexican Revolution|last=McLynn|first=Frank|publisher=Carroll & Graf Publishers|year=2001|isbn=0-7867-1088-8|location=United States|chapter=Villa at His Zenith; The End of Huerta; The Convention of Aguascalientes}}</ref> Zapata was assassinated in 1919 by agents of President Carranza. Many scholars consider the promulgation of the [[Constitution of Mexico|Mexican Constitution of 1917]] as the end point of the armed conflict.
[[File:Camp of Mexican Refugees.jpg|thumb|left|Camp for refugees of the Mexican Revolution]]
For the United States, the Revolution proved profoundly consequential, as the violence unleashed by the conflict led between 600,000 and 1,000,000 [[refugee]]s to flee into the United States.<ref>{{cite web |title=A Growing Community |url=https://www.loc.gov/classroom-materials/immigration/mexican/a-growing-community/ |website=Immigration and Relocation in U.S. History |publisher=Library of Congress |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref> Along the border, residents in American cities feared revolutionary fervor for land redistribution would inspire the poor and working classes in the U.S. to rise up against Anglo property ownership and domination.<ref name="The Injustice Never Leaves You"/> The Texas governor made a formal request for U.S. troops to guard the border and keep the revolutionary ideals out of the United States.<ref name="The Injustice Never Leaves You"/>
The border was effectively turned into a [[Mexican Border War (1910–1919)|militarized zone]]. Barbed wire, spotlights, tanks, machine guns, and airplanes were brought in to surveil Mexican residents and maintain "order" in the borderlands.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Contreras |first1=Russell |title=Why U.S. militarization of border isn't new |url=https://www.pbs.org/newshour/nation/why-u-s-militarization-of-border-isnt-new |website=PBS |date=5 April 2018 |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref> Around sixty-five hundred U.S. troops were stationed in [[El Paso, Texas|El Paso]], where they were ordered to keep Villistas from entering the city and keep watch over the Mexican refugees and Mexican Americans in the city. U.S. consular agent George Carothers wrote, "A large portion [of the] Mexican population of El Paso have arms ... which will create [a] dangerous situation here ... The possibility exists of them starting something serious."<ref>{{cite book |last1=Levario |first1=Miguel |title=Militarizing the Border: When Mexicans Became the Enemy |date=March 2, 2015 |publisher=Texas A&M University Press |isbn=978-1623493028 |page=82 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=CQNKDwAAQBAJ&dq=A+large+portion+%5Bof+the%5D+Mexican+population+of+El+Paso+have+arms...+which+will+create+%5Ba%5D+dangerous+situation+here&pg=PA82 |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref> In January 1916, white Americans took matters into their hands and started a "[[List of ethnic riots|race riot]]" in the majority-Mexican neighborhood of [[Chihuahuita, Texas|Chihuahuita]],<ref>{{cite web |last1=Levario |first1=Miguel A. |title=El Paso Race Riot of 1916 |url=https://www.tshaonline.org/handbook/entries/el-paso-race-riot-of-1916 |website=Handbook of Texas |publisher=Texas Historical Association |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref> attacking every Mexican refugee and Mexican American they found in the streets.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Darrah |first1=Jason T. |title=Anglos, Mexicans, and the San Ysabel Massacre: A Study of Changing Ethnic Relations in El Paso, Texas, 1910-1916 |url=https://ttu-ir.tdl.org/bitstream/handle/2346/19647/31295017115881.pdf?sequence=1 |publisher=Texas Tech University |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref>
[[File:Mathilde Martinez & children (LOC).jpg|thumb|right|200px|Refugees of the [[Mexican Revolution]] standing among tents, possibly in [[Marfa, Texas]], ca. 1910]]
While Mexican American historians have continued to debate the long-term consequences of the Mexican Revolution, one of its most long-lasting legacies was the mass dislocation of entire communities from Mexico to the United States.<ref name="Porous Borders"/> Men, women, and children fled on foot, traveled by wagon and horseback, and by railroad to enter the United States as refugees.<ref name="Mexican Emigration to the United States"/> In the aftermath of [[Pancho Villa Expedition]], one press report described the "hundreds" of refugees fleeing to the United States in the course of a single day: "Following the troops were hundreds of refugees. Prosperous Mormon families rode in comfortable farm wagons or in small motor cars. Some Mexicans rode in carriages, on horses, mules, burros and on the motor lorries of the expeditionary forces while hundreds of them and Chinese residents from the evacuated region walked through the deep dust which had been made by the feet of hundreds of troops."<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Lim |first1=Julian |title=Immigration, Asylum, and Citizenship: A More Holistic Approach |journal=California Law Review |date=August 2013 |volume=101 |issue=4 |pages=1013–1077 |jstor=23784324 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23784324 |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref>
===Treatment in the U.S.=== [[File:Junta Patriótica Mexicana del Pueblo Obrero.jpg|thumb|left|upright|A ''Junta Patriótica'' club]]
Life in the United States was difficult and violent for Mexican refugees and Mexican Americans in the latter half of the [[Progressive Era]]. Reformers in the era believed that non-white people were "primitive" and biologically inferior.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Hovenkamp |first1=Herbert J. |title=The Progressives: Racism and Public Law |url=https://scholarship.law.upenn.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=2767&context=faculty_scholarship |website=Penn Law: Legal Scholarship Repository |publisher=University of Pennsylvania Carey Law School |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref> The eugenics-influenced [[United States Congressional Joint Immigration Commission|Dillingham Commission]] argued for drastic reductions in the number of immigrants to the United States,<ref>{{cite book |last1=Benton-Cohen |first1=Katherine |title=Inventing the Immigration Problem: The Dillingham Commission and Its Legacy |date=May 7, 2018 |publisher=Harvard University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=978-0674976443 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9mFUDwAAQBAJ |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref> while academics such as [[Charles Davenport]] claimed racial "deficiencies" were the root of violence and poverty.<ref>{{cite web |title=Davenport, Charles |url=https://eugenicsarchive.ca/discover/connections/5233ce935c2ec500000000ab |website=Eugenics Archive |publisher=Social Sciences and Humanities Research Council of Canada |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref> This elite nativism had a direct translation on the ground, as refugees of the Mexican Revolution were regularly denied humanitarian aid. In one incident in 1914, refugees fleeing the violence of the revolution crossed into West Texas; in response, white Texans arrested the refugees and imprisoned them in [[Fort Bliss|Ft. Bliss]].<ref name="The Injustice Never Leaves You"/>
Most of the hundreds of thousands of Mexican refugees who fled to the United States during the Revolution settled in California and the U.S. Southwest. In [[Los Angeles]], many of the resettled refugees lived in the historically Mexican American area of [[East Los Angeles, California|East Los Angeles]].<ref name="Becoming Mexican American">{{cite book |last1=Sánchez |first1=George J. |title=Becoming Mexican American: Ethnicity, Culture, and Identity in Chicano Los Angeles, 1900-1945 |date=March 23, 1995 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=New York |isbn=978-0195096484 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HoJa0v7T0REC |access-date=27 November 2020}}</ref> State and local resources were thinly spread, and Mexican refugees and Mexican Americans were specifically targeted for exclusion from welfare programs.<ref name="Mexican Americans and Eugenic Sterilization">{{cite journal |last1=Stern |first1=Alexandra Minna |first2=Natalie |last2=Lira |title=Mexican Americans and Eugenic Sterilization: Resisting Reproductive Injustice in California, 1920–1950 |journal=Aztlán: A Journal of Chicano Studies |date=2014 |volume=39 |issue=2 |pages=9–34 |doi=10.1525/azt.2014.39.2.9 |url=https://www.academia.edu/23286442 |access-date=27 November 2020}}</ref> Mexicans were left to fill pre-existing gaps in the American labor market, and ''[[Colonia (United States)|colonias]]'', or Mexican-majority neighborhoods, were established in [[Chicago]], [[Kansas City metropolitan area|Kansas City]], and [[Salt Lake City]], as railroad companies were one of the main sources of employment.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Cardoso |first1=Lawrence A. |title=Mexican Emigration to the United States, 1897–1931 |date=1980 |publisher=University of Arizona Press |location=Tucson |doi=10.2307/j.ctvss3xzr |jstor=j.ctvss3xzr |isbn=9780816506781 |s2cid=242575639 |edition=1 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/j.ctvss3xzr |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref> The steel and automobile sectors drew Mexicans and Mexican Americans to [[Detroit]], [[San Francisco]], and [[Minneapolis]], as well as farming in [[Oregon]], [[Texas]], and [[Southern California]]. In order to provide middle-class Mexicans with a sense of grounding in their new communities, consulates of the Mexican government in major cities organized a network of ''juntas patrioticas'' (patriotic councils) and ''comisiónes honoríficos'' (honorary committees) to celebrate Mexican national holidays and to express support for the Revolution. The juntas patrioticas proved incredibly popular, and were an early form of [[community organizing]] for the Mexican community in the United States.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Rosales |first1=Francisco A. |title=Chicano! The History of the Mexican American Civil Rights Movement |date=January 1, 1997 |publisher=Arte Publico Press |location=Houston |isbn=978-1558852013 |page=78 |edition=2 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qvGgsGBFQncC&dq=juntas+patrioticas&pg=PA78 |access-date=27 November 2020}}</ref>
[[File:Boy's home, L.A., Calif. (LOC).jpg|thumb|right|A Los Angeles Boys' home. These homes for orphaned and delinquent boys often targeted young Mexican and African American boys for sterilization.]]
Mexican Americans also faced a darker form of discrimination during the Progressive Era. In this period, several dozen states passed [[eugenics]] laws, outlining legal criteria for [[compulsory sterilization]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Kaelber |first1=Lutz |title=Eugenics: Compulsory Sterilization in 50 American States |url=https://www.uvm.edu/~lkaelber/eugenics/ |website=2012 Social Science History Association |publisher=University of Vermont |access-date=27 November 2020}}</ref> In California, eugenicists at public institutions like [[Sonoma Developmental Center|Sonoma State Hospital]] and [[Fred C. Nelles Youth Correctional Facility|Whittier State School]] began their sterilization programs in the 1910s.<ref>{{cite web |title=Latinx Sterilization and Eugenics Programs |url=https://journeys.dartmouth.edu/lats3naren/eugenics-in-southern-california-in-the-mid-20th-century/ |website=Eugenics in California |publisher=Dartmouth University |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref> These doctors believed that "dispositions" for poverty, crime, mental illness, disability, and violence were inheritable, and thus, anyone diagnosed as "insane", "[[Feeble-minded|feebleminded]]", or "epileptic" was unfit for "breeding".<ref name="Fertile Matters">{{cite book |last1=Gutiérrez |first1=Elena R. |title=Fertile Matters: The Politics of Mexican-Origin Women's Reproduction |date=February 1, 2008 |publisher=University of Texas Press |location=Austin |isbn=978-0292716827 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rFTmDAAAQBAJ |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref> They would thus be permanently sterilized in order to solve the nation's social ills. Recommendations for surgical sterilization were heavily influenced by eugenic prejudices regarding the biological inadequacy of poor and non-white people.<ref name="States of Delinquency">{{cite book |last1=Chavez-Garcia |first1=Miroslava |title=States of Delinquency: Race and Science in the Making of California's Juvenile Justice System |date=February 21, 2012 |publisher=University of California Press |location=Berkeley |isbn=978-0520271722 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=eGu-q1Xz6yQC |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref> As a result, Mexican American men, women, and children were disproportionately sterilized in California. Many Mexicans immigrating to the United States struggled through their journey crossing borders because they hoped to find stability and better opportunities in this new environment. Although, this history portrays the beginning of Mexican immigration and how new events have continued to reoccur, whether being influenced by government issues or not.<ref>Garza, James A. "The Long History of Mexican Immigration to the Rural Midwest." Journal of the West, vol. 45, no. 4, Fall 2006, pp. 57–64. EBSCOhost</ref> By some estimates, Mexican Americans were between 40 and 60% more likely to be permanently sterilized than their white counterparts.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Lira |first1=Natalie |last2=Novak |first2=Nicole |last3=O'Connor |first3=Kate |last4=Harlow |first4=Siobán |last5=Kardia |first5=Sharon |last6=Alexandra |first6=Minna Stern |title=Disproportionate Sterilization of Latinos Under California's Eugenic Sterilization Program |journal=American Journal of Public Health |date=April 2018 |volume=108 |issue=5 |pages=611–613 |doi=10.2105/AJPH.2018.304369 |pmid=29565671 |pmc=5888070 |url=}}</ref> Progressive-era elites also passed severe [[Anti-miscegenation laws]], as well as laws prohibiting "normal" individuals from marrying people with low-IQs, in order to "breed" a more healthy nation.<ref name="Mexican Americans and Eugenic Sterilization"/> California's [[Eugenics in California|eugenic laws]] were the basis for [[Nazi eugenics]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Black |first1=Edwin |title=Eugenics and the Nazis: The California connection |url=https://www.sfgate.com/opinion/article/Eugenics-and-the-Nazis-the-California-2549771.php |website=SF Gate |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Kuhl |first1=Stefan |title=The Nazi Connection: Eugenics, American Racism, and German National Socialism |date=1994 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=New York |isbn=978-0195149784 |pages=39–44 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=UGYfRv3DWuQC |access-date=30 November 2020}}</ref>
===La Matanza de Texas=== {{Main|La Matanza (1910–1920)}} [[File:‘Dead Mexican Bandits’.jpg|thumb|right|Three Texas Rangers posing with the corpses of Mexican American men]]
The mid-1910s proved to be one of the most violent periods for Mexican Americans in Texan history. In the summer of 1915, a manifesto attributed to Mexican [[Bandit War#Seditionista campaign|seditionists]] was discovered, entitled [[Plan of San Diego|El Plan de San Diego]].<ref>{{cite web |title=The Plan of San Diego |url=https://www.digitalhistory.uh.edu/disp_textbook.cfm?smtid=3&psid=3692 |website=Digital History |publisher=University of Houston |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref> The plan sought to overthrow American control over the entire borderlands through a panethnic "Liberating Army of race and people", which would include Mexicans and Mexican Americans, [[African Americans]], and [[Japanese Americans]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Coerver |first1=Don M. |title=Plan of San Diego |url=https://www.tshaonline.org/handbook/entries/plan-of-san-diego |website=Handbook of Texas |publisher=Texas State Historical Association |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref> The plan called for the expulsion of Anglos and the execution of all white American men over the age of sixteen years old.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=M. Hager |first1=William |title=The Plan of San Diego Unrest on the Texas Border in 1915 |journal=Journal of the Southwest |date=1963 |volume=5 |issue=4 |pages=327–336 |jstor=40167788 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/40167788 |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref> The plan also called for burning bridges and derailing trains in order to disrupt federal, state, and local infrastructure. The initial steps of the plan were initiated through targeted attacks against prominent ranches.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Harris III |first1=Charles |last2=Sadler |first2=Louis R. |title=The Plan de San Diego: Tejano Rebellion, Mexican Intrigue |date=July 1, 2013 |publisher=University of Nebraska Press |location=Lincoln |isbn=978-0803264779 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qewFUHjX7UIC |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref> On August 8, 1915, around sixty ethnic men raided the [[Raid on Norias Ranch|Los Norias]] headquarters of the Texas Rangers on the [[King Ranch]].<ref>{{cite book |last1=Johnson |first1=Benjamin Heber |title=Revolution in Texas: How a Forgotten Rebellion and Its Bloody Suppression Turned Mexicans Into Americans |date=August 29, 2005 |publisher=Yale University Press |location=New Haven, CT |isbn=978-0300109702 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=iklPdtdzxoAC |access-date=13 December 2020}}</ref> The response to this raid and the discovery of the plan was swift and violent. The Texas Rangers launched an indiscriminate manhunt, killing every Mexican and Mexican American man they could find.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Truett |first1=Samuel |last2=Young |first2=Elliot |title=Continental Crossroads: Remapping U.S.-Mexico Borderlands History |date=2004 |publisher=Duke University Press |location=Durham |isbn=978-0-8223-8632-2 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xBgMPMRUJd4C |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref> One Ranger wrote that the troops showed a "savage radical element" against the Mexicans.<ref name="The Injustice Never Leaves You"/> Estimates range that at least 102 people were killed in the wake of this first incident.<ref name="The Plan of San Diego">{{cite journal |last1=Harris III |first1=Charles H. |last2=Sadler |first2=Louis R. |title=The Plan of San Diego and the Mexican-United States War Crisis of 1916: A Reexamination |journal=Hispanic American Historical Review |date=1978 |volume=58 |issue=3 |page=391 |doi=10.1215/00182168-58.3.381 |url=https://read.dukeupress.edu/hahr/article/58/3/381/150397/The-Plan-of-San-Diego-and-the-Mexican-United |access-date=29 November 2020|doi-access=free }}</ref>
[[File:Texas Rangers pose on a South Texas ranch in 1915 after one of their notorious "bandit raids.".jpg|thumb|left|Texas Rangers smiling alongside the corpses of dead Mexican Americans]]
This event set off a wave of violence across South Texas, described by some as an "orgy of bloodshed" known as ''La Hora de Sangre'' (part of a broader period called ''[[La Matanza (1910–1920)|La Matanza]]'').<ref>{{cite web |last1=Álvarez |first1=Maira Elizabeth |title=Creating an Archive: Froneriza Authors Writing Histories, Documenting U.S.-Mexico Border Militarization |url=https://uh-ir.tdl.org/bitstream/handle/10657/5655/ALVAREZ-DISSERTATION-2019.pdf?sequence=1 |website=Department of Hispanic Studies |publisher=University of Houston |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref> Texas judge [[James B. Wells Jr.|James Wells]] estimated that in [[Hidalgo County, Texas|Hidalgo County]] and [[Cameron County, Texas|Cameron County]] alone, Texas officers and vigilantes executed between 250 and 300 men between the summers of 1915 and 1916.<ref name="The Injustice Never Leaves You"/> Extralegal violence was enacted by white mobs, state police, and local deputies across Texas, and estimates of the dead range from 300 to several thousand.<ref>{{cite web |title=The History of Racial Violence on the Borderlands |url=https://refusingtoforget.org/the-history/ |website=Refusing to Forget |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref> Police often colluded with the vigilante mobs, and Mexican Americans were tortured, hanged, shot, beat, and burned alive.<ref>{{cite web |title=Testimony of Monica Muñoz Martinez |url=https://docs.house.gov/meetings/JU/JU01/20190906/109889/HHRG-116-JU01-Wstate-MunozMartinezM-20190906.pdf |website=Committee on The Judiciary Subcommittee on The Immigration and Citizenship |publisher=United States House of Representatives |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref> Many of the known victims were adult men, though a few women, and some children, were murdered by the white mobs.<ref name="The Injustice Never Leaves You"/> The executions often occurred in isolated and rural areas hidden from public view. Assailants almost never faced arrest, and grand juries refused to indict the accused, as in the case of [[Porvenir massacre (1918)|Porvenir massacre]].<ref>{{cite news |last1=Carrigan |first1=William D. |last2=Webb |first2=Clive |title=When Americans Lynched Mexicans |work=The New York Times |date=20 February 2015 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2015/02/20/opinion/when-americans-lynched-mexicans.html |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref> Mexican Americans had no option but to organize their lives to avoid law enforcement or the white American community.<ref name="The Injustice Never Leaves You"/>
===Immigration policies=== {{See also|Immigration Act of 1917|1917 Bath riots|Bisbee Deportation}}
[[File:Geraldine Portica--This is not a girl, but a boy, who was reared by his mother as a girl.jpg|thumb|right|upright|Geraldine Portica, a transgender Mexican woman deported from the United States to Mexico in 1917]]
The nativism which had been growing in the United States for several decades grew even stronger in the aftermath of the Mexican Revolution. In January 1917, the United States passed the [[Immigration Act of 1917]]. This legislation severely curtailed immigration to the United States and marked a major turning point in U.S. immigration policy. The act introduced a [[Literacy test|literacy requirement]], increased the "head tax" to eight dollars (a significant sum at the time), added new prohibited categories (including peoples with [[Mental disorder|mental illness]], as well as the "[[Sexual inversion (sexology)|gender inverse]]", meaning all members of the [[LGBT community]]),<ref name="Arresting Dress">{{cite book |last1=Sears |first1=Clare |title=Arresting Dress: Cross-Dressing, Law, and Fascination in Nineteenth-Century San Francisco |date=December 26, 2014 |publisher=Duke University Press |location=Durham |isbn=978-0822357582 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=5CywBgAAQBAJ |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref> and extended the [[Chinese Exclusion Act]] to bar all Asian immigrants (except from Japan and the Philippines).<ref>{{cite web |last1=Boissoneault |first1=Lorraine |title=Literacy Tests and Asian Exclusion Were the Hallmarks of the 1917 Immigration Act |url=https://www.smithsonianmag.com/history/how-america-grappled-immigration-100-years-ago-180962058/ |website=[[Smithsonian (magazine)|Smithsonian]] |publisher=Smithsonian Institution |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref> This act created a burden for Mexican immigrants, as the literacy requirements, head taxes, and restricted categories were applied vigorously.<ref>{{cite web |title=The Mexican Revolution 1910-1917 |url=http://www.laits.utexas.edu/jaime/cwp5/cig/text/extramexrev.html |website=College of Liberal Arts |publisher=University of Texas at Austin |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref> In 1917, the [[United States Public Health Service|U.S. Public Health Service]] also implemented invasive [[Medical exclusion of immigrants|medical inspections]] at the border (where men and boys would be stripped naked and examined for "defective" anatomy – including large breasts or small genitalia – and sprayed with chemical agents to be "disinfected").<ref name="The Straight State">{{cite book |last1=Canaday |first1=Margot |title=The Straight State: Sexuality and Citizenship in Twentieth-Century America |date=July 25, 2011 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-0691149936 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4Dkb6hSNDCEC |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref> Outrage at these procedures resulted in the [[1917 Bath riots]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Burnett |first1=John |title=The Bath Riots: Indignity Along the Mexican Border |url=https://www.npr.org/templates/story/story.php?storyId=5176177 |website=NPR |date=28 January 2006 |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref> Soon, agricultural interests in California and Texas, successfully lobbied Secretary of Labor [[William Bauchop Wilson|William B. Wilson]] to create exemptions for agricultural laborers.<ref name="Porous Borders"/> For all other immigrants from Mexico, the protocols remained in place.
With these new policies in place, nativists across the United States were emboldened to enact anti-Mexican violence. In one particularly infamous and egregious incident in [[Bisbee, Arizona]], over 1,000 Mexican and Mexican American laborers were forcibly deported by an army of over 2,000 deputies in an incident known as the [[Bisbee Deportation]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Bisbee Deportation |url=https://azlibrary.gov/dazl/learners/research-topics/bisbee-deportation |website=Digital Arizona Library |publisher=Arizona State Library, Archives & Records |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref> The laborers, who were miners in the nearby copper mine, had been organized by the [[Industrial Workers of the World]], a labor union, on June 26, 1917.<ref>{{cite web |title=An Overview of the Bisbee Deportation of 1917 |url=https://geoalliance.asu.edu/sites/default/files/LessonFiles/GeoHistory/DeMasiBisbee_abbrev/deMasiBisbee_abbrevS.pdf |website=Arizona Geographic Alliance |publisher=Arizona State University |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref> In response, Sheriff [[Harry Wheeler]] and his army of deputies rounded up and arrested over a thousand of the men on July 12, 1917, marching them to a waiting train.<ref>{{cite web |title=Remembering the Bisbee Deportations of 1917 |url=https://speccoll.library.arizona.edu/news/remembering-bisbee-deportation-1917 |website=Special Collections at the University of Arizona Libraries |publisher=University of Arizona |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref> The train carried the men to [[Columbus, New Mexico]], where it left them in the desert without food or water.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Wills |first1=Matthew |title=The Bisbee Deportations |url=https://daily.jstor.org/the-bisbee-deportations/ |date=17 August 2018 |access-date=29 November 2020}}</ref>
===World War I=== [[File:Marcelino Serna, most decorated veteran of World War I.jpg|thumb|left|upright|[[Marcelino Serna]], an immigrant from Mexico, was one of World War I's most highly decorated men.]]
[[United States in World War I|U.S. involvement in World War I]] lasted from April 6, 1917 until the war's end on November 11, 1918. During this time, approximately 200,000 Latino Americans fought for the United States.<ref name="Res. 253">{{cite web |title=Res. 253 (110th): Hispanic American Heroes Resolution |url=https://www.govtrack.us/congress/bills/110/hconres253/text |website=GovTrack |publisher=United States House of Representatives |access-date=23 November 2020}}</ref> The majority of these Latino servicemen were Mexican Americans.<ref name="Res. 253"/> Many of these men experienced discrimination in the service, and some went so far as to hide their Mexican ancestry to avoid prejudicial treatment.<ref>{{cite web |last1=MacDonald |first1=Victoria-María |last2=Taylor |first2=Emma |title=Towards a History of Mexican American Participation in World War I |date=9 October 2018 |url=https://text-message.blogs.archives.gov/2018/10/09/towards-a-history-of-mexican-american-participation-in-world-war-i-part-i/ |publisher=U.S. National Archives |access-date=23 November 2020}}</ref> Unlike [[African Americans|African American]] servicemen, however, Mexican Americans did not serve in [[Racial segregation in the United States Armed Forces|segregated units]] during World War I.<ref name="To the Line of Fire">{{cite book |last1=Ramírez |first1=José A. |title=To the Line of Fire! Mexican Texans and World War I |date=October 5, 2009 |publisher=Texas A&M University Press |location=College Station |isbn=978-1603441360 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=cxxAYNU0dPoC |access-date=23 November 2020}}</ref> Even as white American servicemen harassed the Mexican American soldiers for their "barrio English", Mexican American soldiers proved decisive in several key skirmishes, including the [[Battle of Saint-Mihiel]] and the [[Meuse–Argonne offensive]].<ref name="To the Line of Fire"/>
[[David B. Barkley]], one Mexican American man from [[Laredo, Texas|Laredo]], was posthumously awarded the [[Medal of Honor]] for scouting behind enemy lines in France. And [[Marcelino Serna]], who immigrated to the United States from Mexico as a young man, was awarded the [[Distinguished Service Cross (United States)|Distinguished Service Cross]]. During the war, Serna singlehandedly destroyed a German machine gun site in one battle.<ref name="Serna, Marcelino">{{cite web |last1=Duarte |first1=Rolando |title=Serna, Marcelino (1896–1992) |url=https://www.tshaonline.org/handbook/entries/serna-marcelino |website=Texas State Historical Association: Handbook of Texas |access-date=23 November 2020}}</ref> Two weeks later, he took out 26 enemy soldiers, captured 26 more as prisoners, and successfully prevented his fellow American soldiers from executing the captured men.<ref name="Serna, Marcelino"/> Serna returned to the United States as the most decorated soldier from the state of [[Texas]].<ref>{{cite news |last1=Blanco |first1=César |title=Pvt. Marcelino Serna deserves Medal of Honor for heroics during World War I |url=https://www.elpasotimes.com/story/opinion/columnists/2020/09/10/state-rep-cesar-blanco-pvt-marcelino-serna-deserves-medal-honor/3458322001/ |access-date=23 November 2020 |website=[[El Paso Times]] |date=10 September 2020}}</ref>
==1920s== ===Rise of the KKK=== {{multiple image | direction = vertical | width = 165 | footer = The Ku Klux Klan reached several million members and attained mainstream status in [[White Americans|white America]] in the 1920s. This terrorist organization believed Mexican Americans represented a "threat" to the "purity" of the United States. | image1 = Jane Snyder at K.K.K. services, 8-9-25 LCCN2016840308.jpg | alt1 = Jane Snyder at KKK services | caption1 = | image2 = Ku Klux Klan marching down Pennsylvania Ave., Washington, D.C LCCN2016888117.jpg | alt2 = Ku Klux Klan marching down Pennsylvania Ave., Washington, D.C. | caption2 = | image3 = KKK Services LCCN2016850801.jpg | alt3 = }}
In the years after World War I, the rising tide of nativism continued to grow in the 1920s. The Second [[Ku Klux Klan]] was a new organization patterned after the original KKK of the 1860s.
