It was [[Anti-Catholicism|anti-Catholic]] and [[Opposition to immigration|anti-immigrant]]<ref>{{cite web |last1=Dimick |first1=Kristin |title=The Ku Klux Klan and the Anti-Catholic School Bills of Washington and Oregon |url=https://depts.washington.edu/civilr/kkk_i49.htm |website=The Seattle Civil Rights and Labor History Project |publisher=University of Washington |access-date=12 December 2020}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Immigration Restriction and the KKK |url=https://ehistory.osu.edu/sites/ehistory.osu.edu/files/mmh/clash/Imm_KKK/anti-immigrationKKK-page1.htm |website=Clash of Cultures in the 1910s and 1920s |publisher=Oregon State University |access-date=12 December 2020}}</ref> and taught that all Mexicans and Mexican Americans were "subhuman foreigners".<ref name="The Ku Klux Klan's Campaign Against Hispanics">{{cite book |last1=Sánchez |first1=Juan O. |title=The Ku Klux Klan's Campaign Against Hispanics, 1921-1925: Rhetoric, Violence and Response in the American Southwest |date=July 31, 2018 |publisher=McFarland |location=Jefferson, NC |isbn=978-1476671130 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=R69mDwAAQBAJ |access-date=12 December 2020}}</ref> The KKK mounted an extensive campaign of violence across the Southwest.<ref name="The Ku Klux Klan's Campaign Against Hispanics"/>
The KKK had a strong presence in Texas and California.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Bernstein |first1=Patricia |title=Ten Dollars to Hate: The Texas Man Who Fought the Klan |date=January 20, 2017 |publisher=Texas A&M University Press |location=College Station |isbn=978-1623495299 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=8hNpDwAAQBAJ |access-date=16 December 2020}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Rhomberg |first1=Chris |title=White Nativism and Urban Politics: The 1920s Ku Klux Klan in Oakland, California |journal=Journal of American Ethnic History |date=Winter 1998 |volume=17 |issue=2 |pages=39–55 |jstor=27502269 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27502269 |access-date=16 December 2020}}</ref> The organization had adherents in rural communities in both states,<ref>{{cite book |last1=Kuehler Walters |first1=Katherine |title=The 1920s Texas Ku Klux Klan Revisited: White Supremacy And Structural Power In A Rural County |date=May 2018 |publisher=Texas A&M University |location=College Station |edition=Dissertation |url=https://oaktrust.library.tamu.edu/bitstream/handle/1969.1/173554/WALTERS-DISSERTATION-2018.pdf?sequence=1&isAllowed=y |access-date=16 December 2020}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Bringhurst |first1=Newell G. |title=The Ku Klux Klan in a Central California Community: Tulare County During the 1920s and 1930s |journal=Southern California Quarterly |date=Winter 2000 |volume=82 |issue=4 |pages=365–396 |doi=10.2307/41172036 |jstor=41172036 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/41172036 |access-date=16 December 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref> and held major political control in large cities.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Young |first1=Robin |title=In The 1920s, 1 In 3 Eligible Men In Dallas Were KKK Members |url=https://www.wbur.org/hereandnow/2019/11/06/ku-klux-klan-dallas-texas |website=WBUR |date=6 November 2019 |publisher=National Public Radio |access-date=16 December 2020}}</ref><ref name="San Diego's Ku Klux Klan 1920-1980">{{cite journal |last1=Griswold del Castillo |first1=Richard |last2=Larralde |first2=Carlos M. |title=San Diego's Ku Klux Klan 1920-1980 |journal=The Journal of San Diego History |date=Summer 2000 |volume=46 |issue=2 |url=https://sandiegohistory.org/journal/2000/april/klan/ |access-date=16 December 2020}}</ref> In [[Dallas]], where the KKK gained control over the city's politics, it sought to completely close off the border with Mexico.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philips |first1=Michael |title=White Metropolis: Race, Ethnicity, and Religion in Dallas, 1841-2001 |date=January 2, 2006 |publisher=University of Texas Press |location=Austin |isbn=978-0292712744 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rzJWsTCYza0C |access-date=16 December 2020}}</ref> In [[San Diego]], the KKK was led by many of the city's elite. According to [[Ernesto Galarza]], a labor activist and professor, "Mexicans were seen as an endangerment to traditional American values. [Even the clergy] often ignored the Klan's abuses toward Latinos".<ref name="San Diego's Ku Klux Klan 1920-1980"/> The KKK in Southern California regularly lynched, tortured, dragged, and murdered Mexicans and Mexican Americans. Mercedes Acasan Garcia, a refugee of the Revolution working as a maid in San Diego in the 1920s, stated, "Since they were ragged Wetbacks, nobody cared who they were and nothing was done about it."<ref name="San Diego's Ku Klux Klan 1920-1980"/> The KKK was also extremely politically powerful in [[Los Angeles]], and in 1928, the KKK senior member [[John Clinton Porter]] was elected [[Mayor of Los Angeles|mayor]] of the city.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Stevenson |first1=Brenda Elaine |title=The Contested Murder of Latasha Harlins: Justice, Gender, and the Origins of the LA Riots |date=September 1, 2015 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=New York |isbn=978-0190231019 |page=186 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=GXhpAgAAQBAJ&dq=John+Clinton+Porter+kkk&pg=PA186 |access-date=16 December 2020}}</ref>
[[Colorado]] was another area with extensive Klan activity. During [[World War I]], Colorado companies sought to fill labor gaps left by soldiers through recruiting refugees of the Mexican Revolution (who had arrived in the United States in large numbers during the 1910s).<ref name="Ten Dollars to Hate Somebody">{{cite web |last1=Bowley |first1=Nicoli R. |title='Ten Dollars to Hate Somebody:' Hispanic Communities and the Ku Klux Klan in Colorado, 1917-1925 |url=https://scholar.colorado.edu/concern/undergraduate_honors_theses/x346d479f |website=CU Scholars |publisher=University of Colorado Boulder |access-date=16 December 2020}}</ref> As labor recruiters paid to relocate large numbers of refugees to Colorado, white residents became increasingly enraged at the presence of the "radical aliens".<ref name="Ten Dollars to Hate Somebody"/> During the [[First Red Scare]] of 1920, significant numbers of white Coloradans joined nativist and "one hundred percent American" organizations.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Coben |first1=Stanley |title=A Study in Nativism: The American Red Scare of 1919-20 |journal=Political Science Quarterly |date=March 1964 |volume=79 |issue=1 |pages=52–75 |doi=10.2307/2146574 |jstor=2146574 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/2146574 |access-date=16 December 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Despite these sentiments, corporations continued to recruit Mexicans. In 1921, John Galen Locke harnessed the intensifying anti-Mexican feelings and organized the KKK in Colorado.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Goldberg |first1=Robert Alan |title=Hooded Empire: The Ku Klux Klan in Colorado |date=March 1, 1982 |publisher=University of Illinois Press |location=Urbana, IL |isbn=978-0252008481 |edition=1}}</ref> By 1925, the KKK had emerged as the dominant political force in Colorado, running on campaigns of [[Law and order (politics)|law and order]] and anti-Mexicanism. The KKK deliberately held rallies in cities with large Mexican neighborhoods.<ref name="Ten Dollars to Hate Somebody"/>
===Johnson–Reed Act=== {{See also|Emergency Quota Act|Immigration Act of 1924}} [[File:U.S. border guard and Mexicans behind the border fence.jpg|thumb|left|U.S. border guard and Mexicans behind the border fence, c. 1920s]]
In the years following World War I, the United States nearly entirely shut out immigration from the rest of the world. In 1921, Congress passed the ''[[Emergency Quota Act|"Emergency" Quota Act of 1921]]'', which set immigration levels to "3 percent of the number of foreign-born persons of each nationality" living in the U.S. as determined by the [[1910 United States census]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Closing the Door on Immigration |url=https://www.nps.gov/articles/closing-the-door-on-immigration.htm |website=National Park Service |publisher=Department of the Interior |access-date=22 December 2020}}</ref> This Act was meant to reserve most immigration quota slots to northwestern European countries, which received the largest number of the 350,000 total slots created.<ref>{{cite web |title="Emergency" Quota Law of May 19, 1921 (42 Statutes-at-Large 5) |url=http://people.sunyulster.edu/voughth/quota_laws.htm |website=Summary of 1920s Quota Laws |publisher=SUNY Ulster |access-date=22 December 2020}}</ref> Immigration restrictionists, however, were not satisfied, and U.S. Representative [[Albert Johnson (congressman)|Albert Johnson]] and Senator [[David A. Reed|David Reed]] responded by pushing for even stricter quotas.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Allerfeldt |first1=Kristofer |author1-link=Kristofer Allerfeldt|title='And We Got Here First': Albert Johnson, National Origins and Self-Interest in the Immigration Debate of the 1920s |journal=Journal of Contemporary History |date=January 2010 |volume=45 |issue=1 |pages=7–26 |doi=10.1177/0022009409348019 |jstor=40542903 |s2cid=143764832 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/40542903 |access-date=22 December 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref> The resulting ''[[Immigration Act of 1924|Johnson-Reed Act]]'' reduced the quota from 3 percent to 2 percent, and changed the census base from 1910 to 1890, which meant that only people from West and North Europe could migrate.<ref>{{cite web |title=The Immigration Act of 1924 (The Johnson-Reed Act) |url=https://history.state.gov/milestones/1921-1936/immigration-act |website=Office of the Historian |publisher=U.S. Department of State |access-date=22 December 2020}}</ref>
For Mexicans, however, the effect of the law was complicated, as the quota system applied only to countries outside the [[Western Hemisphere]], meaning there were no caps on immigration from any Latin American country.<ref>{{cite web |title=Immigration Restriction Act of 1924 |url=https://www.digitalhistory.uh.edu/disp_textbook.cfm?smtID=3&psid=1116 |website=Digital History |publisher=University of Houston |access-date=22 December 2020}}</ref> This lack of a quota for Mexicans was controversial amongst the U.S. nativist movement, who were outraged at the large numbers of Mexican immigrants entering the country – including thousands during the [[Cristero War]] (1926-1929).<ref name="Unwanted Mexican Americans in the Great Depression">{{cite book |last1=Hoffman |first1=Abraham |title=Unwanted Mexican Americans in the Great Depression: Repatriation Pressures, 1929-1939 |date=January 1, 1974 |publisher=University of Arizona Press |location=Tucson |isbn=978-0816503667 |url=https://open.uapress.arizona.edu/read/8d568355-80a6-4e11-a9c7-ee8934b3fec8/section/11f7de5f-85f3-4fb9-900d-4cdde15f959f |access-date=22 December 2020}}</ref> Immigration restrictionists, who desired to limit the number of non-white people in the U.S., felt that immigration from Mexico, even when only to fill labor gaps, was dangerous to the nation.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Lytle Hernández |first1=Kelly |title=City of Inmates: Conquest, Rebellion, and the Rise of Human Caging in Los Angeles, 1771–1965 |date=April 10, 2017 |publisher=The University of North Carolina Press |location=Chapel Hill |isbn=978-1469631189 |page=134 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_0YhDgAAQBAJ&dq=nativists+%22mexicans%22&pg=PA134 |access-date=22 December 2020}}</ref> One article in ''[[The Saturday Evening Post]]'' asked, "How much longer [are] we going to defer putting the Mexican Indian under the quota law we have established for Europe?"<ref>{{cite book |last1=Ngai |first1=Mae |title=Impossible Subjects: Illegal Aliens and the Making of Modern America |date=April 27, 2014 |publisher=Princeton University Press |location=Princeton, NJ |isbn=978-0691160825 |page=52 |edition=2 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=X1dzAgAAQBAJ&dq=How+much+longer+%5Bare%5D+we+going+to+defer+putting+the+Mexican+Indian+under+the+quota+law+we+have+established+for+Europe%3F&pg=PA52 |access-date=20 December 2020}}</ref> The East Texas congressman [[John C. Box]] went so far as to state that Mexicans would lead to the "mongrelization" of white America.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Box |first1=John |title=Immigration Restriction (1928) |url=https://www.digitalhistory.uh.edu/disp_textbook.cfm?smtID=3&psid=594 |website=Digital History |publisher=University of Houston |access-date=22 December 2020}}</ref> In general, however, Mexican racial identity was contentious enough for lawmakers to avoid moving forward with increased restrictions. U.S. Secretary of Labor [[James J. Davis|James Davis]], wrote, "The Mexican people are of such a mixed stock and individuals have such a limited knowledge of their racial composition that it would be impossible for the most learned and experienced ethnologist or anthropologist to classify or determine their racial origin. Thus, making an effort to exclude them from admission or citizenship because of their racial status is practically impossible."<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Molina |first1=Natalia |title='In a Race All Their Own': The Quest to Make Mexicans Ineligible for U.S. Citizenship |journal=Pacific Historical Review |date=May 2010 |volume=79 |issue=2 |pages=167–201 |doi=10.1525/phr.2010.79.2.167 |jstor=10.1525/phr.2010.79.2.167 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/10.1525/phr.2010.79.2.167 |access-date=22 December 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref> According to scholars of the period, however, most Americans at the time believed Mexicans' racial heritage was "impure".<ref name="The Future Immense"/>
[[File:U.S. border guards check entering Mexicans.jpg|thumb|right|U.S. border guards check entering Mexicans, c. 1920]]
Even though Mexican immigration was never subjected to quota limits, U.S. immigration officials used increasingly stringent measures to limit entry. For itinerant laborers who lived in Mexico and worked in the United States, weekly disinfection mandates were regularized, and quarantine and "bath certificates" were required to be renewed weekly.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Stern |first1=Alexandra Minna |title=Buildings, Boundaries, and Blood: Medicalization and Nation-Building on the U.S.-Mexico Border, 1910-1930 |journal=The Hispanic American Historical Review |date=February 1999 |volume=79 |issue=1 |pages=41–81 |doi=10.1215/00182168-79.1.41 |jstor=2518217 |pmid=21162338 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/2518217 |access-date=22 December 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Then, in 1924, the U.S. Congress approved the creation of the [[United States Border Patrol|Border Patrol]], led by the previous chief "Chinese Inspector", Clifford Alan Perkins.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Perkins |first1=Clifford A. |title=Reminiscences of a Chinese Inspector |journal=The Journal of Arizona History |date=Summer 1976 |volume=17 |issue=2 |pages=181–200 |jstor=41859448 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/41859448 |access-date=22 December 2020}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=1924: Border Patrol Established |url=https://www.cbp.gov/about/history/1924-border-patrol-established |website=U.S. Customs and Border Protection |publisher=Department of Homeland Security |access-date=22 December 2020}}</ref> The Border Patrol began with headquarters in [[El Paso]] overseeing three district offices in Los Angeles, El Paso, and San Antonio. Beginning in the 1920s, visa controls and deportations became regular mechanisms to regulate Mexican immigration.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Ngai |first1=Mae M. |title=The Strange Career of the Illegal Alien: Immigration Restriction and Deportation Policy in the United States, 1921-1965 |journal=Law and History Review |date=Spring 2003 |volume=21 |issue=1 |pages=69–107 |doi=10.2307/3595069 |jstor=3595069 |s2cid=145344500 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/3595069 |access-date=22 December 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Finally in 1929, Congress passed the ''Aliens Act of 1929'', known as Blease's Law, which turned undocumented entry into the U.S. a misdemeanor and re-entry a felony.<ref>{{cite web |title=Sess. II. Ch. 690. 1929. |url=https://www.loc.gov/law/help/statutes-at-large/70th-congress/session-2/c70s2ch690.pdf |website=Seventieth Congress of the United States |publisher=Library of Congress |access-date=20 December 2020}}</ref> Up to that point, immigration violations were largely considered civil matters.<ref name="Governing Immigration Through Crime">{{cite book |last1=Inda |first1=Jonathan Xavier |last2=Stumpf |first2=Juliet |title=Governing Immigration Through Crime |date=March 27, 2013 |publisher=Stanford University Press |location=Palo Alto |isbn=978-0804785419 |page=63 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FvaeHTVhWpoC |access-date=22 December 2020}}</ref>
===Mexicans in Hollywood=== [[File:Ramona lobby card.jpg|thumb|left|upright|Lobby card for the U.S. drama film ''[[Ramona (1928 film)|Ramona]]'' (1928)]]
In the 1920s, Mexican entertainers entered American popular culture for the first time in U.S. history. [[Dolores del Río]] was an actress, singer, and dancer. Born and raised in Mexico, she and her husband left Mexico in 1925. Both were from upper-class families who were struggling in the aftermath of the Mexican Revolution.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Hall |first1=Linda B. |title=Dolores del Río: Beauty in Light and Shade |date=December 18, 2015 |publisher=Stanford University Press |location=Palo Alto |isbn=978-0804799461 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SwEZx1L5h8sC |access-date=13 December 2020}}</ref> She began her film career in Hollywood almost immediately upon her arrival. She had roles on a string of successful [[silent film]]s, including ''[[Resurrection (1927 film)|Resurrection]]'' (1927), ''[[Ramona (1928 film)|Ramona]]'' (1928) and ''[[Evangeline (1929 film)|Evangeline]]'' (1929). While del Río was proud and insistent of her Mexican heritage, she was nevertheless mostly cast in non-Hispanic white roles, usually appearing as the romantic interest of white actors. Many considered her the most beautiful woman in the world in the 1920s and early 1930s, and she is widely regarded as the first major female [[Latin Americans|Latin American]] star in [[Cinema of the United States|Hollywood]].<ref>{{cite book |last1=Hall |first1=Linda B. |title=Dolores del Río: Beauty in Light and Shade |date=9 January 2013 |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=978-0-8047-8621-8 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SwEZx1L5h8sC |language=en |page=3}}</ref><ref name="filmint.nu">{{cite web|first=Martin|last=Mulcahey|url=http://filmint.nu/the-first-latina-to-conquer-hollywood/|title=The First Latina to Conquer Hollywood |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140625190902/http://filmint.nu/?p=3797|archive-date=25 June 2014|work=[[Film International]]|date=29 December 2011|access-date=19 July 2016}}</ref><ref>[https://screendeco.wordpress.com/2012/05/18/the-face-of-deco-dolores-del-rio-by-annette-bochenek/ ''The Face of Deco: Dolores Del Rio''] {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160107013818/https://screendeco.wordpress.com/2012/05/18/the-face-of-deco-dolores-del-rio-by-annette-bochenek |date=2016-01-07}}, Screendeco.wordpress.com, May 18, 2012.</ref>
[[File:The Gaucho (1927) poster 1.jpg|thumb|right|upright|Doug Fairbanks and Lupe Vélez in ''[[The Gaucho]]'' (1927)]]
In the 1920s, another Mexican actress reached the heights of Hollywood stardom. [[Lupe Vélez]] attended high school in [[San Antonio|San Antonio, Texas]] as a teenager but returned to Mexico after her family lost their home during the Mexican Revolution.<ref name="A Trans-American Dream">{{cite book |last1=Rawson |first1=Kristy A. |title=A Trans-American Dream: Lupe Vélez and the Performance of Transculturation |date=2012 |publisher=The University of Michigan |location=Ann Arbor |edition=Dissertation |url=https://deepblue.lib.umich.edu/bitstream/handle/2027.42/93812/krawson_1.pdf |access-date=18 December 2020}}</ref> The family struggled financially in those years, and Vélez moved to [[Mexico City]] to work in an FAL department store – then considered an upper-class symbol of modern global capitalism. Her breakthrough occurred when she appeared in a popular musical production in the city's revista theater.<ref name="A Trans-American Dream"/> After moving to the U.S., she made her first film appearance in a [[short film|short]] in 1927. By the end of the decade, she was acting in full-length [[silent film]]s and had progressed to leading roles in ''[[The Gaucho]]'' (1927), ''[[Lady of the Pavements]]'' (1928) and ''[[Wolf Song]]'' (1929). Vélez' roles were varied, though she often portrayed "exotic" and "foreign" women.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Sturtevant |first1=Victoria |title=Spitfire: Lupe Vélez and the Ambivalent Pleasures of Ethnic Masquerade |journal=Velvet Light Trap |date=Spring 2005 |volume=55 |pages=19–32 |doi=10.1353/vlt.2005.0008 |s2cid=193164391 |url=https://www.proquest.com/openview/93c19a115dee285441737c19248b791c/1?pq-origsite=gscholar&cbl=35238 |access-date=22 December 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref>
[[File:Revenge lobby card.jpg|thumb|left|upright|Lobby card for the U.S. drama film ''Revenge'' (1928), starring [[Dolores del Río|Dolores Del Rio]]]]
Though Hollywood had two Mexican star actresses in the 1920s (and the male star [[Ramon Novarro]]), there was still controversy over the stereotypical depictions of Latin Americans in film. In the 1920s, Latin America was Hollywood's largest export market.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Hershfield |first1=Joanne |last2=Torres San Martín |first2=Patricia |title=Writing the History of Latin American Women Working in the Silent Film Industry |url=https://wfpp.columbia.edu/essay/writing-the-history-of-latin-american-women-working-in-the-silent-film-industry/ |website=Women Film Pioneers Project |publisher=Columbia University |access-date=18 December 2020}}</ref> In Mexico, nearly 80 percent of all films screened were made in the United States. Nevertheless, Mexicans and other Latin Americans were often depicted on screen as lazy, barbaric, morally degenerate, or buffoonish.<ref name="Mexican-Americans Have Always Battled Movie Stereotypes">{{cite web |last1=Blakemore |first1=Erin |title=Mexican-Americans Have Always Battled Movie Stereotypes |url=https://daily.jstor.org/mexican-americans-have-always-battled-movie-stereotypes/ |date=8 May 2017 |publisher=ITHAKA |access-date=18 December 2020}}</ref> Mexican Americans, already facing an onslaught of discrimination in other aspects of their everyday lives, were concerned that such depictions were contributing to the prejudicial treatment they were receiving in the United States. [[List of Spanish-language newspapers published in the United States|Spanish-language newspapers]] criticized Hollywood "greaser" films' depictions of Latin Americans and even called on the Mexican government to take a stand against Hollywood.<ref name="Making Cinelandia">{{cite book |last1=Serna |first1=Laura Isabel |title=Making Cinelandia: American Films and Mexican Film Culture Before the Golden Age |date=March 28, 2014 |publisher=Duke University Press |location=Durham, NC |isbn=978-0822356530 |edition=1st |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=kGWSAwAAQBAJ |access-date=18 December 2020}}</ref> The Mexican government did launch an influence campaign, but its success was limited. According to one historian, "The Mexican immigrant community in Los Angeles used discussions about cinema to critique American racial and political ideologies."<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Serna |first1=Laura I. |title='As a Mexican I Feel It's My Duty:' Citizenship, Censorship, and the Campaign against Derogatory Films in Mexico, 1922-1930 |journal=The Americas |date=Fall 2006 |volume=63 |issue=2 |pages=225–244 |doi=10.1353/tam.2006.0173 |jstor=4491219 |s2cid=144489656 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/4491219 |access-date=22 December 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref>
===Labor issues=== [[File:Mexican Americans workmen pausing for the camera as they make adobe bricks at the Casa Verdugo, ca.1900-1920 (CHS-5196).jpg|thumb|right|upright|Mexican American workmen making adobe bricks at the Casa Verdugo, California]]
In the 1920s, Mexicans met the increasing demand for cheap labor on the West Coast. Mexican refugees continued to migrate to areas outside the Southwest; they were recruited to work in the steel mills of [[Chicago]] during a strike in 1919, and again in 1923.<ref>Aristide Zoldberg, ''A Nation by Design'', Pg. 256</ref> Many found work on the assembly lines of automobile factories in [[Detroit]], and in the meat plants of Chicago and [[Kansas City, Missouri|Kansas City]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Vargas |first1=Zaragosa |title=Latino Workers in the 19th Century |url=https://www.nps.gov/heritageinitiatives/latino/latinothemestudy/labor.htm |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141013052215/http://www.nps.gov/heritageinitiatives/latino/latinothemestudy/labor.htm |url-status=dead |archive-date=October 13, 2014 |website=The Making of America |publisher=National Park Service |access-date=16 December 2020}}</ref>
Many also worked as agricultural laborers in farming valleys in the border states, such as [[Tucson, Arizona|Tucson]] in Arizona, the [[Rio Grande Valley (Texas)|Rio Grande Valley]] in Texas, and most especially, the [[Imperial Valley]] in California. Anglo-Americans hired Mexicans and Mexican Americans to work in the region's year-round agricultural economy. Mexican farm laborers, along with [[African Americans]], [[Filipino Americans]], [[Japanese Americans|Japanese-Americans]], and even [[Armenian Americans]], [[Punjabi Americans]], [[Native Hawaiians]] and Native Americans, were instrumental in California becoming the nation's top agricultural state. In this shift toward agricultural dominance, California relied on the cheap labor of Mexicans and Mexican Americans in a wide variety of fields. By the mid-1920s, California [[cotton]] farms were around five times larger than farms in the [[Deep South]]. This meant they needed large numbers of manual laborers, as well as technicians, since California farmers adopted [[tractor]]s and [[Cotton picker|picking machines]] at rates faster than any other region.<ref name="A History of California Agriculture">{{cite web |last1=Olmstead |first1=Alan L. |last2=Rhode |first2=Paul W. |title=A History of California Agriculture |url=https://s.giannini.ucop.edu/uploads/giannini_public/19/41/194166a6-cfde-4013-ae55-3e8df86d44d0/a_history_of_california_agriculture.pdf |website=The Giannini Foundation of Agricultural Economics |publisher=University of California |access-date=16 December 2020}}</ref> The ability of Mexican laborers to adapt to [[Intensive farming|industrial farming]] was crucial to the state's success. Nevertheless, even as labor unions in the 1920s grew rapidly to protect workers, some mainstream organizations, such as the [[American Federation of Labor|AFL]], were blatantly anti-Mexican.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Levenstein |first1=Harvey A. |title=The AFL and Mexican Immigration in the 1920s: An Experiment in Labor Diplomacy |journal=The Hispanic American Historical Review |date=May 1968 |volume=48 |issue=2 |pages=206–219 |doi=10.2307/2510743 |jstor=2510743 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/2510743 |access-date=20 December 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref> When Mexicans sought better working conditions, they often faced outright violence.<ref>{{cite web |title=Mexicans in the United States in the 1920s |url=https://sheg.stanford.edu/history-lessons/mexicans-united-states-1920s |website=Stanford History Education Group |publisher=Stanford University |access-date=20 December 2020}}</ref>
[[File:Bodies of Salastino Martinez and Klementz Chavez, Walsenburg, Colorado, 1928.jpg|thumb|left|upright|Salastino Martinez (age 15) and Klementz Chavez killed in [[Walsenburg, Colorado]], 1928]]
Some of the decades' most infamous labor disputes occurred in Colorado. In 1927, Mexican-American coal miners participated in a bloody [[Columbine Mine massacre|coal strike in Colorado]], walking out under the banner of the [[Industrial Workers of the World]]. Mexican-Americans in the southeastern part of the state, particularly from the [[Walsenburg, Colorado|Walsenburg]], [[Pueblo, Colorado|Pueblo]], and [[Trinidad, Colorado|Trinidad]] areas, took leadership roles in the 1927 strike, protesting for better and safer working conditions. Some mine owners in other parts of the state retaliated against the striking miners by refusing to hire any Mexican or Mexican American in their mines.<ref>Phil Goodstein, Slaughter in Serene: the Columbine Coal Strike Reader, 2005, page 110.</ref> In Walsenburg and Trinidad, the mine owners went to more extreme measures to stymie the protests. Mine owners hired armed men to attack the [[Industrial Workers of the World]]'s (IWW) Trinidad hall and Walsenburg branch halls. In Walsenburg, the men used a machine gun to attack the IWW hall, ultimately killing two union strikers, Salastino Martinez (age 15) and Klementz Chavez (age 41), on January 12, 1928.<ref>{{cite web |title=Industrial Workers of the World Photograph Collection: Colorado Mine Strike, 1927-1928 |url=https://guides.lib.uw.edu/c.php?g=341845&p=2299872 |website=University of Washington Libraries |publisher=University of Washington |access-date=16 December 2020}}</ref> [[Josephine Roche]], president of the [[Rocky Mountain Fuel Company]], invited the [[United Mine Workers|United Mine Workers of America]] to unionize her mines, so as to meet some of the strikers' demands without alienating other mine owners, who remained strongly opposed to the IWW.<ref>{{cite web |title=Josephine Roche |url=https://archives.colorado.edu/repositories/2/resources/1196 |website=University of Colorado Libraries |publisher=University of Colorado Boulder |access-date=17 December 2020}}</ref>
===LULAC and activism=== [[File:First LULAC Convention.jpg|thumb|right|upright|The first LULAC Convention, Texas, 1929]]
In 1929, the [[League of United Latin American Citizens]] (LULAC) was formed in [[Corpus Christi, Texas]] through the merging of several smaller Mexican American organizations.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Konicek |first1=Malcolm |title=The Origins and Contributions of LULAC |url=https://library.tamucc.edu/exhibits/s/sts/page/lulac |website=South Texas Stories |publisher=Texas A&M University Corpus Christi |access-date=22 December 2020}}</ref> It was one of the nation's first mainstream Mexican American political organizations and was formed largely through the efforts of Mexican American [[World War I]] veterans who were frustrated at the continued discrimination Mexican Americans faced in the United States.<ref name="LULAC">{{cite book |last1=Márquez |first1=Benjamin |title=LULAC: The Evolution of a Mexican American Political Organization |date=April 1, 1993 |publisher=University of Texas Press |location=Austin |isbn=978-0292751545 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=K_W4CwAAQBAJ |access-date=18 December 2020}}</ref> Ben Garza served as the organizations' first president.<ref name="LULAC History">{{cite web |title=LULAC History - All for One and One for All |url=https://lulac.org/about/history/ |publisher=League of United Latin American Citizens |access-date=18 December 2020}}</ref> LULAC quickly developed into an influential middle-class civil rights organization with councils across the [[Southwestern United States|Southwest]]. Organization members tended to portray themselves as patriotic "white" Americans, and membership was restricted only to English-speaking U.S. citizens.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Marquez |first1=Benjamin |title=The Politics of Race and Assimilation: The League of United Latin American Citizens 1929-40 |journal=The Western Political Quarterly |date=Summer 1989 |volume=42 |issue=2 |pages=355–375 |doi=10.2307/448359 |jstor=448359 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/448359 |access-date=22 December 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Like the [[NAACP]] at this time, LULAC believed an "educated elite" Mexican American leadership would guide the community as a whole toward a higher political and economic standing in the United States. Nevertheless, the organization focused mostly on issues such [[voter registration]] and [[poll tax]] fundraising-drives, aggressively waging legal campaigns against racially discriminatory laws and practices.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Gutiérrez |first1=David Gregory |title=Walls and Mirrors: Mexican Americans, Mexican Immigrants, and the Politics of Ethnicity |date=March 27, 1995 |publisher=University of California Press |location=Berkeley |isbn=978-0520202191 |page=78 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HAd4W9OdLX0C&dq=From+1929+through+World+War+II,+LULAC+organized+successful+voter+registration+and+poll+tax-drives...+and+aggressively+attacked+discrimination+laws+and+practices&pg=PA78 |access-date=22 December 2020}}</ref>
==Great Depression== ===Poverty=== [[File:Mexican family. San Antonio, Texas.jpg|thumb|left|upright|A Mexican family in Texas. The [[Great Depression]] hit communities of color hard.]]
The [[Great Depression]] started in the United States after a major fall in stock prices that began around September 4, 1929, and especially with the stock market crash of October 29, 1929, (known as [[Wall Street Crash of 1929|Black Tuesday]]). The Depression had a major impact for the approximately one and a half million Mexicans and Mexican Americans living in the United States by 1930.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Haverluk |first1=Terrence |title=The Changing Geography of U.S. Hispanics, 1850-1990 |journal=Journal of Geography |date=1997 |volume=96 |issue=3 |page=138 |doi=10.1080/00221349708978775 |pmid=12178550 |bibcode=1997JGeog..96..134H |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/pdf/10.1080/00221349708978775 |access-date=26 December 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Agricultural work fell as one of the depression's first casualties.<ref name="The Future Immense"/> As white Americans increasingly found themselves unemployed, they grew outraged at the fact that farmers in the Southwest employed Mexican and Mexican American laborers. They mounted pressure campaigns on government officials and employers to insist that only "citizens" be hired.<ref name="The Future Immense"/> The campaigns were successful, and soon construction companies, stores, factories, and laundries fired their Mexican employees in favor of white Americans.<ref name="The Future Immense"/> The [[Presidency of Herbert Hoover|Hoover administration]] explicitly blamed Mexicans for taking jobs away from "American citizens".<ref>{{cite book |last1=Gerber |first1=David |last2=Kraut |first2=Allen |title=American Immigration and Ethnicity |date=May 19, 2009 |publisher=Palgrave Macmillan |location=Camden |isbn=978-0312293505 |edition=Revised |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=-20YDAAAQBAJ |access-date=25 December 2020}}</ref>
[[File:Mexican boy sitting on door step, San Antonio, Texas.jpg|thumb|right|upright|Mexican American boy in [[San Antonio|San Antonio, Texas]]]]
When [[Franklin D. Roosevelt|Franklin Delano Roosevelt]] was elected president in 1932, there was hope he would provide relief to the suffering Mexican American communities across the United States. This did not materialize. While no [[New Deal]] program explicitly barred people by race or immigration status from receiving assistance, occupational status was used to exclude Mexican Americans from receiving Depression relief.<ref name="Mexican Communities in the Great Depression">{{cite web |last1=Davis |first1=Tadeo |title=Mexican Communities in the Great Depression |url=https://ssa.uchicago.edu/mexican-communities-great-depression |website=School of Social Service Administration |date=June 2017 |publisher=The University of Chicago |access-date=25 December 2020}}</ref> The [[Social Security Act|Social Security Act of 1935]], for example, barred agricultural and domestic workers from both social security benefits and unemployment insurance, which effectively excluded many African and Mexican Americans from this early social safety net.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Fox |first1=C. |title=Unauthorized Welfare: The Origins of Immigrant Status Restrictions in American Social Policy |journal=Journal of American History |date=2016 |volume=102 |issue=4 |pages=1051–1074 |doi=10.1093/jahist/jav758 |url=https://doi.org/10.1093/jahist/jav758 |access-date=25 December 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref> At the local level, few Mexican migrant laborers received relief, since residency restrictions often required the applicant to have lived in the county for a set amount of time, which excluded migratory laborers.<ref name="Mexican Communities in the Great Depression"/>
[[File:Two young Mexican boy scavengers at early morning market. San Antonio, Texas.jpg|thumb|left|upright|Two boys scavenging for food during the Great Depression in [[Texas]]]]
Without federal or local relief, many jobless Mexican American families adopted an itinerant life, traveling highways in search of work.<ref name="Depression and the Struggle for Survival">{{cite web |title=Depression and the Struggle for Survival |url=https://www.loc.gov/classroom-materials/immigration/mexican/depression-and-the-struggle-for-survival/ |website=Immigration and Relocation in U.S. History |publisher=United States Library of Congress |access-date=24 December 2020}}</ref> Some found temporary housing in [[Farm Security Administration|U.S. Farm Security Administration]] (FSA) work camps, where Mexican American farm families finally received medicine, food, and housing. Because the widespread culture of anti-Mexican demonization in the U.S. had resulted in targeted violence against Mexican American communities, however, the FSA was forced to set up separate camps specifically for Mexican Americans in order "to create safe havens from violent attacks" from white Americans.<ref name="Depression and the Struggle for Survival"/> These segregated camps brought together Mexican American families from various communities, which provided them with the opportunity to organize and discuss many of the main issues of the day, including the harsh working conditions within the agricultural sector.<ref name="Depression and the Struggle for Survival"/> The familial connections developed in these camps would serve as a strong factor in farm labor movements later in the century.<ref name="Depression and the Struggle for Survival"/> Yet, while the FSA work camps did provide relief for some Mexican American families, many others had a very different experience in the Depression. In many cities, when a Mexican or Mexican American family applied for aid, they were sent to designated "Mexican Bureaus", where [[repatriation]] was discussed.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Humphrey |first1=N. |title=Mexican repatriation from Michigan public assistance in historical perspective |journal=Social Service Review |year=1941 |volume=15 |issue=3 |pages=497–513|doi=10.1086/633598 |s2cid=143781039 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hoffman |first1=Abraham |title=Stimulus to Repatriation: The 1931 Federal Deportation Drive and the Los Angeles Mexican Community |journal=Pacific Historical Review |date=Spring 1973 |volume=42 |issue=2 |pages=205–219 |doi=10.2307/3638467 |jstor=3638467 |url=https://doi.org/10.2307/3638467 |access-date=25 December 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref>
===Repatriation=== {{Main article|Mexican Repatriation}}
{{multiple image | direction = vertical | width = 165 | footer = From 1929 to 1936, around 400,000 Mexicans and Mexican Americans repatriated via trains, boats, and buses, some forcibly. | image1 = Hundreds of Mexicans at a Los Angeles train station awaiting deportation.jpg | alt1 = | caption1 = | image2 = El Argil. Transport during Repatriation.jpg | alt2 = | caption2 = | image3 = Mexicans returning home by train.jpg | alt3 = }}
For several hundred thousand Mexicans and Mexican Americans, their lives in the United States during the Great Depression were unbearable – they lost their jobs, were largely denied federal or local relief because of their ethnicity, and faced vilification in politics and in the media as "stealing jobs from real Americans".<ref>{{cite web |last1=Howard |first1=Lisa |title=Research Shows Deporting Immigrants Hurts Local Economies |url=https://research.ucdavis.edu/migration-research-cluster/ |website=Office of Research |date=13 June 2018 |publisher=University of California, Davis |access-date=26 December 2020}}</ref> In addition to these factors, state and county officials across the U.S. began to threaten Mexican and Mexican American families who were seeking government aid with deportation.<ref>{{cite web |title=Repatriation During the Great Depression |url=https://www.digitalhistory.uh.edu/disp_textbook.cfm?smtid=3&psid=3699 |website=Digital History |publisher=University of Houston |access-date=26 December 2020}}</ref> At the same time, the Mexican government introduced a program to entice ethnic Mexicans back to the country with the promise of free land if they returned.<ref name="Unwanted Mexican Americans in the Great Depression"/> All of these factors – overwhelming poverty, the fear of threats from U.S. government officials, and the promises of the Mexican government – led many to leave the U.S. in a period known as the [[Mexican Repatriation]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Lytle Hernandez |first1=Kelly |title=Mexican Repatriation |url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Fw9od3H2dAE |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/varchive/youtube/20211212/Fw9od3H2dAE| archive-date=2021-12-12 |url-status=live|website=UCLA Historiography Project |date=2 July 2019 |publisher=UCLA Center X |access-date=26 December 2020}}{{cbignore}}</ref>
These repatriations, though often initiated under threats of deportation, were considered "voluntary", and thus few federal records exist to provide numbers of how many Mexicans left the country during the Depression.<ref name="INS Records for 1930s Mexican Repatriations">{{cite web |title=INS Records for 1930s Mexican Repatriations |url=https://www.uscis.gov/about-us/our-history/history-office-and-library/featured-stories-from-the-uscis-history-office-and-library/ins-records-for-1930s-mexican-repatriations |website=U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Service |date=29 July 2020 |publisher=U.S. Department of Homeland Security |access-date=26 December 2020}}</ref> Nevertheless, [[Immigration and Naturalization Service|INS]] reported in 1931 that "large proportions" of the nation's Mexican population were leaving the country, and some estimates have concluded that between just November 1929 and December 1931, about 200,000 Mexicans left the United States.<ref name="INS Records for 1930s Mexican Repatriations"/> While a large number willingly departed to Mexico, a significant number were explicitly pressured to leave through state and local repatriation programs.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Alanís Enciso |first1=Fernando Saúl |title=They Should Stay There: The Story of Mexican Migration and Repatriation during the Great Depression |date=September 11, 2017 |publisher=University of North Carolina Press |location=Chapel Hill |isbn=978-1469634265 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2EwDwAAQBAJ |access-date=26 December 2020}}</ref> These programs responded to the Depression's severe effects in Mexican and Mexican American communities by promoting deportation.<ref name="Decade of Betrayal">{{cite book |last1=Balderrama |first1=Francisco E. |last2=Rodriguez |first2=Raymond |title=Decade of Betrayal: Mexican Repatriation in the 1930s |date=May 31, 2006 |publisher=University of New Mexico Press |location=Santa Fe |isbn=978-0826339737 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1A6iBy_0qacC |access-date=26 December 2020}}</ref>
Some scholars contend that the unprecedented number of deportations and repatriations between 1929 and 1933 was part of an "explicit Hoover administration policy",<ref name="Immigration, Repatriation, and Deportation"/> and the manufactured climate of fear was meant to coerce Mexicans into self-repatriating.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Gross |first1=Terry |last2=Balderrama |first2=Francisco |title=America's Forgotten History Of Mexican-American 'Repatriation' |url=https://www.npr.org/2015/09/10/439114563/americas-forgotten-history-of-mexican-american-repatriation |website=Fresh Air |date=10 September 2015 |publisher=NPR |access-date=26 December 2020}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Wagner |first1=Alex |title=America's Forgotten History of Illegal Deportations |date=6 March 2017 |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/politics/archive/2017/03/americas-brutal-forgotten-history-of-illegal-deportations/517971/ |magazine=[[The Atlantic]] |access-date=26 December 2020}}</ref> In fact, local welfare workers did regularly collaborate with immigration officials to provide the names of ethnic Mexicans seeking Depression relief so they might be repatriated.<ref name="INS Records for 1930s Mexican Repatriations"/> During the Repatriation period, local government and federal officials also collaborated in "street sweeps" and "full-scale paramilitary" raids in majority-Mexican neighborhoods to find undocumented migrants and to terrorize others in the community to repatriate.<ref name="Decade of Betrayal"/> In addition to welfare officials, charitable aid agencies worked with state and local governments to provide names of Mexican and Mexican American families seeking aid. Charities also sometimes provided money to pay for one-way tickets to Mexico.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Sanchez |first1=George J. |title=Becoming Mexican American |date=January 10, 1995 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=New York |isbn=9780199762231 |page=212 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HoJa0v7T0REC&dq=although+most+of+the+early+repatriates+came+from+Texas+border+towns,+during+the+winter+of+1929–30+a+sizeable+group+of+Los+Angeles+residents+departed+for+their+homeland&pg=PA212 |access-date=26 December 2020}}</ref> In all, the INS formally deported around 82,000 Mexicans from 1929 to 1935, while the remaining 320,000 repatriated were considered "voluntary". Of the total number of people who left the United States during the Mexican Repatriation, around half were U.S. citizens.<ref name="Immigration, Repatriation, and Deportation">{{cite journal |last1=Gratton |first1=Brian |last2=Merchant |first2=Emily |title=Immigration, Repatriation, and Deportation: The Mexican-Origin Population in the United States, 1920–1950 |journal=The International Migration Review |date=Winter 2013 |volume=47 |issue=4 |pages=944–975 |doi=10.1111/imre.12054 |jstor=24542812 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/24542812 |access-date=26 December 2020|hdl=2027.42/102163 |s2cid=143477568 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>
===New Deal labor=== {{See also|California agricultural strikes of 1933|United Cannery, Agricultural, Packing, and Allied Workers of America}} [[File:Mexican melon picker of the Imperial Valley, unloading his bag. California.jpg|thumb|left|upright|A Mexican melon picker of the [[Imperial Valley]], unloading his bag during the depression]]
During the [[New Deal]] era, Mexican American labor unions made significant strides in organizing; however, they still often faced outright violence. In September 1933, the [[Cannery and Agricultural Workers' Industrial Union]] led a massive strike of cotton pickers in the [[San Joaquin Valley]], California. One contemporary writer later described the mobilization of strikers as "an army of brown skinned people".<ref name="Documentary history of Migratory Farm Labor">{{cite book |last1=Barry |first1=Raymond P. |title=Monographs Prepared for a Documentary History of Migratory Farm Labor, 1938 |date=1938 |publisher=Federal Writers Project |location=Oakland, CA |edition=1 |url=http://content.cdlib.org/view?docId=hb88700929&chunk.id=div00022&br |access-date=25 December 2020}}</ref> In response, the growers initiated an all-out war to avoid paying the strikers higher wages.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Chacon |first1=Ramon |title=The 1933 San Joaquin Valley Cotton Strike: Strikebreaking Activities in California Agriculture |journal=NACCS Annual Conference Proceedings |date=1979 |volume=3 |url=http://scholarworks.sjsu.edu/naccs/1979/1979/3 |access-date=25 December 2020}}</ref> They began with a propaganda campaign, claiming the strike was being agitated by a radical-left "Communist menace",<ref>{{cite web |last1=McDonald |first1=Frank C. |title=Labor Activism |url=https://www.digitalhistory.uh.edu/disp_textbook.cfm?smtID=3&psid=587 |website=Digital History |publisher=University of Houston |access-date=25 December 2020}}</ref> rather than the workers themselves who were fighting against endemic low wages and horrific working conditions.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Rivera |first1=Alicia Judith |title=Solidarity in the San Joaquin Valley, California cotton strike of 1933 |url=https://www.proquest.com/openview/6a021585ff84c25f35788e537fcb6524/1?pq-origsite=gscholar&cbl=18750&diss=y |website=College of Social Sciences |publisher=California State University, Fresno |access-date=25 December 2020}}</ref> Next, the farm owners created armed militias.<ref name="Documentary history of Migratory Farm Labor"/> One, [[Pixley, California|Pixley]]'s Farmers Protection Association, had 600 enrolled members.<ref name="Documentary history of Migratory Farm Labor"/> The farm owners then went after local business owners in town, threatening dire consequences to any business which sold the strikers food. Then, the farmers evicted all striking pickers and their families from their homes, which were usually shacks on the cotton ranches.<ref name="Documentary history of Migratory Farm Labor"/>
When the strikers still refused to break, news began to emerge of mysterious deaths in the cotton fields. The [[List of diplomatic missions of Mexico|Mexican consulate]] sent a representative to [[Tulare County, California|Tulare County]] "to protect the interests of Mexicans".<ref name="Documentary history of Migratory Farm Labor"/> However, this did not stop local welfare officials from denying the strikers' families food relief during the strike.<ref name="Documentary history of Migratory Farm Labor"/> Law enforcement soon also stepped in to preserve "law and order", quickly deputizing an army of white locals and granting them "unlimited power".<ref name="Documentary history of Migratory Farm Labor"/> The Mexican American strikers were then openly attacked, and three were killed – but their killers were soon released with all charges dropped.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Bronfenbrenner |first1=Kate |title=California Farmworkers' Strikes of 1933 |url=https://ecommons.cornell.edu/bitstream/handle/1813/75336/Bronfenbrenner65_California_Farmworker_Strikes_Post_Print.pdf?sequence=1 |website=eCommons |publisher=Cornell University |access-date=25 December 2020}}</ref> Meanwhile, local and federal relief officials continued to deny the strikers any food relief, and soon scores of the strikers' children began to die from malnutrition.<ref name="Documentary history of Migratory Farm Labor"/> The U.S. federal government finally stepped in, establishing an [[arbitral tribunal|arbitration committee]]. By the end of October 1933, a compromise was reached and the strike was finally ended.<ref name="Documentary history of Migratory Farm Labor"/>
[[File:Emmamarriage.jpg|thumb|right|upright|The labor organizer [[Emma Tenayuca]] and her husband Homer Bartchy on their wedding day, January 1937]]
The San Joaquin Cotton Strike of 1933 received national media coverage at the time, much of it in favor of the farm owners. However, Latino American labor activists did make major strides in the 1930s. [[Luisa Moreno]], a [[Guatemala]]n immigrant, became the first Latina in U.S. history to hold a national union office when she became the Vice-President of the [[United Cannery, Agricultural, Packing, and Allied Workers of America]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Ruiz |first1=Vicki |title=Una Mujer Sin Fronteras |journal=Pacific Historical Review |date=February 2004 |volume=73 |issue=1 |pages=1–20 |doi=10.1525/phr.2004.73.1.1 |jstor=10.1525/phr.2004.73.1.1 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/10.1525/phr.2004.73.1.1 |access-date=26 December 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref> At the time, it was the seventh largest affiliate of the [[Congress of Industrial Organizations]].<ref>{{cite book |last1=Ruiz |first1=Vicki |title=Cannery Women, Cannery Lives Mexican Women, Unionization, and the California Food Processing Industry, 1930-1950 |date=August 1987 |publisher=University of New Mexico Press |location=Santa Fe |isbn=9780826324696 |page=XVIII |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PI-dAwAAQBAJ |access-date=26 December 2020}}</ref> Another important labor leader during the Depression was the 21-year old [[Emma Tenayuca]],<ref name="Time">{{Cite magazine|title=These Latinas were pioneers for workers' rights in the United States.|url=https://time.com/5909191/these-latinas-were-pioneers-for-workers-rights-in-the-u-s-here-are-2-you-should-have-learned-about-in-school/|access-date=2020-12-21|magazine=[[Time (magazine)|Time]]}}</ref> who was instrumental in one of the most famous conflicts of Texas labor history–the [[1938 San Antonio pecan shellers strike]] at the Southern Pecan Shelling Company.<ref>{{cite web|title=Emma Tenayuca Biography|url=http://americanswhotellthetruth.org/pgs/portraits/Emma_Tenayuca.php|publisher=Americans Who Tell the Truth|access-date=June 16, 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120101224726/http://www.americanswhotellthetruth.org/pgs/portraits/Emma_Tenayuca.php|archive-date=January 1, 2012|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title = Pecan-Shellers' Strike|url = https://tshaonline.org/handbook/online/articles/oep01|website = Texas Handbook Online|access-date = 2015-12-17|first = Richard|last = Croxdale|publisher = Texas State Historical Association|date = 15 June 2010}}</ref> During the strike, nearly 12,000 workers at over 130 plants protested a wage reduction of one cent per pound of shelled pecans and inhumane working conditions by walking off the job.<ref name="Time"/> Mexicana and [[Chicano|Chicana]] workers who picketed were clubbed, gassed, arrested, and jailed. A photo of Tenayuca ran in ''[[Time (magazine)|Time]]'' magazine where she was called "the forefront of most of its civil commotions".<ref>{{Cite magazine|title=National Affairs: Labor |date=February 28, 1938|url=https://time.com/vault/issue/1938-02-28/page/19/|access-date=2020-12-21|magazine=Time}}</ref>
===Depression politics=== [[File:Dennis Chavez cph.3b20083.jpg|thumb|left|upright|U.S. Senator [[Dennis Chávez|Dennis Chavez]], the first Latino to serve a full senate term]]
Despite the intense anti-Mexican sentiment pervading the country in the Depression, the era also saw the first Mexican American senators in the history of the country. Sen. [[Octaviano Ambrosio Larrazolo|Octaviano Larrazolo]] was elected to the U.S. senate in 1928, but he died in office three months later.<ref>{{cite web |title=Octaviano Larrazolo: A Featured Biography |url=https://www.senate.gov/senators/FeaturedBios/Featured_Bio_Larrazolo.htm |website=United States Senate |access-date=6 January 2021}}</ref> Sen. [[Dennis Chávez]] first served in the [[United States House of Representatives]] from 1931 to 1935, until he was appointed to a full term in the U.S. Senate in 1934. When Chávez was sworn in, six senators allegedly stood abruptly, turned their backs to Chávez, and angrily left the chamber.<ref name="Dennis Chávez & The Hispanic New Deal">{{cite web |last1=Morgan |first1=Brandon |title=Dennis Chávez & The Hispanic New Deal |url=https://mytext.cnm.edu/lesson/dennis-chavez-the-hispanic-new-deal/ |website=The History of New Mexico |publisher=Central New Mexico Community College |access-date=26 December 2020}}</ref> During his time in office, Senator Chávez was a major proponent of the [[New Deal]], and he was at least partially successful at securing benefits for New Mexicans. What became known in New Mexico as the "Latino New Deal" was a rare extension of New Deal benefits to Mexican Americans.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Forrest |first1=Suzanne |title=The Preservation of the Village: New Mexico's Hispanics and the New Deal |date=1998 |publisher=University of New Mexico |location=Santa Fe |isbn=9780826319739 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=EPiAxSZbbS4C |access-date=31 December 2020}}</ref> In the state, newly funded education programs improved literacy rates, and vocational programs revived the production of Hispano craft goods like santero artwork, woven goods, and furniture.<ref name="Dennis Chávez & The Hispanic New Deal"/> [[Works Progress Administration|WPA]] agents taught New Mexicans how to market their items to tourists.<ref name="Dennis Chávez & The Hispanic New Deal"/> Later, Senator Chávez became known for his civil rights advocacy, as he fought to expand notions of American citizenship.<ref>{{cite web |title=Dennis Chavez: A Featured Biography |url=https://www.senate.gov/senators/FeaturedBios/Featured_Bio_Chavez.htm |publisher=United States Senate |access-date=26 December 2020}}</ref>
In 1935, a [[United States federal judge|federal judge]] in [[New York (state)|New York]] upheld an immigration officer's decision to deny the [[naturalization]] petitions of three Mexicans on the grounds that they were not white, but instead individuals "of Indian and Spanish blood".<ref>{{cite book |last1=Lukens |first1=Patrick D. |title=A Quiet Victory for Latino Rights: FDR and the Controversy Over "Whiteness" |date=January 1, 2012 |publisher=University of Arizona Press |location=Tucson |isbn=978-0816529025 |page=121 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VM0GskdhifoC |access-date=26 December 2020}}</ref> Whiteness, which had been a requirement for naturalized citizenship since 1790, remained so until 1940.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Coulson |first1=Doug|title=British Imperialism, the Indian Independence Movement, and the Racial Eligibility Provisions of the Naturalization Act: United States v. Thind Revisited |journal=Georgetown Journal of Law & Modern Critical Race Perspectives |date=2015|issue=7|page=2|ssrn=2610266}}</ref> If the 1935 ruling had been upheld, it would have rendered the majority of Mexicans ineligible for citizenship.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Molina |first1=Natalia |title='In a Race All Their Own': The Quest to Make Mexicans Ineligible for U.S. Citizenship |journal=Pacific Historical Review |date=May 2010 |volume=79 |issue=2 |pages=167–201 |doi=10.1525/phr.2010.79.2.167 |jstor=10.1525/phr.2010.79.2.167 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/10.1525/phr.2010.79.2.167 |access-date=26 December 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref> President Roosevelt – who had only recently replaced American interventionist policies, such as the [[Roosevelt Corollary]], with his own diplomatic approach of the [[Good Neighbor policy|Good Neighbor Policy]] - was concerned that denying Mexicans the opportunity to naturalize would hurt Mexico-U.S. relations.<ref>{{cite web |title=Good Neighbor Policy, 1933 |url=https://history.state.gov/milestones/1921-1936/good-neighbor |website=Office of the Historian |publisher=U.S. Department of State |access-date=26 December 2020}}</ref><ref name="White by Law"/> He thus urged the [[United States Department of State|State Department]] to "quiet the controversy", pressuring the judge to reverse the decision.<ref name="White by Law">{{cite web |last1=Fox |first1=Cybelle |last2=Bloemraad |first2=Irene |title=Beyond 'White by Law': Explaining the Gulf in Citizenship Acquisition between Mexican and European Immigrants, 1930 |url=https://sociology.berkeley.edu/sites/default/files/faculty/bloemraad/Fox_Bloemraad_White_by_Law_Natz_Social_Forces_2015.pdf |website=Beyond 'White by Law' |publisher=University of California, Berkeley |access-date=26 December 2020}}</ref> The [[United States Department of Labor|Labor Department]] issued guidance to its border officials that "in all future cases, [Mexican] immigrants be classified as 'white.{{'"}}<ref>{{cite book |last1=Fox |first1=Cybelle |title=Three Worlds of Relief: Race, Immigration, and the American Welfare State from the Progressive Era to the New Deal |date=April 29, 2012 |publisher=Princeton University Press |location=Princeton, NJ |isbn=9781400842582 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=2eMW3clGFHgC |access-date=26 December 2020}}</ref>
[[File:Josefina Fierro de Bright El Congreso.jpg|thumb|right|upright|[[Josefina Fierro de Bright|Josefina Fierro]], a founder of [[El Congreso de Pueblos de Habla Española|El Congreso]] in 1938]]
In April 1938, [[Luisa Moreno]] and group of Mexican American labor activists, including [[Josefina Fierro de Bright|Josefina Fierro]], Eduardo Quevedo, and [[Bert Corona]], organized the inaurural conference of [[El Congreso de Pueblos de Habla Española|El Congreso de Pueblos de Hablan Española]] in Los Angeles, an organization meant to promote a broad agenda of working class empowerment, civil rights, and Latino unity.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Oropeza |first1=Lorena |title=Raza Si, Guerra No: Chicano Protest and Patriotism During the Viet Nam War Era |date=April 25, 2005 |publisher=University of California Press |location=Berkeley |isbn=9780520937994 |page=22 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4ceWBb8lSYEC&dq=el+congreso+de+pueblos+de+habla+española+%22Latino+unity%22&pg=PA22 |access-date=26 December 2020}}</ref> In their founding constitution, the organizers also endorsed the rights of Mexicans to live and work in the U.S. without having to fear coerced deportation.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Larralde |first1=Carlos M. |title=El Congreso in San Diego: An Endeavor for Civil Rights |url=https://sandiegohistory.org/journal/v50-1/el_congreso.pdf |website=San Diego History |publisher=San Diego History Center |access-date=26 December 2020}}</ref> El Congreso also never promoted [[Cultural assimilation|assimilation]],<ref>{{cite book |last1=Welky |first1=David |title=America Between the Wars, 1919-1941 |date=January 24, 2012 |publisher=Wiley |location=Hoboken, NJ |isbn=9781444338966 |page=169 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=22G7x_UN5G0C&dq=el+congreso+de+pueblos+de+habla+española+%22assimilation%22&pg=PA169 |access-date=26 December 2020}}</ref> in direct contradiction to other organizations like LULAC (which focused on the [[Desegregation in the United States|desegregation]] of employment, housing, education, and all public facilities).<ref>{{cite book |last1=Danver |first1=Steven L. |title=Encyclopedia of Politics of the American West |date=April 25, 2013 |publisher=Sage Publications |location=Newbury Park |isbn=9781452276069 |page=239 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=UCRzAwAAQBAJ&dq=el+congreso+de+pueblos+de+habla+española+%22desegregation%22&pg=PA239 |access-date=26 December 2020}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Sánchez Korrol |first1=Virginia |last2=Ruiz |first2=Vicki |title=Latinas in the United States |date=May 3, 2006 |publisher=Indiana University Press |location=Bloomington |isbn=9780253111692 |page=5 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_62IjQ-XQScC&dq=el+congreso+de+pueblos+de+habla+española+%22desegregation%22&pg=PA5 |access-date=26 December 2020}}</ref> El Congreso was notable for their early stands promoting immigrant rights despite the widespread culture of demonization occurring during the Mexican Repatriation. In fact, Moreno spoke to the [[American Committee for the Protection of Foreign Born|American Committee for the Protection of the Foreign Born]], critiquing the exploitation of Mexican workers, saying, "[Mexicans make] a barren land fertile for new crops and greater riches. These people are not aliens, they have contributed their endurance, sacrifices, youth, and labor to the Southwest."<ref name="Nuestra América"/>
==World War II era== ===World War II=== {{See also|Hispanic Americans in World War II}} [[File:Mexican American servicemen in WWII.png|thumb|left|Mexican American servicemen in [[World War II]], taken between 1941 and 1944]]
The United States entered [[World War II]] against the [[Axis powers|Axis Powers]] on December 7, 1941 after the [[attack on Pearl Harbor]]. Several hundred thousand Latino men served in the U.S. military during the war, about 500,000 of whom were Mexican American.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Mendoza |first1=Alex |title='I Know No Other Country': Tejanos and the American Wars of the Twentieth Century, 1917-1972 |journal=Military History of the West |date=2011 |volume=41 |issue=1 |pages=31–59 |url=https://texashistory.unt.edu/ark:/67531/metapth638973/m2/1/high_res_d/UNT-0023-0009.pdf |access-date=28 December 2020}}</ref><ref name="Latinos in World War II: Fighting on Two Fronts">{{cite web |last1=Oropeza |first1=Lorena |title=Latinos in World War II: Fighting on Two Fronts |url=https://www.nps.gov/articles/latinoww2.htm |website=National Park Service |publisher=U.S. Department of the Interior |access-date=28 December 2020}}</ref> Unlike their African American counterparts, who mostly served in [[Racial segregation in the United States Armed Forces|segregated units]], most Mexican American soldiers served in integrated units in World War II,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Arredondo |first1=Niko |title=The Forgotten Soldiers: Mexican-American Soldiers of WWII and the Creation of the G.I. Forum |url=https://escholarship.org/uc/item/44t510pc |journal=The Undergraduate Historical Journal at UC Merced |year=2014 |publisher=University of California |doi=10.5070/H311022630 |access-date=28 December 2020|doi-access=free }}</ref> though recent research has discovered at least one unit composed entirely of Mexican Americans.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Gutierrez |first1=Dave |title=Patriots from the Barrio: The Story of Company E, 141st Infantry: The Only All Mexican American Army Unit in World War II |date=April 30, 2019 |publisher=Westholme Publishing |location=Yardley, PA |isbn=978-1594163289 |edition=1}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Hispanics in the U.S. Army |url=https://www.army.mil/hispanics/history.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150918191912/http://www.army.mil/hispanics/history.html |url-status=dead |archive-date=September 18, 2015 |website=United States Army |publisher=United States Armed Forces |access-date=28 December 2020}}</ref> The majority of World War II Mexican American service members were [[Second-generation immigrants in the United States|second-generation]] Americans who had grown up in the anti-Mexican hysteria of the Great Depression. Thus, the transition for them into the role of an "American soldier" could at times be surprising. One man, Private Armando Flores of [[Corpus Christi, Texas]], remembered the shock he felt the first time he was ever referred to as an "American soldier", because, as he recalled later, "Nobody had ever called me an American before!"<ref name="Latinos in World War II: Fighting on Two Fronts"/> According to some scholars, the U.S. government made efforts during the war to address some of the domestic issues facing the Mexican American community.<ref name="World War II and Mexican American Civil Rights"/> These efforts were part of an overarching campaign to win broad domestic support for the war effort.<ref name="World War II and Mexican American Civil Rights">{{cite book |last1=Griswold del Castillo |first1=Richard |title=World War II and Mexican American Civil Rights |date=April 1, 2008 |publisher=University of Texas Press |location=College Station |isbn=978-0292717398 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KynmDAAAQBAJ |access-date=28 December 2020}}</ref> Perhaps not surprisingly, this new feeling of wartime social "inclusion" created a strong sense of patriotic pride within the Mexican American community.<ref name="Latinos in World War II: Fighting on Two Fronts"/> On a single two-block stretch of [[Silvis, Illinois]], 45 Mexican American boys and men [[Military volunteer|volunteered]] to fight; it was dubbed in the press as "Hero Street".<ref>{{cite web |last1=Simpson |first1=Rita |title=Hero Street U.S.A. |url=http://migration.lib.uiowa.edu/exhibits/show/war/hero-street-u-s-a- |website=Iowa Women's Archives |publisher=The University of Iowa |access-date=28 December 2020}}</ref>
[[File:Ernest Gallego with first cousin Alex Mahlovich.png|thumb|right|upright|Ernest Gallego with first cousin, both serving in [[World War II]]]]
During the war, Mexican American soldiers gained renown for their bravery. At least eleven Mexican Americans received the [[Medal of Honor]] during the war.<ref name="Latinos in World War II: Fighting on Two Fronts"/> One, [[Joe P. Martínez]], who was a [[Beetroot|beet]] harvester before the war, led a strategically critical charge up a snow-covered mountain on [[Attu Island]].<ref>{{cite book |last1=Cloe |first1=John Haile |title=Attu Island: The Forgotten Battle |date=2017 |publisher=National Park Service |location=Washington D.C. |isbn=9780996583732 |edition=1 |url=https://www.nps.gov/aleu/planyourvisit/upload/Attu-Forgotten-Battle-Optimized-508.pdf |access-date=29 December 2020}}</ref> He died during the action, and became the first draftee to win the Medal of Honor posthumously.<ref>{{cite news |title=Private Is Honored For Leading in Attu; Colorado Farm Worker Gets Congressional Medal Posthumously |work=The New York Times |url=https://www.nytimes.com/1943/11/12/archives/private-is-honored-for-leading-in-attu-colorado-farm-worker-gets.html |access-date=29 December 2020 |agency=The New York Times |date=November 12, 1943}}</ref> Another, [[Silvestre S. Herrera|Silvestre Herrera]], explained his single-handed attack on a Nazi hold, saying, "I am a Mexican-American and we have a tradition. We're supposed to be men."<ref name="Latinos in World War II: Fighting on Two Fronts"/>
Perhaps the most famous Mexican American servicemen was [[Guy Gabaldon]], an 18-year old from [[East Los Angeles, California|East Los Angeles]], who had been adopted by a Japanese American family at the age of twelve. When his family was sent to an [[Internment of Japanese Americans|internment camp]], Gabaldon joined the [[United States Marine Corps|Marines]].<ref>{{cite book |last1=Gabaldon |first1=Guy |title=Saipan: Suicide Island |date=January 1, 1990 |publisher=Guy Gabaldon |isbn=978-0972893114 |edition=1}}</ref> He was sent to the [[Pacific War|Pacific theater]] and saw action in [[Saipan]]. In the [[Battle of Saipan]], Gabaldon killed thirty-three enemy combatants, and then, using his ability to speak conversant Japanese, Gabaldon convinced the surrounded remaining soldiers to surrender.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Stewart |first1=Jocelyn Y. |title=Guy Gabaldon, 80; WWII Hero Captured 1,000 Japanese on Saipan |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2006-sep-06-me-gabaldon6-story.html |website=[[Los Angeles Times]] |date=6 September 2006 |access-date=29 December 2020}}</ref> The eight hundred Japanese soldiers surrendered peacefully,<ref>{{cite web |title=Latino and Native Americans |url=https://www.pbs.org/thewar/at_war_democracy_latino.htm |website=Fighting for Democracy |publisher=PBS |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201127015526/https://www.pbs.org/thewar/at_war_democracy_latino.htm |access-date=28 December 2020|archive-date=2020-11-27 }}</ref> and Gabaldon earned the nickname the "Pied Piper of Saipan".<ref name="World War II Mexican Americans in World War II">{{cite web |title=World War II Mexican Americans in World War II |url=https://research.pomona.edu/zootsuit/en/zoot-suit-la/world-war-ii/ |website=Zoot Suit Discovery Guide |publisher=Pomona College |access-date=28 December 2020}}</ref> Though he was recommended for the Medal of Honor, Gabaldon was instead awarded the [[Silver Star]].<ref>{{cite news |last1=Goldstein |first1=Richard |title=Guy Gabaldon, 80, Hero of Battle of Saipan, Dies |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2006/09/04/us/04gabaldon.html?auth=login-email&login=email |website=The New York Times |date=4 September 2006 |access-date=29 December 2020}}</ref> The 1960 film ''[[Hell to Eternity]]'' was based on Gabaldon and his life.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Joshi |first1=Ruchika |title=Guy Gabaldon |url=https://voces.lib.utexas.edu/collections/stories/guy-gabaldon |website=Voces Oral History Center |date=22 March 1926 |publisher=University of Texas at Austin |access-date=29 December 2020}}</ref>
===Women's wartime labor=== [[File:Rita Rodriguey, (LOC).jpg|thumb|left|upright|Rita Rodriguez, a woman from [[Fort Worth, Texas|Ft. Worth]], hard at work]]
Women played a hugely important role during World War II, entering the industrial workforce in record numbers to fill crucial manufacturing positions left empty by the departing soldiers.<ref>{{cite web |title=Women in the Work Force during World War II |date=15 August 2016 |url=https://www.archives.gov/education/lessons/wwii-women.html |publisher=U.S. National Archives |access-date=28 December 2020}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Women & World War II |url=https://www.msudenver.edu/camphale/thewomensarmycorps/womenwwii/ |website=Camp Hale |publisher=Metropolitan State University Denver |access-date=28 December 2020}}</ref> Additionally, countless Mexican American women joined [[Women's Army Corps]], [[WAVES]], and other all-female auxiliary units in the military.<ref name="World War II Mexican Americans in World War II"/> It is estimated thousands of Mexican Americans found jobs in the defense industries during the war, though they still often encountered anti-Mexican prejudice on the job market, despite [[Franklin D. Roosevelt|Franklin Roosevelt]]'s 1941 [[Executive Order 8802]], which barred discrimination in defense industry hiring.<ref name="Latinos in World War II: Fighting on Two Fronts"/><ref>{{cite web |title=Executive Order 8802: Prohibition of Discrimination in the Defense Industry |url=https://www.archives.gov/historical-docs/todays-doc/?dod-date=625 |website=U.S. National Archives | date=15 August 2016 |access-date=31 December 2020}}</ref> Nevertheless, the war's insatiable demand for labor ultimately overcame employers' reluctance to hire Mexican Americans. Soon, thousands of Mexican American women across the country had joined the workforce as a "Rosita the Riveter".<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Santillán |first1=Richard |title=Rosita the Riveter: Midwest Mexican American Women During World War II, 1941-1945 |journal=Perspectives in Mexican American Studies |date=1989 |volume=2 |hdl=10150/623019 |url=http://hdl.handle.net/10150/623019 |access-date=28 December 2020}}</ref>
[[File:Mexican American women at Friedrich Refrigeration.png|thumb|right|upright|Mexican American women at [[Friedrich Air Conditioning|Friedrich Refrigeration]]]]
In addition to efforts on the formal job market, Mexican American women made significant material and moral contributions through the formation of wartime community organizations. These organizations aimed to support American troops abroad, but specifically the young Mexican-American soldiers from local barrios.<ref name="Latinx Women on the Home Front"/> A few community projects consisted of cooperation between Mexican American and Anglo neighborhoods, yet the vast majority of Mexican American [[United States home front during World War II|home front]] activities were organized separately from those of the white community.<ref name="Mexican Americans on the Home Front">{{cite journal |last1=Marín |first1=Christine |title=Mexican Americans on the Home Front: Community Organizations in Arizona During World War II |journal=Perspectives in Mexican American Studies |date=Spring 1987 |volume=4 |pages=75–92 |url=https://files.eric.ed.gov/fulltext/ED315252.pdf |access-date=28 December 2020}}</ref> One of these organizations, the Spanish-American Mothers and Wives Association of [[Tucson, Arizona]], sought to roll bandages, raise money for a veterans center for after the war's end, and write letters to help the boys fight their "internal battle of loneliness".<ref name="Latinx Women on the Home Front">{{cite web |last1=Ramirez |first1=Bridgette C. |title=Latinx Women on the Home Front |url=https://library.tamucc.edu/exhibits/s/hist4350/page/latinx-women-on-the-home-front#_ftn1 |website=Narratives of World War II in the Pacific |publisher=Texas A&M University-Corpus Christi |access-date=28 December 2020}}</ref> The organization peaked at 300 members during the war.<ref> * Julie A. Campbell, "Madres Y Esposas: Tucson's Spanish-American Mothers and Wives Association", ''Journal of Arizona History'' 1990 31(2): 161-182 * {{cite journal|jstor=970404|title=Regionalism, Politics, and Gender in Southwest History: The League of United Latin American Citizens' Expansion into New Mexico from Texas, 1929-1945|first=Cynthia E.|last=Orozco|date=16 January 1998|journal=The Western Historical Quarterly|volume=29|issue=4|pages=459–483|doi=10.2307/970404}}</ref> Other organizations, such as [[Phoenix, Arizona|Phoenix]]' Lenadores del Mundo, organized wartime festivals and collected rubber for the war effort, but also sought to fight against the still rampant racism and discrimination experienced by the community.<ref name="Mexican Americans on the Home Front"/>
===Sleepy Lagoon murder=== {{Main article|Sleepy Lagoon murder}} [[File:Mexican American male gang.jpg|thumb|left|upright|Mexican American "gang" brought in for questioning regarding the murder]]
In late-1942, California Governor [[Culbert Olson]], who was facing a tough [[1942 California gubernatorial election|re-election battle]] against future incumbent [[Earl Warren]], sent a memo to [[Los Angeles County, California|Los Angeles County]]'s law enforcement agencies, ordering them to launch a vicious campaign against the city's youth gangs.<ref name="Murder at the Sleepy Lagoon">{{cite book |last1=Obregón Pagán |first1=Eduardo |title=Murder at the Sleepy Lagoon: Zoot Suits, Race, and Riot in Wartime L.A. |date=July 21, 2004 |publisher=University of North Carolina Press |location=Chapel Hill |isbn=9780807862094 |page=3 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3W-rk4EaeYEC&dq=In+the+months+that+followed,+Californians+would+again+revive+the+discourse+on+"the+Mexican+problem"+and+debate+whether+Mexican+citizens+and+their+American-born+children+were+culturally,+politically,+intellectually,+and+biologically+capable+of+living+within+a+white,+civilized,+democratic+society.&pg=PA3 |access-date=28 December 2020}}</ref> Under these orders, the office of the [[Los Angeles County District Attorney]] decided to use the August 2, 1942 death of José Gallardo Díaz, a Mexican American youth, as a test case to launch the new war against [[juvenile delinquency]] by turning the investigation into a major media event.<ref name="Murder at the Sleepy Lagoon"/> In the weeks following Díaz' death, the [[Los Angeles Police Department|LAPD]] launched mass raids in Mexican and African American neighborhoods, arresting more than 600 young men and women to be held in custody and interrogated.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Weitz |first1=Mark A. |title=The Sleepy Lagoon Murder Case: Race Discrimination and Mexican-American Rights |publisher=University Press of Kansas |location=Topeka |isbn=9780700617470 |edition=1 |date=29 October 2010}}</ref> The Los Angeles press hailed the arresting officers as heroes.<ref name="Murder at the Sleepy Lagoon"/> As a result of their often brutal interrogations of the adolescents, the police announced they had found the murderers: twenty-two alleged members of the [[38th Street gang]] and two female accomplices.<ref name="Sleepy Lagoon
