Trial">{{cite web |title=Sleepy Lagoon Trial: The Sleepy Lagoon Murder Trial of 1942 |url=https://research.pomona.edu/zootsuit/en/trial/ |website=Zoot Suit Discovery Guide |publisher=Pomona College |access-date=28 December 2020}}</ref> The public discourse in California surrounding the arrest of the youths was viciously racist, and high-profile debates emerged whether Mexicans and Mexican Americans were "culturally, politically, intellectually, and biologically capable of living within a white, civilized, democratic society".<ref name="Murder at the Sleepy Lagoon"/>
[[File:Sleepy Lagoon murder case acquittal.png|thumb|right|upright|The Sleepy Lagoon murder case acquittal, [[Los Angeles]], October 1944]]
The resulting criminal trial, ''People v. Zammora'' (1942), is infamous for its fundamental denial of [[due process]]. Of the twenty-four charged youth, seventeen were indicted on murder charges and placed on trial.<ref>{{Citation|title=People v. Zammora|date=1944|url=https://scholar.google.com/scholar_case?q=sleepy+lagoon+case&hl=en&as_sdt=2,5&case=2516729798903775955&scilh=0|volume=66|pages=166|access-date=2018-04-09}}</ref> The courtroom was small and, during the trial, the defendants were not allowed to sit near, or to communicate with, their attorneys.<ref>{{cite web |title=Sleepy Lagoon and the Sailor Riots of 1943 (a.k.a. Zoot Suit Riots) |url=https://www.calstatela.edu/orgs/mecha/zoot-suit.htm |website=La Noche Triste |date=22 October 2013 |publisher=California State University, Los Angeles |access-date=29 December 2020}}</ref> None of those charged were permitted to change their clothes during the entirety of the trial by order of the presiding Judge Charles W. Fricke at the request of the district attorney.<ref>{{cite web |last1=McLellan March |first1=Dennis |title=Defendant in notorious '42 Sleepy Lagoon murder case was unjustly convicted |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2008-mar-07-me-reyes7-story.html |website=Los Angeles Times |date=7 March 2008 |access-date=29 December 2020}}</ref> Judge Fricke also permitted the chief of the Foreign Relations Bureau of the [[Los Angeles County Sheriff's Department|Los Angeles sheriff]]'s office, E. Duran Ayres, to testify as an "expert witness" that Mexicans as a community had a "blood-thirst" and a "biological predisposition" to crime and killing, citing the supposed [[Human sacrifice in Aztec culture|human sacrifice]] practiced by their [[Aztecs|Aztec]] ancestors.<ref name="Stacy">{{cite book | last = Stacy | first = Lee | year = 2002 | title = Mexico and the United States, Volume 1 | publisher = [[Marshall Cavendish]] | page = 185 | isbn = 978-0-7614-7402-9}}</ref> After Judge Fricke's guilty verdict in January, the Mexican-American youths were imprisoned. The Mexican American community was outraged and several attorneys challenged Judge Fricke's decisions.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Larralde|first=Carlos|title=Josefina Fierro and the Sleepy Lagoon Crusade, 1942-1945|journal=Southern California Quarterly|date=Summer 2010|volume=92|issue=2|pages=117–160|jstor=41172517|doi=10.2307/41172517}}</ref> The famous journalist [[Carey McWilliams (journalist)|Carey McWilliams]] noted that a few months earlier, over 120,000 [[Japanese Americans]] were [[Internment of Japanese Americans|detained and interned in detention camps]], and argued that there were common links between the Japanese-American internment and the anti-Mexican response to the Sleepy Lagoon case.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.chicano.ucla.edu/center/events/Sleepy_Lagoon/SleepyLagoon.htm|title=Conference: 'The Sleepy Lagoon Case, Constitutional Rights, and the Struggle for Democracy'|date=8 January 2013|website=ucla.edu}}</ref> In October 1944, the state [[California Courts of Appeal|Court of Appeals]] unanimously decided the evidence was not sufficient to sustain a guilty verdict.<ref name="Sleepy Lagoon Trial"/> It reversed 12 of the defendants' convictions and directly criticized Fricke for his bias in and mishandling of the case.<ref>Eduardo Obregón Pagán, ''Murder at the Sleepy Lagoon: Zoot Suits, Race, and Riot in Wartime LA'' (Univ of North Carolina Press, 2003) p 207-8</ref>
===Zoot Suit Riots=== {{Main article|Zoot Suit Riots}}
{{multiple image | direction = vertical | width = 165 | footer = During the [[Zoot Suit Riots]], [[U.S. Navy]] sailors beat and stripped over 150 Mexican American boys. The [[Los Angeles Police Department|LAPD]] responded to the violence by arresting over 500 Mexican American teen boys and girls. | image1 = Soldier, sailors, and marines stop a streetcar during their search for pachuca “zoot suiters” in Los Angeles, June 7, 1943.jpg | alt1 = | caption1 = | image2 = Dora Barrios, Frances Silva, and Lorena Encinas.png | alt2 = | caption2 = | image3 = Zoot suiters lined up outside Los Angeles jail en route to court after feud with sailors.tif | alt3 = }}
In the 1940s, Mexican American youth had grown up fully immersed in American popular culture, including films, music, and other media. When they came of age, these youth diverged from the expectations of both their parents and dominant society by using culture and fashion to undermine the norms of [[Racial segregation in the United States|American segregation]] and [[white supremacy]].<ref>{{cite book |last1=Obregón Pagán |title=Murder at the Sleepy Lagoon: Zoot Suits, Race, and Riot in Wartime L.A. |page=19}}</ref> These teens developed their own music, language, and dress. For boys, called [[Pachuco]]s, the style was to wear a flamboyant long coat [[zoot suit]] with baggy pegged pants, a pork pie hat, a long key chain and shoes with thick soles.<ref>{{cite web |title=L.A. in the Zoot Suit Era |url=http://research.pomona.edu/zootsuit/en/zoot-suit-la/ |website=Zoot Suit Discovery Guide |publisher=Pomona College |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200617221256/http://research.pomona.edu/zootsuit/en/zoot-suit-la/ |access-date=29 December 2020|archive-date=2020-06-17 }}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Perez |first1=Gilberto |title=Pachuco: Subculture and Sociology |url=https://haenfler.sites.grinnell.edu/subcultures-and-scenes/fashion-art-and-dance-culture/pachuco/ |website=Grinnell College |access-date=29 December 2020}}</ref> The style was meant to serve as a rebuke of American [[Cultural assimilation|assimilation]] and war efforts to conserve materials like fabrics.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Becker |first1=Lauren |title=The Myth Still Lives: Pachuco Subculture and Symbolic Styles of Resistance |url=http://scholarship.claremont.edu/scripps_theses/360 |journal=Scripps Senior Theses |date=January 2014 |publisher=Scripps College |access-date=29 December 2020}}</ref> Meanwhile, girls, called [[Pachucas]], wore black drape jackets, tight skirts, fishnet stockings and heavily emphasized make-up. For the Pachucas, participation in the movement was a way to openly challenge conventional notions of feminine beauty and sexuality, especially in traditional Mexican culture.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Pagán|first=Eduardo|date=2000|title=Los Angeles Geopolitics and The Zoot Suit Riot, 1943|journal=Social Science History|volume=1|pages=223–256|doi=10.1017/S0145553200010129|s2cid=145233558}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite news|title=Hobble Skirts Hide Razors: Zoot Suitors Run for Cover But their 'Cholitas' Carry On.|last=Payette|first=William|date=June 11, 1943|newspaper=The Washington Post}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=From Coveralls to Zoot Suits: The Lives of Mexican American Women on the World War II Home Front.|last=Escobedo|first=Elizabeth|publisher=University of North Carolina Press|year=2015|location=Chapel Hill, NC|pages=11, 24, 37}}</ref><ref name=":4">{{Cite journal|last=Ramirez|first=Catherine|date=2006|jstor=4137381|title=Saying 'Nothin': Pachucas and the Languages of Resistance|journal=Frontiers: A Journal of Women Studies|volume=27|issue=3|pages=1–33|doi=10.1353/fro.2006.a209989 }}</ref> In both instances, the Zoot-suiters were considered un-American.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Coroian |first1=George |title=Zoot Suit Riots |url=https://www.britannica.com/event/Zoot-Suit-Riots |website=American History |publisher=Britannica Encyclopedia |access-date=28 December 2020}}</ref>
This sentiment created issues in Los Angeles, where a new [[United States Navy|Navy]] base was installed in [[Chavez Ravine]], a segregated Mexican American neighborhood of Los Angeles.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Franco |first1=Antonio |title=Threads of the Zoot Suit Riots: How the initial explanations for the riots hold up today |url=https://digitalcommons.chapman.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1117&context=vocesnovae |website=Digital Commons |publisher=Chapman University |access-date=28 December 2020}}</ref> This brought over 50,000 service members into a largely Mexican neighborhood, many of whom were white and from areas with few Mexican Americans.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Obregón Pagán |first1=Eduardo |title=Los Angeles Geopolitics and the Zoot Suit Riot, 1943 |journal=Social Science History |date=Spring 2000 |volume=24 |issue=1 |pages=223–256 |doi=10.1017/S0145553200010129 |jstor=1171657 |s2cid=145233558 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1171657 |access-date=29 December 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref> The sailors – who frequently walked through the Chavez Ravine neighborhood on their way to the bars in [[Downtown Los Angeles]] - would harass the Zoot-suited youth for their seemingly disrespectful attitudes.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Addis |first1=Cameron |title=World War II, 1942-43 |url=http://sites.austincc.edu/caddis/world-war-ii-1942-43/ |website=History Hub |publisher=Austin Community College |access-date=29 December 2020}}</ref><ref name="Zoot Suit Riots: Los Angeles Erupts in Violence"/> As the anti-Mexican atmosphere surrounding the Sleepy Lagoon murder trial grew more tense over the course of 1943, minor attacks by Navy sailors against Mexican American boys became an almost daily occurrence.<ref name="Zoot Suit Riots: Los Angeles Erupts in Violence">{{cite web |title=Zoot Suit Riots: Los Angeles Erupts in Violence |url=https://www.pbs.org/wgbh/americanexperience/films/zoot/#transcript |website=American Experience |publisher=PBS |access-date=29 December 2020}}</ref>
In June 1943, these tensions exploded in one of the worst race riots in the city's history. After a Mexican American boy raised his hand in a way that a sailor considered to be "threatening", the man and his friends attacked the boy.<ref name="Zoot Suit Riots: Los Angeles Erupts in Violence"/> This sparked a skirmish in the street, which ended quickly after the initial sailor had his nose broken.<ref>{{cite web |title=City, Navy Clamp Lid on Zoot Suit Warfare |url=https://research.pomona.edu/zootsuit/en/resources/newspaper-articles/city-navy-clamp-lid-on-zoot-suit-warfare/ |newspaper=Los Angeles Times |date=June 9, 1943|via=Zoot Suit Discovery Guide of Pomona College |access-date=29 December 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170421022123/https://research.pomona.edu/zootsuit/en/resources/newspaper-articles/city-navy-clamp-lid-on-zoot-suit-warfare/ |archive-date=April 21, 2017}}</ref> That night, hundreds of sailors went into the neighborhood and attacked every Mexican American boy they could find.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Mazón |first1=Mauricio |title=The Zoot-Suit Riots: The Psychology of Symbolic Annihilation |date=January 1, 1988 |publisher=University of Texas Press |location=Austin |isbn=978-0292798038 |edition=1}}</ref> For the next ten straight days, the Navy sailors went into Chavez Ravine, Downtown LA, and even [[East Los Angeles, California|East Los Angeles]], dragging, beating, and stripping naked every Zoot suited boy out in public – some as young as twelve and thirteen years old.<ref>{{cite web |title=The Zoot Suit Riots |url=https://www.digitalhistory.uh.edu/disp_textbook.cfm?smtID=3&psid=606 |website=Digital History |publisher=University of Houston |access-date=29 December 2020}}</ref> The Los Angeles press cheered on the racist attacks, even printing guides on how to "de-zoot" a zoot-suiter.<ref>{{cite web |title=The Zoot Suit Riots |url=http://users.clas.ufl.edu/jimenez/spw4304teatro/C1%20%20zoot%20suit.htm |publisher=University of Florida |access-date=29 December 2020}}</ref> The LAPD responded by joining the sailors, arresting hundreds of Zoot suiters, both teenaged boys and girls, and charging them with "disturbing the peace".<ref>{{cite book |last1=Ramírez |first1=Catherine S. |title=The Woman in the Zoot Suit: Gender, Nationalism, and the Cultural Politics of Memory |date=January 16, 2009 |publisher=Duke University Press |location=Durham, NC |isbn=9780822388647 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SO5b9OvxuG4C |access-date=29 December 2020}}</ref> Progressive activists at the time, such as Carey McWilliams, blamed the riots on [[William Randolph Hearst]]'s "proto-fascist" promotion of "anti-Mexican hysteria" during the Sleepy Lagoon murder trial.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Kirsch |first1=Jonathan |title=When music, fashion and politics clashed |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2003-nov-30-bk-kirsch30-story.html |website=Los Angeles Times |date=30 November 2003 |access-date=29 December 2020}}</ref> Scholars, however, have focused on the complex social matrix operating within Los Angeles at the time, and interpret the riots as an example of the "social cleavages" within the segregation-era U.S.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Obregón Pagán |title=Murder at the Sleepy Lagoon: Zoot Suits, Race, and Riot in Wartime L.A. |page=8}}</ref>
===Postwar activism=== [[File:Truman and Garcia.jpg|thumb|left|upright|[[Marcario García|Macario Garcia]], receiving the [[Medal of Honor]] from President [[Harry S. Truman|Harry Truman]]. A month later, he was refused service at a Texas cafe because of his ethnicity. He refused to leave the cafe and was arrested.]]
World War II formally ended on September 2, 1945 after the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombings]] of [[Hiroshima]] and [[Nagasaki]], and the final surrender of [[Japan during World War II|Japan]] to the [[Allies of World War II|Allied powers]]. For the millions of returning veterans, the adjustment back to civilian life was difficult. For African and Latino Americans, in particular, there was significant difficulty transitioning from being war heroes and liberators in Europe, back into second-class citizens in the race-segregated United States. [[African Americans]] had sought to address some of these discrepancies with their [[Double V campaign|Double V]] campaign;<ref>{{cite web |title=Double V Campaign |url=https://hti.osu.edu/sites/hti.osu.edu/files/double-v-campaign.pdf |website=U.S. History in Context |publisher=Ohio State University |access-date=29 December 2020}}</ref> meanwhile, Mexican Americans began their own fight for civil rights at home. The historian [[Thomas A. Guglielmo]] writes, "Patriotic sacrifice and service only further fired Mexicans' and Mexican Americans' determination to gain first-class citizenship."<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Guglielmo |first1=Thomas A. |title=Fighting for Caucasian Rights: Mexicans, Mexican Americans, and the Transnational Struggle for Civil Rights in World War II Texas |journal=The Journal of American History |date=Spring 2006 |volume=94 |issue=2 |pages=1212–1237 |doi=10.2307/4485889 |jstor=4485889 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Returning Mexican Americans challenged discrimination and segregation in many ways, including by sitting in "whites only" seating sections in town theaters, demanding service at white restaurants, and attempting to enter segregated public pools.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Garcia |first1=Matt |last2=Cianci Salvatore |first2=Susan |title=Civil Rights in America: Racial Desegregation of Public Accommodations |date=2009 |publisher=National Park Service |location=Washington D.C. |edition=Revised |url=https://www.nps.gov/subjects/tellingallamericansstories/upload/CivilRights_DesegPublicAccom.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170409131802/https://www.nps.gov/subjects/tellingallamericansstories/upload/CivilRights_DesegPublicAccom.pdf |url-status=dead |archive-date=April 9, 2017 |access-date=29 December 2020}}</ref> In one notorious instance, [[Marcario García|Macario Garcia]] received the [[Medal of Honor]] in a ceremony at the [[White House]], and less than a month later, he was refused service at the Oasis Cafe in [[Richmond, Texas]] because of his ethnicity.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Olivas |first1=Michael A. |title=The 'Trial of the Century' that Never Was: Staff Sgt. Macario Garcia, the Congressional Medal of Honor, and the Oasis Café |url=http://ilj.law.indiana.edu/articles/83/83_4_Olivas.pdf |website=Indiana Law Journal |publisher=Indiana University Maurer School of Law |access-date=29 December 2020}}</ref> He refused to leave the cafe, the police were called, and Garcia was arrested and charged with "aggravated assault".<ref>{{cite web |last1=Cepeda |first1=Esther J. |title=El Movimiento |url=https://www.neh.gov/humanities/2013/septemberoctober/feature/el-movimiento |website=Humanities |publisher=National Endowment for the Humanities |access-date=29 December 2020}}</ref> In Arizona, the governor named August 14, 1945 in honor after another Medal of Honor recipient, [[Silvestre S. Herrera|Silvestre Herrera]]. Coverage of the event was marred, however, by the governor's need to request Phoenix businesses to take down signs barring Mexicans.<ref>{{cite web |title=Acculturation and the Roots of Social Change: 1940-1956 |url=https://www.phoenix.gov/pddsite/Documents/HP/pdd_hp_pdf_00046.pdf |website=Hispanic Historic Property Survey |publisher=City of Phoenix |access-date=29 December 2020}}</ref>
[[File:Young woman at the community laundry on Saturday afternoon, FSA ... camp, Robstown, Tex. (LOC).jpg|thumb|right|upright|Many Mexican Americans were denied the full benefits of the [[G.I. Bill|GI Bill]]. As a result, many Mexican American families remained in cycles of poverty.]]
Discrimination against returning Mexican American veterans hurt the prospects of the entire Mexican American community. While medical, financial, and educational benefits from the [[GI Bill]] helped lift millions of Anglo-American families into the growing American middle-class, the application of the bill's benefits to African and Mexican American veterans was uneven.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Munsey |first1=C. |title=But not all Americans benefited equally |url=https://www.apa.org/monitor/2010/11/gi-bill |website=Monitor on Psychology |publisher=American Psychological Association |access-date=29 December 2020}}</ref> As a result, the Mexican American community did not ever gain full economic and political equality in the postwar era.<ref name="World War II and Mexican American Civil Rights"/><ref name="Latinx Women on the Home Front"/> Rather than being simply exclusionary, however, the GI Bill had several important failings which resulted in its discriminatory outcomes.<ref name="Fighting the Peace at Home"/> The bill offered loan guarantees, yet few banks honored such guarantees to non-white veterans, and for those who did, restrictive [[Covenant (law)#United States|racial covenants]] meant that African and Mexican American veterans were only able to live in [[Redlining|redlined]] neighborhoods, where property values often remained low.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Woods II |first1=Louis Lee |title=Almost 'No Negro Veteran ... Could Get A Loan': African Americans, The GI Bill, and The NAACP Campaign Against Residential Segregation, 1917–1960 |journal=The Journal of African American History |date=Summer 2013 |volume=98 |issue=3 |pages=392–417 |doi=10.5323/jafriamerhist.98.3.0392 |jstor=10.5323/jafriamerhist.98.3.0392 |s2cid=224836171 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/10.5323/jafriamerhist.98.3.0392 |access-date=29 December 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Martinez Govea |first1=Vicenta |title=America's Finest Housing Crisis: Racialized Housing & Suburban Development |url=https://digital.sandiego.edu/mcnair-summer/1 |journal=Mcnair Summer Research Program |date=7 August 2020 |publisher=University of San Diego |access-date=29 December 2020}}</ref> Furthermore, many Mexican American veterans complained about consistently late tuition disbursements, which forced them to drop out of their job training and university programs, and reports of "outright racism within the [[United States Department of Veterans Affairs|VA]]" were common.<ref name="Fighting the Peace at Home">{{cite journal |last1=Rosales |first1=Steven |title=Fighting the Peace at Home: Mexican American Veterans and the 1944 GI Bill of Rights |journal=Pacific Historical Review |date=2011 |volume=80 |issue=4 |pages=597–627 |doi=10.1525/phr.2011.80.4.597 |jstor=10.1525/phr.2011.80.4.597 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/10.1525/phr.2011.80.4.597 |access-date=30 December 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Contemporary scholars have found that overall, the GI Bill "did not profoundly alter the occupational profile of all Mexicans ... and its immediate impact on upward mobility among families ... was inconsistent".<ref>Dennis Valdés, "The Mexican American Dream and World War II: A View from the Midwest", in Rivas-Rodríguez, ed., ''Mexican Americans & World War II''.</ref>
[[File:Hector P. Garcia.jpg|thumb|left|upright|[[Hector P. Garcia]], [[World War II]] [[veteran]], [[Civil and political rights|civil rights]] advocate, and founder of the [[American GI Forum|American G.I. Forum]]]]
Some of these issues were challenged directly. In 1948, the [[Corpus Christi, Texas|Corpus Christi]] physician, [[Hector P. Garcia]], founded the [[American GI Forum]] in order to demand equal rights to GI Benefits, medical care, burial rights, desegregated education, and other civil rights.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Allsup |first1=Vernon Carl |title=American G.I. Forum: Origins and Evolution |date=October 1, 1982 |publisher=University of Texas Press |location=Austin |isbn=978-0292703629 |edition=1}}</ref> In a famous early instance of the Forum's civil rights advocacy, Garcia took up the case of Private [[Felix Z. Longoria Jr.|Felix Longoria]] of [[Three Rivers, Texas]]. Longoria had died in combat in 1945, but his remains weren't shipped home for several years.<ref>{{cite web |title=Felix Longoria |url=https://www.digitalhistory.uh.edu/disp_textbook.cfm?smtid=3&psid=3704 |website=Digital History |publisher=University of Houston |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref> When they finally arrived, his widow Beatrice went to the local funeral home to plan a wake in his honor.<ref>{{cite web |title='The Longoria Affair' |url=https://www.denverpost.com/2010/11/09/navarrette-the-longoria-affair/ |website=The Denver Post |date=9 November 2010 |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref> The funeral home director refused to allow the family to use the chapel, because of their race.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Martin |first1=Mitchell |last2=Valadez |first2=John |title=Documentary Recounts Anti-Mexican Racism In The South |url=https://www.npr.org/templates/story/story.php?storyId=131188043 |publisher=National Public Radio |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref> The director told the media at the time that he would never allow Mexican Americans to use his facilities, saying, "We just never made it a practice to let them use the chapel and we don't want to start now."<ref>{{cite web |last1=Hart |first1=Lianne |title=WWII Dispute Again Divides Town |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2004-may-31-na-threerivers31-story.html |website=Los Angeles Times |date=31 May 2004 |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref> When Garcia found out about the situation, he was outraged and immediately sent letters of protest to the media, elected politicians, and government officials. One of these letters was sent to Texas' junior senator, [[Lyndon B. Johnson]], who arranged for Longoria to receive full honors and burial at [[Arlington National Cemetery]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Saavedra |first1=Rebecca |title=The Longoria Incident |url=https://www.utmb.edu/drgarcia/longoria.htm |website=Hispanic Center of Excellence |publisher=The University of Texas Medical Branch |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref> The Longoria incident pushed the American G.I. Forum to the forefront of the postwar Mexican American civil rights strategy.<ref name="Latinos in World War II: Fighting on Two Fronts"/> The Forum, alongside [[League of United Latin American Citizens|LULAC]] and [[El Congreso de Pueblos de Habla Española|El Congreso]], greatly expanded their operations after World War II and began their fights to end segregation.<ref>Cynthia E. Orozco, "Regionalism, Politics, and Gender in Southwest History: The League of United Latin American Citizens' Expansion into New Mexico from Texas, 1929-1945", ''Western Historical Quarterly'' (1998) 29#4 pp. 459-483</ref>
===Court cases=== {{See also|Mendez v. Westminster|Perez v. Sharp}} [[File:Mexican school in Peñasco, New Mexico.jpg|thumb|right|Segregated school for Mexican American children in [[New Mexico]]]]
In the 1940s, there were two major court cases involving the civil rights of Mexican Americans. The first, ''[[Mendez v. Westminster]]'' (1947), involved Gonzalo Méndez, a naturalized U.S. citizen born in Mexico, and his Puerto Rican wife Felicitas, who joined four Mexican American families to sue four [[Orange County, California|Orange County]] school districts.<ref name="Before Brown v. Board of Education There Was Méndez v. Westminster">{{cite web |last1=Macías |first1=Francisco |title=Before Brown v. Board of Education There Was Méndez v. Westminster |url=https://blogs.loc.gov/law/2014/05/before-brown-v-board-of-education-there-was-mendez-v-westminster/ |website=Law Librarians of Congress |date=16 May 2014 |publisher=U.S. Library of Congress |access-date=29 December 2020}}</ref> The families challenged the common California practices of drawing school boundaries around majority-Mexican American neighborhoods and of placing Mexicans who lived in majority-white communities into segregated Spanish "remedial" schools.<ref>{{cite web |title=Mendez v. Westminster School District |url=https://www.archives.gov/education/lessons/mendez-case |website=Teaching With Documents |date=17 October 2019 |publisher=U.S. National Archives |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Viera |first1=Mariana |title=How Méndez vs. Westminster Helped End Segregated Schools for Latinx Children in 1940s California |url=https://www.teenvogue.com/story/mendez-vs-westminster-helped-end-segregated-schools-latinx-children |website=Teen Vogue |date=12 October 2018 |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref> During the trial, the Orange County superintendents justified school segregation because the Mexican American children had inferior "personal hygiene", "scholastic ability", and "economic outlook".<ref name="Before Brown v. Board of Education There Was Méndez v. Westminster"/> On the plaintiff's side, the constitutionality of educational segregation was questioned on the basis of the [[Fourteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution|Fourteenth Amendment]], and social scientists were brought in as expert witnesses to dispute the supposed educational benefits of segregated schools.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Rosenberg |first1=Jeremy |title='No Dogs or Mexicans Allowed': Mendez v. Westminster and its Legacy |url=https://www.kcet.org/history-society/no-dogs-or-mexicans-allowed-mendez-v-westminster-and-its-legacy |website=KCET |date=April 2013 |publisher=Public Broadcasting Service |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref> In 1946, Judge [[Paul John McCormick|Paul McCormick]] ruled that school segregation was a "clear denial" of the [[Equal Protection Clause]], and the next year, McCormick's decision was upheld by the [[United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit|U.S. Court of Appeals]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Background - Mendez v. Westminster Re-Enactment |url=https://www.uscourts.gov/educational-resources/educational-activities/background-mendez-v-westminster-re-enactment |website=Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts |publisher=Federal Judiciary |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref> The Méndez case was significant for several reasons, including: the support of NAACP counsel [[Thurgood Marshall]];<ref>{{cite web |last1=Sadlier |first1=Sarah |title=Méndez V. Westminster: The Harbinger Of Brown V. Board |url=https://ecommons.cornell.edu/bitstream/handle/1813/47956/4.8%20Mendez.pdf |website=eCommons |publisher=Cornell University |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref> the plaintiffs' revolutionary use of social scientific research as a basis for law;<ref>{{cite web |title=U.S. Court House & Post Office: Los Angeles, California |url=https://www.nps.gov/nr/travel/american_latino_heritage/Los_Angeles_US_Court_House_and_Post_Office.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150308182052/http://www.nps.gov/nr/travel/american_latino_heritage/Los_Angeles_US_Court_House_and_Post_Office.html |url-status=dead |archive-date=March 8, 2015 |website=National Park Service |publisher=U.S. Department of the Interior |access-date=1 January 2021}}</ref> the legal precedent marking educational segregation as unconstitutional;<ref>{{cite web |last1=Moreno |first1=Maria |title=Before Brown v. Board of Education There Was 8-Year-Old Sylvia Mendez |url=https://oie.duke.edu/brown-v-board-education-there-was-8-year-old-sylvia-mendez |website=Office for Institutional Equity |publisher=Duke University |access-date=1 January 2021}}</ref> and the case's influence for the ''Anderson Bill'' (1947), which repealed California school segregation.<ref>{{cite web |title=Westminster School / Seventeenth Street School |url=https://www.nps.gov/parkhistory/online_books/5views/5views5h99.htm |website=A History of Mexican Americans in California |publisher=National Park Service |access-date=29 December 2020}}</ref>
[[File:Leon Watson and Rosina Rodriquez.png|thumb|left|upright|Leon Watson and Rosina Rodriquez, an interracial couple who were allowed to marry because of the ''[[Perez v. Sharp]]'' (1948) case]]
The second major civil rights court victory for Mexican Americans also occurred in California. Andrea Pérez, who was Mexican American, and Sylvester Davis, who was African American, had met and developed a friendship immediately before he was drafted to fight in World War II. When he returned, they resumed their relationship, fell in love, and married.<ref name="Loving V. Virginia in a Post-Racial World">{{cite book |last1=Noble Maillard |first1=Kevin |last2=Cuison Villazor |first2=Rose |title=Loving V. Virginia in a Post-Racial World: Rethinking Race, Sex, and Marriage |date=June 7, 2012 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=New York |isbn=9780521198585 |pages=73–88 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=DluOsvcPAcoC |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref> At the time, California's [[Anti-miscegenation laws|anti-miscegenation code]] barred interracial marriage.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Mangrum |first1=Erik |title=Before Loving |url=https://www.pbs.org/wgbh/americanexperience/features/before-loving/ |website=American Experience |publisher=PBS |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref> Pérez and Davis hired the civil rights attorney Dan Marshall to represent them in challenging the ban.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Leslie |first1=Christopher R. |title=Justice Alito's Dissent in Loving v. Virginia |journal=Boston College Law Review |date=November 2014 |volume=55 |issue=5 |url=https://lawdigitalcommons.bc.edu/bclr/vol55/iss5/5/ |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref> When the Los Angeles County clerk denied them a marriage license, Pérez formally filed suit. In 1948, the [[Supreme Court of California|California Supreme Court]] ruled in favor of the couple, becoming the first state in the country to overturn a ban on [[interracial marriage]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Lenhardt |first1=R.A. |title=Beyond Analogy: Perez v. Sharp, Antimiscegenation Law, and the Fight for Same-Sex Marriage |journal=California Law Review |date=August 2008 |volume=96 |issue=4 |url=https://ir.lawnet.fordham.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1454&context=faculty_scholarship |access-date=29 December 2020}}</ref> The decision in part relied on arguments based on the inability of the law to account for Mexican [[Mestizo|mestizaje]].<ref name="Void for Vagueness">{{cite journal |last1=Orenstein |first1=Dara |title=Void for Vagueness:Mexicans and the Collapse of Miscegenation Law in California |journal=Pacific Historical Review |date=Summer 2005 |volume=74 |issue=3 |pages=367–408 |doi=10.1525/phr.2005.74.3.367 |url=https://doi.org/10.1525/phr.2005.74.3.367 |access-date=29 December 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref> The court, noting Pérez' "in-between" racial status, ruled that blanket bans on interracial marriage were "too vague and uncertain" since they did not consider people who were of "mixed ancestry".<ref name="Void for Vagueness"/> Also, the court held county clerks and other government employees could not realistically be expected to successfully ascertain the racial makeup of each marriage applicant. Finally, Judge [[Roger J. Traynor|Roger Traynor]]'s majority opinion found that the law also violated the equal protection clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Carbado |first1=Devon W. |last2=Moran |first2=Rachel F. |title=The Story of Perez V. Sharp: Forgotten Lessons on Race, Law, and Marriage |journal=Fordham Law Legal Studies Research Paper |date=March 21, 2011 |url=https://ssrn.com/abstract=952711 |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref> [[Earl Warren]] was then the governor of California, and he oversaw the legal implementation of the ruling in the state. Nineteen years later, he served as chief justice in ''[[Loving v. Virginia]]'' (1967), the U.S. Supreme Court case that struck down all remaining state bans on interracial marriage.<ref name="Loving V. Virginia in a Post-Racial World"/>
==Mid-20th century== ===Korean War=== {{Main article|Mexicans and Mexican Americans in the Korean War}} [[File:Obregon EA.jpg|thumb|right|upright|Private First Class [[Eugene A. Obregon]], [[United States Marine Corps]] and posthumous [[Medal of Honor]] recipient]]
In June 1950, the United States entered the [[Korean War]] as part of a [[United Nations]]-led coalition supporting [[South Korea]].<ref>{{cite web |title=US Enters the Korean Conflict |url=https://www.archives.gov/education/lessons/korean-conflict |website=Social Education |date=15 August 2016 |publisher=U.S. National Archives |access-date=29 December 2020}}</ref> Thousands of Mexican Americans served in the conflict from its beginning, many of them inspired to join the war effort because of the service of their fathers and older brothers in World War II.<ref name="Soldados Razos at War">{{cite book |last1=Rosales |first1=Steven |title=Soldados Razos at War: Chicano Politics, Identity, and Masculinity in the U.S. Military from World War II to Vietnam |date=April 18, 2017 |publisher=University of Arizona Press |location=Tucson |isbn=9780816532445 |page=74 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=g-8ZDgAAQBAJ&q=korean%20war |access-date=29 December 2020}}</ref> Staff Sgt. Joe Campos, from [[Miami, Arizona]] became one of the first soldiers [[missing in action]] of the war after his plane was shot down on June 28, 1950 over the [[Yellow Sea]]. A few days later, Florentino Gonzales, from [[Chicago]], was part of the first group of [[Prisoner of war|prisoners of war]].<ref name="Mexicans: Forgotten soldiers of 1950-53 Korean War">{{cite web |last1=Figueroa |first1=Bruno |last2=Barcena |first2=Martha |title=Mexicans: Forgotten soldiers of 1950-53 Korean War |url=https://www.koreatimes.co.kr/www/nation/2020/06/176_291257.html |website=[[The Korea Times]] |date=22 June 2020 |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref> One Mexican American soldier, Jesus Rodriguez, later remembered how his harsh upbringing in the United States prepared him for the Korean War.<ref>{{cite web |title=Jesus Rodriguez |url=https://koreanwarlegacy.org/interviews/jesus-rodriguez/ |website=Korean War Veterans Digital Memorial |publisher=Korean War Legacy Foundation |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Carpenter |first1=Eric |title=Korean War hero shares story |url=https://www.ocregister.com/2008/05/28/korean-war-hero-shares-story/ |website=Orange County Register |date=28 May 2008 |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref> He stated, "I used to pray a lot. Another thing that helped me was that I was street smart from before going into the service. On the streets I learned how to fight ... Something else that helped me survive Korea was that going hungry wasn't new to me and didn't hurt me."<ref name="On the 70th anniversary of the Korean War">{{cite web |last1=Bárcena |first1=Martha |last2=Figueroa |first2=Bruno |title=On the 70th anniversary of the Korean War, it's time to finally recognize contributions of Mexicans |url=https://www.dallasnews.com/opinion/commentary/2020/06/25/on-the-70th-anniversary-of-the-korean-war-its-time-to-finally-recognize-contributions-of-mexicans/ |website=The Dallas Morning News |date=25 June 2020 |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref> Ten Mexican Americans were awarded the [[Medal of Honor]] for their valor in the Korean War;<ref name="On the 70th anniversary of the Korean War"/> they included: [[Joe R. Baldonado]], [[Victor H. Espinoza]], [[Eduardo C. Gomez]], [[Edward Gómez]], [[Ambrosio Guillen]], [[Rodolfo P. Hernández|Rodolfo P. Hernandez]], [[Benito Martinez (soldier)|Benito Martinez]], [[Eugene A. Obregon|Eugene Arnold Obregon]], [[Mike C. Pena]], and [[Joseph C. Rodríguez|Joseph C. Rodriguez]].<ref>{{cite book |last1=Aguirre |first1=Frederick P. |last2=Martinez Aguire |first2=Linda |title=Freedom Is Not Free: Mexican Americans in the Korean War |date=January 1, 2007 |publisher=Latino Advocates for Education |location=Orange, CA |edition=1 |url=https://aplh.webs.com/Korean%20War%201.pdf |access-date=30 December 2020 |archive-date=4 April 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210404161014/https://aplh.webs.com/Korean%20War%201.pdf |url-status=dead }}</ref> Finally, Gen. [[Richard E. Cavazos]], born in [[Kingsville, Texas]], became the first Mexican American [[General (United States)|four-star general]] and head of the [[United States Army Forces Command|U.S. Army Forces Command]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Morgan |first1=Matthew J. |title=Hispanic Heritage Month |url=https://www.dm.af.mil/Media/Commentaries/Display/Article/944360/hispanic-heritage-month/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210302012802/https://www.dm.af.mil/Media/Commentaries/Display/Article/944360/hispanic-heritage-month/ |url-status=dead |archive-date=March 2, 2021 |website=Davis-Monthan Air Force Base |publisher=United States Air Force |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref>
===Bracero program=== {{Main|Bracero program}} [[File:Contract Mexican laborers being fumigated with the pesticide DDT in Hidalgo, Texas, in 1956.jpg|thumb|left|upright|[[Bracero program|Bracero]] workers were subject to invasive medical examinations and harmful [[DDT]] sprays before they were allowed to enter the U.S.]]
In 1951, the U.S. government passed ''Public Law 78'', a law which formalized the [[Bracero program|Bracero Program]], a temporary work exchange program with Mexico.<ref>{{cite web |title=The Bracero Program: A Historical Investigation |url=https://americanhistory.si.edu/sites/default/files/Bracero%20Historical%20Investigation.pdf |website=National Museum of American History |publisher=Smithsonian Institution |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref> First begun as a scheme to fill wartime labor shortages during [[World War II]], Congress decided to formalize the program for a number of reasons: first, new concerns were raised about domestic labor gaps during the Korean War; second, political concerns were raised about a potential rise in [[Illegal immigration to the United States|undocumented immigration]] if Mexican laborers were not offered a legal pathway to work in the U.S.; third, agricultural employers' associations lobbied hard for the program since they benefited the most from its [[Subsidy|subsidizations]].<ref name="Bracero Program">{{cite web |last1=Vigil |first1=Ralph H. |title=Bracero Program |url=http://plainshumanities.unl.edu/encyclopedia/doc/egp.ha.005 |website=Encyclopedia of the Great Plain |publisher=University of Nebraska–Lincoln |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref> Estimates place the annual number of laborers entering the U.S. in the 1950s at around 300,000,<ref>{{cite web |last1=Koestler |first1=Fred L. |title=Bracero Program |url=https://www.tshaonline.org/handbook/entries/bracero-program |website=Handbook of Texas |publisher=Texas State Historical Association |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref> and a significant number of these Braceros were [[Indigenous peoples of Mexico|indigenous Mexicans]], who hardly spoke Spanish.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Loza |first1=Mireya |title=Defiant Braceros: How Migrant Workers Fought for Racial, Sexual, and Political Freedom |date=September 2, 2016 |publisher=University of North Carolina Press |location=Chapel Hill |isbn=9781469629773 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=X6YwDAAAQBAJ |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref> Domestically, the program was controversial. The [[United States Department of Labor|U.S. Department of Labor]] official overseeing the program called it "legalized slavery",<ref>{{cite book|author=Lynn Stephen|title=Transborder Lives: Indigenous Oaxacans in Mexico, California, and Oregon|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nK8yNEGUuP0C&pg=PA72|year=2007|publisher=Duke U.P.|page=72|isbn=978-0822389965}}</ref> and some Mexican Americans believed the program suppressed their own wages.<ref>{{cite web |title=About the Bracero Program |url=http://braceroarchive.org/about |website=Bracero History Archive |publisher=Center for History and New Media |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref> [[Dolores Huerta]], a prominent labor organizer, first began her work as an organizer with the Stockton Community Service Organization (CSO) in the 1950s, where she organized [[Voter registration campaign|voter registration drives]], and pressed for barrio improvements.<ref>{{cite web |title=Dolores Huerta |url=https://doloreshuerta.org/dolores-huerta/ |website=Dolores Huerto Foundation |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=History of the UFW |url=http://www.laits.utexas.edu/jaime/cwp2/ccg/historyofufw.html |website=Texas Liberal Arts |publisher=University of Texas at Austin |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref> She was a vehement opposer of the Bracero program and was a key actor lobbying for its termination.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Davis |first1=Jennifer |title=Viva la Causa! |url=https://blogs.loc.gov/law/2018/10/viva-la-causa-dolores-huerta-and-hispanic-heritage-month/ |website=Law Librarians of Congress |date=3 October 2018 |publisher=U.S. Library of Congress |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref>
===Operation Wetback=== {{Main article|Operation Wetback}} [[File:Members of the Texas Border Patrol guard Mexican immigrants apprehended near the border in June 1948.jpg|thumb|right|upright|[[United States Border Patrol|Border Patrol]] hold teenage Mexican immigrant boys at gunpoint in Texas]]
In 1951, the [[Presidency of Harry S. Truman|Truman administration]]'s Commission on Migratory Labor released a scathing and xenophobic report blaming the Southwest's social ills on [[Illegal immigration|undocumented immigration]]. The report stated, "The magnitude [of undocumented migration] ... has reached entirely new levels in the past 7 years ... In its newly achieved proportions, it is virtually an invasion."<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Long |first1=Erven J. |title=The President's Commission on Migratory Labor in American Agriculture |journal=Land Economics |date=Summer 1951 |volume=27 |issue=3 |pages=249–251 |doi=10.2307/3159381 |jstor=3159381 |bibcode=1951LandE..27..249L |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/3159381 |access-date=30 December 2020|url-access=subscription }}</ref> This report, and an August 1953 tour of Southern California, influenced Attorney General [[Herbert Brownell Jr.|Herbert Brownell, Jr.]] to push Congress to sanction the employers of undocumented workers.<ref name="Depression, War, and Civil Rights">{{cite web |title=Depression, War, and Civil Rights |url=https://history.house.gov/Exhibitions-and-Publications/HAIC/Historical-Essays/Separate-Interests/Depression-War-Civil-Rights/ |website=History, Arts, and Archive |publisher=United States House of Representatives |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref> This did not become law. Nevertheless, the new [[Presidency of Dwight D. Eisenhower|Eisenhower administration]] moved forward with planning a mass deportation operation. On June 9, 1954, the INS Commissioner [[Joseph May Swing|Joseph Swing]] announced [[Operation Wetback]], a campaign to deport all undocumented people from the United States. Extensive media coverage during the period exaggerated the [[United States Border Patrol|Border Patrol]]'s menace and strength, and extensively reported displays of strength as part of a broader PR campaign against undocumented migration.<ref name="Depression, War, and Civil Rights"/> While 1.1 million INS apprehensions occurred that year, estimates place the number of deportations resulting directly from Operation Wetback at around 33,000 people,<ref>{{cite book |last1=Lytle Hernandez |first1=Kelly |title=Migra! A History of the U.S. Border Patrol |year=2010 |page=173 |publisher=University of California Press |isbn=9780520257696 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HruW69B1VPwC&q=operation%20wetback |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref> with perhaps another 60,000 who voluntarily repatriated.<ref name="Depression, War, and Civil Rights"/> Mexican American organizations, such as [[League of United Latin American Citizens|LULAC]] and the [[American GI Forum|American G.I. Forum]] - as well as the broader Mexican American middle-class – were largely supportive of the campaign surrounding Operation Wetback, believing that unauthorized Mexican immigration had "materially retarded" the acceptance and assimilation of Mexican Americans into American culture.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Lytle Hernandez |first1=Kelly |title=Migra! A History of the U.S. Border Patrol |year=2010 |page=174 and 193 |publisher=University of California Press |isbn=9780520257696 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HruW69B1VPwC&q=operation%20wetback |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref>
===Law and politics=== {{See also|Edward R. Roybal|Hernandez v. Texas}} [[File:Francisco Miranda, Billy Sweeney, and Councilman Edward R. Roybal.jpg|thumb|left|upright| LA councilman [[Edward R. Roybal]] with two young boys]]
Electorally, Mexican Americans made small but important strides in the 1950s. In Los Angeles, [[Edward R. Roybal]] served as the only Mexican American member on the [[Los Angeles City Council]]. During his time on council, he took a series of important positions, including: fighting against an ordinance which required communists to register with the [[LAPD|police]];<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.proquest.com/docview/166109712|title=Preview unavailable - ProQuest|website=www.proquest.com|id={{ProQuest|166109712}} }}</ref> opposing the tearing down of the Mexican American neighborhood of [[Chavez Ravine]] to build [[Dodger Stadium]];<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.proquest.com/docview/167107367|title=Preview unavailable - ProQuest|website=www.proquest.com|id={{ProQuest|167107367}} }}</ref> and pushing for the establishment of a [[Fair Employment Practice Committee|Fair Employment Practices Commission]] for the city.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.proquest.com/docview/167179593|title=Preview unavailable - ProQuest|website=www.proquest.com|id={{ProQuest|167179593}} }}</ref> In 1954, he also ran for [[Lieutenant Governor of California|Lieutenant Governor]], but he lost to the incumbent [[Goodwin Knight]] (R) by more than ten points.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.ourcampaigns.com/RaceDetail.html?RaceID=749962|title=Our Campaigns - CA Lt. Governor Race - Nov 02, 1954|website=www.ourcampaigns.com}}</ref> Despite these gains in political visibility, however, Mexican American Angelenos also experienced several high-profile incidents of police brutality in the early 1950s, including [[Bloody Christmas (1951)|Bloody Christmas]].<ref>{{cite book |last1=Hobson |first1=Emily |title=The Rising Tide of Color: Race, State Violence, and Radical Movements Across the Pacific |date=July 2014 |publisher=University of Washington Press |location=Seattle |isbn=9780295805030 |page=194 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=37MyBQAAQBAJ |access-date=10 March 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Escobar |first1=Edward J. |title=The Unintended Consequences of the Carceral State: Chicana/o Political Mobilization in Post–World War II America |journal=Journal of American History |date=June 2015 |volume=102 |issue=1 |pages=174–184 |doi=10.1093/jahist/jav312 |doi-access=free }}</ref>
In the courts, Mexican Americans continued to challenge the legal infrastructure of [[Racial segregation in the United States|American segregation]]. Throughout the Southwest, Mexican Americans were often deliberately excluded from serving as jurors in cases involving Mexican American defendants.<ref>{{Handbook of Texas |id=jrh01 |name=Hernandez v. the State of Texas}}</ref> In 1954, Pete Hernandez, an agricultural worker, was convicted by an all-Anglo jury in [[Jackson County, Texas]] for murder. Hernandez's [[pro bono]] legal team, including [[Gustavo C. Garcia|Gustavo C. García]], appealed the ruling, arguing that he was being discriminated against because there were no Mexicans in the jury that convicted him. They argued that Hernandez had the right to be tried by a jury of his peers under the [[Fourteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution|14th Amendment]]. The State of Texas denied their claim, but they appealed to the United States Supreme Court through a [[Certiorari|writ of certiorari]].<ref>{{ussc|name=Hernandez v. Texas|346|811|1953|el=no}}.</ref> The legal team included García, [[Carlos Cadena]] and [[John J. Herrera]] of the [[League of United Latin American Citizens]], and [[James DeAnda]] and Cris Alderete of the [[American GI Forum|G. I. Forum]], both activist groups for civil rights for Mexican Americans. These were the first Mexican-American lawyers to represent a defendant before the U.S. Supreme Court, which heard their arguments on January 11, 1954. [[Chief Justice of the United States|Chief Justice]] [[Earl Warren]] and the Supreme Court unanimously ruled in favor of Hernandez, and required he be retried by a jury composed of his peers.
==Chicano Movement== {{main|Chicano Movement}}{{see also|Chicana/o studies}}
===1960s=== {{multiple image | direction = vertical | width = 165 | footer = The [[Chicano Movement]], also referred to as ''El Movimiento'', was a social and political movement during the [[Civil Rights Movement]]. It was inspired by prior acts of resistance of Mexican Americans, perhaps most directly the [[Pachucos]] of the 1940s and 1950s. | image1 = Founding co-editor of La Raza Ruth Robinson (right) with Margarita Sanchez at the Belmont High School walkout, part of a series of 1968 student protests for education reform in LA.jpg | alt1 = | caption1 = | image2 = San Jose Chicano Rights Marches California003.jpg | alt2 = | caption2 = | image3 = Cesar_chavez_en_huelga_hall_de_colegio_cesar_chavez.jpg | alt3 = | image4 = Pancho Medrano and others (10019445).jpg | alt4 = }}
In the 1960s, a sub-group of Mexican American student organizations developed ideologies of [[Chicano nationalism]] and the [[Chicano movement]], highlighting American discrimination against Mexican Americans and emphasizing the overarching failures of a culturally pluralistic society.<ref name="Chicanismo">{{cite book |last1=García |first1=Ignacio M. |title=Chicanismo: The Forging of a Militant Ethos among Mexican Americans |date=September 1, 1997 |publisher=University of Arizona Press |location=Tucson |isbn=978-0816517886 |edition=3 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RNHrjTRoY-kC |access-date=18 August 2020}}</ref> Calling themselves [[La Raza]], Chicano activists sought to affirm Mexican Americans' racial distinctiveness and working-class status, create a pro-barrio movement, and assert that "brown is beautiful."<ref name="Chicanismo"/> Urging against both ethnic assimilation and the mistreatment of low-wage workers, the Chicano Movement was the first large-scale mobilization of Mexican American activism in United States history.<ref>{{cite web |title=Chicano Movement |url=https://www.brown.edu/Research/Coachella/chicano.html |website=Educating Change: Latina Activism and the Struggle for Educational Equality |publisher=Brown University |access-date=18 August 2020}}</ref>
The [[Chicano]] movement blossomed in the 1960s. The movement had roots in the civil rights struggles that had preceded it, adding to it the cultural and generational politics of the era.
In 1963, in [[Crystal City, Texas]] the mainly Mexican-American [[Immigration to the United States|migrant]] community together with the support of the [[International Brotherhood of Teamsters|Teamsters Union]] and the [[Political Association of Spanish-Speaking Organizations]] (PASSO), an outgrowth of the Viva Kennedy clubs of 1960, encouraged Mexican-American men and women to pay their [[Poll taxes in the United States|poll tax]] and choose their own candidates. Led by Teamsters business agent and [[cannery]] employee, Juan Cornejo, five Mexican-Americans, despite intimidation by the [[Texas Ranger Division|Texas Rangers]], won the support of their community young and old alike who thanks to the protection provided by the Teamsters and PASSO mobilized for electoral victory. This "revolt" was covered nationwide and reported in the ''[[The New York Times|New York Times]]'' and the ''[[The Wall Street Journal|Wall Street Journal]]''. This election led Americans outside of the [[Southwestern United States|Southwest]] to take note of America's other [[minority group|minority]] community as a political force.
The early proponents of the movement — [[Rodolfo Gonzales]] in [[Denver|Denver, Colorado]] and [[Reies Tijerina]] in New Mexico — adopted a historical account of the preceding hundred and twenty-five years that obscured much of Mexican-American history. Gonzales and Tijerina embraced a form of nationalism that was based on the failure of the United States government to live up to the promises that it had made in the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.lasculturas.com/lib/sd/blsd092200a.php|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070927085843/http://www.lasculturas.com/lib/sd/blsd092200a.php|title=A History of Mexican Americans in California|url-status=dead|archivedate=September 27, 2007|website=www.lasculturas.com}}</ref>
The [[Immigration and Nationality Act of 1965]] set strict quotas on the number of persons who could legally enter the U.S. from Latin American nations, and most new Mexican migration to the U.S. in the 1960s was temporary and short-term. Seasonal migration between the United States and Mexico became illegal in 1965. Nevertheless, the numbers involved with seasonal agriculture kept growing, often forced to resort to undocumented migration. They made money in the U.S. but returned to the villages to spend it, tend to the family business, and participate in extended kinship rituals such as baptisms, weddings, and funerals.
The most significant union struggle involving Mexican-Americans was the [[United Farm Workers]]' long strike and boycott aimed at grape growers in the [[San Joaquin Valley|San Joaquin]] and [[Coachella Valley]]s in the late 1960s, followed by campaigns to organize lettuce workers in California and Arizona, farm workers in Texas, and orange grove workers in Florida.
The most prominent civil rights organization in the Mexican-American community is the [[Mexican American Legal Defense and Educational Fund]] (MALDEF), founded in 1968. Although modeled after the [[NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund]], MALDEF has also taken on many of the functions of other organizations, including political advocacy and training of local leaders.
Instead, when the movement dealt with practical problems most activists focused on the most immediate issues confronting Mexican-Americans: unequal educational and employment opportunities, political disenfranchisement, and police brutality. In the heady days of the late 1960s, when the student movement was active around the globe, the Chicano movement brought about more or less spontaneous actions, such as the mass walkouts by high school students in Denver and [[East Los Angeles, California|East Los Angeles]] in 1968.
The movement was particularly strong at the college level, where activists formed [[MEChA]], ''el Movimiento Estudiantil Chicano de Aztlán'', which promoted Chicano Studies programs and a generalized nationalist agenda. The student movement produced a generation of future political leaders, including [[Richard Alatorre]] and [[Cruz Bustamante]] in California.
===1970s=== {{multiple image | direction = vertical | width = 165 | footer = '''Top''': The [[Brown Berets]], a pro-[[Chicano]] organization which revolved around farm worker's struggles, educational reform, and anti-war activism.<br />'''Bottom''': [[Raúl Héctor Castro]], the [[Governor of Arizona]] from 1975-1977. | image1 = Brown Berets.png | alt1 = | caption1 = | image2 = Raul_Hector_Castro.jpg | alt2 = | caption2 = | image3 = | alt3 = }}
Some women who worked within the Chicano movement felt that participants were more worried about other issues, such as immigration than solving problems that affected women. This led Chicanas to form the [[Comisión Femenil Mexicana Nacional]] in 1970. The [[Chicano Moratorium|National Chicano Moratorium March]] was also held in Los Angeles in 1970.
La Raza Unida Party campaigns in the early 1970s had the practical effect of defeating Mexican-American Democratic candidates, embittering many activists against the party and the form of nationalism it represented.
As a result of the [[Voting Rights Act of 1965|Voting Rights Act]], followed up by intensive political organizing, Mexican-Americans were able to achieve a new degree of political power and representation in Texas and elsewhere in the Southwest. The [[Raza Unida Party|La Raza Unida Party]], headed by [[José Ángel Gutiérrez]] of Crystal City, Texas made startling progress in the poorest regions in the [[Rio Grande Valley (Texas)|Rio Grande Valley]] with its base of operations at Crystal City, Texas in the early 1970s, spreading for a while to Colorado, Wisconsin, California, Michigan, Oregon, Kansas, Illinois and several other states. The party faded in the mid-1970s and held on only in Crystal City, Texas before collapsing in the early 1980s. Veterans from the party, such as Willie Velasquez, became active in [[Democratic Party (United States)|Democratic]] politics and in organizing projects such as the [[Southwest Voter Registration Education Project]], which boosted the electoral fortunes of Mexican-American candidates throughout the Southwest.
While the UFW suffered severe setbacks in California in 1973 and never established a strong union presence in other states, its struggle propelled [[Cesar Chavez|César Chávez]] and [[Dolores Huerta]] into national prominence, while providing the foot soldiers who helped increase the visibility of Mexican-Americans within the [[Democratic Party (United States)|Democratic Party]] in California and elect a number of Mexican-American candidates in the 1970s and 1980s.
By the late 1970s, tactics had forced growers to recognize the [[United Farm Workers|UFW]] as the bargaining agent for 50,000 field workers in California and Florida.
==Reagan era== [[File:Lujan_manuel.jpg|thumb|right|[[Manuel Lujan Jr.]] the [[United States Secretary of the Interior]] from 1989 to 1993]] Since the 1980s, Mexican migration has increased dramatically. The [[Immigration Reform and Control Act of 1986]] granted amnesty to illegal immigrants who had resided in the U.S. before 1982 while imposing penalties on employers who hired illegal immigrants. Several factors led to an increase in Mexican immigration to the U.S. The [[Latin American debt crisis]] of the 1980s led to high rates of unemployment in Mexico and destroyed the savings of a large portion of the middle-class.
In the 1980s, the first Mexican-American was elected to the Los Angeles City Council in over twenty years. A landmark lawsuit was also filed by the [[American Civil Liberties Union]] and the [[Mexican American Legal Defense and Educational Fund]], which argued "the Los Angeles Supervisors in 1981 adopted a plan that fragmented the Latino population into three districts, thus dividing their political power." The outcome of this litigation permitted a Mexican-American to win election to the [[Los Angeles County Board of Supervisors]], the first Mexican American to join that body in more than a century. [[Ben Fernandez]] an American politician, financial consultant and special ambassador ran for [[President of the United States]] three times, seriously in 1980 and with more perfunctory campaigns in 1984 and 1988, making him America's first major-party presidential contender of [[Hispanic and Latino Americans|Latin American]] origin.<ref name="Lodi">{{cite news|last=UPI|url=https://news.google.com/newspapers?id=V-AzAAAAIBAJ&pg=5338%2C3265641|title=Presidential race gets second entry|work=[[Lodi News-Sentinel]]|date=1978-11-30|access-date=2012-12-06}}</ref> Fernandez was born in [[Kansas]] to [[Mexico|Mexican]] parents who were [[Illegal immigration to the United States|illegal immigrants]]. At the same time and for similar reasons, [[Neoliberalism|neoliberal]] politician like [[Henry Cisneros]], the mayor of San Antonio, Texas who was a serious contender for the 1984 [[Democratic Party (United States)|Democratic Party]] vice-presidential nomination; [[Federico Peña|Federico Pena]] the mayor of Denver, Colorado; and Toney Anaya, former governor of New Mexico, emerged as the new voices of Mexican Americans political leadership.
==1990s== ===National politics=== [[File:President Clinton's Latino Appointees.png|thumb|left|upright|[[Bill Clinton|President Clinton]] with his Latino political appointees]]
In the final two years of the [[Presidency of George H. W. Bush|Bush administration]], Congress made several important adjustments to U.S. immigration law. The ''[[Immigration Act of 1990]]'' made marrying to evade immigration laws, voting in federal elections as a non-citizen, and "falsely claiming citizenship" to attain employment criminal violations which would lead to incarceration and [[deportation]].<ref name="Governing Immigration Through Crime"/> The bill also created the [[temporary protected status]] (TPS visa), lifted the English testing process for naturalization for permanent residents over 55, and eliminated exclusion of homosexuals as "[[Paraphilia|sexual deviants]]".<ref>{{Cite web | url=https://www.congress.gov/bill/101st-congress/senate-bill/358 | title=S.358 - 101st Congress (1989-1990): Immigration Act of 1990| date=29 November 1990}}</ref><ref name=Boundless>{{cite web|title=The Immigration Act of 1990|url=https://www.boundless.com/u-s-history/textbooks/boundless-u-s-history-textbook/the-challenges-of-globalization-and-the-coming-century-after-1989-31/america-s-emerging-culture-230/the-immigration-act-of-1990-1311-6480/|publisher=Boundless|access-date=September 30, 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304092310/https://www.boundless.com/u-s-history/textbooks/boundless-u-s-history-textbook/the-challenges-of-globalization-and-the-coming-century-after-1989-31/america-s-emerging-culture-230/the-immigration-act-of-1990-1311-6480/|archive-date=March 4, 2016|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="Homosexual liberation">{{cite web|last1=Davis|first1=Tracy|title=Opening the Doors of Immigration: Sexual Orientation and Asylum in the United States|url=http://www.wcl.american.edu/hrbrief/v6i3/immigration.htm|publisher=Washington College of Law|access-date=September 29, 2014|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20020822211541/http://www.wcl.american.edu/hrbrief/v6i3/immigration.htm|archive-date=August 22, 2002}}</ref>
During the [[Presidency of Bill Clinton|Clinton administration]], several cabinet-level Mexican Americans served under President [[Bill Clinton]]. These included [[Henry Cisneros]] ([[United States Department of Housing and Urban Development|Department of Housing and Urban Development]]), [[Federico Peña]] ([[United States Department of Transportation|Department of Transportation]] and [[United States Department of Energy|Department of Energy]]), and [[Bill Richardson]] ([[List of ambassadors of the United States to the United Nations|U.S. Ambassador to the United Nations]] and [[United States Department of Energy|Department of Energy]]).<ref>{{cite web |last1=Varela |first1=Julio Ricardo |title=Latinos in the US Cabinet: A Report Card |url=https://www.latinousa.org/2015/11/03/latinos-in-the-us-cabinet-a-report-card/ |website=Latino USA |date=3 November 2015 |publisher=National Public Radio |access-date=8 January 2021}}</ref> Clinton was widely praised for his overall cabinet selections, which were significantly more diverse than prior administrations.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Locin |first1=Mitchel |title=Clinton Finishes Cabinet of Diversity |url=https://www.chicagotribune.com/news/ct-xpm-1992-12-25-9204270273-story.html |website=Chicago Tribune |date=25 December 1992 |access-date=8 January 2021}}</ref> During his presidency, however, Clinton's legacy with both Mexico and the Mexican American community was more mixed. His passage of the [[Violent Crime Control and Law Enforcement Act|1994 Crime Bill]] is recognized as disproportionately targeting and incarcerating young Mexican American and African American men.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Groenke |first1=Gabriela |title=Law and Society: The Criminalization of Latinx in the United States |url=https://academicworks.cuny.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=4456&context=gc_etds |website=The Graduate Center |publisher=City University of New York |access-date=8 January 2021}}</ref> Further, his policy of pushing [[Washington Consensus]] policies on Mexico led to the [[Mexican peso crisis|1994 Mexican Peso Crisis]],<ref>{{cite web |last1=Guíllen |first1=Arturo |title=Mexico, an example of the anti-development policies of the Washington Consensus |url=https://www.scielo.br/pdf/ea/v26n75/en_05.pdf |website=Scientific Electronic Library Online |publisher=SciELO Brasil |access-date=8 January 2021}}</ref> which decimated the Mexican working class and increased migration to the United States. Finally, his signing of the [[North American Free Trade Agreement|NAFTA]] is recognized for its harmful effect on both nation's working classes, and the expansion of [[maquiladora]]s in states like [[Baja California]], [[Chihuahua (state)|Chihuahua]], and [[Coahuila]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Ebner |first1=Nina |last2=Crossa |first2=Mateo |title=Maquiladoras and the Exploitation of Migrants on the Border |url=https://nacla.org/news/2019/10/03/maquiladores-exploitation-migrants-border |website=The North American Congress on Latin America |access-date=8 January 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Cypher |first1=James |title=Mexico Since NAFTA: Elite Delusions and the Reality of Decline |url=https://newlaborforum.cuny.edu/2011/10/05/mexico-since-nafta-elite-delusions-and-the-reality-of-decline/ |website=New Labor Forum |date=5 October 2011 |publisher=City University of New York |access-date=8 January 2021}}</ref>
===Gang culture=== {{multiple image | direction = vertical | width = 165 | footer = The 1990s saw an explosion of [[Chicano]] [[gang activity]] in cities such as [[Los Angeles]], [[San Antonio, Texas|San Antonio]], and [[Chicago]]. | image1 = | alt1 = | caption1 = | image2 = 38th street gang photo.jpg | alt2 = | caption2 = | image3 = Surenos tag.jpg | alt3 = }}
In the 1990s, Chicano youth gang involvement continued to rise across the country, as the result of both the expansion of [[cocaine]] markets and widespread socioeconomic changes in the United States.<ref name="Youth Gangs, Drugs, and Violence">{{cite web |title=The Youth Gangs, Drugs, and Violence Connection |url=https://www.ncjrs.gov/pdffiles1/93920.pdf |website=Office of Juvenile Justice and Delinquency Prevention |publisher=U.S. Department of Justice |access-date=5 January 2021}}</ref> [[Manufacturing]] jobs had been [[Deindustrialisation by country#United States|decreasing]] across the U.S. for over twenty years, and as the American economy turned increasingly turned toward the [[Technology|technological]] and [[Service economy|service]] industries, unemployment rates among young men of color soared in [[urban area]]s.<ref name="Youth Gangs, Drugs, and Violence"/> Unable to find work in this changing economy, drug markets became the only source of survival for these displaced workers, as the rising prices for [[crack cocaine]] became a way for desperate youth to make money.<ref name="Youth Gangs, Drugs, and Violence"/> Over time, however, drug markets were monopolized by [[Gangs in the United States|organized gangs]], which actively recruited increasingly younger African American and Chicano youth.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Vigil |first1=James Diego |title=Street Baptism: Chicano Gang Initiation |journal=Human Organization |date=Summer 1996 |volume=55 |issue=2 |pages=149–153 |doi=10.17730/humo.55.2.3358547x86552mg4 |jstor=44127157 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/44127157 |access-date=7 January 2021|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Traditional American pathways away from a "[[Gangster#United States|gangster lifestyle]]", such as marriage, family, and stable employment, were largely unavailable to these youth, and in many black, Chicano, and immigrant communities, gang influence emerged as "a dominant informal control and socialization force".<ref name="Youth Gangs, Drugs, and Violence"/>
Chicano youth gang activity in the 1990s rose notably in [[Los Angeles]] and [[Chicago]], two of the cities with the nation's highest numbers of Mexican Americans,<ref>{{cite web |title=Youth Gang Homicides in the 1990's |url=https://www.ncjrs.gov/pdffiles1/ojjdp/fs200103.pdf |website=Office of Juvenile Justice and Juvenile Delinquency |publisher=U.S. Department of Justice |access-date=5 January 2021}}</ref> but gang activity rose in almost every U.S. city, including throughout [[Texas]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Pierce |first1=Ellise |title=the Other Gang Wars |url=https://www.dmagazine.com/publications/d-magazine/1992/october/the-other-gang-wars/ |website=[[D Magazine]] |date=October 1992 |access-date=6 January 2021}}</ref> The political response to gangs such as the [[Mexican Mafia]] and [[38th Street gang|38th Street]] was a dramatic escalation of the [[Presidency of Ronald Reagan|Reagan administration]]'s [[War on drugs|War on Drugs]]. Racialized fears of black and brown "[[Superpredator theory|superpredators]]" resulted in [[Law and order (politics)|tough-on-crime]] policies in the 1990s, culminating in the infamous ''[[Violent Crime Control and Law Enforcement Act]]'' of 1994.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Rodriguez |first1=Joseph |title=The Gangs of L.A. |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2017/03/18/opinion/sunday/the-gangs-of-la.html |website=The New York Times |date=18 March 2017 |access-date=5 January 2021}}</ref> The law provided local funding to hire over 100,000 new [[police officer]]s; created grant programs to incentivize [[Drug-related crime|drug-related arrests]]; gave states funds to build massive new [[Incarceration in the United States|prison facilities]]; and disproportionately criminalized [[crack cocaine]] over [[cocaine]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Violent Crime Control and Law Enforcement Act of 1994 |url=https://www.ncjrs.gov |website=National Criminal Justice Reference Service |publisher=U.S. Department of Justice |access-date=7 January 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Lopez |first1=German |title=The controversial 1994 crime law that Joe Biden helped write, explained |url=https://www.vox.com/policy-and-politics/2019/6/20/18677998/joe-biden-1994-crime-bill-law-mass-incarceration |website=VOX |date=20 June 2019 |access-date=7 January 2021}}</ref> Some have argued this bill led directly to the U.S.' rise of [[Incarceration in the United States|mass incarceration]].<ref>{{cite web |title=The War on Drugs and Mass Incarceration |url=https://guides.ll.georgetown.edu/c.php?g=592919&p=4172706 |website=Georgetown University Law Library |publisher=Georgetown University |access-date=7 January 2021}}</ref>
The ramifications of [[Militarization of police|police militarization]] were extremely severe, particularly in cities like Los Angeles. In the late-80s, [[Los Angeles Police Department|LAPD]] had responded to gang violence with a series of extremely violent community raids, including [[Operation Hammer (1987)|Operation Hammer]], which was organized under the [[Community Resources Against Street Hoodlums]] (CRASH).<ref>{{cite web |last1=Mitchell |first1=John L. |title=The Raid That Still Haunts L.A. |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2001-mar-14-mn-37553-story.html |website=Los Angeles Times |date=14 March 2001 |access-date=7 January 2021}}</ref> In the 1990s, as many as 2,000 people a year were killed in [[Los Angeles County, California|Los Angeles County]] due to gang violence.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Becerra |first1=Hector |title=When childhood innocence and gang violence lived side by side in Boyle Heights |url=https://www.latimes.com/local/california/la-me-gang-history-20160512-story.html |website=Los Angeles Times |date=12 May 2016 |access-date=5 January 2021}}</ref> In September 1993, a mass meeting in [[Elysian Park]] organized by [[Mexican Mafia|La Eme]] was called to put an end to "violence between Mexicans", however such truces were not usually long-lasting.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Hayden |first1=Tom |title=Street Wars: Gangs and the Future of Violence |date=May 1, 2004 |publisher=The New Press |location=New York |isbn=978-1565848764 |edition=1}}</ref> So, after the passage of the 1994 Crime Bill, the LAPD responded by ramping up its anti-gang tactics, including home and apartment raids, street sweeps, and [[Gang injunction|civil gang injunctions]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Gangs in Los Angeles |url=http://www.justicepolicy.org/images/upload/07-07_ch3_gangwars_gc-ps-ac-jj.pdf |website=Justice Policy Institute |access-date=5 January 2021 |archive-date=4 September 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200904030925/http://www.justicepolicy.org/images/upload/07-07_Ch3_GangWars_GC-PS-AC-JJ.pdf |url-status=dead }}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Grogger |first1=Jeffrey |title=The Effects Of Civil Gang Injunctions On Reported Violent Crime: Evidence From Los Angeles County |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/24101083 |website=Journal of Law and Economics |publisher=The University of Chicago |access-date=7 January 2021}}</ref>
===Union campaigns=== {{See also|Justice for Janitors}}
In the late 1980s and early 1990s, the [[Service Employees International Union]] (SEIU) initiated a series of "[[Justice for Janitors]]" campaigns to unionize U.S. [[janitor]]s.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Musynske |first1=Gavin |title=Los Angleles<!--sic--> Justice for Janitors campaign for economic justice at Century City, 1989-1990 |url=https://nvdatabase.swarthmore.edu/content/los-angleles-justice-janitors-campaign-economic-justice-century-city-1989-1990 |website=Global Nonviolent Action Database |publisher=Swarthmore University |access-date=7 January 2021}}</ref> At a march in [[Los Angeles]] on June 15, 1990, the striking janitors – many of whom were undocumented Mexican and Central American immigrants – were attacked by the Los Angeles Police Department.<ref>{{cite web |title=Justice for Janitors |url=https://www.labor.ucla.edu/what-we-do/research-tools/campaigns-and-research/justice-for-janitors/ |website=UCLA Labor Center |publisher=University of California, Los Angeles |access-date=7 January 2021 |archive-date=2 February 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210202181853/https://www.labor.ucla.edu/what-we-do/research-tools/campaigns-and-research/justice-for-janitors/ |url-status=dead }}</ref> The police at first claimed they were acting in "self defense", but TV news footage aired later showed the police violently beating unarmed and peaceful strikers.<ref name="25 Years Later">{{cite magazine |last1=Lerner |first1=Stephen |last2=Shaffer |first2=Jono |title=25 Years Later: Lessons From the Organizers of Justice for Janitors |url=https://www.thenation.com/article/archive/25-years-later-lessons-from-the-organizers-of-justice-for-janitors/ |website=The Nation |date=16 June 2015 |access-date=7 January 2021}}</ref> 60 strikers were jailed, 38 were hospitalized, and two women [[Miscarriage|miscarried]].<ref name="25 Years Later"/><ref>{{cite web |last1=Chávez |first1=Citlalli |title=Justice for Janitors Day Marks 25th Anniversary in L.A. |url=https://www.kcet.org/shows/departures/justice-for-janitors-day-marks-25th-anniversary-in-la |website=KCET |date=16 June 2015 |publisher=Public Broadcasting Services |access-date=7 January 2021}}</ref> One striker told reporters, "What they did to us today in front of the TV cameras is the way the police treat us every day", and another woman stated, "I wasn't robbing a bank or selling drugs, I'm simply asking for an increase in pay, but the police beat us as if we were garbage."<ref name="25 Years Later"/> Public outrage followed coverage of the event, and the janitors won the union, which doubled their pay and earned them benefits. The strike also inspired janitors in other parts of the country, including [[Houston]], where jailed strikers were held on $20 million dollar bail for "[[civil disobedience]]".<ref name="25 Years Later"/><ref>{{cite web |last1=Carter |first1=Elizabeth |title=The Influence of Janitors on Fair Labor |url=https://www.countryclubjanitorial.com/blog/the-influence-of-janitors-on-fair-labor |website=Country Club Janitorial |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220328062439/https://www.countryclubjanitorial.com/blog/the-influence-of-janitors-on-fair-labor |access-date=7 January 2021|archive-date=2022-03-28 }}</ref> Outrage over the incident in Houston was global, with workers across the U.S. striking in support, and allies in Europe occupying buildings in protest.<ref name="25 Years Later"/> The Houston strike was successful, and pushed [[pension fund]] trustees to develop "responsible contractor" procedures.<ref name="25 Years Later"/><ref>{{cite web |last1=Sonn |first1=Paul K. |last2=Gebreselassie |first2=Tsedeye |title=The Road To Responsible Contracting: Lessons from States and Cities for Ensuring That Federal Contracting Delivers Good Jobs and Quality Services |url=https://www.inthepublicinterest.org/wp-content/uploads/The_Road_to_Responsible_Contracting.pdf |website=National Employment Law Project |access-date=7 January 2021}}</ref> The 2000 film ''[[Bread and Roses (2000 film)|Bread and Roses]]'' was based on the Los Angeles campaign.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Loach |first1=Ken |title=Bread And Roses -
