Trailer |url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IrDpd4sCswY | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120506003219/http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IrDpd4sCswY&gl=US&hl=en| archive-date=2012-05-06 | url-status=dead|website=Ken Loach Films |access-date=7 January 2021}}</ref> In the mid-90s, the [[Industrial Workers of the World]] (IWW) also sought to organize Mexican workers, including [[Truck driver|troqueros]] and [[Taxicab|taxi drivers]] in LA.<ref>{{cite web |title=Are The Truckers Independent Contractors? |url=https://archive.iww.org/history/campaigns/stocktontroqueros/5/ |website=Industrial Workers of the World |access-date=7 January 2021}}</ref>
===Los Angeles uprising=== {{See also|1992 Los Angeles riots}} {{multiple image | direction = vertical | width = 165 | footer = 51% of people arrested during the [[1992 Los Angeles riots|1992 Los Angeles uprising]] were Latino. Many of them were Mexican and Central American immigrants living in [[South Los Angeles]]. | image1 = LA Riots - aftermath (159598182).jpg | alt1 = | caption1 = | image2 = Los Angeles Riots, 1992 (17093440842).jpg | alt2 = | caption2 = | image3 = Los Angeles Riots, 1992 (16908738909).jpg | alt3 = }}
The [[1992 Los Angeles riots|1992 Los Angeles uprising]] was an uprising against [[police brutality]] that occurred in [[Los Angeles County, California|Los Angeles County]] in April and May 1992.<ref name="LA Uprising">{{cite book |editor-last=Danver |editor-first=Steven L. |title=Revolts, Protests, Demonstrations, and Rebellions in American History: An Encyclopedia, Volume 3 |date=2011 |publisher=ABC-CLIO |location=Santa Barbara, Calif. |isbn=978-1-59884-222-7 |pages=1095–1100 |chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=eSncBZ9E14UC&q=%22los+angeles+uprising%22 |chapter=Los Angeles Uprising (1992)}}</ref> Unrest began in [[South Los Angeles|South Central Los Angeles]] on April 29, after a [[Rodney King#Los Angeles riots and the aftermath|trial jury acquitted four officers]] of the [[Los Angeles Police Department]] (LAPD) for usage of excessive force in the [[arrest]] and beating of [[Rodney King]], which had been [[videotape]]d and widely viewed in [[Television|TV]] [[Broadcasting|broadcasts]]. Though much of the [[Mass media in the United States|media coverage]] surrounding the events focused on constructing either a "[[African Americans|black]] vs. [[White Americans|white]]" or a "black vs. [[Asian Americans|Asian]]" narrative (due to the tensions caused by the murder of [[Killing of Latasha Harlins|Latasha Harlins]]),<ref>{{cite web |last1=Garcia |first1=Sid |title=South L.A. vigil recalls Latasha Harlins shooting that fueled 1992 LA riots |url=https://abc7.com/racial-tensions-latasha-harlins-rodney-king-1992-los-angeles-riots/1249486/ |website=ABC News |date=17 March 2016 |access-date=30 March 2021}}</ref> the majority of people arrested during the uprising were [[Latino Americans|Latino]].<ref>{{cite news |last1=Sastry |first1=Anjali |last2=Grigsby Bates |first2=Karen |title=When LA Erupted In Anger: A Look Back At The Rodney King Riots |url=https://www.npr.org/2017/04/26/524744989/when-la-erupted-in-anger-a-look-back-at-the-rodney-king-riots |website=WSKG |date=26 April 2017 |publisher=National Public Radio |access-date=5 January 2021}}</ref> Analysis of charges in the riot's peak days showed 51% of defendants were Latino and 36% were black, and, of the 58 total people killed during the riots, more than one-third were Latino.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Lieberman |first1=Paul |title=51% of Riot Arrests Were Latino, Study Says : Unrest: RAND analysis of court cases finds they were mostly young men. The figures are open to many interpretations, experts note. |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1992-06-18-me-734-story.html |website=Los Angeles Times |date=18 June 1992 |access-date=5 January 2021}}</ref><ref name="After the Riots">{{cite news |last1=Newman |first1=Maria |title=After the Riots: Riots Put Focus on Hispanic Growth and Problems in South-Central Area |url=https://www.nytimes.com/1992/05/11/us/after-riots-riots-put-focus-hispanic-growth-problems-south-central-area.html |website=The New York Times |date=11 May 1992 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201122102020/https://www.nytimes.com/1992/05/11/us/after-riots-riots-put-focus-hispanic-growth-problems-south-central-area.html |access-date=5 January 2021|archive-date=2020-11-22 }}</ref> [[Stanford University]] professor [[Joan Petersilia]] stated about the uprising, "This was clearly not a black riot. It was a minority riot."<ref>{{cite web |last1=Wang |first1=Oliver |title=The Essential L.A. Riots Mixtape |url=https://www.kcet.org/shows/artbound/the-essential-la-riots-mixtape |website=KCET |date=17 May 2012 |publisher=Public Broadcasting Service |access-date=7 January 2021}}</ref>
The majority of the Latino residents of [[South Los Angeles|South Central Los Angeles]] in the early 1990s were recent Mexican immigrants and [[Central America]]ns.<ref name="After the Riots"/> As a result, when the city's Latino leadership met during the uprising to discuss resolution strategies, the disconnect between LA's Mexican-American establishment and South LA's recent migrant community was more fully understood.<ref name="After the Riots"/> [[Los Angeles County, California|Los Angeles County]]'s only Latino supervisor, [[Gloria Molina]], told ''[[The New York Times]]'' that in the days when Los Angeles was burning, she received multiple calls from Mexican American constituents urging her to denounce South Central's Mexican population. Molina stated, "They would say, 'Well, Gloria, it wasn't us doing the looting and the burning. It was those immigrants.{{'"}}<ref name="After the Riots"/> Molina went further and stated, "They wanted me to denounce them. But I say, let's not let that divide us."<ref name="After the Riots"/>
Even white journalists, such as the ''[[Los Angeles Times]]'' reporter [[Jack Miles]], noted the tension between the South and East LA Latino communities.<ref name="Blacks vs. Browns">{{cite web |last1=Miles |first1=Jack |title=Blacks vs. Browns |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/magazine/archive/1992/10/blacks-vs-browns/306655/ |website=The Atlantic |date=October 1992 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201207140356/https://www.theatlantic.com/magazine/archive/1992/10/blacks-vs-browns/306655/ |access-date=5 January 2021|archive-date=2020-12-07 }}</ref> He wrote, the "law-abiding Mexican-American community" of [[East Los Angeles, California|East Los Angeles]] resented being associated with the Latinos of South Los Angeles, and that the incident marked the beginning of a Mexican American "anti-immigrant stance".<ref name="Blacks vs. Browns"/> In the years after the uprising, some journalists focused on the long-term interracial scars left by the racial uprising,<ref>{{cite news |last1=Cannon |first1=Lou |title=Scars Remain Five Years After Los Angeles Riots |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/wp-srv/national/longterm/lariots/lariots.htm |newspaper=The Washington Post |access-date=5 January 2021}}</ref> while others focused on the multiple intra-ethnic meanings the uprising held for Latino communities in Los Angeles.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Berestein Rojas |first1=Leslie |title='It wasn't just about Rodney King': Perspectives on the riots, 20 years later |url=https://www.scpr.org/blogs/multiamerican/2012/04/25/8220/it-wasnt-just-about-rodney-king-perspectives-on-th/ |website=SCPR |date=25 April 2012 |publisher=National Public Radio |access-date=7 January 2021}}</ref> Historians have also explained some of the reasons why there was so much Latino participation, including: the [[police brutality|brutality]] that Latinos also experienced from the [[Los Angeles Police Department|LAPD]]; the near-constant threat of [[Deportation and removal from the United States|deportation]]; and the [[Neoliberalism|neoliberal]] defunding of inner-city municipal services.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Reft |first1=Ryan |title=Policing a Global City: Multiculturalism, Immigration and the 1992 Uprising |url=https://www.kcet.org/shows/lost-la/policing-a-global-city-multiculturalism-immigration-and-the-1992-uprising |website=KCET |date=2 June 2020 |publisher=Public Broadcasting Service |access-date=7 January 2021}}</ref>
===Proposition 187=== {{Main article|1994 California Proposition 187}} [[File:1994 No On 187 March Kings Canyon Rd Fresno.jpg|thumb|left|upright|A young member of the [[Brown Berets]] at a [[Fresno, California|Fresno]] march for the "No on Proposition 187" campaign.]]
[[1994 California Proposition 187|Proposition 187]] (also known as the ''Save Our State'' (SOS) initiative) was a 1994 [[Initiatives and referendums in the United States|ballot initiative]] to establish a state-run citizenship screening system and prohibit [[Illegal immigration|undocumented immigrants]] from using non-emergency health care, public schooling, and other services in the State of [[California]].<ref>{{cite news |last1=Denkmann |first1=Libby |title=California's Prop 187 Vote Damaged GOP Relations With Immigrants |url=https://www.npr.org/2019/11/08/777466912/californias-prop-187-vote-damaged-gop-relations-with-immigrants |website=WSKG |publisher=National Public Radio |access-date=7 January 2021}}</ref> The bill was widely opposed by the state's Latino communities, though some Mexican Americans did express support for the measure.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Newton |first1=Lina Y. |title=Why Some Latinos Supported Proposition 187: Testing Economic Threat and Cultural Identity Hypotheses |journal=Social Science Quarterly |date=Spring 2000 |volume=81 |issue=1 |pages=180–193 |jstor=42864375 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/42864375 |access-date=7 January 2021}}</ref> In the lead-up to the November vote, there were widespread "No on 187" protests throughout California, as activists urged that a full denial of basic rights to undocumented people would be detrimental to the state.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Hinojosa Ojeda |first1=Raul |title=Manifest Destiny's Last Stand? Prop 187 and the Future of North American Labor and Civil Rights |url=https://www.naid.ucla.edu/uploads/4/2/1/9/4219226/small_manifest_destinys_last_stand__prop_187_and_the_future_of_north_american_labor_and_human_rights.pdf |website=UCLA North American Integration and Development Center |publisher=University of California, Los Angeles |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210415075214/https://www.naid.ucla.edu/uploads/4/2/1/9/4219226/small_manifest_destinys_last_stand__prop_187_and_the_future_of_north_american_labor_and_human_rights.pdf |access-date=8 January 2021|archive-date=2021-04-15 }}</ref> In October 1994, an estimated seventy thousand people marched in [[Los Angeles]] to protest Prop. 187 in one of the [[List of protests in the United States by size#List|largest protests]] in U.S. history.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Lopez |first1=Robert J. |last2=McDonnell |first2=Patrick J. |title=L.A. March Against Prop. 187 Draws 70,000 : Immigration: Protesters condemn Wilson for backing initiative that they say promotes 'racism, scapegoating.' |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1994-10-17-mn-51339-story.html |website=Los Angeles Times |date=17 October 1994 |access-date=8 January 2021}}</ref> The political atmosphere in California at the time, however, was extremely [[Xenophobia|xenophobic]],<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Alvarez |first1=R. Michael |last2=Butterfield |first2=Tara L. |title=The Resurgence of Nativism in California? The Case of Proposition 187 and Illegal Immigration |journal=Social Science Quarterly |date=Spring 2000 |volume=81 |issue=1 |pages=167–179 |jstor=42864374 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/42864374 |access-date=8 January 2021}}</ref> and coverage of the protest focused on the large number of Latino, Mexican, and Mexican American participants and especially expressed outrage at the presence of [[Flag of Mexico|Mexican flags]] at the protest.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Gutiérrez |first1=David G. |title=Migration, Emergent Ethnicity, and the 'Third Space': The Shifting Politics of Nationalism in Greater Mexico |journal=The Journal of American History |date=Fall 1999 |volume=86 |issue=2 |pages=481–517 |doi=10.2307/2567042 |jstor=2567042 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/2567042 |access-date=8 January 2021|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Proposition 187 passed with 58% of the vote.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Lustig |first1=Jeff |last2=Walker |first2=Dick |title=No Way Out: Immigrants and the New California |url=http://geog.berkeley.edu/PeopleHistory/faculty/R_Walker/Walker_1.html |website=Campus Coalition for Human Rights and Social Justice |publisher=University of California, Berkeley |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180513150910/http://geog.berkeley.edu/PeopleHistory/faculty/R_Walker/Walker_1.html |access-date=8 January 2021|archive-date=2018-05-13 }}</ref> Shortly after the proposition's passage, U.S. District Court Judge [[Mariana Pfaelzer|Mariana R. Pfaelzer]] in Los Angeles ruled that preventing undocumented children from attending K-12 schools was [[Judicial review in the United States|unconstitutional]] and prevented the implementation of most of the measure's other provisions.<ref>{{cite web |title=California: Proposition 187 Unconstitutional |url=https://migration.ucdavis.edu/mn/more.php?id=1391 |website=Migration News |publisher=University of California, Davis |access-date=8 January 2021}}</ref> Proposition 187, though overturned, significantly eroded Mexican American and Latino support for the [[California Republican Party]].<ref>{{citation |mode=cs1 |last1=Monogan III |first1=James E. |title=Immigration Politics and Partisan Realignment |year=2012 |publisher=Pennsylvania State University |citeseerx=10.1.1.675.3121 }}</ref> In many ways, however, its basic tenets shaped the future of American debates regarding the rights of undocumented people in the U.S.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Silva |first1=Andrea |title=How California's Prop. 187 is still shaping immigration policy — 25 years after it passed |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2019/11/25/how-californias-prop-is-still-shaping-immigration-policy-years-after-it-passed/ |newspaper=The Washington Post |access-date=8 January 2021}}</ref> Culturally, the measure also had a strong impact on the community. In ''[[Selenidad]]'' (2009), the poet [[Deborah Paredez]] connected the collective trauma of the 1995 death of [[Selena]] to the community's response to the measure's initial passage, writing, "Selena's death galvanized Latino efforts to publicly mourn collective tragedies (such as approved anti-Latino legislation in California, Proposition 187 and Proposition 229) and to envision a brighter future."<ref>{{cite book |last1=Paredez |first1=Deborah |title=Selenidad: Selena, Latinos, and the Performance of Memory |date=July 22, 2009 |publisher=Duke University Press |location=Durham |isbn=9780822390893 |page=9 |edition=1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=E0ahwKxObQoC |access-date=11 March 2021}}</ref>
==Early 2000s== [[File:O'ConnorGonzalesCloseUp.jpg|thumb|right|upright|[[List of justices of the Supreme Court of the United States|Justice]] [[Sandra Day O'Connor]] presents [[Alberto Gonzales]] to the audience after swearing him in as [[United States Attorney General|Attorney General]], as Mrs. Gonzales looks on.]]
The 2000 Census showed that the foreign-born population of the U.S. increased by 11.3 million people in the 1990s, and Mexican immigrants accounted for 43% of that growth.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.sph.sc.edu/cli/pdfs/final_final[1].pdf|title=MEXICAN IMMIGRANTS IN SOUTH CAROLINA: A PROFILE|website=Sph.sc.edu|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070206012431/http://www.sph.sc.edu/cli/pdfs/final_final[1].pdf |access-date=16 January 2018|archive-date=2007-02-06 }}</ref> The region which had the fastest-growing immigrant population was the [[Southern United States|Southeast]], where many Mexicans who found work in construction, as migrant agricultural laborers, and in textile mills and chicken processing plants. The Latino populations of [[Georgia (U.S. state)|Georgia]], [[North Carolina|North]] and [[South Carolina]], and [[Arkansas]] increased between 300 and 400 per cent from 1990 to 2000.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.census.gov/population/www/socdemo/hispanic/hispanic.html|title=US Census Bureau: Page has moved|website=Census.gov|access-date=16 January 2018}}</ref>
A major focus of Chicano activists in the 21st century has been to advance the representation of Chicanos in all American mainstream media.<ref>[http://mexican-american.org/chicano-movement/index.html "The Chicano Movement Today: El Poder de Hoy" <!-- Bot generated title -->] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120425075943/http://mexican-american.org/chicano-movement/index.html |date=2012-04-25 }} - Xcano Media, Los Angeles</ref> Criticism of the American mainstream news media and U.S. educational institutions by Chicano activists has been particularly harsh in recent years subsequent to the massive displays of support for immigrant rights such as that seen during La Gran Marcha<ref>[http://www.granmarcha.org "GranMarcha.org"<!-- Bot generated title -->] - Commemorative site for La Gran Marcha, March 25, 2006,</ref> (The Great March) on March 25, 2006 in Los Angeles. As of today, this self-proclaimed "largest march in U.S. history" which was primarily organized by Mexican American organizations, Chicano activists, and fueled through a large network of active Internet users, L.A. Spanish language television, and Spanish language news radio coverage, is still virtually ignored by American mainstream (English language) news media and all textbooks of the American educational system.
After the increased border security following the 9-11 attacks in 2001, the back-and-forth pattern became dangerous. People kept coming north, but they stayed in the U.S. and sent money home every month. Locked into the American economy year-round, millions of these undocumented workers moved out of season agricultural jobs into year-round jobs in restaurants, hotels, construction, landscaping and semiskilled factory work, such as meatpacking. Most paid federal social security taxes into imaginary accounts (and thus were not eligible for benefits.) Few had high enough incomes to pay federal or state income taxes, but all paid local and state sales taxes on their purchases as well as local property taxes (via their rent payments to landlords). By 2007 there were 12  million or so undocumented workers in the U.S.
[[File:Hilda_Solis_Secrétaire_au_travail.jpg|thumb|left|upright|[[Hilda Solis]] in February 2009, becoming the first Latina to serve in the [[Cabinet of the United States|U.S. Cabinet]]]] In 2005, [[Antonio Villaraigosa]] was elected mayor of [[Los Angeles]], the first Latino in 130 years to hold the seat. [[Eric Garcetti]] became the second consecutive Mexican American mayor.
Mexican-Americans tend to vote Democratic (in 1960, the [[John F. Kennedy]] presidential campaign boosted the Mexican American vote to over 80% for Kennedy). However, Mexican Americans in recent decades had a low turnout on election day. In 1984, 37 percent of [[Latino Americans]] voted for [[Ronald Reagan]] and [[George W. Bush]] targeted Latinos and won 35% of their votes in 2000, and 40% in 2004 and the fact that his brother [[Jeb Bush]] is married to a Mexican woman ([[Columba Bush]]).
Voters have elected a number of governors of Mexican descent in the Southwest, include [[Ezequiel Cabeza De Baca]], [[Octaviano Ambrosio Larrazolo]], [[Jerry Apodaca]], [[Toney Anaya]], [[Bill Richardson]], and [[Raúl Héctor Castro]] in Arizona. [[Cruz Bustamante]] was the first Democratic lieutenant governor of California in 130 years from his election in 1999 to 2007, but Bustamante lost the gubernatorial election to Austrian-born actor [[Arnold Schwarzenegger]], who went on to be state governor. [[Romualdo Pacheco]] served as 12th governor of California and remains the only Latino governor in the state's history as part of the United States.
==2010s== ===Obama era=== ====Electoral politics==== [[File:Hispanic education SA-0032.jpg|thumb|right|upright=0.8|President Obama signed [[White House Initiative on Educational Excellence for Hispanics#Obama administration|Exec. Order 13555]], October 19, 2010]]
Mexican Americans made significant electoral strides during the [[Presidency of Barack Obama|Obama Era]]. In 2010, the Republican [[Brian Sandoval]] was elected governor of [[Nevada]]; he was the first Latino to ever do so.<ref>{{cite web |title=Brian Edward Sandoval – Republican |url=https://ag.nv.gov/uploadedFiles/agnvgov/Content/Bios/30-BrianSandoval_Bio_FINAL.pdf |website=Office of the Attorney General |publisher=State of Nevada |access-date=15 January 2021}}</ref> That same year, the Republican [[Susana Martinez]] became the first woman and Latina to be elected as governor of [[New Mexico]] and was the first Republican Latina governor in U.S. history.<ref>{{cite magazine |last1=Newton-Small |first1=Jay |title=Meet the First Hispanic Republican Female Governor |url=https://time.com/collection/american-voices-2017/4402950/susana-martinez/ |magazine=Time |access-date=15 January 2021}}</ref> Also in 2010, [[Joseph Garcia (American politician)|Joseph García]] was elected the first Latino [[List of lieutenant governors of Colorado|lieutenant governor of Colorado]] in the state's history.<ref>{{cite web|last=Moore |first=John |url=http://www.denverpost.com/election2012/ci_16506656 |title=Hickenlooper wins easily – The Denver Post |date=2 November 2010 |publisher= Denver Post |access-date=2020-04-07}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Bartels |first1=Lynn |title=Hickenlooper taps CSU-Pueblo's president as gubernatorial running mate; choice hailed |url=https://www.denverpost.com/2010/08/05/hickenlooper-taps-csu-pueblos-president-as-gubernatorial-running-mate-choice-hailed/ |website=Denver Post |date=5 August 2010 |access-date=15 January 2021}}</ref>
During the [[2012 United States presidential election]], President Obama ran against the former-governor of Massachusetts [[Mitt Romney]]. During the campaign, Romney sparked outrage amongst many Mexican and Mexican American officials when he suggested the economic disparities between the United States and Mexico were the result of the "hand of providence".<ref>{{cite web |last1=Amira |first1=Dan |title=Mitt Romney Insults Whole New International Populace |url=https://nymag.com/intelligencer/2012/07/mitt-romney-palestinian-israel.html |website=[[New York (magazine)|New York]] |date=30 July 2012 |access-date=15 January 2021}}</ref> [[Latino Americans|Latinos]] voted overwhelmingly for President Obama's re-election in 2012, when he earned as much as 71% of the community's vote.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Santos |first1=Fernanda |title=A Record Latino Turnout, Solidly Backing Obama |work=The New York Times |date=8 November 2012 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2012/11/08/us/politics/with-record-turnout-latinos-solidly-back-obama-and-wield-influence.html |access-date=15 January 2021}}</ref> In response to their overwhelming defeat, [[Reince Priebus]], the chair of the [[Republican National Committee]], ordered an "autopsy report" to assess deficiencies and paths forward for the party.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Walshe |first1=Shushannah |title=RNC Completes 'Autopsy' on 2012 Loss, Calls for Inclusion Not Policy Change |url=https://abcnews.go.com/Politics/OTUS/rnc-completes-autopsy-2012-loss-calls-inclusion-policy/story?id=18755809 |website=ABC News |access-date=15 January 2021}}</ref> The report called for greater inclusion of Mexican Americans, Latinos, and other minority groups, stating, "If Hispanic Americans hear that the GOP doesn't want them in the United States, they won't pay attention to our next sentence. It doesn't matter what we say about education, jobs or the economy; if Hispanics think that we do not want them here, they will close their ears to our policies."<ref>{{cite web |last1=Franke-Ruta |first1=Garance |title=What You Need to Read in the RNC Election-Autopsy Report |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/politics/archive/2013/03/what-you-need-to-read-in-the-rnc-election-autopsy-report/274112/ |website=The Atlantic |date=18 March 2013 |access-date=15 January 2021}}</ref> The report, however, was largely ignored by the GOP in the years ahead.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Stanage |first1=Niall |title=GOP front-runners defy 'autopsy' of party defeats |url=https://thehill.com/homenews/campaign/265835-gop-front-runners-defy-autopsy-of-party-defeats/ |website=The Hill |date=14 January 2016 |access-date=15 January 2021}}</ref>
====Immigration==== {{See also|Arizona SB 1070|Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals}} [[File:Immigration Reform Leaders Arrested 15.jpg|thumb|left|upright|Protest against [[Arizona SB 1070|SB1070]].]]
In the 2010s, [[Nativism (politics)|nativism]] in the United States increased, particularly in border states like [[Arizona]]. In 2010, the [[Arizona State Legislature]] proposed and passed the ''[[Arizona SB 1070|Support Our Law Enforcement and Safe Neighborhoods Act]]'' (SB 1070). Critics of the bill argued it was a fundmantally racist law designed to legalize [[racial profiling]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Newman |first1=Paige |title=Arizona's Anti-Immigration Law and the Pervasiveness of Racial Profiling |journal=Georgetown Immigration Law Journal |date=Spring 2017 |volume=31 |issue=3 |url=https://www.law.georgetown.edu/immigration-law-journal/in-print/volume-31-issue-3-spring-2017/arizonas-anti-immigration-law-and-the-pervasiveness-of-racial-profiling/ |access-date=11 January 2021}}</ref> In ''[[Arizona v. United States]]'' (2012), the [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]] overturned three provisions of the bill, including: requiring "legal" immigrants to carry registration documents at all time; allowing state police to arrest any individual for suspicion of being undocumented; and making it a crime for undocumented people to search for or hold jobs in the state.<ref name=wapo>{{cite news |title= Supreme Court Rejects Much of Arizona Immigration Law |first= Robert |last= Barnes |newspaper= The Washington Post |date= June 25, 2012 |url= https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/supreme-court-rules-on-arizona-immigration-law/2012/06/25/gJQA0Nrm1V_story.html?hpid=z1}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=Supreme Court mostly rejects Arizona immigration law; gov says 'heart' remains|url=https://edition.cnn.com/2012/06/25/politics/scotus-arizona-law/index.html|publisher=CNN|access-date=26 June 2012|author=Tom Cohen and Bill Mears|date=26 June 2012}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=At a glance: Supreme Court decision on Arizona's immigration law|url=https://edition.cnn.com/interactive/2012/06/us/scotus.immigration/index.html|publisher=CNN|access-date=26 June 2012}}</ref> Despite its overturning, the bill had negative social effects on the Mexican and Mexican American communities, particularly for children and youth.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Lopez |first1=Tomas |title=Left Back: The Impact of SB 1070 on Arizona's Youth |url=https://law.arizona.edu/sites/default/files/Left_Back%20Report.pdf |website=James E. Rogers College of Law |publisher=The University of Arizona |access-date=11 January 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Santos |first1=Carlos |last2=Menjívar |first2=Cecilia |chapter=Effects of SB 1070 on Children |title=Latino Politics and Arizona's Immigration Law SB 1070 |date=Spring 2013 |pages=79–92 |doi=10.1007/978-1-4614-0296-1_6 |isbn=978-1-4614-0295-4 |chapter-url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/289582940 |access-date=11 January 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Harvey-Mendoza |first1=Elizabeth |last2=Williams |first2=David R. |last3=Umaña-Taylor |first3=Adriana J. |last4=Toomey |first4=Russell B. |title=Impact of Arizona's SB 1070 Immigration Law on Utilization of Health Care and Public Assistance Among Mexican-Origin Adolescent Mothers and Their Mother Figures |journal=American Journal of Public Health |date=Fall 2014 |volume=104 |issue=1 |pages=S28-34 |doi=10.2105/AJPH.2013.301655 |pmid=24354823 |pmc=3924594 |url=https://scholar.harvard.edu/files/davidrwilliams/files/2014a.pdf |access-date=11 January 2021}}</ref> In the same year Arizona passed SB1070, it also passed [[Ethnic studies|Arizona House Bill 2281]], effectively barring [[Mexican American Studies Department Programs, Tucson Unified School District|Mexican American Studies]] (history and literature) from being taught in the state's public schools.<ref name=latimes>{{cite news|last1=Santa Cruz|first1=Nicole|title=Arizona bill targeting ethnic studies signed into law|url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2010-may-12-la-na-ethnic-studies-20100512-story.html|newspaper=Los Angeles Times|access-date=14 November 2014|date=2010-05-12}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Palos |first1=Ari Luis |title=Precious Knowledge |url=https://www.pbs.org/independentlens/documentaries/precious-knowledge/ |website=Independent Lens |publisher=Public Broadcasting Service |access-date=11 January 2021}}</ref>
[[File:Kamala Harris with Dreamers - 2017-12-06.jpg|thumb|right|upright|[[Kamala Harris]] with a group of [[Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals|DACA]]-recipients in 2017.]]
On June 15, 2012, President [[Barack Obama]] announced an [[Presidential memorandum|executive branch memorandum]] known as ''Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals'' (DACA).<ref>{{cite web |title=Remarks by the President on Immigration |url=https://obamawhitehouse.archives.gov/the-press-office/2012/06/15/remarks-president-immigration |website=Office of the Press Secretary |date=15 June 2012 |publisher=The White House |access-date=12 January 2021}}</ref> This change to [[Immigration to the United States|immigration policy]] allowed eligible undocumented individuals brought to the country as children to receive a renewable two-year period of [[deferred action]] from [[deportation]] and become eligible for a [[Employment authorization document|work permit]] in the U.S. (without providing a path to citizenship for recipients).<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Young|title=To Dream or Not to Dream: A Cost-Benefit Analysis of the Development, Relief, and Education for Alien Minors (Dream) Act|journal=Cornell Journal of Law and Public Policy|date=Fall 2006|volume=16|issue=1|pages=8, 9|url=https://scholarship.law.cornell.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1107&context=cjlpp|access-date=February 12, 2018|ref=CornJournal |quote=The DREAM Act is a bipartisan Congressional effort to allow certain undocumented students who were brought into the U.S. as a child the opportunity to attend college and eventually to become permanent residents and citizens of the United States.}}</ref> [[United States Citizenship and Immigration Services|U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services]] (USCIS) began accepting applications for the program on August 15, 2012, and in less than one year, over half a million people applied to the DACA program.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Prchal Svajlenka |first1=Nicole |last2=Singer |first2=Audrey |title=Immigration Facts: Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals |url=https://www.brookings.edu/research/immigration-facts-deferred-action-for-childhood-arrivals-daca/ |website=Brookings Institution |date=14 August 2013 |access-date=12 January 2021}}</ref> The next year, in 2013, a [[Bipartisanship|bi-partisan]] group of eight [[United States Senate|United States Senators]]—known as the [[Gang of Eight (immigration)|Gang of Eight]]—wrote the first draft of the ''[[Border Security, Economic Opportunity, and Immigration Modernization Act of 2013]]''.<ref>{{cite news|last=Cox|first=Ramsey|title=Schumer introduces comprehensive immigration reform bill|url=https://thehill.com/blogs/floor-action/senate/147871-schumer-introduces-comprehensive-immigration-reform-bill/|access-date=22 July 2013|newspaper=The Hill|date=April 17, 2013}}</ref> The bill would have provided a path to [[citizenship]] for millions of undocumented Americans.<ref>{{cite web|title=8 U.S. Code § 1101 - Definitions|url=https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/8/1101|website=Cornell Law School, Legal Information Institute|access-date=2 November 2017}}</ref> It passed the Senate with a strong majority—68–32, with 14 Republicans joining all Democrats; however, the [[United States House of Representatives]] under Speaker [[John Boehner]] did not act on the bill, and it expired at the end of the [[113th United States Congress|113th Congress]].<ref name="NakamuraO'Keefe">[[David Nakamura]] & Ed O'Keefe, [https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/post-politics/wp/2014/06/26/timeline-the-rise-and-fall-of-immigration-reform/ Timeline: The rise and fall of immigration reform], ''Washington Post'' (June 26, 2014).</ref>
[[File:ICE.XCheckII.3cops1arrest.jpg|thumb|left|upright|Young man apprehended by [[U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement|immigration]] officers.]]
During his time in office, President Obama was known in the Mexican American community as the "Deporter in Chief".<ref>{{cite news |last1=Tau |first1=Byron |title=Univision Anchor to Obama: 'You Destroyed Many Families' |url=https://blogs.wsj.com/washwire/2014/12/10/univision-anchor-obama-destroyed-many-families/ |website=[[The Wall Street Journal]] |date=10 December 2014 |access-date=12 January 2021}}</ref> Between 2009 and 2015, his administration removed more than 2.5 million people from the country, though some analysts claimed these numbers were "misleading" due to the way they were calculated.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Marshall |first1=Serena |title=Obama Has Deported More People Than Any Other President |url=https://abcnews.go.com/Politics/obamas-deportation-policy-numbers/story?id=41715661 |website=ABC News |access-date=12 January 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Bennett |first1=Brian |title=High deportation figures are misleading |url=https://www.latimes.com/nation/la-na-obama-deportations-20140402-story.html |website=Los Angeles Times |date=2 April 2014 |access-date=12 January 2021}}</ref> During his time in office, the [[United States Department of Homeland Security|Department of Homeland Security]] initiated two policies which represented a major shift from prior administrations. First, DHS began placing undocumented border crossers through formal [[removal proceedings]]; second, it made non-citizens "with [[criminal record]]s" the agency's top deportation target.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Chishti |first1=Muzaffar |last2=Pierce |first2=Sara |last3=Bolter |first3=Jessica |title=The Obama Record on Deportations: Deporter in Chief or Not? |url=https://www.migrationpolicy.org/article/obama-record-deportations-deporter-chief-or-not |website=Migration Policy Institute |date=25 January 2017 |access-date=12 January 2021}}</ref> Thus, when faced with criticism for his administration's record high deportation numbers, President Obama responded by saying, "The statistics are actually a little deceptive ... we've been apprehending folks at the borders and sending them back. That is counted as a deportation even though they may have only been held for a day or 48 hours."<ref>{{cite web |last1=Silverleib |first1=Alan |title=Obama's deportation record: inside the numbers |url=https://www.cnn.com/2011/10/19/politics/deportation-record/index.html |website=CNN |date=19 October 2011 |access-date=12 January 2021}}</ref> Nevertheless, Obama faced significant criticism for his failure to pass immigration reform and for the detrimental effects of deportation on families' lives.<ref>{{cite web |title=Obama Leaves Office As 'Deporter-In-Chief' |url=https://www.npr.org/2017/01/20/510799842/obama-leaves-office-as-deporter-in-chief |website=WSKG |date=20 January 2017 |publisher=National Public Radio |access-date=12 January 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Enriquez |first1=Laura E. |title=Multigenerational Punishment: Shared Experiences of Undocumented Immigration Status Within Mixed-Status Families |journal=Journal of Marriage and Family |date=2015 |volume=77 |issue=4 |pages=939–953 |doi=10.1111/jomf.12196 |s2cid=142561195 |url=https://escholarship.org/uc/item/8mm698k2 |access-date=12 January 2021}}</ref>
====Gentrification==== [[File:Kadist Art Foundation, San Francisco.JPG|thumb|right|upright|A French arts organization in the [[Mission District, San Francisco|SF Mission District]]]]
One of the most serious issues facing urban Mexican Americans in the 2010s was residential displacement through [[gentrification]]. In [[San Francisco]] at this time, the predominantly Latino neighborhood, the [[Mission District, San Francisco|Mission District]], during the rapid expansion of [[Silicon Valley]], created a [[San Francisco housing shortage|housing shortage]], which incentivized [[Real estate investing|investors]] to buy properties in historically [[Poverty|low-income]] neighborhoods (like the Mission) in order to renovate them and sell them at higher rates to [[List of computer occupations|tech workers]].<ref>{{cite news |last1=Pogash |first1=Carol |title=Gentrification Spreads an Upheaval in San Francisco's Mission District |work=The New York Times |date=22 May 2015 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2015/05/23/us/high-rents-elbow-latinos-from-san-franciscos-mission-district.html |access-date=9 January 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=McElroy |first1=Erin |title=The Racial Contours of YIMBY/NIMBY Bay Area Gentrification |url=https://escholarship.org/content/qt4sw2g485/qt4sw2g485_noSplash_a4a78f6499d4e010591b543fef163936.pdf |website=eScholarship |publisher=Berkeley Planning Journal |access-date=14 January 2021}}</ref> As a result, many of the neighborhoods' [[Taco stand|taquerias]], [[Mexican breads|bakeries]], [[Bar (establishment)|bar]]s, and [[auto mechanic]] shops were replaced with luxury [[Condominium (living space)|condominium]]s, organic [[Ice cream parlor|ice cream stores]], international [[Art gallery|art galleries]], and upscale [[Coffeehouse|cafes]].<ref name=":Revitalized Streets of San Francisco">{{cite thesis |last1=Phillips |first1=Lucy |title=Revitalized Streets of San Francisco: A Study of Redevelopment and Gentrification in SoMa and the Mission |date=20 April 2012 |url=https://scholarship.claremont.edu/scripps_theses/99/ }}</ref> Significant resistance to the Mission's [[Gentrification of San Francisco#By neighborhood|gentrification]] emerged, including the "[[Restoration economy|restorative economics]]" of La Cocina Community Kitchen, the [[Protest art|resistance art]] projects of the [[Clarion Alley Mural Project]], the [[San Francisco tech bus protests]], and the "radically inclusive" [[social services]] provided by the Dolores Street Community Services Shelter.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Duren |first1=Derrick |title=Unpacking Gentrification Trends in SF Mission District |url=https://belonging.berkeley.edu/unpacking-gentrification-trends-sf-mission-district |website=Othering and Belonging Institute |publisher=University of California, Berkeley |access-date=9 January 2021}}</ref>
[[File:Vandalism Following Controversial Gentrification Ad .jpg|thumb|left|upright|Gentrification protest, 2017]]
In Los Angeles, gentrification affected the traditionally Mexican American neighborhoods of [[Echo Park, Los Angeles|Echo Park]], [[Highland Park, Los Angeles|Highland Park]], and large swaths of [[East Los Angeles, California|East Los Angeles]], particularly the neighborhood of [[Boyle Heights, Los Angeles|Boyle Heights]]. As white, college-educated young people increasingly moved to Los Angeles in the 2010s, they wanted to live in "hip", "urban", "edgy" and importantly, affordable, neighborhoods.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Nazaryan |first1=Alexander |title=The 'Artwashing' of America: The Battle for the Soul of Los Angeles Against Gentrification |url=https://www.newsweek.com/2017/06/02/los-angeles-gentrification-california-developers-art-galleries-la-art-scene-608558.html |website=[[Newsweek]] |date=21 May 2017 |access-date=15 January 2021}}</ref> Their presence increasingly pushed rent up and made the neighborhoods unaffordable to long-time residents.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Delgadillo |first1=Natalie |title=The Neighborhood That Went to War Against Gentrifiers |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2017-03-01/the-gentrification-battles-of-east-l-a-s-boyle-heights |newspaper=Bloomberg |date=March 2017 |access-date=15 January 2021}}</ref> In Boyle Heights, this process was sped up by the arrival of a [[Los Angeles Metro Rail|subway]] station, called [[Mariachi Plaza station]], which made the neighborhood even more desirable to young white gentrifiers wanting easy access to the rest of Los Angeles.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Sager |first1=Rebekah |title=Gentrification in L.A.'s Boyle Heights Leaves Some Latinos Threatened, Others Hopeful |url=https://www.nbcnews.com/news/latino/gentrification-l-s-boyle-heights-leaves-some-latinos-threatened-others-n820381 |website=NBC |date=14 November 2017 |access-date=9 January 2021}}</ref> In 2016, significant controversy arose when the message "Fuck White Art" was spray-painted on the entrance to the Nicodim Gallery in Boyle Heights, especially when the [[Los Angeles Police Department|LAPD]] classified the graffiti as a "[[hate crime]]".<ref>{{cite web |last1=Neuendorf |first1=Henri |title=Police Investigate LA Gallery Vandalism as Hate Crime |url=https://news.artnet.com/art-world/la-gallery-vandalism-hate-crime-734186 |website=Artnet News |date=4 November 2016 |access-date=14 January 2021}}</ref> Modern commentators compared gentrification to the [[urban renewal#By country|urban renewal]] projects of the 20th century and connected urban displacement to American [[capitalism]], [[Racism in the United States|racism]], and [[Colonial history of the United States|colonization]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Miranda |first1=Kimberly Ivette |title=Latinas for Housing Justice: The Fight for Rent Control in Unincorporated East Los Angeles |url=https://escholarship.org/uc/item/32h8f45n |website=eScholarship |publisher=UCLA |access-date=15 January 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Estrada |first1=Gilbert |title=The Historical Roots of Gentrification in Boyle Heights |url=https://www.kcet.org/shows/city-rising/the-historical-roots-of-gentrification-in-boyle-heights |website=KCET |date=14 September 2017 |publisher=Public Broadcasting Service |access-date=9 January 2021}}</ref>
[[File:Traditional Workers May Day Rally and March Chicago Illinois 5-1-18 1225 (41859191911).jpg|thumb|right|upright|Protest against rising [[Gentrification of Chicago|gentrification]] in [[Chicago]]]]
Gentrification affected many major urban Mexican American populations in the country, including in cities like [[San Diego]], [[Albuquerque, New Mexico|Albuquerque]], and [[Chicago]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Wiltse-Ahmad |first1=Alyssa |title=Study: Gentrification And Cultural Displacement Most Intense In America's Largest Cities, And Absent From Many Others |url=https://ncrc.org/study-gentrification-and-cultural-displacement-most-intense-in-americas-largest-cities-and-absent-from-many-others/ |website=NCRC |date=18 March 2019 |access-date=14 January 2021}}</ref> In the historically Mexican American neighborhood of [[Lower West Side, Chicago|Pilsen]] in Chicago, working class families experienced high levels of displacement due to rising rents and property taxes.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Ballasteros |first1=Carlos |title=Pilsen schools hollow out as neighborhood gentrifies |url=https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2019/11/29/20913976/pilsen-schools-gentrification-enrollment-elementary-housing-whittier-cooper-walsh-ruiz-jungman-casas |website=[[Chicago Sun-Times]] |date=29 November 2019 |access-date=9 January 2021}}</ref> Pilsen (which had grown as a Mexican American enclave in the 1960s and 1970s when expansions to the [[University of Illinois at Chicago]]'s campus forced them into the neighborhood)<ref>{{cite web |last1=Agrelo |first1=Justin |title=Looking at gentrification in Pilsen |url=https://sjnnchicago.medill.northwestern.edu/blog/2018/12/18/looking-gentrification-pilsen/ |website=SJNN |date=18 December 2018 |publisher=Northwestern University |access-date=15 January 2021}}</ref> became a "tourist attraction" in the 2010s, where sanitized versions of "Latinoness" were marketed to developers and new potential residents.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Fan |first1=Yuxin |title=The Identity of Pilsen—Spanish Language Presence, Cultural Appropriation, and Gentrification |url=https://voices.uchicago.edu/findingchicago/2019/08/27/the-identity-of-pilsen-spanish-language-presence-cultural-appropriation-and-gentrification/ |website=English Language Institute |publisher=University of Chicago |access-date=9 January 2021}}</ref> The result was the opening of new businesses catered primarily to upscale non-resident visitors and recent arrivals. Pilsen's primary tool to resist gentrification was a [[grassroots]] effort to designate the neighborhood a [[Historic site|historic landmark]].<ref>{{cite news |last1=Rotenberk |first1=Lori |title=Can Historic Preservation Cool Down a Hot Neighborhood? |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2019-06-20/when-historic-preservation-can-curb-gentrification |newspaper=Bloomberg |date=20 June 2019 |access-date=15 January 2021}}</ref>
====LGBT rights==== [[File:2019.06.08 Capital Pride Parade, Washington, DC USA 1590096 (crop).jpg|thumb|left|upright|Young Latinos at the D.C. [[Capital Pride (Washington, D.C.)|Capital Gay Pride]] Parade.]]
LGBTQ+ rights made significant strides in the 2010s. Between 2010 and 2019, public support for [[Same-sex marriage|marriage equality]] rose by over 19 points, and 2011 marked the first time in U.S. history that more people supported same-sex marriage than opposed it.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Cauterucci |first1=Christina |title=The Decade When the Fight for LGBTQ Rights Went Mainstream |url=https://slate.com/news-and-politics/2019/12/decade-fight-lgbtq-rights-mainstream-marriage-equality-trans-rights.html |journal=Slate |date=17 December 2019 |access-date=10 January 2021}}</ref> In 2011, President Obama formally ended the policy of "[[Don't ask, don't tell]]", allowing openly identified [[gay men]] and [[lesbian]]s to serve in the [[United States Armed Forces|U.S. military]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Slack |first1=Megan |title=The End of Don't Ask, Don't Tell |url=https://obamawhitehouse.archives.gov/blog/2012/09/20/archives-end-dont-ask-dont-tell |website=Obama White House Archives |date=20 September 2012 |publisher=The White House |access-date=10 January 2021}}</ref> In 2015, the [[Supreme Court of the United States|U.S. Supreme Court]] ruled in ''[[Obergefell v. Hodges]]'' that same-sex marriage was a constitutionally protected right under the [[Fourteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution|14th Amendment]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Obergefell v. Hodges |url=https://www.law.cornell.edu/supremecourt/text/14-556 |website=Legal Information Institute |publisher=Cornell University Law School |access-date=10 January 2021}}</ref> Around the time of the Supreme Court's ruling, Latino support for marriage equality was amongst the highest of any ethnic group.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Bencosme |first1=Melanie |title=Majority of Latinos Favor Same-Sex Marriage; Public Support Rises |url=https://www.nbcnews.com/news/latino/pew-majority-latinos-favor-same-sex-marriage-approval-rises-n371816 |website=NBC News |date=8 June 2015 |access-date=10 January 2021}}</ref>
Despite these significant strides, however, the Mexican American and Latino LGBT communities continued to struggle with many challenges. In the aftermath of the [[Great Recession]], an approximately 4.2 million youth experienced [[homelessness]] in the U.S., of which 700,000 were [[unaccompanied minor]]s.<ref>{{cite web |title=Youth Homelessness Overview |url=https://www.ncsl.org/research/human-services/homeless-and-runaway-youth.aspx |website=Children and Families Program |publisher=National Conference on State Legislatures |access-date=10 January 2021}}</ref> Of these 4.2 million, estimates range that between eleven and forty percent were [[Homelessness among LGBT youth in the United States|LGBTQ]], many of whom were Mexican American or Latino.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Shelton |first1=Jama |last2=Santa Maria |first2=Diane |last3=Barman-Adhikari |first3=Anamika |last4=Hsu |first4=Hsun-Ta |last5=Narendorf |first5=Sarah |title=Homelessness and Housing Experiences among LGBTQ Young Adults in Seven U.S. Cities |journal=Youth Homelessness |date=2018 |volume=20 |issue=3 |pages=9–34 |jstor=26524870 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/26524870 |access-date=10 January 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Gibbard Kline |first1=Megan |title=Centering Youth of Color and LGBTQ Young People in Efforts to End Homelessness |url=https://www.usich.gov/news-events/news/centering-youth-color-lgbtq-young-people-efforts-end-homelessness |website=United States Interagency Council on Homelessness |publisher=U.S. Presidential Council on Homelessness |access-date=9 January 2021}}</ref> [[Transgender]] equality also remained a major issue for many Mexican Americans. In the 2010s, black and Latino [[Trans woman|transgender women]] experienced [[homicide]] rates widely disproportionate to their percentage of the overall population.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Dinno |first1=Alexis |title=Homicide Rates of Transgender Individuals in the United States: 2010–2014 |journal=American Journal of Public Health |date=Fall 2017 |volume=107 |issue=9 |pages=1441–1447 |doi=10.2105/AJPH.2017.303878 |pmid=28727530 |pmc=5551594 }}</ref><ref>{{cite news |last1=Trotta |first1=Daniel |title='Walking while trans' can be a death sentence in the U.S. |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-lgbt-violence/walking-while-trans-can-be-a-death-sentence-in-the-u-s-idUSKCN12K0CT |website=Reuters |date=20 October 2016 |access-date=10 January 2021}}</ref> The failure of the Obama administration or mainstream LGBT organizations to address any of these difficulties created outrage amongst the LGBT Mexican American and Latinx communities. In 2015, the activist [[Bamby Salcedo]] disrupted the opening session of the [[National LGBTQ Task Force]]'s annual conference to protest the white LGBT community's continued ignorance regarding violence against transgender women of color.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.frontiersmedia.com/frontiers-blog/2015/02/06/bamby-salcedo-leads-translivesmatter-demo-creating-change/ |title=Bamby Salcedo Leads #TransLivesMatter Demo at Creating Change |publisher=Frontiers Media |date=2015-02-06 |access-date=2015-04-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150401065226/http://www.frontiersmedia.com/frontiers-blog/2015/02/06/bamby-salcedo-leads-translivesmatter-demo-creating-change/ |archive-date=2015-04-01 |url-status=dead }}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Activists Rally for Trans Women and Against Police Killing |url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XRNycuH3SLQ |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/varchive/youtube/20211212/XRNycuH3SLQ| archive-date=2021-12-12 |url-status=live|website=National LGBTQ Task Force | date=6 February 2015 |access-date=4 March 2021}}{{cbignore}}</ref> In the 2010s, LGBT Mexican and Latino [[U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement|ICE]] detainees also experienced alarmingly high rates of [[sexual abuse]] in U.S. [[Immigration detention in the United States|immigration detention centers]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Hananel |first1=Sam |title=LGBT Immigrants in Detention Centers at Severe Risk of Sexual Abuse, CAP Analysis Says |url=https://www.americanprogress.org/press/release/2018/05/30/451380/release-lgbt-immigrants-detention-centers-severe-risk-sexual-abuse-cap-analysis-says/ |website=Center for American Progress |date=30 May 2018 |access-date=10 January 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Moore |first1=Robert |title=Rights groups allege ICE, private detention firms, provide poor care to LGBTQ migrants and those with HIV |url=https://www.seattletimes.com/nation-world/rights-groups-allege-ice-private-detention-firms-provide-poor-care-to-lgbtq-migrants-and-those-with-hiv/ |website=[[The Seattle Times]] |date=25 September 2019 |access-date=10 January 2021}}</ref> The undocumented activist [[Jennicet Gutiérrez]] interrupted a speech by President Obama during a White House LGBT celebration dinner in 2015 to protest violent detention center conditions for LGBT Latinos.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Stack |first1=Liam |title=Activist Removed After Heckling Obama at L.G.B.T. Event at White House |work=The New York Times |date=25 June 2015 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2015/06/25/us/politics/activist-removed-after-heckling-obama-at-lgbt-event.html |access-date=9 January 2021}}</ref>
==="Build that wall!"=== [[File:Latinos for Trump 2016 RNC.jpg|thumb|right|upright|"Latinos para Trump" signs waved at the [[2016 Republican National Convention|2016 RNC]].]]
From the start of [[Donald Trump 2016 presidential campaign|Donald Trump's campaign for president]], he faced accusations of racism and discrimination against Mexicans and Mexican Americans. In his announcement speech in August 2015, Trump said the United States had become a "dumping ground" for unwanted people from other countries and received pushback in particular for his statement: "When Mexico sends its people, they're not sending their best. They're bringing drugs. They're bringing crime. They're rapists. And some, I assume, are good people."<ref>{{cite magazine |title=Here's Donald Trump's Presidential Announcement Speech |url=https://time.com/3923128/donald-trump-announcement-speech/ |magazine=Time |access-date=13 January 2021}}</ref> The comment was interpreted by many to imply that [[Emigration from Mexico|immigrants from Mexico]] had been "sent" to the U.S., and that many could be generalized as "criminals and rapists".<ref>{{cite news |last1=Hee Lee |first1=Michelle Ye |title=Donald Trump's false comments connecting Mexican immigrants and crime |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/fact-checker/wp/2015/07/08/donald-trumps-false-comments-connecting-mexican-immigrants-and-crime/ |newspaper=The Washington Post |access-date=13 January 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title='Drug dealers, criminals, rapists': What Trump thinks of Mexicans |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/av/world-us-canada-37230916 |publisher=British Broadcasting Corporation |access-date=13 January 2021}}</ref> In June 2016, Trump was again accused of racism when he said he would be unable to receive a fair hearing from the Mexican American judge [[Gonzalo P. Curiel|Gonzalo Curiel]] because, "The judge, who happens to be, we believe, Mexican ... [has] an absolute conflict ... I'm building a wall. It's an inherent [[conflict of interest]]."<ref>{{cite web |last1=Contreras |first1=Russell |title=Trump's 'Mexican' label against judge brings up word's history |url=https://www.pbs.org/newshour/politics/trumps-mexican-label-against-judge-brings-up-words-history |website=PBS Newshours |date=18 June 2016 |publisher=Public Broadcasting Service |access-date=13 January 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |last1=Kendall |first1=Brent |title=Trump Says Judge's Mexican Heritage Presents 'Absolute Conflict' |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/donald-trump-keeps-up-attacks-on-judge-gonzalo-curiel-1464911442 |website=The Wall Street Journal |date=2 June 2016 |access-date=13 January 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Wolf |first1=Z. Byron |title=Trump's attacks on Judge Curiel are still jarring to read |url=https://www.cnn.com/2018/02/27/politics/judge-curiel-trump-border-wall/index.html |website=CNN |date=27 February 2018 |access-date=13 January 2021}}</ref> The [[Defensive wall|border wall]] was one of the central themes of Trump's 2016 campaign, with many of his rallies featuring the chant, "Build that wall!"<ref>{{cite web |title='Build that wall!' Donald Trump Chants After Major Endorsement |url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZGSAhNZnisk |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/varchive/youtube/20211212/ZGSAhNZnisk| archive-date=2021-12-12 |url-status=live|website=NewsNOW | date=25 May 2016 |publisher=Fox News |access-date=13 January 2021}}{{cbignore}}</ref> Numerous commentators noted the racial and [[Nativism (politics)|nativist]] connotations underlying the campaign pledge to build a wall.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Varela |first1=Julio Ricardo |title=Trump's border wall was never just about security. It's meant to remind all Latinos that we're unwelcome. |url=https://www.nbcnews.com/think/opinion/trump-s-border-wall-was-never-just-about-security-it-ncna952011 |website=NBC News |date=28 December 2018 |access-date=13 January 2021}}</ref> Studies conducted during the 2016 presidential election showed negative [[Computer-mediated communication|online discourse]] surrounding Latinos was so severe that it negatively affected the community's [[mental health|mental well-being]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hswen |first1=Yulin |title=Online negative sentiment towards Mexicans and Hispanics and impact on mental well-being: A time-series analysis of social media data during the 2016 United States presidential election |journal=Heliyon |date=Fall 2020 |volume=6 |issue=9 |article-number=e04910 |doi=10.1016/j.heliyon.2020.e04910 |doi-access=free |pmid=33005781 |pmc=7519357 |bibcode=2020Heliy...604910H }}</ref> Nevertheless, Trump won at least 18% of the Latino vote in 2016.<ref>{{cite book|first1=Matt|last1=Barreto|first2=Thomas|last2=Schaller|first3=Gary|last3=Segura|chapter=Latinos and the 2016 Election|chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=01t7DgAAQBAJ&pg=PA10|title=Trumped: The 2016 Election That Broke All the Rules|editor1-first=Larry|editor1-last=Sabato|editor2-first=Kyle|editor2-last=Kondik|editor3-first=Geoffrey|editor3-last=Skelley|publisher=[[Rowman & Littlefield]]|location=Lanham, Maryland|date=2017|pages=123–35|isbn=9781442279407}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|first=Geoffrey|last=Skelley|date=March 23, 2017|url=http://www.centerforpolitics.org/crystalball/articles/another-look-back-at-2016/|title=Another Look Back at 2016: Comparing the exit poll and the Cooperative Congressional Election Study|website=[[Sabato's Crystal Ball]]|access-date=March 8, 2018}}</ref>
[[File:OTB Wichita 2019-031.jpg|thumb|left|upright|During the Trump administration, agencies, including the [[U.S. Marshals]] and [[Immigration and Customs Enforcement|ICE]], collaborated in raids.]]
While in office, the Trump administration unleashed a series of controversial policies affecting Latinos. On May 7, 2018, [[Jeff Sessions]], then Trump's [[United States Attorney General|attorney general]], announced a new "[[Trump administration family separation policy|zero tolerance]]" policy at the U.S.-Mexico border, meaning any person apprehended crossing the border without documentation would be charged criminally and any children accompanying them would be held separately.<ref name="lead ref 1"> * {{cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2018/06/16/us/politics/family-separation-trump.html|title=How Trump Came to Enforce a Practice of Separating Migrant Families|last1=Davis|first1=Julie Hirschfeld|date=June 16, 2018|work=The New York Times|access-date=June 19, 2018|url-status=live|archive-url=https://archive.today/20180618122934/https://www.nytimes.com/2018/06/16/us/politics/family-separation-trump.html|archive-date=June 18, 2018|last2=Shear|first2=Michael D.|quote=exceptions were generally made for adults who were traveling with minor children|author-link=Julie Hirschfeld Davis|author-link2=Michael D. Shear|url-access=limited}} * {{cite news|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/trump-cites-as-a-negotiating-tool-his-policy-of-separating-immigrant-children-from-their-parents/2018/06/15/ade82b80-70b3-11e8-bf86-a2351b5ece99_story.html|title=Trump cites as a negotiating tool his policy of separating immigrant children from their parents|last1=Scherer|first1=Michael|date=June 15, 2018|newspaper=[[The Washington Post]]|access-date=June 17, 2018|url-status=live|archive-url=https://archive.today/20191026190809/https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/trump-cites-as-a-negotiating-tool-his-policy-of-separating-immigrant-children-from-their-parents/2018/06/15/ade82b80-70b3-11e8-bf86-a2351b5ece99_story.html|archive-date=October 26, 2019|last2=Dawsey|first2=Josh|author-link=Michael Scherer (journalist)|author-link2=Josh Dawsey|url-access=limited}}*{{Cite news|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/trump-defiant-as-crisis-grows-over-family-separation-at-the-border/2018/06/18/210c78ca-730f-11e8-805c-4b67019fcfe4_story.html|title=Trump defiant as crisis grows over family separation at the border|last1=Rucker|first1=Philip|date=June 18, 2018|newspaper=[[The Washington Post]]|access-date=June 19, 2018|url-status=live|archive-url=https://archive.today/20191026194347/https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/trump-defiant-as-crisis-grows-over-family-separation-at-the-border/2018/06/18/210c78ca-730f-11e8-805c-4b67019fcfe4_story.html|archive-date=October 26, 2019|last2=Dawsey|first2=Josh|last3=Kim|first3=Seung Min|author-link=Philip Rucker|author-link2=Josh Dawsey|author-link3=Seung Min Kim|url-access=limited}} * {{cite news|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-politics/un-trump-children-family-torture-separation-border-mexico-border-ice-detention-a8411676.html|title=UN says Trump separation of migrant children from parents 'may amount to torture'|last=Mindock|first=Clark|date=June 22, 2018|work=[[The Independent]]|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190514163658/https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-politics/un-trump-children-family-torture-separation-border-mexico-border-ice-detention-a8411676.html|archive-date=May 14, 2019}}</ref> Though the policy was ended by executive order within a month, more than 500 children remained separated from their parents more than two years later.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Dickerson |first1=Caitlin |title=Parents of 545 Children Separated at the Border Cannot Be Found |work=The New York Times |date=21 October 2020 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2020/10/21/us/migrant-children-separated.html |access-date=13 January 2021}}</ref> During the [[2018 United States elections|2018 midterm elections]], Trump politicized the [[Central America]]n [[refugee]] crisis, claiming "terrorists" and "gang members" were secretly hiding alongside women and children in order to gain entry into the United States.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Haberman |first1=Maggie |last2=Landler |first2=Mark |title=A Week After the Midterms, Trump Seems to Forget the Caravan |work=The New York Times |date=14 November 2018 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2018/11/13/us/politics/trump-caravan-midterms.html |access-date=13 January 2021}}</ref> After the midterm elections, the Trump administration adopted the "Remain in Mexico" program, which essentially sought to shut down [[Asylum in the United States|asylum entry]] into the country.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Narea |first1=Nicole |title=The demise of America's asylum system under Trump, explained |url=https://www.vox.com/2019/11/5/20947938/asylum-system-trump-demise-mexico-el-salvador-honduras-guatemala-immigration-court-border-ice-cbp |website=Vox |date=5 November 2019 |access-date=13 January 2021}}</ref> The policy resulted in a [[humanitarian crisis]], as tens of thousands of refugees were forced to live in makeshift tent encampments in Mexico while they awaited an asylum hearing.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Narea |first1=Nicole |title=The abandoned asylum seekers on the US-Mexico border |url=https://www.vox.com/policy-and-politics/2019/12/20/20997299/asylum-border-mexico-us-iom-unhcr-usaid-migration-international-humanitarian-aid-matamoros-juarez |website=Vox |date=20 December 2019 |access-date=13 January 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Silva |first1=Daniella |title=Trapped in Tijuana: Migrants face a long, dangerous wait to claim asylum |url=https://www.nbcnews.com/news/latino/trapped-tijuana-migrants-face-long-dangerous-wait-claim-asylum-n981721 |website=NBC News |access-date=13 January 2021}}</ref> Trump's efforts on the border resulted in the erection of over 53 miles of new border wall and the renovation of over 400 miles of existing border walls;<ref>{{cite magazine |last1=Aguilera |first1=Jasmine |title=At President Trump's Last Visit to the Border Wall, Here's What to Know About Its Legacy |url=https://time.com/5928808/trump-border-wall-what-to-know/ |magazine=Time |access-date=13 January 2021}}</ref> this wall construction sparked outrage for its impact on [[Indigenous peoples|indigenous communities]].<ref>{{cite news |last1=Armus |first1=Teo |title='You don't control the border': Indigenous groups protesting wall construction clash with federal agents |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/nation/2020/09/23/border-wall-construction-protests/ |newspaper=The Washington Post |access-date=13 January 2021}}</ref> During the Trump years, [[Immigration and Customs Enforcement]] also collaborated with other U.S. law enforcement agencies, such as the [[U.S. Marshals]], to conduct large "crime raids", during which hundreds of people were arrested and deported if found to be undocumented.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Nystrom |first1=Brittney |title=This Is What Immigration Enforcement Looks Like Under President Trump |url=https://www.aclu.org/blog/immigrants-rights/ice-and-border-patrol-abuses/what-immigration-enforcement-looks-under |website=ACLU |date=6 March 2018 |access-date=12 March 2021}}</ref> In fact, in 2019, ICE conducted the largest single-state immigration raid in U.S. history.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Hauslohner |first1=Abigail |title=ICE agents raid Miss. work sites, arrest 680 people in largest single-state immigration enforcement action in U.S. history |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/immigration/ice-agents-raid-miss-work-sites-arrest-680-people-in-largest-single-state-immigration-enforcement-action-in-us-history/2019/08/07/801d5cfe-b94e-11e9-b3b4-2bb69e8c4e39_story.html |newspaper=Washington Post |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210128210325/https://www.washingtonpost.com/immigration/ice-agents-raid-miss-work-sites-arrest-680-people-in-largest-single-state-immigration-enforcement-action-in-us-history/2019/08/07/801d5cfe-b94e-11e9-b3b4-2bb69e8c4e39_story.html |access-date=12 March 2021|archive-date=2021-01-28 }}</ref>
====Trump protests==== {{See also|Protests against the Trump administration family separation policy}} [[File:Gallego - June 2018 detention protest 03.jpg|thumb|right|upright|Boy holding up a sign at a [[Families Belong Together]] march in [[Phoenix, Arizona]].]]
The 2010s saw levels of [[Demonstration (political)|mass protest]] unseen in the United States since the [[Civil rights movement|Civil Rights movement]] of the 1960s. In 2017, the [[Day Without Immigrants 2017|Day without Immigrants]] protested President [[Donald Trump]]'s plans to build a [[Mexico–United States barrier|border wall]] and to potentially deport millions of [[Illegal immigration to the United States|undocumented immigrants]].<ref>Esmeralda Bermudez (February 16, 2017). [http://www.latimes.com/local/california/la-me-ln-day-without-immigrants-20170216-story.html "In Los Angeles, 'A Day Without Immigrants' resonates"]. ''Los Angeles Times''.</ref> In 2018, [[Protests against the Trump administration family separation policy|protests]] against Trump's [[Trump administration family separation policy|family separation policy]] occurred in over 700 cities and towns in the United States, many under the title, "[[Families Belong Together]]".<ref name="cbsnews.com">{{Cite news|url=https://www.cbsnews.com/news/trump-immigration-family-separation-protests-today-nationwide-2018-06-30-live-updates/|title=Protesters flood U.S. cities to fight Trump immigration policy|access-date=July 1, 2018}}</ref><ref name="NYTimes">{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2018/06/30/us/politics/trump-protests-family-separation.html|title=Protests Across U.S. Call for End to Migrant Family Separations|last1=Yoon-Hendricks|first1=Alexandra|date=June 30, 2018|work=The New York Times|access-date=July 1, 2018|last2=Greenberg|first2=Zoe}}</ref> Also in response to the Trump's family separation policies, widespread calls emerged to shut down [[U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement]] (ICE),<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.cnn.com/2018/06/30/politics/abolish-ice-movement-gaining-support-democrats/index.html|title=The movement to 'Abolish ICE' is heating up -- and going mainstream|first=Gregory|last=Krieg|date=30 June 2018 |publisher=CNN}}</ref> usually expressed under the slogan "[[Abolish ICE]]".<ref>{{cite web |last1=Shah |first1=Silky |title=Why America still needs to abolish ICE |url=https://www.nbcnews.com/think/opinion/why-america-still-needs-abolish-ice-ncna1243293 |website=NBC News |date=14 October 2020 |access-date=14 January 2021}}</ref> In 2019, as the Trump administration sought to end the [[Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals|DACA]] program, the [[Supreme Court of the United States|United States Supreme Court]] agreed to hear their case.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Williams |first1=Pete |title=Supreme Court agrees to hear DACA case in win for Trump administration |url=https://www.nbcnews.com/politics/supreme-court/supreme-court-agrees-hear-daca-case-win-trump-administration-n1020481 |website=NBC News |date=28 June 2019 |access-date=14 January 2021}}</ref> ''[[Department of Homeland Security v. Regents of the University of California|DHS v. University of California]]'' created significant controversy, and major protests in support of "Dreamers" occurred across the country.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Coleman |first1=Justin |last2=Lancaster |first2=Jordan |title=DACA arguments draw large protests at Supreme Court |url=https://thehill.com/regulation/court-battles/470065-daca-arguments-draw-protests-at-supreme-court/ |website=The Hill |date=12 November 2019 |access-date=14 January 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Milano |first1=Brett |title=Hundreds rally to defend DACA |url=https://news.harvard.edu/gazette/story/2019/11/at-harvard-hundreds-rally-to-defend-daca/ |website=The Harvard Gazette |date=12 November 2019 |publisher=Harvard University |access-date=14 January 2021}}</ref> Major American corporations also voiced their support for the DACA program,<ref>{{cite web |last1=Bernal |first1=Rafael |title=More than 140 businesses call on Supreme Court to protect DACA |url=https://thehill.com/latino/464375-more-than-140-businesses-call-on-supreme-court-to-protect-daca/ |website=The Hill |date=4 October 2019 |access-date=14 January 2021}}</ref> and the Supreme Court ultimately upheld the program.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Totenberg |first1=Nina |title=Supreme Court Rules For DREAMers, Against Trump |url=https://www.npr.org/2020/06/18/829858289/supreme-court-upholds-daca-in-blow-to-trump-administration |website=WSKG |date=18 June 2020 |publisher=National Public Radio |access-date=14 January 2021}}</ref> Mexicans Americans were also involved with many of the major social movements during the Trump presidency, including the movement for [[Black Lives Matter]] and [[Racial equality|racial justice]],<ref>{{cite web |last1=Fountains |first1=Aaron |title=How African American Activists are Influencing Latinos |url=https://www.aaihs.org/how-african-american-activists-are-influencing-latinos/ |website=Black Perspectives |date=25 July 2017 |publisher=African American Intellectual History Society |access-date=14 January 2021}}</ref> the [[2017 Women's March|Women's March]] (the Chicana activist [[Carmen Perez]] was one of four national co-chairs of the 2017 March),<ref>{{cite web |title=Carmen Perez-Jordan |url=https://nmaahc.si.edu/latinx/carmen-perez-jordan |website=National Museum of African American History and Culture |publisher=Smithsonian Institution |access-date=14 January 2021}}</ref> and [[gun control]] movements like [[March for Our Lives|March For Our Lives]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Ruiz |first1=Stefany |title=The Powerful Reasons These Young People Of Color Joined The March For Our Lives |url=https://fierce.wearemitu.com/calladitas-no-more/powerful-reasons-young-people-joined-march-for-our-lives-washington-dc/ |website=mitú |access-date=14 January 2021}}</ref> According to some scholars, the intense activist energy during the Trump presidency motivated young Mexican Americans to adopt a political identity of "neo-Chicanismo", defined by ethnic pride, cultural heritage and expression, and protecting immigrants' rights.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Romero |first1=Dennis |title=A Chicano renaissance? A new Mexican-American generation embraces the term |url=https://www.nbcnews.com/news/latino/chicano-renaissance-new-mexican-american-generation-embraces-term-n869846 |website=NBC News |date=15 July 2018 |access-date=14 January 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Aguirre |first1=Abby |title=Latinas in Los Angeles |url=https://www.vogue.com/projects/13528517/mexican-american-women-east-los-angeles-chicano-fashion-latina-style |website=Vogue |date=9 March 2017 |access-date=14 January 2021}}</ref> This younger activist generation also actively adopted [[Gender neutrality|gender neutral]] language, such as the terms "[[Wikt:Chicanx#Noun|Chicanx]]" and "[[Xicanx]]" as a way to push back against [[Sexism|gender bias]] in politics, society, and activism.
====El Paso shooting==== {{Main article|2019 El Paso shooting}} [[File:Deb Haaland visits El Paso, Texas in 2019 03.jpg|thumb|left|upright|House Rep. [[Deb Haaland]] visits a memorial to the victims of the [[2019 El Paso shooting|El Paso shooting]].]]
Over the course of the 2010s, the rise of [[White nationalism|white nationalist]] [[domestic terrorism]] increased dramatically.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Davis |first1=Linsey |title=Homegrown Hate: The War Among Us |url=https://abcnews.go.com/US/video/homegrown-hate-war-us-73464825 |website=ABC News |access-date=13 January 2021}}</ref> There was a large surge in [[White supremacy|white supremacist]] and other [[hate group]] membership over the course of the [[Presidency of Barack Obama|Obama administration]],<ref>{{cite news |last1=Severson |first1=Kim |title=Number of U.S. Hate Groups Is Rising, Report Says |work=The New York Times |date=8 March 2012 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2012/03/08/us/number-of-us-hate-groups-on-the-rise-report-says.html |access-date=13 January 2021}}</ref> and the United States saw a record number of [[hate crime]]s committed during the [[First presidency of Donald Trump|Trump administration]].<ref>{{cite news |last1=Singh |first1=Arjun |title=The FBI recorded a surge of hate crimes last year. But it undercounted — by a lot. |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/outlook/2018/11/14/fbi-recorded-surge-hate-crimes-last-year-it-undercounted-by-lot/ |newspaper=The Washington Post |access-date=13 January 2021}}</ref> In 2019, one of the largest [[Mass shootings in the United States|mass shootings]] against Latino Americans occurred at a [[Walmart]] store in [[El Paso, Texas]] when a 21-year old white terrorist open-fired, killing 23 people and injuring 23 others.<ref name="NYT23">{{cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/aponline/2020/04/26/business/ap-mass-shooting-texas.html|title=El Paso Shooting Victim Dies Months Later, Death Toll Now 23|work=The New York Times|date=April 26, 2020|access-date=April 26, 2020}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Chavez |first1=Nicole |title=Police believe the El Paso shooter targeted Latinos. These are the victims' stories |url=https://www.cnn.com/2019/08/04/us/el-paso-shooting-victims/index.html |website=CNN |date=4 August 2019 |access-date=13 January 2021}}</ref> The terrorist, named Patrick Crusius, was mostly [[Online youth radicalization|radicalized online]], a trend which has occurred with increasing frequency in the United States.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Ailworth |first1=Erin |title=Lost in Life, El Paso Suspect Found a Dark World Online |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/lost-in-life-el-paso-suspect-found-a-dark-world-online-11565308783 |website=The Wall Street Journal |date=9 August 2019 |access-date=13 January 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite magazine |last1=Bergengruen |first1=Vera |last2=Hennigan |first2=W.J. |title='We Are Being Eaten From Within.' Why America Is Losing the Battle Against White Nationalist Terrorism |url=https://time.com/5647304/white-nationalist-terrorism-united-states/ |magazine=Time |access-date=13 January 2021}}</ref> National security experts have compared the [[radicalization]] of white and other [[Far-right politics|far-right]] terrorists to the online recruitment of terror organizations like [[Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant|ISIS]].<ref>{{cite news |last1=Fisher |first1=Max |title=White Terrorism Shows 'Stunning' Parallels to Islamic State's Rise |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2019/08/05/world/americas/terrorism-white-nationalist-supremacy-isis.html |website=The New York Times |date=5 August 2019 |access-date=13 January 2021}}</ref> Shortly before the attack, the terrorist posted his "[[manifesto]]" to the online message board [[8chan]], where he wrote about the "Latino invasion of Texas" and detailed a plan to separate America by race, claiming "white people were being [[Great Replacement|replaced]] by foreigners."<ref>{{cite news |last1=Arango |first1=Tim |last2=Benner |first2=Katie |title=Minutes Before El Paso Killing, Hate-Filled Manifesto Appears Online |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2019/08/03/us/patrick-crusius-el-paso-shooter-manifesto.html |website=The New York Times |date=4 August 2019 |access-date=13 January 2021}}</ref> The terrorist faces 90 [[Federal crime in the United States|federal charges]], including 23 counts of [[Hate crime laws in the United States|federal hates crimes]] resulting in death, and he will face the [[Capital punishment|death penalty]] if found guilty.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Corchado |first1=Alfredo |title=El Paso shooter faces 90 federal counts, including hate crime charges that could bring death penalty |url=https://www.dallasnews.com/news/texas/2020/02/06/el-paso-shooter-expected-to-face-federal-hate-crime-charges/ |website=The Dallas Morning News |date=6 February 2020 |access-date=13 January 2021}}</ref>
==2020s== ===Politics=== [[Image:Julian Castro (49375217773).jpg|thumb|right|upright|Secretary [[Julian Castro]] became the second Mexican American to mount a serious campaign for [[President of the United States|President]].]]
In 2020, former [[United States House of Representatives|House Representative]] and [[United States Secretary of Housing and Urban Development|Secretary of Housing and Urban Development]] [[Julian Castro]] became the first serious Mexican American candidate for president since [[Ben Fernandez]] ran in the [[1980 Republican Party presidential primaries|1980 Republican primaries]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Cadava |first1=Geraldo |title=Marco Rubio, meet 'Boxcar Ben' Fernandez, America's first Latino presidential candidate |url=https://www.latimes.com/opinion/op-ed/la-oe-1122-cadava-boxcar-ben-rubio-20151122-story.html |website=Los Angeles Times |date=22 November 2015 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210103143616/https://www.latimes.com/opinion/op-ed/la-oe-1122-cadava-boxcar-ben-rubio-20151122-story.html |access-date=30 December 2020|archive-date=2021-01-03 }}</ref> Castro dropped out of the race on January 6, 2020, after polling far behind other candidates,<ref>{{cite news |last1=Medina |first1=Jennifer |last2=Stevens |first2=Matt |title=Julián Castro Ends Presidential Run: 'It Simply Isn't Our Time' |work=The New York Times |date=2 January 2020 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2020/01/02/us/politics/julian-castro-dropping-out.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210325064224/https://www.nytimes.com/2020/01/02/us/politics/julian-castro-dropping-out.html |access-date=30 December 2020|archive-date=2021-03-25 }}</ref>
