and he announced his support for [[Joe Biden]] on June 2, 2020.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Svitek |first1=Patrick |title=Julián Castro makes his support for Joe Biden official |url=https://www.texastribune.org/2020/06/02/julian-castro-joe-biden-endorsement/ |website=The Texas Tribune |date=2 June 2020 |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref> During the [[2020 United States presidential election|2020 general election]], some experts were surprised at the strength of [[Donald Trump]]'s support amongst Mexican Americans in areas like the [[Rio Grande Valley (Texas)|Rio Grande Valley]], which increased by 10% from 2016.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Byler |first1=David |title=Trump shocked the world with his Latino support. Here's how it happened. |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/opinions/2020/11/12/trump-shocked-world-with-his-latino-support-heres-how-it-happened/ |newspaper=The Washington Post |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210212031151/https://www.washingtonpost.com/opinions/2020/11/12/trump-shocked-world-with-his-latino-support-heres-how-it-happened/ |access-date=30 December 2020|archive-date=2021-02-12 }}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Hennessy-Fiske |first1=Molly |title='We've only started': How Latino support for Trump grew in Texas borderlands |url=https://www.latimes.com/world-nation/story/2020-11-12/latino-support-grew-for-trump-on-the-texas-border |website=Los Angeles Times |date=12 November 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210303030315/https://www.latimes.com/world-nation/story/2020-11-12/latino-support-grew-for-trump-on-the-texas-border |access-date=30 December 2020|archive-date=2021-03-03 }}</ref> Others, however, noted that the increase toward Trump in 2020 represented the culmination of long-term trends within the Mexican American and Latino communities.<ref>{{cite magazine |last1=Cadava |first1=Geraldo |title=The Deep Origins of Latino Support for Trump |url=https://www.newyorker.com/news/the-political-scene/the-deep-origins-of-latino-support-for-trump |magazine=[[The New Yorker]] |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210225041409/https://www.newyorker.com/news/the-political-scene/the-deep-origins-of-latino-support-for-trump |access-date=30 December 2020|archive-date=2021-02-25 }}</ref> In December 2020, [[Alex Padilla]] was appointed to replace Vice President [[Kamala Harris]] as the junior senator from [[California]].<ref>{{cite news |last1=Hubler |first1=Shawn |title=Alex Padilla Will Replace Kamala Harris in the Senate |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2020/12/22/us/politics/alex-padilla-kamala-california-senate.html |website=The New York Times |date=22 December 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210126065136/https://www.nytimes.com/2020/12/22/us/politics/alex-padilla-kamala-california-senate.html |access-date=30 December 2020|archive-date=2021-01-26 }}</ref> He was sworn in by Harris in January 2021, thus becoming the first Latino senator in the state's history.<ref>{{cite web|title=Kamala Harris Swears In New Democratic Senators|url=https://www.latimes.com/world%2Dnation/story/2020%2D11%2D12/latino%2Dsupport%2Dgrew%2Dfor%2Dtrump%2Don%2Dthe%2Dtexas%2Dborder|website=Los Angeles Times|access-date=22 January 2021|archive-date=2021-01-18|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210118172421/https://www.latimes.com/world-nation/story/2020-11-12/latino-support-grew-for-trump-on-the-texas-border|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Willon |first1=Phil |title=Alex Padilla to become California's first Latino U.S. senator, replacing Kamala Harris |url=https://www.latimes.com/california/story/2020-12-22/gavin-newsom-alex-padilla-senate-california-kamala-harris |website=Los Angeles Times |date=22 December 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210331201831/https://www.latimes.com/california/story/2020-12-22/gavin-newsom-alex-padilla-senate-california-kamala-harris |access-date=30 December 2020|archive-date=2021-03-31 }}</ref>
===Police violence=== [[File: -DefundThePolice in Hillsborough (2021 Apr) (51130533546).jpg|thumb|Sign reading, "''Justicia para Adam Toledo''", calling for justice for [[Adam Toledo]] in April 2021.]]
In the 2020s, Mexican Americans and other Latinos joined calls for [[racial justice]] and an end to [[police brutality]] in the United States.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Gamboa |first1=Suzanne |title='We are tired of it': Latinos denounce police violence as they protest George Floyd death |url=https://www.nbcnews.com/news/latino/we-are-tired-it-latinos-denounce-police-violence-they-protest-n1224056 |website=NBC News |date=4 June 2020 |access-date=10 April 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |last1=Silva |first1=Christianna |title=Joaquin Castro Calls Police Violence A Major, If Underreported, Problem For Latinos |url=https://www.npr.org/sections/live-updates-protests-for-racial-justice/2020/06/28/884351918/rep-joaquin-castro-on-police-violence-in-the-latino-community |newspaper=NPR.org |access-date=10 April 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Koran |first1=Mario |title='We're suffering the same abuses': Latinos hear their stories echoed in police brutality protests |url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2020/jun/12/latinos-police-brutality-protests-george-floyd |website=The Guardian |date=12 June 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210330004916/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2020/jun/12/latinos-police-brutality-protests-george-floyd |access-date=10 April 2021|archive-date=2021-03-30 }}</ref> Analysis of fatal U.S. police shootings in October 2020 showed that in the prior five years, [[Native Americans in the United States|Native Americans]], [[black Americans]], and Latinos were the three ethnic groups killed by police at the highest rates in the country, respectively.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Belli |first1=Brita |title=Racial disparity in police shootings unchanged over 5 years |url=https://news.yale.edu/2020/10/27/racial-disparity-police-shootings-unchanged-over-5-years |website=Yale News |date=27 October 2020 |publisher=Yale University |access-date=10 April 2021}}</ref> Activists within the community, however, expressed frustration that in the years leading up to the [[2020–21 United States racial unrest]], police killings of Latino boys and men had largely been met with sustained national indifference.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Domínguez |first1=Arturo |title=Say Their Names: Latino Lives Lost to Police Brutality |url=https://www.latinorebels.com/2020/07/17/latinoliveslosttopolicebrutality/ |website=Latino Rebels |date=17 July 2020 |access-date=10 April 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Santa Cruz |first1=Nicole |title=Why the deaths of Latinos at the hands of police haven't drawn as much attention |url=https://www.latimes.com/local/crime/la-me-0718-latino-police-20150718-story.html |website=Los Angeles Times |date=18 July 2015 |access-date=7 April 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Downs |first1=Kenya |title=Why aren't more people talking about Latinos killed by police? |url=https://www.pbs.org/newshour/nation/black-men-werent-unarmed-people-killed-police-last-week |website=PBS |date=14 July 2016 |access-date=10 April 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Mejia |first1=Brittny |title=Latinos hope George Floyd protests bring focus on police violence in their community |url=https://www.latimes.com/california/story/2020-07-20/how-george-floyd-protests-affect-latino-police-shootings |website=Los Angeles Times |date=20 July 2020 |access-date=7 April 2021}}</ref> Several cases did generate national headlines.<ref name="Activists: Police killings of Latinos lack attention">{{cite web |last1=Contreras |first1=Russell |title=Activists: Police killings of Latinos lack attention |url=https://abcnews.go.com/Health/wireStory/activists-police-killings-latinos-lack-attention-72424063 |website=ABC News |access-date=6 April 2021}}</ref> Outrage was expressed when eighth-grader [[Shooting of Andy Lopez|Andy Lopez]] (13 years old) was shot and killed by the [[Sonoma County]] sheriff's deputy who mistook Lopez' toy gun for a real gun, and no criminal charges were ever filed.<ref name="It's Long Past Time">{{cite magazine |last1=Arce |first1=Julissa |title=It's Long Past Time We Recognized All the Latinos Killed at the Hands of Police |url=https://time.com/5869568/latinos-police-violence/ |magazine=Time |access-date=7 April 2021}}</ref> The death of [[Killing of Andres Guardado|Andres Guardado]] (18 years old) also sparked outrage after a [[Los Angeles Sheriff's Department]] [[whistleblower]] claimed the officer killed Guardado as part of an "[[initiation]]".<ref>{{cite web |last1=Acevedo |first1=Nicole |title=Family of Andrés Guardado, killed by sheriff's deputy, files lawsuit amid Compton station gang allegations |url=https://www.nbcnews.com/news/latino/family-andr-s-guardado-killed-sheriff-s-deputy-files-lawsuit-n1239024 |website=NBC News |date=3 September 2020 |access-date=7 April 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Tchekmedyian |first1=Alene |title=Compton Executioners deputy gang lied about guns and hosted inking parties, deputy says |url=https://www.latimes.com/california/story/2020-08-20/lasd-gangs-who-are-the-compton-executioners |website=Los Angeles Times |date=21 August 2020 |access-date=7 April 2021}}</ref> Nationally, over 900 Latinos were killed by the police in just the five years leading up to 2020.<ref name="It's Long Past Time"/><ref>{{cite web |title=Police killings tracker |url=https://www.latimes.com/projects/los-angeles-police-killings-database/ |website=Los Angeles Times |access-date=7 April 2021}}</ref> Some of these killings of young unarmed Latino men drew outrage for their sheer brutality. In the case of [[Shooting of Sean Monterrosa|Sean Monterrosa]] (22 years old) in [[Vallejo, California]], Monterrosa was apprehended, on his knees, with his hands held above his waist, when police opened fired and killed him.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Cruz Guevarra |first1=Ericka |title=Police Kill A Latino Man In California, Admit He Didn't Have Gun |url=https://www.npr.org/2020/06/05/871083536/police-kill-a-latino-man-in-california-admit-he-didnt-have-gun%3C/ |newspaper=NPR.org |publisher=KQED |access-date=7 April 2021}}</ref> In the case of [[Shooting of Alex Nieto|Alex Nieto]] (28 years old), Nieto was eating his dinner of a burrito and chips before work at a [[San Francisco]] park when a passerby called [[9-1-1]] because he considered Nieto suspicious. When the [[SFPD]] arrived, one officer shot 23 rounds at Nieto, another officer shot him 20 times, and two more officers shot Nieto at least 5 more times while he lay dying, yet no officer was criminally charged.<ref>{{cite web |title=Alex Nieto's Hands In Pockets When Fatally Shot By SFPD |url=https://sanfrancisco.cbslocal.com/2016/03/03/witness-alex-nietos-hands-in-pockets-when-fatally-shot-by-sfpd/ |website=CBS Bay Area |date=3 March 2016 |access-date=10 April 2021}}</ref><ref name="It's Long Past Time"/> In March 2021, 13-year old [[Adam Toledo]] in [[Chicago]] was chased into an alley before being shot and killed by police.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Dudek |first1=Mitch |title=Adam Toledo remembered as kid with 'big imagination' and an affinity for shows about zombies |url=https://chicago.suntimes.com/2021/4/5/22369082/adam-toledo-remembered-as-kid-big-imagination-affinity-shows-zombies |website=Chicago Sun-Times |date=5 April 2021 |access-date=7 April 2021}}</ref>
===COVID-19=== [[File:Food box packing facility during covid.jpg|thumb|right|upright|Food box packing facility during [[COVID-19 pandemic in the United States|COVID]], July 17, 2020]]
The [[COVID-19 pandemic in the United States|COVID-19 pandemic]] disproportionately impacted the Mexican American and Latino communities. Professor [[Rogelio Sáenz]] described the pandemic's effect on Tejanos and Latinos in Texas, saying, 2020 was "a hellish, hellish year. Once you adjusted for age, you really see clearly that Latinos were dying at rates more than three times as high as the white population."<ref>{{cite web |last1=Gamboa |first1=Suzanne |title='Hellish': Covid deaths have struck younger Latinos. Here's the economic, social fallout. |url=https://www.nbcnews.com/news/latino/hellish-covid-deaths-have-struck-younger-latinos-here-s-economic-n1251613 |website=NBC News |date=28 December 2020 |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref> In 2020 alone, approximately 1 in 1,150 Latinos in the United States died from [[COVID-19]].<ref>{{cite web |title=The Color of Coronavirus: COVID-19 Deaths by Race and Ethnicity in the U.S. |url=https://www.apmresearchlab.org/covid/deaths-by-race |website=APM Research Lab |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Gamboa |first1=Suzanne |title=Coronavirus is causing the 'historic decimation' of Latinos, medical expert says |url=https://www.nbcnews.com/news/latino/coronavirus-causing-historic-decimation-latinos-medical-expert-says-n1241576 |website=NBC News |date=30 September 2020 |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref> Because many Mexican Americans and Latinos disproportionately work in the [[Tertiary sector of the economy|service-sector]] and were thus considered part of the "essential" workforce, they had to bear a major part of the pandemic's force.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Andrei |first1=Mary Anne |last2=Honig |first2=Esther |title=How The Family Members Of Meat Plant Workers In Nebraska Became Advocates Amid Pandemic |url=https://www.latinousa.org/2020/08/13/familymemberssmithfield/ |website=Latino USA |date=13 August 2020 |publisher=NPR |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Branson-Potts |first1=Hailey |last2=Campa |first2=Andrew J. |last3=Reyes-Velarde |first3=Alejandra |last4=Stiles |first4=Matt |title=The price of being 'essential': Latino service workers bear brunt of coronavirus |url=https://www.latimes.com/california/story/2020-05-17/latino-essential-workers-coronavirus |website=Los Angeles Times |date=17 May 2020 |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref> Complications from [[COVID-19]] were especially dire for Latinos without [[health insurance]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Hayes-Bautista |first1=David E. |last2=Hsu |first2=Paul |title=Uninsured Working Latinos and COVID19: Essential Businesses at Risk |url=https://www.uclahealth.org/ceslac/workfiles/Research/Uninsured-Working-Latinos-andCOVID19-Apr-23.pdf |website=Center for the Study of Latino Health and Culture |publisher=UCLA Health |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref> In the economic fallout of the pandemic, many Mexican Americans and Latinos relied on [[food bank]]s and [[Unemployment insurance in the United States|unemployment insurance]] to keep their families alive.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Block |first1=Deborah |title=US Food Banks Overwhelmed with Demand During Pandemic |url=https://www.voanews.com/a/covid-19-pandemic_us-food-banks-overwhelmed-demand-during-pandemic/6199584.html |website=Voice of America |date=15 December 2020 |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Klein |first1=Aaron |last2=Shiro |first2=Ariel Gelrud |title=The COVID-19 recession hit Latino workers hard. Here's what we need to do. |url=https://www.brookings.edu/blog/how-we-rise/2020/10/01/the-covid-19-recession-hit-latino-workers-hard-heres-what-we-need-to-do/ |website=Brookings Institution |date=October 2020 |access-date=30 December 2020}}</ref>

{{Short description|Aspect of musical history}} {{Very long|date=June 2023|words=37,000}} {{Use dmy dates|date=December 2022}} [[File:Armide_Lully_by_Saint-Aubin.jpg|thumb|upright=1.2|Performance of the opera ''[[Armide (Lully)|Armide]]'' by [[Jean-Baptiste Lully]] at the [[Théâtre du Palais-Royal (rue Saint-Honoré)|Théâtre du Palais-Royal]] in Paris (1761)]] The '''history of [[opera]]''' has a relatively short duration within the context of the [[history of music]] in general: it appeared in 1597, when the first opera, ''[[Dafne]]'', by [[Jacopo Peri]], was created. Since then it has developed parallel to the various musical currents that have followed one another over time up to the present day, generally linked to the current concept of classical music.
Opera (from the Latin ''opera'', plural of ''opus'', "work") is a musical genre that combines symphonic music, usually performed by an orchestra, and a written dramatic text—expressed in the form of a [[libretto]]—interpreted vocally by singers of different [[tessitura]]: [[tenor]], [[baritone]], and [[Bass (voice)|bass]] for the male register, and [[soprano]], [[mezzo-soprano]], and [[contralto]] for the female, in addition to the so-called [[white voice]]s (those of children) or in [[falsetto]] (''[[castrato]]'', [[countertenor]]). Generally, the musical work contains [[overture]]s, [[Intermedio|interludes]] and musical accompaniments, while the sung part can be in choir or solo, [[duet]], [[Trio (music)|trio]], or various combinations, in different structures such as [[recitative]] or [[aria]]. There are various genres, such as classical opera, [[chamber opera]], [[operetta]], musical, ''[[singspiel]]'', and [[zarzuela]].<ref>{{harvtxt|''Diccionario de Música''|2003|p=206|ref=Lar}}</ref> On the other hand, as in theater, there is dramatic opera ([[opera seria]]) and comic opera ([[opera buffa]]), as well as a hybrid between the two: the ''[[dramma giocoso]]''.<ref>{{harvtxt|Souriau|1998|p=848}}</ref>
As a multidisciplinary art form, opera combines music, drama, dance, [[scenography]], costume, and makeup, relying on collaborative work between the composer, librettist, performers, conductor, and production team. Designed for live audiences, opera has historically reflected prevailing cultural, philosophical, religious, and political ideas.<ref>{{harvtxt|Souriau|1998|p=845}}</ref>
Opera originated with the ''[[Florentine Camerata]]'', a group of late 16th-century humanists who sought to revive the musical and dramatic traditions of [[Theatre of ancient Greece|Ancient Greek theater]]. This led to early works by Jacopo Peri, including ''[[Dafne]]'' (1597) and ''[[Euridice (Peri)|Euridice]]'' (1600), and [[Claudio Monteverdi]]'s ''[[Orfeo (opera)|L'Orfeo]]'' (1607), which helped define the genre by introducing structured ''[[aria]]s'' and instrumental sinfonias.
The genre evolved through major musical periods. The [[Music of the Baroque|Baroque]] era (17th–mid-18th century) established many operatic conventions and was marked by elaborate vocal and scenic elements, accessible mainly to elites. The Classical period emphasized clarity and balance, with major contributions from [[Mozart]] and [[Beethoven]]. In the 19th century, Romanticism elevated the status of composers and vocalists, reflecting bourgeois tastes and giving rise to national operatic traditions. Later developments included French [[Impressionism in music|impressionism]] and Italian [[verismo]]. The 20th century introduced Modernist approaches and new technologies—radio, phonograph, and television—which expanded opera’s reach, while earlier works remained central to repertory.
During the course of history, within opera there have been differences of opinion as to which of its components was more important, the music or the text, or even whether the importance lay in the singing and virtuosity of the performers, a phenomenon that gave rise to ''[[bel canto]]'' and to the appearance of figures such as the diva or ''[[prima donna]]''. From its beginnings until the consolidation of classicism, the text enjoyed greater importance, always linked to the visual spectacle, the lavish decorations and the complex baroque scenographies; [[Claudio Monteverdi]] said in this respect: "the word must be decisive, it must direct the harmony, not serve it." However, since the reform carried out by [[Gluck]] and the appearance of renowned composers such as Mozart, music as the main component of opera became more and more important. Mozart himself once commented: "poetry must be the obedient servant of music". Other authors, such as [[Richard Wagner]], sought to bring together all the arts in a single creation, which he called "[[total work of art]]" (''Gesamtkunstwerk'').<ref>{{harvtxt|Souriau|1998|pp=846–847}}</ref>
== Background == [[File:Medici_wedding2.jpg|thumb|250x250px|Scenario designed by [[Bernardo Buontalenti]] for the third [[intermedio]] (of six) to commemorate the wedding of [[Ferdinand I de Medici]] and [[Christina of Lorraine]] (1589). The libretto was by [[Ottavio Rinuccini]], who reused some of the material in the first opera, ''[[Dafne]]'', in 1597<ref name="Atlas-2011">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=17|ref=Atl}}</ref>]] Opera traces its origins to various forms of sung or musical theater found throughout history. Music, dance, and dramatic expression are fundamental aspects of human culture, practiced since [[prehistoric times]]. In [[Ancient Greece]], the [[Theatre of ancient Greece|theater]] was a central cultural activity. The main dramatic genres—tragedy and comedy—emerged there, along with early developments in scenography and performance. Although little Greek music survives, historical accounts confirm that theatrical performances were sung and accompanied by music. Playwrights such as [[Aeschylus]], [[Sophocles]] and [[Euripides]], established dramatic principles that continue to influence theater today.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|pp=12–13|ref=Atl}}</ref>
During the [[Middle Ages]], music and drama remained closely intertwined, particularly in religious contexts. Two primary forms emerged: [[liturgical drama]]s, performed in [[Latin]] within churches; and "[[mystery play]]", presented in vernacular languages outside church settings. These works combined spoken dialogue with choral singing, instrumental accompaniment, and sometimes dance.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|pp=14–15|ref=Atl}}</ref>
Outside Europe, similar traditions developed. In medieval Japan, the ''[[nō]]'' theater combined chanted narration, stylized gesture, and music, performed by a small ensemble of actors, singers, and instrumentalists.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=15|ref=Atl}}</ref> In China, [[Chinese opera]] evolved as a ritualistic, sung theater in which music served more to create atmosphere than to accompany the text directly.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=222|ref=DOI}}</ref>
During the [[Renaissance]], several musical-vocal genres emerged, such as the [[Madrigal (music)|madrigal]], [[oratorio]], [[intermedio]], and ''[[ballet de cour]]'', that laid the groundwork for opera. The ''intermedio'' (or ''intermezzo'') was particularly influential. These musical interludes, performed between the acts of spoken plays, integrated song, dance, instrumental music, and elaborate staging, often drawing on mythological or pastoral themes. Florence, under the patronage of the Medici, became the main center of ''intermedio'' production and the birthplace of opera.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=16|ref=Atl}}</ref>
== Origins == {{Main|Origins of opera}}
[[File:Peri-Corsi_La_Dafne.jpg|thumb|197x197px|Libretto of the first opera, ''[[Dafne]]'' (1597), by [[Jacopo Peri]] and [[Ottavio Rinuccini]]]] At the end of the 16th century, in [[Florence]], a group of scholars sponsored by Count [[Giovanni de' Bardi]] created a society called ''[[Florentine Camerata]]'', aimed to study and critical discussion of the arts, especially drama and music.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|pp=16–17|ref=Atl}}</ref> Among its members was [[Vincenzo Galilei]] —father of the scientist [[Galileo Galilei|Galileo]]— a celebrated Hellenist and musicologist, author of a method of tablature for [[lute]] and composer of madrigals and recitatives.<ref name="ETA-1986a">{{harvtxt|''Enciclopedia temática Argos. Bellas Artes II''|1986|p=143|ref=BAII}}</ref> In the course of their studies of Ancient Greek theater they found that in Greek theatrical performances the text was sung to individual voices. This idea struck them, since nothing like it existed at the time, at a time when almost all sung music was choral ([[polyphony]]) and, in cases of individual voices, occurred only in the religious sphere. They then had the idea of setting dramatic texts of a profane nature to music, which germinated in opera (''opera in musica'' was the name given to it by the Camerata).<ref name="Larousse-1990a">{{harvtxt|''Diccionario Enciclopédico Larousse''|1990|p=2324|ref=DEL}}</ref><ref group="note">Also ''dramma per musica''. —{{harvtxt|Menéndez Torrellas|2013|p=17}}</ref> Galilei was the main advocate of a single melodic line —the [[monody]]— as opposed to polyphony, which he considered to generate an incoherent musical discourse, a theory he expounded in ''Dialogo della musica antica et della moderna'' (1581). Another of the Camerata's scholars, [[Girolamo Mei]], who was the one who mainly investigated Greek theater, pointed out the emotional affect that individual singing generated in the audience in Greek theatrical performances (''De modis musicis antiquorum'', 1573).<ref>{{harvtxt|Menéndez Torrellas|2013|pp=17–20}}</ref>
Thus, one of the members of the Camerata, the composer [[Jacopo Peri]], created ''[[Dafne]]'' (1597),<ref>{{harvtxt|Comellas|2010|p=91}}</ref> with libretto by [[Ottavio Rinuccini]], based on the myth of [[Apollo and Daphne|Apollo and Dafne]], with a prologue and six scenes; of this work only the libretto and small fragments of the music are preserved. Peri, who was also a singer, represented [[Apollo]]. This was followed, in 1600, by ''[[Euridice (Peri)|Euridice]] —''by the same authors— the first surviving complete opera, on the myth of [[Orpheus and Eurydice]]. It was composed on the occasion of the wedding between [[Henry IV of France]] and [[Maria de Medici]], celebrated in Florence in 1600. Peri played [[Orpheus]].<ref name="Atlas-2011a">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=41|ref=Atl}}</ref> [[File:Jacopo_Peri_1.jpg|left|thumb|180x180px|[[Jacopo Peri]] as ''Arione'' in the fifth interlude of ''[[The Pilgrim Woman|La Pellegrina]]'' (1589)]] Another member of the Camerata, [[Giulio Caccini]], composed in 1602 ''[[Euridice (Caccini)|Euridice]]'', with the same libretto by Rinuccini as his namesake of two years earlier, with prologue and three acts.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=34|ref=Atl}}</ref> He was the author of the first theoretical treatise on the new genre, ''Le nuove musiche'' (1602).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=177|ref=DOI}}</ref> His daughter, [[Francesca Caccini]], was a singer and composer, the first woman to compose an opera: ''[[La liberazione di Ruggiero|La liberazione di Ruggiero dall'isola d'Alcina]]'' (1625).<ref name="Atlas-2011b">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=48|ref=Atl}}</ref>
One of the main novelties of this new form of artistic expression was its secularism, at a time when artistic and musical production was mostly of a religious nature. Another was the appearance of monody, the singing of a single voice, as opposed to medieval and Renaissance polyphony; it was a vocal line accompanied by a [[basso continuo]] of [[harpsichord]] or [[lute]].<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|pp=22–23|ref=Atl}}</ref><ref group="note">The basso continuo was generally formed by a keyboard instrument ([[spinet]], [[harpsichord]], organ) and one or more string or wind instruments ([[lute]], [[theorbo]], ''[[chitarrone]]'', [[viola da gamba]], cello, ''[[violone]]'', bassoon, trombone). —{{harvtxt|Menéndez Torrellas|2013|p=23}}</ref> Thus, the first operas had parts sung by soloists and parts spoken or declaimed in monody, known as ''stile rappresentativo''.<ref name="ETA-1986a" /> [[File:Orfeo_-_Toccata.ogg|thumb|''[[Toccata]]'', from ''[[La favola d'Orfeo]]'', one of the first operas (1607), composed by [[Claudio Monteverdi]]]] These first experiences were a great success, especially among the nobility: the [[Medici]] became patrons of these performances and, from Florence, they spread to the rest of Italy. The [[House of Gonzaga]] of [[Mantua]] then commissioned the famous madrigal composer [[Claudio Monteverdi]] to write an opera: ''[[La favola d'Orfeo]]'' in 1607, with libretto by [[Alessandro Striggio the Younger|Alessandro Striggio]], an ambitious work composed for orchestra of forty-three instruments —including two [[Organ (musical instrument)|organs]]—<ref name="Comellas-2010a">{{harvtxt|Comellas|2010|p=92}}</ref> with prologue and five acts. In this work, libretti were printed for the first time so that the audience could follow the performance.<ref name="Atlas-2011c">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=28|ref=Atl}}</ref> Monteverdi added a musical introduction which he called "[[symphony]]", and divided the sung parts into "[[aria]]s", giving structure to modern opera.<ref name="Atlas-2011d">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=21|ref=Atl}}</ref> These arias alternated with the [[recitative]], a musical line that included spoken and sung parts.<ref name="Atlas-2011e">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=23|ref=Atl}}</ref> He also introduced the ''[[ritornello]]'', an instrumental stanza repeated between the five acts.<ref name="Atlas-2011e" /> As for the voices, although he left some parts in polyphony, he differentiated the main solo voices: Orpheus was tenor, Eurydice soprano, and Charon bass.<ref name="Comellas-2010a" /> In 1608, Monteverdi premiered ''[[L'Arianna]]'', with libretto by Rinuccini, of which the music has not been preserved, except for one fragment: the ''[[L'Arianna#"Lamento d'Arianna"|Lamento d'Arianna]]''.<ref name="Comellas-2010a" /> [[File:Landi_sant_alessio.jpg|left|thumb|Scene from ''[[Il Sant'Alessio]]'' by [[Stefano Landi]] (1632)]] During the first decades of the 17th century the opera gradually spread: in 1608 was premiered in Mantua ''[[La Dafne]]'', again with Rinuccini's libretto, this time set to music by [[Marco da Gagliano]].<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=392|ref=DOI}}</ref> In [[Rome]], ''Rappresentazione di anima e di corpo'', by [[Emilio de' Cavalieri]], a ''melodramma spirituale'' that is often considered a precedent of opera, was premiered in 1600, and which pointed out some of the variants of Roman opera as opposed to Florentine opera: greater presence of sacred themes, the presence of allegorical characters, and greater use of choirs.<ref>{{harvtxt|Menéndez Torrellas|2013|pp=31–32}}</ref> Pope [[Paul V]] and the [[Barberini]] family sponsored the new genre: the Barberini built an auditorium for 3000 people in their [[Palazzo Barberini|palace]].<ref name="Atlas-2011f">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=40|ref=Atl}}</ref> They also promoted the creation of a school of operatists, among whom [[Stefano Landi]], author of ''[[La morte d'Orfeo]]'' (1619), which as a novelty introduced comic elements. Religious themes were also introduced, as in ''[[Il Sant'Alessio]]'' (1631), by Landi, with libretto by cardinal Giulio Rospigliosi (future pope [[Clement IX]]). In this work, Landi introduced the fast-slow-fast structure —called [[Italian overture]]— which would be widely used ever since.<ref name="Atlas-2011f" />
Another introducer of the monodic style in Rome was [[Paolo Quagliati]], who adapted old madrigals of his in opera form: ''Il carro di fedeltà d'amore'' (1606), ''La sfera armoniosa'' (1623).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=266|ref=DOII}}</ref> In the Roman school also stood out [[Luigi Rossi]], who worked for the Barberini. In 1642 he premiered in their theater ''Il palazzo incantato'', a sumptuous production that was a great success. When in 1644 the Barberini had to go into exile in Paris, Rossi accompanied them, and obtained the protection of [[Cardinal Mazarin]], thus helping to introduce opera in France. There he composed his ''[[Orfeo (Rossi)|Orfeo]]'' (1647), with prologue and three acts, a large production that featured numerous visual effects.<ref name="Atlas-2011g">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=42|ref=Atl}}</ref> Mention should also be made of [[Domenico Mazzocchi]], impresario as well as composer, who initiated the hiring of singers for opera. He was the author of ''[[La catena d'Adone]]'' (1626).<ref>{{cite web |title=MAZZOCCHI, Domenico |url=http://www.treccani.it/enciclopedia/domenico-mazzocchi_%28Dizionario-Biografico%29/ |language=italian |access-date=17 June 2022 |archive-date=17 June 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220617052649/https://www.treccani.it/enciclopedia/domenico-mazzocchi_(Dizionario-Biografico)/ |url-status=live }}</ref> Another exponent was [[Michelangelo Rossi]] (''Erminia sul Giordano'', 1633; ''Andromeda'', 1638).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2011|p=34}}</ref>
In Rome it premiered in 1639 ''[[Chi soffre, speri]]'', by [[Virgilio Mazzocchi]] and [[Marco Marazzoli]], considered the first comic opera.<ref name="Alier-2011a">{{harvtxt|Alier|2011|p=340}}</ref>
In these first works one of the first opera singers of recognized talent stood out vocally: [[Francesco Rasi]], a tenor who was already famous before the rise of opera, who participated in the first performances of Peri's ''Euridice''. In 1598 he entered the service of the Gonzaga of Mantua, for which he performed in this city Monteverdi's ''Orfeo'' in 1607; the following year he participated in Gagliano's ''Dafne''. He was also a composer, author of ''Cibele ed Ati'' (1617), whose music has been lost.<ref name="Atlas-2011b" />
== Baroque == {{Main|Baroque music}}
[[File:Ballet_de_la_nuit_1653.jpg|thumb|[[Louis XIV]] in ''Ballet Royal de la Nuit'' (1653)]] The [[Baroque]]<ref group="note">The term "baroque" comes from a word of [[Portuguese language|Portuguese]] origin (''barrôco''), whose feminine denominated the [[pearl]]s that had irregular shapes (as in Spanish the word "''barrueca''s"). The word arose in the context of [[music criticism]]: in 1750, the French essayist [[Noël-Antoine Pluche]] compared the playing of two violinists, one more serene and the other more extravagant, commenting of the latter that "he tries at all costs to surprise, to attract attention, with unbridled and extravagant sounds. It seems but that in this way it was a matter of diving to the bottom of the seas to extract ''berruecos'' (''baroc'' in French) with great efforts, while on land it would be possible to find with much more ease valuable jewels." —{{harvtxt|Comellas|2010|p=82}}</ref> it developed during the 17th century. It was a time of great disputes in the political and religious fields, in which a division emerged between the [[Catholic Church|Catholic]] [[Counter-Reformation]]ist countries, where the absolutist state was entrenched, and the [[Protestant]] countries, of a more [[Democracy|parliamentary]] sign.<ref>{{harvtxt|Azcárate Ristori|Pérez Sánchez|Ramírez Domínguez|1983|pp=479–480}}</ref>
[[Baroque music]] was generally characterized by contrast, violent chords, moving volumes, exaggerated ornamentation, varied and contrasted structure. Despite this, not all baroque music is exaggerated: [[Johann Sebastian Bach|Bach]] is an author of harmonic balance, [[Vivaldi]] creates simple and radiant melodies. The basis of baroque music is serene and balanced, with ornamentation: [[arpeggio]]s, [[mordent]]s, [[tremolo]]s, sonorous arabesques that are the salt of the baroque musician. It was also at this time that the [[tempo]]s were introduced to regulate the speed of interpretation: ''[[Largo (music)|long]]'', ''[[Adagio (music)|adagio]]'', [[Tempo#Basic tempo markings|''andante'', ''allegro'']] and ''[[Presto (music)|presto]]''; or the intensity: [[Dynamics (music)#Dynamic markings|''forte'' and ''piano'']].
During this period, the main musical centers were in the monarchic courts, aristocratic circles and episcopal sees. The instrumentation reached heights of great perfection, especially in the violin<ref>{{harvtxt|''Diccionario de Música''|2003|p=35|ref=Lar}}</ref> The Baroque orchestra was small, usually only a chamber orchestra, or about forty instruments at most. Most were [[string instruments]], plus some oboes, flutes, bassoons and trumpets. Alongside these was a section for basso continuo, usually composed of one or two [[harpsichord]]s with accompaniment by cello, double bass, [[lute]], viola or [[theorbo]].<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|pp=52–53|ref=Atl}}</ref>
Baroque opera was noted for its complicated and ornate scenography, with sudden changes and complicated lighting and sensory effects. Numerous sets were used, up to fifteen or twenty changes of scenery per performance.<ref name="Atlas-2011h">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=37|ref=Atl}}</ref> There began a taste for solo voices, mainly treble (tenor, soprano), which led in parallel to the phenomenon of the ''[[castrati]]''. The latter were children who excelled in singing and who were [[Castration|castrated]] before entering puberty so that their voice would not change; thus, as adults they maintained a high-pitched voice, close to the female one, but more powerful, flexible and penetrating.<ref name="Atlas-2011c" /><ref group="note">The last opera in which he sang a ''castrato'' was ''[[Il crociato in Egitto]]'', by [[Giacomo Meyerbeer]] (1824). Its presence continued in the sphere of the [[Catholic Church]] until the beginning of the 20th century: the voice of the last ''castrato'', [[Alessandro Moreschi]], could be recorded on [[phonograph]]. —{{harvtxt|Menéndez Torrellas|2013|p=69}}</ref> Singing in ''[[vibrato]]'' was also introduced, consisting of fluctuations in timbre, pitch and intensity, which provided greater emotionality to the interpretation.<ref name="Atlas-2011i">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=32|ref=Atl}}</ref>
In the first half of the 17th century, the rules of operatic librettos were established, which would undergo few variations until almost the 20th century: simple dialogues and conventional language, stanzas of rigorous forms, distinction between "recitative" —declaimed parts that develop the action — and "number" (or "closed piece") — ornamental parts in the form of aria, duet, choir, or other formats.
=== French opera === {{Main|French opera}}
[[File:Jean-Baptiste_Lully_1.jpeg|thumb|221x221px|[[Jean-Baptiste Lully]]]] One of the first countries where opera was introduced after Italy was France, where it was called ''[[tragédie en musique]]''.<ref>{{harvtxt|Comellas|2010|p=98}}</ref> In 1645, the first Italian opera, the pastoral ''[[La finta pazza]]'', by [[Francesco Sacrati]], was performed in Paris.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=333|ref=DOII}}</ref> Two years later Luigi Rossi's ''Orfeo'' was performed, which caused a great sensation.<ref>{{harvtxt|Oliva|Torres Monreal|2005|p=266}}</ref> Pier Francesco Cavalli premiered his ''Ercole amante'' in Paris in 1662, at the newly opened [[Tuileries Palace]] a commission of [[Cardinal Mazarin]] for the celebration of the wedding of [[Louis XIV]] with [[Maria Theresa of Spain]]. The work was not much liked, as it was in Italian and with ''castrati'', a strange phenomenon for the French public, more fond of ''[[Ballet de cour|ballets de cour]]''.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=210|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[File:Représentation_d'Alceste_de_Lully-Quinault,_Versailles,_fête_de_1674,_Le_Pautre_-_Gallica.jpg|left|thumb|Performance of ''[[Alceste (Lully)|Alceste]]'' by [[Jean-Baptiste Lully]], Jeu de Paume du Bel-Air theater, Paris, 4 July 1674.]] In 1669, the poet [[Pierre Perrin]] and the composer [[Robert Cambert]] created a company to perform operas in the French taste and obtained from King Louis XIV the royal privilege to constitute an academy, the [[Paris Opera|Académie Royale de Musique]], located in the Jeu de Paume de la Bouteille theater. Both authors produced the opera ''[[Pomone (opera)|Pomone]]'', the first written in French, premiered in 1671.<ref name="Larousse-1990a" /> However, the following year, Perrin was imprisoned for debt and the royal privilege passed to [[Jean-Baptiste Lully]], a composer of Florentine origin (his real name was Giovanni Battista Lulli).<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=30|ref=Atl}}</ref> Lully adapted opera to French taste, with choirs, ballets, a richer orchestra, musical interludes and shorter arias.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2011|p=64}}</ref> He developed [[French overture]]<ref name="Larousse-1990a" /> in a slow-fast-slow structure, unlike the Italian, which became almost autonomous pieces of the operatic ensemble.<ref name="DicMusica-2003a">{{harvtxt|''Diccionario de Música''|2003|p=207|ref=Lar}}</ref> On the other hand, he used to accompany the recitative with a [[harpsichord]] continuo bass.<ref>{{harvtxt|Comellas|2010|p=99}}</ref> At the same time, he gave greater prominence to the text, which provided greater expressiveness to the opera.<ref name="Azcarate Ristori-1983a">{{harvtxt|Azcárate Ristori|Pérez Sánchez|Ramírez Domínguez|1983|p=648}}</ref> Likewise, he adapted the singing to the prosody of the French language, creating a typically Gallic vocality called ''déclamation mélodique''.<ref>{{harvtxt|Menéndez Torrellas|2013|p=48}}</ref> From 1673 until 1687, the date of his death, he composed one opera per year-most with libretti by [[Philippe Quinault]]-among them: ''[[Cadmus et Hermione]]'' (1673), ''[[Alceste (Lully)|Alceste]]'' (1674), ''[[Atys (Lully)|Atys]]'' (1676), ''[[Proserpine (Lully)|Proserpine]]'' (1680), ''[[Persée (Lully)|Persée]]'' (1682), ''[[Phaëton (Lully)|Phaëton]]''(1683)'', [[Amadis (Lully)|Amadis]]'' (1684), ''[[Armide (Lully)|Armide]]'' (1686) and ''[[Acis et Galatée]]'' (1686). The most successful was ''[[Alceste (Lully)|Alceste]]'', based on a tragedy by Euripides and composed to celebrate the victory of Louis XIV in the [[Franche-Comté]], which included a minor plot with a comic air and some catchy melodies that greatly pleased the public.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|pp=30–31|ref=Atl}}</ref> [[File:Lully_-_Armide._Enfin,_il_est_en_ma_puissance.oga|thumb|''Finally'', from the ópera ''[[Armide (Lully)|Armide]]'' (1686), by [[Jean-Baptiste Lully]]]] Another exponent was [[Marc-Antoine Charpentier]], who musicalized some plays by [[Molière]] (''Le mariage forcé'', 1672; ''[[Le malade imaginaire]]'' [''Le malade imaginaire''], 1673) and composed several plays for the [[Comédie-Française]], such as ''Les amours de Vénus et d'Adonis'' (1678), ''[[David et Jonathas]]'' (1688) and ''La noce du village'' (1692). His masterpiece is the tragedy ''Médée(''Medea, 1693), on a play by [[Pierre Corneille]].<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|pp=38–39|ref=Atl}}</ref>
[[Marin Marais]] was a composer and performer of [[viola da gamba]]. He worked at the court of Louis XIV and was conductor of the orchestra of the Académie Royale de Musique. He composed four Lullian-style operas: ''Alcide'' (1693), ''Ariane et Bacchus'' (1695), ''[[Alcyone (opera)|Alcyone]]'' (1706) and ''[[Sémélé]]'' (1709).<ref name="Atlas-2011a"/>
After Lully's operas, and because of the French fondness for ballet, there arose the ''[[opera-ballet]]'', a hybrid genre of the two, in which sung parts with music alternated with danced interludes, with generally comic and amorous themes. This genre was introduced by [[André Campra]] with ''[[L'Europe galante]]'' (1689), which was followed by numerous works by this author, most notably ''[[Les fêtes vénitiennes]]'' (1710).<ref name="Larousse-1990a" /> Campra also produced conventional operas, such as ''[[Tancrède]]'' (1702) and ''[[Idoménée]]'' (1712).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2011|p=65}}</ref>
=== Venetian opera === [[File:Bernardo_Strozzi_-_Claudio_Monteverdi_(c.1630).jpg|thumb|213x213px|[[Claudio Monteverdi]]]] In the middle decades of the 17th century the major opera-producing center was [[Venice]], the first place where music was detached from religious or aristocratic protection to be performed in public places: in 1637 the [[Teatro San Cassiano]] was founded (demolished in 1812), the first opera center in the world, located in a palace that belonged to the Tron family.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=20|ref=Atl}}</ref> The first opera performed was ''L'Andromeda'', by [[Francesco Mannelli]].<ref name="Alier-2007a">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=514|ref=DOII}}</ref> The theater was founded by Domenico Mazzocchi, who after losing papal favor moved from Rome to the Venetian city.<ref name="Alier-2011a" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Los inicios de la ópera |url=https://laopera.net/historia-de-la-opera/los-inicios-de-la-opera |website=La Opera |date=17 August 2013 |language=spanish |access-date=17 June 2022 |archive-date=4 December 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211204160955/https://www.laopera.net/historia-de-la-opera/los-inicios-de-la-opera |url-status=live }}</ref> As opera became a business, it began to depend more and more on the tastes of the public, since by its attendance at performances it could favor a certain compositional line or relegate it to oblivion.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2011|p=36}}</ref> One of the first consequences was that the public showed more and more taste for high-pitched voices, those of tenors, sopranos and ''castrati'', while the use of choirs and large orchestras declined, since an accompaniment of few instruments was preferred, between ten and fifteen, generally. As the genre became more popular, more comic elements were introduced and the texts were simplified, to the point where, especially in the arias, the interpretation was more important than the words that were expressed.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2011|pp=39–40}}</ref> After the San Cassiano, the opening of new theaters flourished in Venice: [[Teatro Santi Giovanni e Paolo|Santi Giovanni e Paolo]] (1639), [[Teatro San Moisè|San Moisè]] (1640), [[Teatro Novissimo|Novissimo]] (1641), [[Teatro San Samuele|San Samuele]] (1655) and Santi Apostoli (1649).<ref name="Alier-2007a" /> It was in these theaters that the piazza-like layout surrounded by [[box]]es, typical of opera houses, began.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2011|p=37}}</ref> [[File:Pomodoro.jpg|left|thumb|250x250px|Scenography by [[Ludovico Ottavio Burnacini]] for the opera ''[[Il pomo d'oro]]'' by [[Marc' Antonio Cesti|Marc'Antonio Cesti]] (1668)]] In Venice the concept of ''[[bel canto]]'' emerged, which became popular especially in the first half of the 18th century and again in the first half of the 19th century in Italy. It is a vocal technique of difficult execution consisting of maintaining a uniform tone passing from one musical phrase to another without pauses, in a sustained ''[[legato]]'' that requires a powerful timbre and harmonic articulation. The main interpreters of ''[[bel canto]]'' were the ''[[castrati]]'' and the ''[[prima donna]]'' sopranos.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=24|ref=Atl}}</ref> On the other hand, at this time emerged the [[arioso]], an intermediate song between the recitative and the aria, which over time became closed numbers with a tendency to ornamentation.<ref name="Menendez Torrellas-2013">{{harvtxt|Menéndez Torrellas|2013|p=34}}</ref> Likewise, from the years 1630–1640 the [[Musical ensemble|ensemble]], the conjunction of voices in different numbers, was gaining relevance: duet, trio, quartet, quintet, sextet, septet and octet.<ref>{{harvtxt|Menéndez Torrellas|2013|p=50}}</ref>
The Venetian opera was particularly influenced by the Spanish theater of the so-called [[Spanish Golden Age|Golden Age]] —especially [[Lope de Vega]]— perceptible in the plot confluence between fantasy and reality —a path started with ''[[Don Quixote]]'' by [[Cervantes]]— the abandonment of the Aristotelian units, the coexistence of comic and tragic characters, the relationships between lords and servants, the taste for entanglement and for disguises and cross-dressed characters. In the Venetian operatic milieu these features were called ''all'usanza spagnuola.''<ref name="Menendez Torrellas-2013" />
Claudio Monteverdi settled in 1613 in Venice, where he was maestro di cappella of the [[St. Mark's Basilica]].<ref name="Atlas-2011d" /> There he composed twelve operas, the principal of which are ''[[Il ritorno d'Ulisse in patria]]'' (1640) and ''[[L'incoronazione di Poppea]]'' (1642).<ref name="Comellas-2010a" /> The latter is considered his best opera, with libretto by [[Giovanni Francesco Busenello]], based on works by [[Tacitus]] and [[Suetonius]]. It was one of the first operas with a historical rather than a mythological plot.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=29|ref=Atl}}</ref> [[File:Anna_Renzi.jpg|thumb|188x188px|[[Anna Renzi]], considered the first ''prima donna'' of the opera]] One of his disciples, [[Pier Francesco Cavalli]], introduced some novelties, such as the use of short arias, in works like ''[[L'Egisto (opera)|L'Egisto]]'' (1643), ''[[La Calisto]]'' (1651), ''[[Statira principessa di Persia]]'' (1656) and ''[[Ercole amante]]'' (1662). He was director of the Teatro San Cassiano, with a contract to write one opera a year.<ref name="Atlas-2011h" /> Another exponent was [[Antonio Cesti|Marc'Antonio Cesti]], who still further reduced the orchestra, as in ''[[Orontea]]'' (1649) and ''Cesare amante'' (1651). He performed some operas for the emperor [[Leopold I, Holy Roman Emperor|Leopold I]] (''[[Il pomo d'oro]]'', 1668), thus introducing opera into the Germanic sphere.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=38|ref=Atl}}</ref> [[Antonio Sartorio]] was for ten years maestro di cappella at the ducal court of [[Electorate of Brunswick-Lüneburg|Brunswick-Lüneburg]], until he returned to his native city and was appointed maestro di cappella of St. Mark's Basilica. He is remembered as the introducer of the [[Lamento#Music|musical parliament]]. His major work was ''[[Orfeo (Sartorio)|Orfeo]]'' (1672), which reaped great success.<ref name="Atlas-2011g" /> [[Giovanni Legrenzi]] was director of the Ospedale dei Mendicanti in Venice and maestro di cappella of St. Mark's Basilica. He composed fourteen operas for various Venetian theaters, many of which have been lost. He introduced the heroic-comic genre, as in his operas ''Totila'' (1677), ''Giustino'' (1683) and ''I due Cesari'' (1683).<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|pp=40–41|ref=Atl}}</ref> [[Pietro Andrea Ziani]] started in religious music, which he abandoned to devote himself to opera. He succeeded Cavalli as organist of San Marco. His most important works were ''L'Antigona delusa da Alceste'' (1660) and ''La Circe'' (1665). His nephew, [[Marco Antonio Ziani]], was also an operist in [[Naples]] and [[Vienna]].<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=580|ref=DOII}}</ref>
In the vocal field, [[Anna Renzi]], the first soprano to achieve the status of ''prima donna'', stood out. She played [[Claudia Octavia|Octavia]], wife of [[Nero]], in Monteverdi's ''L'incoronazione di Poppea''.<ref name="Atlas-2011b" /> The ''castrato'' [[Siface]] was also popular with the public, and counted among his admirers [[Christina of Sweden]] and [[Henry Purcell]].<ref name="Atlas-2011b" />
=== Development in Europe === [[File:Oper_am_Gänsemarkt.jpg|thumb|230x230px|[[Oper am Gänsemarkt]], Hamburg]] Throughout the seventeenth century opera spread throughout Europe, generally under Italian influence. In Germany -then divided into numerous states of varying political configuration-, the pioneer was [[Heinrich Schütz]], who in 1627 adapted Rinuccini's ''Dafne'',<ref name="Larousse-1990a" /> with a clear Monteverdian influence.<ref name="ReferenceA">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=34|ref=DOI}}</ref> In 1644, [[Sigmund Theophil Staden]] composed ''[[Seelewig]]'', the first opera in German.<ref>{{cite book |title=Seelewig |year=2002 |doi=10.1093/gmo/9781561592630.article.O007811 |isbn=978-1-56159-263-0 |url=https://www.oxfordmusiconline.com/grovemusic/view/10.1093/gmo/9781561592630.001.0001/omo-9781561592630-e-5000007811 |last1=Wade |first1=Mara R. |archive-date=30 June 2022 |access-date=17 June 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220630222411/https://www.oxfordmusiconline.com/grovemusic/view/10.1093/gmo/9781561592630.001.0001/omo-9781561592630-e-5000007811 |url-status=live }}</ref> In 1678 the [[Oper am Gänsemarkt]] in Hamburg opened, becoming the leading operatic city in the Germanic area.<ref name="DicMusica-2003a" /> In [[Munich]], the elector [[Ferdinand Maria of Bavaria]] sponsored opera, to which [[Johann Caspar Kerll]] and [[Agostino Steffani]] devoted themselves. The latter inaugurated the Schlossopernhaus in [[Hannover]] in 1689 with ''Enrico Leone''.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Historia de la cultura occidental''|1966|p=363|ref=Occ}}</ref>
The leading composer of this period was [[Reinhard Keiser]], the first to write operas entirely in German. He composed several operas that enjoyed great success, such as ''Adonis'' (1697), ''Claudius'' (1703), ''Octavia'' (1705) and ''[[Croesus (opera)|Croesus]]'' (1710). He was director of the Theater am Gänsemarkt, where he premiered on average about five operas per season; his output is estimated to be between seventy-five and one hundred operas, although only nineteen complete ones survive.<ref name="Atlas-2011j">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=39|ref=Atl}}</ref> Other authors of the period were: [[Johann Wolfgang Franck]] (''Die drey Töchter Cecrops,'' 1679)''<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|pp=382–383|ref=DOI}}</ref>'' and [[Christoph Graupner]] (''Dido, Königin von Carthage'', 1707; Bellerophon, 1708).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=438|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[File:Henry_Purcell_by_John_Closterman.jpg|left|thumb|185x185px|[[Henry Purcell]]]] In [[Austria]], Emperor [[Leopold I of Habsburg|Leopold I of the Holy Roman Empire]] encouraged opera after hearing Cesti's ''Il pomo d'oro'' (1668). Its performance took place at the [[Hofburg Imperial Palace]] in Vienna, to which only the court had access.<ref name="Alier-2007b">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=524|ref=DOII}}</ref> Most were Italian operas, although Austrian composers were gradually added, most notably [[Heinrich Ignaz Biber]]: ''Applausi festivi di Giove'' (1686), ''Chi la dura la vince'' (1687), ''[[Arminio (Biber)|Arminio]]'' (1692).<ref name="DOI126">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=126.|ref=DOI}}</ref> In 1748 the [[Burgtheater]], also located in the Hofburg, was opened. The first public theater was the [[Theater am Kärntnertor]], opened in 1761, which eventually became the official court theater.<ref name="Alier-2007b" /> [[File:Dido&Aeneas-ukr-32(air&choir).ogg|thumb|''[[Dido's Lament]]'', final aria from the opera ''[[Dido and Aeneas]]'' (1689), by [[Henry Purcell]]]] In England there was a precedent for opera, the ''[[masque]]'' (masque), which combined music, dance and theater in verse.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=99|ref=DOII}}</ref> During the [[English Revolution|Puritan Revolution]] of [[Oliver Cromwell]] music was banned. Despite this, the first English opera, ''[[The Siege of Rhodes]]'' (''The Siege of Rhodes''), was given in 1656, with libretto by playwright [[William Davenant]] and music by five composers: [[Henry Lawes]], [[Matthew Locke (composer)|Matthew Locke]], Henry Cooke, Charles Coleman and George Hudson. Subsequently, the reign of [[Charles II of England|Charles II]] saw a revival of theatrical and operatic performances.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2011|p=100}}</ref> The first opera composer of note was [[John Blow]], organist and composer of the [[Chapel Royal]]. He composed preferably religious and entertainment music for the court and was the author of an opera, ''Venus and Adonis'' (1682), a short work that included French-influenced ballets.<ref name="Atlas-2011a" /> Of particular note was his pupil, [[Henry Purcell]], author of ''[[Dido and Aeneas]]'' (1689), a French-influenced work with short arias and dances.<ref>{{harvtxt|Azcárate Ristori|Pérez Sánchez|Ramírez Domínguez|1983|p=647}}</ref> It was a commission for a college of young ladies, which they were to perform themselves, with a small instrumental ensemble. It is a short work, lasting an hour, but which Purcell resolved brilliantly: its final aria, the so-called "[[Dido's lament]]" (''When I am laid in earth''), enjoys just fame.<ref>{{harvtxt|Comellas|2010|p=95}}</ref> He also composed several semi-operas inspired by masquerades, including ''[[divertissement]]s'', songs, choirs and dances, such as ''[[Dioclesian]]'' (1690), ''[[King Arthur (Purcell)|King Arthur]]'' (1691), ''[[The Fairy-Queen|The Fairy Queen]]'' (1692), ''[[Timon of Athens]]'' (1694), ''[[The Indian Queen (opera)|The Indian Queen]]'' (1695), and ''[[The Tempest]]'' (1695).<ref name="Atlas-2011i" /> This period saw the life of the playwright [[William Shakespeare]], whose plays were adapted to opera on numerous occasions from his time to the present day.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=387|ref=DOII}}</ref> [[File:Opera_Hall_Castle_Warsaw.jpg|left|thumb|Building of the opera house of [[Ladislaus IV of Poland|Ladislaus IV]] (right) in the [[Royal Castle, Warsaw]]]] In [[Poland]], opera was sponsored by King [[Ladislaus IV of Poland|Ladislaus IV]], who had witnessed an opera performance in [[Tuscany]]. In 1632 he created a company with Italian singers, who performed several operas in [[Warsaw]]. This company premiered the first opera by a Polish author-although written in Italian-, ''La fama reale'' (1633), by Piotr Elert.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=248|ref=DOII}}</ref> [[File:Palacio_de_la_Zarzuela_(Anónimo_madrileño,_s._XVII).jpg|thumb|''[[Palacio de la Zarzuela]]'' in [[Madrid]], where the genre of ''[[zarzuela]]'' was born]] In Spain, opera arrived with some delay due to the social crisis caused by the [[Thirty Years' War]]. The first opera was premiered in 1627 at the [[Royal Alcázar of Madrid|Alcázar de Madrid]]: ''[[La selva sin amor]]'', a pastoral eclogue composed by Bernardo Monanni and Filippo Piccinini on a text by [[Lope de Vega]]; the score is not preserved.<ref name="Larousse-1990a" /> [[Tomás de Torrejón y Velasco]] composed in 1659 ''[[La púrpura de la rosa]]'', with text by [[Pedro Calderón de la Barca]], the first opera composed and performed in America, premiered at the Viceroyal Palace of [[Lima]]. [[Juan Hidalgo de Polanco|Juan Hidalgo]] was the author of ''[[Celos aun del aire matan]]'' (1660), also with text by Calderón.<ref name="Atlas-2011j" />
In the mid 17th century, King [[Philip IV of Spain|Felipe IV]] sponsored the performance of operettas at the [[Zarzuela Palace]] in Madrid, giving rise to a new genre: the [[zarzuela]].<ref name="Azcarate Ristori-1983a"/> The first zarzuelista was Juan Hidalgo, author in 1658 of ''El laurel de Apolo'', with text by Calderón, which was followed by ''Ni amor se libra de amor'' (1662, Calderón de la Barca), ''La estatua de Prometeo'' (1670, Calderón de la Barca), ''Alfeo y Aretusa'' (1672, [[Juan Bautista Diamante]]), ''Los juegos olímpicos'' (1673, Agustín de Salazar y Torres), ''Apolo y Leucotea'' (1684, Pedro Scotti de Agoiz) and ''Endimión y Diana'' (1684, Melchor Fernández de León). Subsequently, the work of [[Sebastián Durón]] stood out, with librettos by [[José de Cañizares]] (''[[Salir el amor del mundo]]'', 1696), and by [[Antonio Literes]] ''(Júpiter y Dánae'', 1708).<ref name="DicMusica-2003b">{{harvtxt|''Diccionario de Música''|2003|p=311|ref=Lar}}</ref> After the death of Philip IV, the zarzuela moved to the Teatro del Buen Retiro in Madrid.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=575|ref=DOII}}</ref>
=== Neapolitan opera === {{Main|Neapolitan School}}
[[File:Sommer,_Giorgio_(1834-1914)_-_n._0278_-_Napoli_-_Teatro_San_Carlo_2.jpg|thumb|[[Teatro San Carlo]], [[Napoli]]]] In the second half of the 17th century, the [[Neapolitan School]] introduced a more purist, classicist style, with simplified plots and more cultured and sophisticated operas. At that time, Naples was a Spanish possession, and the opera had the patronage of the Spanish viceroys, especially conde de Oñate. Among the main novelties, a distinction was made between two types of recitative: ''secco'', a declamatory song with basso continuo accompaniment; and ''accompagnato'', a melodic song with orchestral music.<ref name="DicMusica-2003a" /> In the arias, space was left for vocal improvisations by the singers. Comic characters were eliminated, which gave as a counterpart the inclusion of comic ''intermezzi'' which, already in the 18th century, gave rise to the [[opera buffa]].<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=159|ref=DOII}}</ref> In 1737, the [[Teatro San Carlo]] of Naples, one of the most prestigious in the world, was inaugurated.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=343|ref=DOII}}</ref>
One of its first composers was [[Francesco Provenzale]], author of ''Lo schiavo di sua moglie'' (1672) and ''Difendere l'offensore overo La Stellidaura vendicante'' (1674).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2011|pp=53–54}}</ref> Also considered a precursor of this type of opera is [[Alessandro Stradella]], despite the fact that he composed most of his operas in [[Genoa]]: ''Trespolo tutore'' (1677), ''La forza dell'amor patterno'' (1678), ''La gare dell'amor eroico'' (1679).<ref name="Atlas-2011k">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=43|ref=Atl}}</ref> [[File:Alessandro_Scarlatti.jpg|left|thumb|[[Alessandro Scarlatti]]]] Its main representative was [[Alessandro Scarlatti]], who was chapel master of [[Christina, Queen of Sweden]] and of the viceroy of Naples, as well as director of the Teatro San Bartolomeo in Naples.<ref name="Atlas-2011k" /> In his works he introduced numerous novelties: he created the three-part aria (''[[aria da capo]]''), with a theme-variation-theme structure (ABA);<ref name="Comellas-2010b">{{harvtxt|Comellas|2010|p=93}}</ref> he reduced vocal ornamentations and suppressed the improvisations often performed by the singers; he also reduced the recitatives and lengthened the arias, in a recitative-aria alternation, and added a shorter type of arias (''[[cavatina]]'') to give greater speed to the work, performed by secondary characters;<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2011|p=60}}</ref> on the other hand, together with his usual librettist, [[Apostolo Zeno]], he eliminated the comic characters and the interspersed plots, following only the original story. Scarlattian melody was generally simpler than Monteverdi's, but endowed with a special charm, which led Luigi Fait to comment that "with Scarlatti, Italian opera reaches the height of its beauty".<ref name="Comellas-2010b" /> There are 114 known operas by Scarlatti, including ''[[Mitridate Eupatore|Il Mitridate Eupatore]]'' (1707), ''[[Tigrane (Scarlatti)|Tigrane]]'' (1715), ''[[Il trionfo dell'onore]]'' (1718) and ''[[Griselda (Scarlatti)|Griselda]]'' (1721).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|pp=358–359|ref=DOII}}</ref> His son [[Domenico Scarlatti]] also composed operas: ''L'Ottavia restituita al soglio'' (1703), ''La Dirindina'' (1715).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=359|ref=DOII}}</ref> [[File:Alessandro_Scarlatti_-_Griselda._(BL_Add_MS_14168_f._5r).jpg|thumb|Manuscript score by [[Alessandro Scarlatti]] of the opera ''[[Griselda (Scarlatti)|Griselda]]'' (1721), act I, scene I]] Another outstanding exponent was [[Giovanni Battista Pergolesi]]. He died at the age of twenty-six, but had a successful career. In 1732 he composed his first opera, ''[[La Salustia]]'', which failed, and ''[[Lo frate 'nnamorato]]'', of comic genre, which reaped a notable success. The following year he premiered ''[[Il prigionier superbo]]'', in whose intermissions was performed the comic play ''[[La serva padrona]]'', which was more successful than the main work. In 1734 he composed ''[[Adriano in Siria (Pergolesi)|Adriano in Siria]]'', with libretto by [[Pietro Metastasio]], in whose intermissions was performed the comic play ''Livietta e Tracollo'', which was more successful than the main work. The following year he premiered ''[[L'Olimpiade]]'', still with libretto by Metastasio, and his last work, ''Il Flaminio'', of comic genre, which reaped a notable success.<ref name="Atlas-2011l">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=74|ref=Atl}}</ref>
[[Nicola Porpora]] taught singing – he had [[Farinelli]] and [[Caffarelli]] as pupils – and composition – among his pupils is [[Johann Adolph Hasse]]. He was one of the first to musicalize librettos by Pietro Metastasio. Among his early operas are ''Agrippina'' (1708), ''Arianna e Teseo'' (1714) and ''Angelica'' (1720). In 1726 he moved to Venice and, in 1733, to London, where he was musical director of the [[Opera of the Nobility]] located at the [[King's Theatre, Glasgow|King's Theatre]], where he premiered ''Arianna in Nasso'' (1733). He later worked in [[Dresden]], where he premiered ''Filandro'' (1747), as well as Vienna, before returning to Naples, where he died in poverty.<ref name="Atlas-2011l" />
Other distinguished representatives were [[Leonardo Vinci]], [[Leonardo Leo]] and [[Giovanni Bononcini]]. Vinci made his debut at the age of twenty-three with ''Lo cecato fauzo'' (''The false blind man'', 1719). In 1722 he composed ''[[Li zite 'ngalera]]'' (The lovers of the galley), of comic genre. That same year he reaped a great success with ''Publio Cornelio Scipione'', which turned him towards serious opera. In 1724, he renewed his success with Farnace, which took him to Rome and Venice. It was followed by ''Didone abbandonata'' and ''Siroe, re di Persia'', both from 1726 and with libretto by Pietro Metastasio. Until his death in 1730 he composed two operas per year, including ''Alessandro nell'Indie'' and ''Artaserse'', both from 1730 and again with librettos by Metastasio.<ref name="Atlas-2011m">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=75|ref=Atl}}</ref> Leo was an organist and church musician. His first opera was ''Il Pisistrate'' (1714), at the age of nineteen. In 1725 he was appointed organist of the chapel of the viceroy of Naples. His works were more conservative than those of Vinci and Porpora, based largely on [[counterpoint]]. Among them stands out ''L'Olimpiade'' (1737), with libretto by Metastasio. He also composed several comic operas, to which he gave respectability for the commitment he put into them.<ref name="Atlas-2011n">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=73|ref=Atl}}</ref> Bononcini had a great initial success with ''Il trionfo di Camilla'' (1696, with libretto by [[Silvio Stampiglia]]), which was performed in nineteen Italian cities. In 1697 he settled in Vienna, in the service of the emperor Joseph I, until in 1719 he was hired by the [[Royal Academy of Music]] in London. His works include ''Gli affetti più grandi vinti dal più giusto'' (1701), ''[[Griselda (Bononcini)|Griselda]]'' (1722) and ''Zenobia'' (1737).<ref name="Atlas-2011o">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=72|ref=Atl}}</ref> Mention should also be made of: [[Francesco Feo]] (''L'amor tirannico'', 1713; ''Siface'', 1723; ''Ipermestra'', 1724),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=356|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[Gaetano Latilla]] (''Angelica e Orlando'', 1735)<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=18|ref=DOII}}</ref> and Francesco Mancini Mancini (''L'Alfonso'', 1699; ''Idaspe fedele'', 1710; ''Traiano'', 1721).<ref name="DOII79">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=79.|ref=DOII}}</ref>
== Late Baroque: opera seria and buffa == {{Main|Opera seria|Opera buffa}}
[[File:La_Serva_Padrona.jpg|thumb|''[[La serva padrona]]'' by [[Giovanni Battista Pergolesi]] (1733), contemporary edition of the libretto]] The musical production of the first half of the 18th century is usually called late Baroque. The main center of production continued to be Italy, especially Naples and Venice. In 1732 the [[Teatro Argentina]] in Rome was inaugurated.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=309|ref=DOII}}</ref> Italian influence spread throughout Europe: Italian companies were established at the Dresden Opera House, at the court of the [[Electorate of Saxony|Elector of Saxony]] and at the Royal Academy of Music in London. The only non-Italian national school was in France.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=52|ref=Atl}}</ref> During this period, scenography developed notably, thanks above all to the Italian [[Ferdinando Galli-Bibiena|Ferdinando Galli Bibbiena]] —whose work was continued by his sons Antonio and [[Giuseppe Galli da Bibiena|Giuseppe]]— who introduced theatrical perspective and numerous stage effects.<ref name="DOI126" /> Between 1700 and 1750 was the era of classical ''bel canto'', when the stages were dominated by ''castrati'' and ''prime donne''.<ref>{{harvtxt|Menéndez Torrellas|2013|p=68}}</ref>
Around the second quarter of the 18th century opera was gradually divided into two contrasting genres: the [[opera seria]] and the [[opera buffa]].<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=53|ref=Atl}}</ref> The themes of opera seria were generally drawn from classical mythology, with a moralistic component, showing the more virtuous side of the heroes of old age.<ref name="Atlas-2011p">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=54|ref=Atl}}</ref> Generally, the action was centered on the recitatives and the arias were left for lyrical incursions by the singers, with a preference for the ''da capo'' aria, which experienced its golden age.<ref name="Atlas-2011q">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=84|ref=Atl}}</ref> Serious opera wanted to strip lyrical dramas of the extravagances and convoluted plots used until then, with a more sober style inspired by ancient Greek theater. One of its major theorists was [[Giovanni Vincenzo Gravina]], co-founder of the [[Academy of Arcadia]].<ref name="Atlas-2011r">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=82|ref=Atl}}</ref>
However, the disappearance of comic characters left a certain void in a sector of the public, generally lower class, who liked these characters. To satisfy them, ''intermezzi'' were introduced in the intermissions of the plays, which gradually gained success until they became separate works.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2011|p=70}}</ref> A good example was ''[[La serva padrona]]'' by Pergolesi (1733). From then on these ''[[intermezzi]]'' became independent and became shows of their own. The opera buffa was of comic genre, intended for a more popular audience, influenced by the ''[[Commedia dell'arte]]''. It included arias and recitatives, but with second-rank performers, who sang with their natural voices, without ''[[castrati]]'' or sopranos. The arias were shorter and the recitatives kept the music, unlike in Spanish zarzuela or German ''[[singspiel]]'', which included only spoken parts. There were also duets, trios and quartets, absent from opera seria.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=85|ref=Atl}}</ref> In opera buffa a type of rapid, syllabic declamation called ''[[parlando]]'' was characteristic. Also frequent was the presence of stuttering, yawning or sneezing.<ref>{{harvtxt|Menéndez Torrellas|2013|p=86}}</ref> The music, generally stringed, was simple, of small orchestras, although over time it would be equated with that of opera seria. Eventually, both genres tended to merge, with the appearance of the [[Opera semiseria|semi-serious opera]] or ''[[dramma giocoso]]''.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=83|ref=Atl}}</ref> [[File:Metastasio_by_Batoni.jpg|left|thumb|245x245px|[[Pietro Metastasio]]]] Some of the characteristic elements of the opera buffa were: the use of the recitative ''accompagnato'', greater use of choir and ensembles, and the use of the cavatina and the aria ''con pertichini'', a modality based on an aria -generally ''da capo'' – to which were added comments in recitative by characters who watched the scene from outside, generating a form of ensemble. The ''introduzione'' and the ''[[Finale (music)|finale]]'', scenes that usually brought together all the characters of the work, singing in ensemble, also became more relevant.<ref>{{harvtxt|Menéndez Torrellas|2013|pp=92–101}}</ref>
In the field of opera seria, a unique phenomenon in the history of opera took place in this period: librettists were more relevant than composers, especially two: [[Apostolo Zeno]] and [[Pietro Metastasio]].<ref name="Atlas-2011r" /> Zeno was a Venetian historian and librarian who, after dabbling in the field of opera libretti, was always eager to have them considered a literary genre of the first rank. In 1718, he replaced [[Silvio Stampiglia]] as imperial poet of [[Charles VI, Holy Roman Emperor|Charles VI]], for whom he was also imperial historiographer. He introduced historical themes into opera and, in his plots, always incorporated a code of honor to serve as an example for the public. He wrote thirty-five librettos, some of them in collaboration with [[Pietro Pariati]], which were set to music by composers such as [[Albinoni]], [[Caldara]], [[Francesco Gasparini|Gasparini]], [[Alessandro Scarlatti|Scarlatti]] and [[Händel]].<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=77|ref=Atl}}</ref> A generation younger, Metastasio was directly influenced by Zeno, whom he replaced as imperial poet. In a fifty-year career he wrote twenty-seven librettos that were set to music in some one hundred operas. He was a great reformer of operatic writing, to the point that one often speaks of "metastasian opera" for his works. His librettos were elegant and sophisticated, and he gave greater prominence to the soloists.<ref name="Atlas2011s">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=111|ref=Atl}}</ref> Zeno and Metastasio introduced the so-called "doctrine of the affections", whereby every emotion (love, hate, sadness, hope, despair) was expressed by a certain musical form (aria, chorale, instrumental movement).<ref name="Atlas-2011t">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=105|ref=Atl}}</ref> [[File:Vivaldi.jpg|thumb|[[Antonio Vivaldi]]]] Venice remained one of the main operatic centers. [[Antonio Vivaldi]], the main representative of the Italian late-baroque school, stood out. He was chapel master of the [[Ospedale della Pietà]] in Venice, where he was able to rely on the orphans of the hospice to form a large orchestra, for which he is considered the first great master of orchestral music.<ref>{{harvtxt|Comellas|2010|pp=105–106}}</ref> He devoted himself to opera both as a composer and impresario. In 1739 he claimed to have written ninety-four operas, although only about fifty librettos and twenty scores survive. Notable among his works are: ''[[Ottone in villa]]'' (1713), ''[[Orlando finto pazzo]]'' (1714), ''La costanza trionfante degl'amori e degl'odii'' (1716), ''[[Teuzzone]]'' (1719), ''[[Tito Manlio]]'' (1719), ''[[Giustino (Vivaldi)|Il Giustino]]'' (1724), ''[[Farnace]]'<nowiki/>'' (1727), ''[[Orlando furioso (Vivaldi)|Orlando furioso]]''(1727), ''[[Bajazet (opera)|Bajazet]]'' (1735), ''Ginevra'' (1736) and ''[[Catone in Utica]]'' (1737).<ref name="Atlas-2011m" />
[[Tomaso Albinoni]] was a brilliant composer, famous for his ''[[Albinoni's Adagio|Adagio]]'', although he also dabbled in opera. Concerned with the harmony of the vocal ensemble, he provided serenity and balance to the slow movements, with special attention to the lower voices.<ref>{{harvtxt|Comellas|2010|p=105}}</ref> His works include ''Zenobia, regina dei Palmireni'' (1694), ''[[Pimpinone]]'' (1708) and ''Didone abbandonata'' (1724).<ref name="ReferenceB">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=31|ref=DOI}}</ref> Other Venetian composers of the period were: [[Ferdinando Bertoni]] (''Orfeo ed Euridice'', 1776; ''Quinto Fabio'', 1778)<ref name="ReferenceC">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=124|ref=DOI}}</ref> and [[Francesco Gasparini]] (''Roderico'', 1694; ''Ambleto'', 1705; ''Il Bajazet'', 1719).<ref name="Alier-2007c">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=400|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[File:Tommaso_Traetta_-_Olimpiade_-_titlepage_of_the_libretto_-_Florenz_1767.png|left|thumb|Cover from the libretto of ''L'Olimpiade'' (1767), by [[Tommaso Traetta]] ''(1767),'' by [[Tommaso Traetta]]]] The Neapolitan school also continued, where the split between opera seria and opera buffa took place. Its main representatives in this period were [[Niccolò Jommelli]] and [[Tommaso Traetta]]. Jommelli premiered his first operas in Naples: ''L'errore amoroso'' (1737), ''Ricimero'' (1740) and ''Astianatte'' (1741). After reaping fame for these works he was appointed chapel master of St. Peter's at the Vatican in 1749 and, four years later, the same position in [[Stuttgart]], for the [[Duchy of Württemberg|duke of Württemberg]]. In Germany he composed thirty-three operas, and his style became so Germanized that on his return to his homeland his works were rejected by the public.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=106|ref=Atl}}</ref> Traetta was influenced by [[gluck]]ian influence, which he combined with his prodigious ability to compose chorales and elegant melodies. His first compositions were for opera houses in Naples and [[Parma]] (''Ippolito e Aricia'', 1759; ''Iphigenia in Tauride'', 1763). In 1765 he moved to Venice and, in 1768, to Russia, where he was appointed opera director by Empress [[Catherine the Great|Catherine II]]. There he composed, among others, ''Antigone'' (1772).<ref name="Atlas-2011u">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=109|ref=Atl}}</ref> [[File:Retrato_del_compositor_Domènec_Terradellas_(1713_-_1751).jpg|thumb|245x245px|[[Domingo Terradellas]]]] The Spanish [[Domingo Terradellas]] (also known in Italian as Domenico Terradeglias) is considered to belong to the Neapolitan school. He studied in Naples, where he was a pupil of [[Francesco Durante]]. In 1736 he premiered his first opera, ''Giuseppe riconosciuto''. He moved to Rome, where he was chapel master of [[Nostra Signora del Sacro Cuore|San Giacomo degli Spagnoli]] and premiered ''Astarto'' (1739), ''Merope'' (1743) and ''Semiramide riconosciuta'' (1746). He then went to London, where he composed ''Mitridate'' (1746) and ''Bellerofonte'' (1747). Back in Italy, he premiered in [[Turin]] ''Didone'' (1750), in Venice ''Imeneo in Atene'' (1750) and in Rome ''Sesostri'' (1751).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=464|ref=DOII}}</ref>
Mention should also be made of [[Gian Francesco de Majo]] and [[Davide Perez]]. The former began with ''Ricimero, re dei Goti'' (1759) and ''L'Almeria'' (1761). He spent some time in [[Mannheim]], where he premiered ''Iphigenia in Tauride'' (1764), which influenced [[Gluck]]. Back in Naples, he made ''L'Ulisse'' (1769) and ''L'eroe cinese'' (1770).<ref name="DOII74">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=74|ref=DOII}}</ref> Perez, of Spanish origin, composed some buffa operas (''La nemica amante'', 1735), but mainly serious ones: ''[[Siroe (Metastasio)|Siroe]]'' (1740), ''Astarto'' (1743), ''Merope'' (1744), ''La clemenza di Tito'' (1749), ''Demofoonte'' (1752), ''Olimpiade'' (1754), ''Solimano'' (1757).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=230|ref=DOII}}</ref> Other representatives of the Neapolitan school were: [[Girolamo Abos]] (''Artaserse'', 1746; ''Alessandro nell'Indie'', 1747),<ref name="ReferenceD">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=17|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[Pasquale Cafaro]] (''La disfatta di Dario'', 1756; ''Creso'', 1768),<ref name="DOI178">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=178.|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[Ignazio Fiorillo]] (''L'Olimpiade'', 1745),<ref name="DOI368">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=368.|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[Pietro Alessandro Guglielmi]] (''L'Olimpiade'', 1763; ''Farnace'', 1765; ''Sesostri'', 1766; ''Alceste'', 1768),<ref name="ReferenceE">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=449|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[Giacomo Insanguine]] (''L'osteria di Marechiaro'', 1768; ''Didone abbandonata'', 1770)<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|pp=499–500|ref=DOI}}</ref> and [[Antonio Sacchini]] (''[[La contadina in corte]]'', 1765).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=332|ref=DOII}}</ref>
From the rest of Italy it is worth mentioning [[Antonio Caldara]]. He was maestro de cappella in Mantua and Rome before being appointed vice maestro de cappella of Emperor Charles VI in Vienna, where he remained for about twenty years. He collaborated regularly with the librettists Zeno and Metastasio. His work is notable for its use of large chorales.
