[[Virgil Thomson]] was the author of what is considered the first great American opera, ''[[Four Saints in Three Acts]]'' (1928), with libretto by [[Gertrude Stein]]. The two collaborated again in ''[[The Mother of Us All]]'' (1947), based on the life of the feminist [[Susan B. Anthony]]. His third and final opera was ''[[Lord Byron]]'' (1972).<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=301|ref=Atl}}</ref> [[Henry Kimball Hadley]] studied in [[Boston]] and Vienna, and was conductor of the [[Seattle Symphony]]. His operas include ''[[Safie]]'' (1909), ''Azora'' (1917) and ''[[Cleopatra's Night]]'' (1919). In 1933 he premiered ''A Night in Old Paris'' on radio.<ref name="DOI456">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=456.|ref=DOI}}</ref>
[[George Gershwin]] combined classical music with elements of American popular music, especially jazz and blues. In 1922 he premiered the jazz-opera ''Blue Monday'', which was not very successful. His next project was ''[[Porgy and Bess]]'' (1935), a folk-opera incorporating jazz and blues rhythms, with a libretto by his brother, [[Ira Gershwin]], and [[DuBose Heyward]]. It was not very well received initially, although over time it grew in prestige until it was considered one of the best American operas.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=287|ref=Atl}}</ref>
==== Baltic countries ==== [[File:Opera_Nacional,_Riga,_Letonia,_2012-08-07,_DD_04.JPG|thumb|[[Latvian National Opera]], [[Riga]]]] The current [[Baltic republics]] had a period of independence between 1918 and 1940, to be then absorbed by the [[Soviet Union]], until they became independent again in 1991. In [[Estonia]], the first opera in the local language was ''Sabina'' (1906), by [[Artur Lemba]]. Notable in the interwar period was [[Evald Aav]], author of ''Vikerlased'' (''The Vikings'', 1928).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|pp=338–339|ref=DOI}}</ref>
[[Latvia]] was in the Germanic cultural sphere, so this influence predominated in the origin of its operatic tradition. In 1919 the [[Latvian National Opera]] (Latvijas Nacionālā Opera) opened in [[Riga]], where the Latvian opera ''Banjuta'' by [[Alfrēds Kalniņš]] premiered in 1920. His son Jānis Kalniņš was the author of ''Hamlet'' (1936). Other exponents of Latvian opera were the brothers [[Jānis Mediņš]] (''Uguns un nakts'' [''Fire and Night''], 1921) and Jāzeps Mediņš (''Vaidelote'' [''The Vestal''], 1927).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=32|ref=DOII}}</ref>
[[Lithuania]], closer to Poland, received like Poland the initial influence of Italian opera. In 1922 the Lithuanian National Theater (Lietuvos Tautas Teatras) was founded in [[Vilnius]]. The first Lithuanian opera was [[Birutė (opera)|''Birutė'']] (1906) by [[Mikas Petrauskas]]. Subsequent highlights included [[Antanas Račiūnas]]' (''Trys talismanai'' [''Three Talismans''], 1936) and [[Jurgis Karnavičius (composer)|Jurgis Karnavičius]] (''Gražina'', 1932).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|pp=45–46|ref=DOII}}</ref>
==== Balkan countries ==== [[File:IvanVazov_National_Theatre_7.jpg|thumb|[[Ivan Vazov National Theater|Bulgarian National Theater]], [[Sofia]]]] [[Bulgaria]] became independent from the [[Ottoman Empire]] in 1885. In 1908 the [[Ivan Vazov National Theater|Naroden Teatar]] (National Theater) in [[Sofia]] was founded. The first opera in Bulgarian was ''Shiromakhinya'' (''The Poor Woman'', 1910), by [[Emanuil Manolov]]. The main architect of the Bulgarian national opera was Georgi Atanasov, a pupil of [[Pietro Mascagni]] in Italy. He was the author of ''Borislav'' (1911), ''Gergana'' (1917), ''Zapustyalata vodenitsa'' (''The Abandoned Mill'', 1923) and ''Tsveta'' (''The Flower'', 1923). Other exponents were [[Ivan Ivanov (singer)|Ivan Ivanov]], author of ''Kamen i Tsena'' (''The Stone Pyramid'', 1911); Dimitar Hadjigeorgiev (''Takhin Begovitsa'', 1911); and [[Pancho Vladigerov]] (''Tsar Kalojan'', 1936).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|pp=168–169|ref=DOI}}</ref>
[[Romania]] gained independence from the Ottoman Empire in 1878, after which it joined European cultural life. The closeness of Romanian – a language of Latin origin – to Italian favored the spread of Italian opera.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=326|ref=DOII}}</ref> Among the pioneers was [[Ciprian Porumbescu]], author of the operetta ''Crai nou'' (''New Moon'', 1880). Later [[Nicolae Bretan]] (''Luceafărul'', 1921) and, mainly, [[George Enescu]], trained in France, where he was a pupil of [[Gabriel Fauré]] and [[Jules Massenet]]. In 1917 he composed his opera ''Œdipe'' (''Oedipus''), whose manuscript he lost during a trip, so he had to rewrite it and finally premiered it at the Paris Opera in 1936. It was a grandiloquent work, requiring three groups of timpani and machinery to simulate wind.<ref name="Atlas-2011ap">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=286|ref=Atl}}</ref>
[[Croatia]] was an integral part of the Austro-Hungarian Empire, until the [[Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes]] (since 1929, [[Yugoslavia]]) was formed in 1918. It became independent in 1991.<ref name="DOI255">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=255.|ref=DOI}}</ref> In 1895, the National Theatre in [[Zagreb]] opened.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=570|ref=DOII}}</ref> The first composer to write in his language was [[Vatroslav Lisinski]]: ''Ljubav i zloba'' (''Love and malice'', 1846).<ref name="DOII44">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=44.|ref=DOII}}</ref> Later, [[Krešimir Baranović]] was the author of comic operas, such as ''Nevjesta od Cetingrada'' (''The Bride of Cetingrada'', 1942).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=88|ref=DOI}}</ref> Other authors were: [[Blagoje Bersa]] (''Der Eisenhammer'' [''The Iron Hammer''], 1911; ''Der Schuster von Delft'' [''The Shoemaker of Delft''], 1914),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=123|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[Jakov Gotovac]] (''[[Ero s onoga svijeta]]'' [''Ero from the Otherworld''], 1935)<ref name="DOI431"/> and [[Ivan Zajc]] (''Nikola Šubić Zrinjski'', 1876).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=571|ref=DOII}}</ref>
[[Slovenia]] had the same historical development as Croatia. In 1892, the [[Ljubljana Opera]] was founded. The first opera in Slovenian is due to Jakob Frančišek Zupan (''Belin'', 1782), now lost.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Zupan, Jakob Frančišek (1734–1810) – Slovenska biografija |url=https://www.slovenska-biografija.si/oseba/sbi880527/ |access-date=2022-06-23 |website=www.slovenska-biografija.si}}</ref> Throughout the 19th century some Slovene composers occasionally devoted themselves to opera, with poor results. In the transition to the 20th century, [[Risto Savin]] stood out (''Lepa Vida'' [''Beautiful Life''], 1907).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=47|ref=DOII}}</ref>
In [[Serbia]], under Ottoman rule, opera was not introduced until the last quarter of the 19th century. It became independent in 1882, to become part after World War I of the Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes, later Yugoslavia. In 1894 the National Theater (Pozorište Narodno) of [[Belgrade]] was founded. The pioneer of Serbian opera was [[Petar Konjović]] (''Koštana'', 1931).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|pp=108–109|ref=DOI}}</ref>
The [[San Giacomo theater]] on [[Corfu]], began hosting a regular opera season as early as 1771 and in 1791, the Ionian-Greek composer [[Stefano Pogiago]] premiered his opera ''Gli amanti confusi'' there.<ref name="Greeknationalopera">{{Cite web |last= Ignatidou|first= Artemis|title=Opera in Greece|url=https://operabox.nationalopera.gr/en/ti-einai-i-opera/i-opera-stin-ellada |website=operabox.nationalopera.gr}}</ref>
[[Greece]] became independent from the Ottoman Empire in 1830. Soon several Italian companies were established and a taste for opera began. In the 19th century, several Greek cities established theatres that staged both drama and opera. In 1864, the [[Apollon Theater, Syros|Apollon Theatre]] opened on the island of [[Syros]], followed in 1872 by Patras inaugurating its own Apollon Theatre. Later, in 1888 the [[Greek National Opera|Greek Opera]] was founded in [[Athens]], since 1939 Royal Opera and, nowadays, National Opera. Finally, in 1895, the city of Piraeus introduced the Piraeus Municipal Theatre.<ref name="Greeknationalopera"/> The most significant privately-run Greek opera company was the ''Hellenic Melodrama'', operated from 1888 until 1943.<ref name="Greek-National-Opera">{{Cite web |title=History - Greek National Opera|url=https://www.nationalopera.gr/en/greek-national-opera/history|website=nationalopera.gr}}</ref> In 1858, the Greek composer [[Pavlos Carrer]] wrote his opera ''Marco Bozzari'', originally set to an Italian libretto and translated into Greek shortly thereafter. He later followed it with the operas ''Kyra Frosini'' (premiered 1868) and ''Despo'' (premiered 1882). [[Spyridon Xyndas]], composed some operas in Italian and also the first opera to be set originally to a Greek libretto called ''O ypopfisios vouleftis'' [''The Parliamentary Candidate''] (premiered 1888 in Athens).<ref name="Greeknationalopera"/> Subsequent highlights included [[Manolis Kalomiris]] (''O Protomastoras'' [''The Master Builder''], 1916), Dionysios Lavrangas (''Ta dyo adelfia'', [''The Two Brothers''], 1900; ''Dido'', 1909) and [[Theophrastos Sakellaridis]] (''O Vaftistikos'' [''The Godson''], 1918).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|pp=439–440|ref=DOI}}</ref> Greek composers frequently developed successful careers beyond the limited Greek opera scene. For example, [[Spyridon Samaras]] operas, such as ''Flora Mirabilis'' (1866) and ''La Martire'' (1894) were well received in Italy and [[Napoleon Lambelet]] who spent several years in London’s West End.<ref name="Greeknationalopera"/>
==== Turkey and Caucasus countries ==== [[File:Azerbaijan_State_Academic_Opera_and_Ballet_Theatre_main_façade,_2015.jpg|thumb|[[Azerbaijan State Academic Opera and Ballet Theatre]], [[Baku]]]] In the [[Ottoman Empire]] (present-day [[Turkey]]) there was presence of Italian companies since the end of the 18th century. The first Turkish opera premiered at the Vienna Volksoper in 1918: ''Kenan çobanları'', by Vedî Sabra. With the process of Europeanization initiated by [[Kemal Atatürk]] in the 1920s, opera became more widespread in the country: in 1929, ''Köyde bir facia'', by [[Cemal Reşit Rey]] was premiered; in 1934, [[Ahmet Adnan Saygun]] received an official commission for an opera for the visit of [[Reza Shah|shah of Persia]], for which he composed ''[[Özsoy]]''.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=496|ref=DOII}}</ref>
In [[Armenia]], whose territory was divided between Turkey and Russia, opera enjoyed great popularity. The first Armenian opera was due to Tigran Chukhachean (''Arshak Erkrod'', 1868). Subsequently, Armen Tigranian (''Anusha'', 1912) and [[Alexander Spendiaryan]] (''Almast'', 1928) stood out.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=63|ref=DOI}}</ref>
In Georgia, Italian opera was introduced in the mid-19th century. The first Georgian composer of some renown was [[Meliton Balanchivadze]], author of ''Daredžan Tsviery'' (''Daredžan the Sly'', 1898). Later, [[Zakharia Paliashvili]] (''Abesalom da Eteri'', 1919) and [[Iona Tuskiya]], author of ''Rodina'' (''Homeland'', 1939) stood out.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=407|ref=DOI}}</ref>
In [[Azerbaijan]] there was a traditional genre of sung music, the ''[[mugam]]''. Opera was introduced in the late 19th century: the first Azerbaijani opera ''Leyli and Medzhnun'' (1908), by [[Uzeyir Hajibeyov]], is considered to be the first Azerbaijani opera. In 1910 the [[Tagiyev's Theater]] (now [[Azerbaijan State Academic Opera and Ballet Theater]]) in [[Baku]] opened, which promoted local opera, led by [[Muslim Magomayev (composer)|Müslüm Maqomayev]] (''Shah Ismail'', 1919).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=79|ref=DOI}}</ref>
==== Portugal ==== In [[Portugal]], Italian opera predominated during the 19th century, with few local productions. In 1793, the [[Teatro Nacional de São Carlos|Teatro São Carlos]] in Lisbon was inaugurated. The first prominent composer was [[José Augusto Ferreira Veiga]] (''L'elisir di giovinezza'', 1876; ''Dina la derelitta'', 1885).<ref name="Alier-2007k" /> In a ''tardo romanticismo'' he emphasized [[Alfredo Keil]] (''Dona Branca'', 1883; ''Irene'', 1893; ''Serrana'', 1899).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=534|ref=DOI}}</ref>
==== Spain ==== {{Main|Spanish opera}}
[[File:Liceu1847Norma.jpg|thumb|Performance of ''[[Norma (opera)|Norma]]'' by [[Vincenzo Bellini]] at the [[Liceu|Gran Teatre del Liceu]] in Barcelona (1847)]] In Spain it is not possible to speak of nationalism as such: a Spanish nationality was not claimed against a foreign dominator, no patriotic identities or lost cultural essences were asserted, no formulas from the past were sought, neither popular legends nor traditional folklore were resorted to. On the other hand, the different regional modalities of popular music were used ([[Andalusia]]n, [[Asturias|Asturian]], Aragonese, [[Catalonia|Catalan]], Basque, Galician), together with a certain orientalist influence fashionable in Europe and considered here as more genuine because of the [[Al-Andalus|Andalusian]] past of the [[Iberian Peninsula]].<ref>{{harvtxt|Comellas|2010|pp=286–288}}</ref> During practically the first half of the century the influence of Italian opera, especially Rossinian opera, continued. Some composers followed that line, such as [[Baltasar Saldoni]] and [[Hilarión Eslava]]. In 1832 the [[Teatro Principal (Valencia)|Teatro Principal]] in [[Valencia]] was inaugurated, in 1847 the [[Liceu|Gran Teatro del Liceo]] in [[Barcelona]] and, in 1850, the [[Teatro Real]] in [[Madrid]].<ref name="Larousse-1990a" />
The pioneer of opera in Spain was [[Felipe Pedrell]], one of the fathers—with [[Francisco Asenjo Barbieri]]—of modern Spanish musicology. In his first works, some of them in Italian, he showed the influence of Italian opera, as in ''L'ultimo abenzerraggio'' (''The last abenzerrage'', 1874), ''[[Quasimodo]]'' (1875) and ''[[Cleopatra]]'' (1878). Later he denoted Wagnerian influence: ''[[Els Pirineus|I Pirinei]]'' (''The Pyrenees'', 1902), ''[[La Celestina (opera)|La Celestina]]'' (1904).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=226|ref=DOII}}</ref> [[File:Breton_La_Dolores_Romero_BDH.jpg|left|thumb|223x223px|Poster for ''[[La Dolores]]'' (1895), by [[Tomás Bretón]]]] Other pioneers were [[Ruperto Chapí]] and [[Tomás Bretón]]. Chapí studied in Paris thanks to a scholarship obtained after composing the short opera ''Las naves de Cortés'' (1874). In 1876 he premiered ''La hija de Jefté'' at the Teatro Real. From then on he devoted himself mainly to zarzuela, but still composed several operas, such as ''[[Roger de Flor (opera)|Roger de Flor]]'' (1878), ''Circe'' (1902) and ''Margarita la tornera'' (1909).<ref name="Alier-2007x">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=217|ref=DOI}}</ref> Bretón studied in Italy and Vienna. He began with the short opera ''Guzmán el Bueno'' (1878), which was followed by ''Los amantes de Teruel'' (1889), ''Garín'' (1892), ''[[La Dolores]]'' (1895) and ''Raquel'' (1900).<ref name="Alier-2007y">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=158|ref=DOI}}</ref> In some of his works he introduced Spanish folk music, such as a [[sardana]] in ''Garín'' and a [[Jota (music)|jota]] in ''La Dolores''.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|1983|p=94}}</ref> [[File:Intermezzo_from_Goyescas_-_U.S._Marine_Band.ogg|thumb|Interlude of ''[[Goyescas (opera)|Goyescas]]'' (1916), by [[Enrique Granados]]]] Among the great composers of the period are [[Isaac Albéniz]], [[Enrique Granados]] and [[Manuel de Falla]]. Albéniz began in zarzuela, until he signed a contract with an English banker to write operas, the fruit of which were ''The Magic Opal'' (1893), ''[[Henry Clifford (opera)|Henry Clifford]]'' (1895), ''[[Pepita Jiménez (opera)|Pepita Jiménez]]'' (1896) and ''[[Merlin (Albéniz)|Merlin]]'' (1897–1902), the latter on a cycle based on [[King Arthur]] of which he only made this first title.<ref name="DOI30">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=30.|ref=DOI}}</ref> Granados achieved fame with his piano pieces. In 1898 he premiered his opera ''[[María del Carmen (opera)|María del Carmen]]'', close to zarzuela. Later, he adapted several compositions for piano in his opera ''[[Goyescas (opera)|Goyescas]]'' (1916), which he premiered at the Metropolitan in New York.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=436|ref=DOI}}</ref> Falla, one of the best modern Spanish composers, combined musical nationalism —with some influence of the Norwegian [[Edvard Grieg]]— with [[impressionism]], which he met during a stay in France.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Diccionario de Música''|2003|p=114|ref=Lar}}</ref> Although he was not very prolific in opera, he left two notable works: ''[[La vida breve (opera)|La vida breve]]'' (1913) and ''[[El retablo de Maese Pedro]]'' (1923). He began an adaptation of ''[[L'Atlàntida|La Atlántida]]'' by [[Jacinto Verdaguer]], which was completed by [[Ernesto Halffter]] and premiered in 1962 as ''[[Atlántida (opera)|Atlántida]]''.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=346|ref=DOI}}</ref>
Other exponents were: [[Enric Morera i Viura|Enric Morera]], who began in opera with ''La fada'' (1897), in Wagnerian style. In 1906 he premiered two works at the Liceo de Barcelona, ''Bruniselda'' and ''Emporium'', followed by ''Titaina'' (1912) and ''Tassarba'' (1916).<ref name="DOII142">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=142.|ref=DOII}}</ref> [[Amadeo Vives]] was a pupil of Pedrell and, together with [[Lluís Millet]], founder of the [[Orfeó Català]]. In 1897 he premiered his first opera, ''Artús'', followed by ''Euda d'Uriac'' (1900), ''Colomba'' (1910), ''[[Maruxa]]'' (1914) and ''Balada de Carnaval'' (1919).<ref name="DicMusica-2003d">{{harvtxt|''Diccionario de Música''|2003|p=300|ref=Lar}}</ref> [[Jesús Guridi]] was a professor of organ at the Madrid Conservatory. He composed two operas: ''Mirentxu'' (1910, in Basque) and ''Amaya'' (1920).<ref name="Alier-2007z">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=453|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[Jaume Pahissa]], a pupil of Enric Morera, wrote several operas in Catalan, such as ''Gal-la Placídia'' (1913), ''La morisca'' (1919), ''Marianela'' (1923) and ''La princesa Margarida'' (1928).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|pp=207–208|ref=DOII}}</ref> [[Joaquín Turina]] met Falla and Albéniz in Paris, through whose influence he turned to Spanish national music, with a certain impressionist influence. His most relevant opera is ''[[Jardín de Oriente]]'' (1923).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=495|ref=DOII}}</ref>
Also worth mentioning are: [[Conrado del Campo]] (Fantochines, 1923; Lola, la piconera, 1950),<ref>{{harvtxt|''Diccionario de Música''|2003|p=65|ref=Lar}}</ref> [[Óscar Esplá]] (La bella durmiente, 1909),<ref>{{harvtxt|''Diccionario de Música''|2003|p=111|ref=Lar}}</ref> [[Joan Lamote de Grignon]] (Hesperia, 1907),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=13|ref=DOII}}</ref> [[Juan Manén]] (Giovanna di Napoli, 1903; Acté, 1904),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|1983|p=95}}</ref> [[Manuel Penella|Manuel Penella Moreno]] (''[[El gato montés]]'', 1917; ''Don Gil de Alcalá,'' 1932),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|pp=228–229|ref=DOII}}</ref> [[Emilio Serrano y Ruiz|Emilio Serrano]] (''Doña Juana la Loca'', 1890; Irene de Otranto, 1891)<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=385|ref=DOII}}</ref> and [[Eduard Toldrà]] (''El giravolt de maig'', 1928).<ref name="ReferenceH"/> [[File:Dona_Francisquita.jpg|thumb|Cover of ''[[Doña Francisquita]]'' (1923), by [[Amadeo Vives]]]] In this century the [[zarzuela]] resurfaced again, recovered by the new romantic taste and the nationalist revival. In 1856, a group of composers formed by [[Francisco Asenjo Barbieri]], [[Joaquín Gaztambide]], [[José Inzenga]], [[Rafael Hernando]] and [[Cristóbal Oudrid]] created the Sociedad Artística to promote the genre and promoted the creation of the [[Teatro de la Zarzuela]] in Madrid (1856).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=456|ref=DOII}}</ref> It was then differentiated into "[[género grande]]" and "[[género chico]]", the former divided into three acts and the latter composed of only one. It included sung and spoken parts, as well as popular dances, with a generally costumbrista theme —especially casticista— and comic tendency.<ref name="DicMusica-2003b"/> It developed especially in the third quarter of the century, the era of the "género grande", three-act plays influenced by the French ''buf parisien''.<ref name="ReferenceJ">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=576|ref=DOII}}</ref> Among its exponents are: [[Cristóbal Oudrid]] (''Buenas noches, señor don Simón'', 1852; ''El postillón de La Rioja'', 1856),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=204|ref=DOII}}</ref> [[Emilio Arrieta]] (''El grumete'', 1853; ''Marina'', 1855; ''La suegra del Diablo'', 1867; ''Las fuentes del Prado'', 1870),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=67|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[Joaquín Gaztambide]] (''Catalina'', 1854; ''Los magiares'', 1857; ''[[El juramento (zarzuela)|El juramento]]'', 1858),<ref name="ReferenceF"/> [[Francisco Asenjo Barbieri]] (''[[Los diamantes de la corona]]'', 1854; ''Pan y toros'', 1864; ''[[El barberillo de Lavapiés]]'', 1874; ''[[El diablo cojuelo]]'', 1878)<ref>{{harvtxt|''Diccionario de Música''|2003|p=33|ref=Lar}}</ref> and [[Ruperto Chapí]] (''La tempestad'', 1883; ''La bruja'', 1887; ''El rey que rabió'', 1891).<ref name="Alier-2007x" />
Towards the end of the century the "género chico" was more fashionable, one-act plays, with more recitative, with a certain influence of Viennese operetta.<ref name="ReferenceJ"/> Of note are: [[Tomás Bretón]] (''[[La verbena de la Paloma]]'', 1884),<ref name="Alier-2007y" /> [[Federico Chueca]] (''[[La Gran Vía]]'', 1886; ''El año pasado por agua'', 1889; ''Agua, azucarillos y aguardiente'', 1897),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=223|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[Ruperto Chapí]] (''La Revoltosa'', 1897; ''[[El puñao de rosas]]'', 1902),<ref name="Alier-2007x" /> [[Manuel Fernández Caballero]] (''El dúo de La africana'', 1893; ''Gigantes y cabezudos'', 1898),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=357|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[Gerónimo Giménez]] (''El baile de Luis Alonso'', 1896; ''La boda de Luis Alonso'', 1897; ''La tempranica'', 1900),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=516|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[Amadeo Vives]] (''La balada de la luz'', 1900; ''Bohemios'', 1903; ''[[Doña Francisquita]]'', 1923),<ref name="DicMusica-2003d" /> [[José Serrano Simeón]] (''La Reina mora'', 1903; ''[[Moros y cristianos]]'', 1905; ''La canción del olvido'', 1916; ''Los claveles'', 1929; ''[[La dolorosa]]'', 1930),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|pp=385–386|ref=DOII}}</ref> [[Vicente Lleó Balbastre]] (''[[La corte de Faraón (zarzuela)|La corte de Faraón]]'', 1910),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=48|ref=DOII}}</ref> [[Pablo Luna]] (''[[Molinos de viento (zarzuela)|Molinos de viento]]'', 1910; ''Los cadetes de la reina'', 1913; ''El asombro de Damasco'', 1916; ''El niño judío'', 1918),<ref>{{harvtxt|''Diccionario de Música''|2003|p=172|ref=Lar}}</ref> [[José María Usandizaga]] (''[[Las golondrinas (zarzuela)|The Swallows]]'' 1914),<ref name="Alier-2007aa">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=504|ref=DOII}}</ref> [[Francisco Alonso]] (''La linda tapada'', 1924; ''Las Leandras'', 1931),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=39|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[Jacinto Guerrero]] (''Los gavilanes'', 1923; ''[[El huésped del sevillano]]'', 1926; ''La rosa del azafrán'', 1930),<ref name="ReferenceE"/> [[José Padilla Sánchez]] (''La bien amada'', 1924),<ref name="Alier-2007i" /> the tandem [[Juan Vert]]-[[Reveriano Soutullo]] (''La leyenda del beso'', 1924; ''La del Soto del Parral'', 1927; ''El último romántico'', 1928),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=411|ref=DOII}}</ref> [[Jesús Guridi]] (''El caserío'', 1926; ''La meiga'', 1928; ''Peñamariana'', 1944),<ref name="Alier-2007z" /> [[Federico Moreno Torroba]] (''[[Luisa Fernanda]]'', 1932)<ref name="DOII142" /> and [[Pablo Sorozábal]] (''La del manojo de rosas'', 1934; ''[[La tabernera del puerto]]'', 1936; ''La eterna canción'', 1945; ''Los burladores'', 1948; ''La ópera del mogollón'', 1954).<ref name="Alier-2007g" /> It is also worth mentioning ''[[Cançó d'amor i de guerra]]'' (1926), by [[Rafael Martínez Valls]], zarzuela written in Catalan.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=190|ref=DOI}}</ref>
After the [[Spanish Civil War]], zarzuela was in decline, until its virtual disappearance in the 1960s. Today, the classics of the genre are still performed, but there is no new production.<ref name="ReferenceJ"/>
==== Latin America ==== {{Main|Opera in Latin America}}
[[File:Reapertura_del_Teatro_Colón_-_Vista_exterior_del_Teatro.jpg|thumb|[[Teatro Colón]], Buenos Aires]] [[Opera in Latin America|Latin American opera]] underwent a gradual evolution over time: during the colonial period, Italian or Spanish operas were performed; after the independence of the colonies, native works began to be produced, but which followed the rules of Italian opera; over time, local elements were added, generally with a folkloric or popular air; finally, works of a universal character began to be produced.<ref name="Larousse-1990a" />
In [[Argentina]], opera was carried by Italian companies, after the country's independence. In 1857, the [[Teatro Colón]] in Buenos Aires was opened. One of the first local composers was [[Arturo Berutti]], author of ''Pampa'' (1897) and ''Yupanqui'' (1899). In 1908, [[Héctor Panizza]] had a great success with ''[[Aurora (opera)|Aurora]]''. In 1916, [[Felipe Boero]] composed ''[[Tucumán]]'' to commemorate the centenary of independence; another work of his was ''[[El Matrero]]'' (1929), considered the Argentine national opera.<ref name="Alier-2007ab">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=59|ref=DOI}}</ref> Other authors were: Pascual De Rogatis (''Huémac'', 1916),<ref name="Alier-2007ab" /> [[Constantino Vicente Gaito]] (''Petronio'', 1919; ''Ollantay'', 1926), [[Floro Ugarte]] (''Saika'', 1920) and [[Athos Palma]] (''La novia del hereje'', 1935).<ref name="Alier-2007ab" /> [[File:Il_Guarany_Score_Front_Cover.jpg|left|thumb|273x273px|Poster of ''[[Il Guarany]]'' (1870), by [[Antônio Carlos Gomes]]]] In Brazil, when the Portuguese court moved to the new continent in 1808, due to the Napoleonic invasion, it brought with it a taste for Italian opera. Gradually, local composers emerged, among them Carlos Gomes, the first Latin American composer to triumph in Europe (''Joana de Flandres'', 1863; ''[[Il Guarany]]'', 1870; ''[[Salvator Rosa (opera)|Salvator Rosa]]'', 1874; ''[[Maria Tudor (opera)|Maria Tudor]]'', 1878; ''[[Lo Schiavo]]'', 1889), who, however, wrote in Italian, in a late-Verdian style.<ref name="Alier-2007ac">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=428|ref=DOI}}</ref> The first attempts in Portuguese were by composers such as [[Francisco Braga]] (''O contractador de diamantes'', 1901) and [[Oscar Lorenzo Fernández]] (''Malazarte'', 1921).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=155|ref=DOI}}</ref>
In [[Colombia]], the first production was ''Ester'' (1874), by [[José María Ponce de León]]. In Chile, ''Telésfora'' (1841), by [[Aquinas Ried]].<ref name="Larousse-1990b">{{harvtxt|''Diccionario Enciclopédico Larousse''|1990|p=2325|ref=DEL}}</ref>
[[Cuba]] began in the operatic tradition while a Spanish colony. Throughout the 19th century, Italian opera triumphed above all. In 1875, [[Laureano Fuentes Matons]] composed the first Cuban opera, ''La hija de Jefté''. Later, [[Ignacio Cervantes]] (''Los Saltimbanquis'', 1899) and [[Eduardo Sánchez de Fuentes]] (''El náufrago'', 1901). There were also composers of zarzuelas, such as [[Ernesto Lecuona]] (''María la O'', 1930; ''El cafetal'', 1930) and [[Eliseo Grenet]] (''Niña Rita'', 1927; ''La virgen morena'', 1928).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|pp=256–257|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[File:Teatrocarrera1880.jpg|thumb|Teatro Carrera, Guatemala.]] In [[Guatemala]], Italian opera was introduced in the early 19th century. In 1859 the Teatro Carrera, later called Nacional and, since 1886, Colón, was inaugurated. The first local opera was ''La mora generosa'' (1850), by [[José Escolástico Andrino]]. Already in the 20th century, [[Jesús Castillo (composer)|Jesús Castillo]] (''Quiché Vinak'', 1924) stood out.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|pp=447–448|ref=DOI}}</ref>
Mexico had an operatic tradition from its colonial past: a Mexican opera, ''La Parténope'' (1711), by [[Manuel de Sumaya]], was produced as early as the 18th century. The opera tradition was centered in [[Mexico City]] (Palacio de Bellas Artes, inaugurated in 1934) and cities such as [[Guanajuato]] and [[Guadalajara]].<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=120|ref=DOII}}</ref> In the early 19th century Italian opera predominated, until the second half saw the first local productions with authors such as [[Melesio Morales]] (''Romeo and Juliet'', 1863; ''Ildegonda'', 1866; ''Gino Corsini'', 1877), [[Aniceto Ortega del Villar|Aniceto Ortega]] (''Guatemotzin'', 1871), [[Felipe Villanueva]] (''Keofar'', 1892), [[Ricardo Castro]] (''Atzimba'', 1900) and [[Gustavo Campa]] (''El rey poeta'', 1901).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=121|ref=DOII}}</ref>
In [[Nicaragua]], [[Luis Abraham Delgadillo]] was outstanding: ''Final de Norma'' (1930), ''Mabaltayán'' (1942).<ref name="Larousse-1990b" />
In [[Peru]], European cultural life was closely followed in the 19th century. The first opera in the country was ''Atahualpa'' (1875), by the Italian Carlo Enrico Pasta. Subsequently, [[José María Valle Riestra]] (''Ollanta'', 1900) and [[Ernesto López Mindreau]] (''Nueva Castilla'', 1926) stood out.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=232|ref=DOII}}</ref>
In [[Uruguay]], the [[Teatro Solís]] in Montevideo, the country's main operatic center, was opened in 1856.<ref name="DOII139">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=139.|ref=DOII}}</ref> Among its composers were: [[Tomás Giribaldi]] (''[[La Parisina]]'', 1878), [[Alfonso Broqua]] (''Tabaré'', 1888), [[León Ribeiro]] (''Colón'', 1892; ''Liropeya'', 1912), [[Manuel M. Ponce]] (''El patio florido'', 1913) and [[Carlos Pedrell]] (''Ardid de amor'', 1917; ''La guitarra'', 1924).<ref name="Alier-2007aa" />
In [[Venezuela]], it is worth mentioning: [[José Ángel Montero]] (''[[Virginia (Montero)|Virginia]]'', 1873) and [[Reynaldo Hahn]] (''Le Marchand de Venise'', 1935).<ref name="Larousse-1990b" />
=== Verismo === {{Main|Verismo}}
[[File:Enrico_Caruso_VIII.png|thumb|[[Enrico Caruso]] as Canio, in the opera ''[[Pagliacci]]'' by [[Ruggero Leoncavallo]]]] Italian [[Verismo#Opera|verismo]] —also known as [[Giovane Scuola]] (Young School)— sought to reflect reality, with more popular plots, in rural and [[proletarian]]s environments, where the protagonists were ordinary characters. This movement grew out of the naturalista literature initiated in France by [[Émile Zola]], which had as exponents in Italy [[Giovanni Verga]] and [[Luigi Capuana]].<ref name="Atlas-2011aq">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=220|ref=Atl}}</ref> Verist composers denoted the influence of Verdi and Wagner, although they broke with the Romantic tradition. The music was continuous, transcomposed in the Wagnerian style,<ref group="note">Especially from Giordano and Puccini, while, in the early verismo, Mascagni and Leoncavallo still showed compact numbers in their works. —{{harvtxt|Menéndez Torrellas|2013|p=363}}</ref> without ''cabaletta'' and with arias without a fixed standard. They eliminated overtures, but added ''intermezzi'', of a showy orchestration of dramatic tone.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=500|ref=DOI}}</ref> The voice was taken to the limit of its expressive possibilities. In verismo, leading roles were even given to villains and reprehensible characters, such as murderers, pimps, prostitutes and other characters of low extraction.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=162|ref=Atl}}</ref> However, over time, the didactic message that verismo sought to convey drifted into sensationalist plots, which together with colorful and gimmicky orchestration reflected the new tastes of the public.<ref name="Atlas-2011aq" /> [[File:No_Pagliaccio_non_son.ogg|left|thumb|[[Enrico Caruso]] performing ''No Pagliaccio non-son'', from the opera ''[[Pagliacci]]'' by [[Ruggiero Leoncavallo]]]] The beginning of the success of verismo was with two operas: ''[[Cavalleria rusticana]]'' by [[Pietro Mascagni]] and ''[[Pagliacci]]'' by [[Ruggero Leoncavallo]]. Mascagni worked as a conductor with various companies throughout Italy until 1888, when he entered a musical competition organized by the publisher Edoardo Sanzogno with the one-act opera ''[[Cavalleria rusticana]]'' (premiered in 1890), with which he won the competition and reaped an enormous success. Based on a play by Giovanni Verga, it was notable for its ordinary characters driven by violent passions. He did not repeat such success with his following plays: ''[[L'amico Fritz]]'' (1891), ''[[I Rantzau]]'' (1893) and ''[[Guglielmo Ratcliff]]'' (1895).<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=252|ref=Atl}}</ref> Leoncavallo composed his first opera at the age of nineteen (''[[Chatterton (opera)|Chatterton]]'', 1876). Following a commission from the publisher [[Casa Ricordi|Giulio Ricordi]], he undertook a trilogy in the Wagnerian style, which he never completed. After a time in which he devoted himself mainly to writing, the success of ''Cavalleria rusticana'' encouraged him again and he composed his greatest success, ''Pagliacci'' (1892), a tragic story about four itinerant actors, with libretto written by himself. His next works were not so successful: ''I Medici'' (1893), ''[[La bohème (Leoncavallo)|La bohème]]'' (1897) and ''[[Zazà]]'' (1900).<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=251|ref=Atl}}</ref> [[File:Pietro_Mascagni_-_Cavalleria_Rusticana_-_Intermezzo_Sinfonico.ogg|thumb|''[[Intermezzo]]'', from ''[[Cavalleria rusticana]]'', opera by [[Pietro Mascagni]] (1890)]] [[File:GiacomoPuccini.jpg|left|thumb|279x279px|[[Giacomo Puccini]]]] The most outstanding composer of this trend was [[Giacomo Puccini]]. A pupil of Ponchielli, he had a great instinct for suggestive melodies and passionate plots, as well as for combining music and drama in perfect harmony, always with the voice as the central axis of his composition. An admirer of Wagner, he used the ''leitmotiv'' in several of his works.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2011|p=302}}</ref> He had a first success with ''[[Le Villi]]'' (''The Willis'', 1884), but for various reasons his next operatic work, ''[[Edgar (opera)|Edgar]]'' (1889), which was not well received, was delayed. He did achieve great success with ''[[Manon Lescaut (Puccini)|Manon Lescaut]]'' (1893), which brought him fame and fortune. In collaboration with librettists [[Giuseppe Giacosa]] and [[Luigi Illica]] he created his three most relevant operas: ''La Bohème'' (1896), ''Tosca'' (1900) and ''[[Madama Butterfly]]'' (1904). The former, about Parisian bohemian life, blended tragedy, passion and humor, along with seductive music that greatly pleased audiences. Tosca ''presented an equally tragic plot enhanced by musical dissonances and twisted harmonies, with one of the most complex female roles ever sketched. Her aria'' [[E lucevan le stelle]] ''is one of the opera's most famous, also known as the'' Farewell to Life''.'' [[Madama Butterfly]] ''is set in Japan, in keeping with the exotic taste of the time. Although it was not well received at its premiere, over time its tonal coloring and harmonic language have been appreciated. It includes the famous aria'' [[Un bel dì, vedremo]]''.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2011|pp=300–308}}</ref>'' [[File:Geraldine_Farrar_in_the_role_of_Madame_Butterfly_1.png|thumb|[[Geraldine Farrar]] as [[Madama Butterfly]]]] In 1910 he premiered in New York ''[[La fanciulla del West]]'' (''The Girl from the West''). After ''[[La rondine]]'' (''La golondrina'', 1917), his next project was ''Il trittico'' (''The Triptych''), a set of three operas to be performed in a single session: ''[[Il tabarro]]'' (''The tabard''), ''[[Suor Angelica]]'' and ''[[Gianni Schicchi]]'' (1918). His last opera was ''[[Turandot (Puccini)|Turandot]]'', on which he worked between 1920 and the year of his death (1924), and which was completed by [[Franco Alfano]] and premiered in 1926. In this work he synthesized his musical style: conjunction of drama and music, real characters of great emotional force and music of great symphonic intensity. It includes the famous aria ''[[Nessun dorma]]''.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|pp=224–229|ref=Atl}}</ref>
Other prominent composers of verismo were [[Umberto Giordano]], [[Alfredo Catalani]] and [[Francesco Cilea]]. Giordano began in Sanzogno's competition with the one-act opera ''[[Marina]]'' (1889). It was followed by ''[[Mala vita]]'' (1892) and ''[[Regina Diaz]]'' (1894). He achieved his greatest success with ''[[Andrea Chénier]]'' (1896), with libretto by Luigi Illica. He repeated success with ''[[Fedora (opera)|Fedora]]'' (1898), which was followed by a series of failures, until he renewed fame with ''[[La cena delle beffe]]'' (''The Supper of Mockery'', 1924).<ref name="Atlas-2011an" /> Catalani evolved from an early Wagnerian influence towards verismo. His first opera, ''La falce'' (1875), was to a libretto by Arrigo Boito. It was followed by ''Elda'' (1880), transformed ten years later into ''[[Loreley (opera)|Loreley]]''. His greatest success was ''[[La Wally]]'' (1892), in a Germanizing style, with libretto by Luigi Illica.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|pp=206–207|ref=DOI}}</ref> Cilea abandoned law for music. He achieved an early success with ''Gina'' (1889), which was followed by ''La tilda'' (1892), ''L'arlesiana'' (1897) and his masterpiece, ''[[Adriana Lecouvreur]]'' (1902), a mixture of tragedy and comedy,<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=247|ref=Atl}}</ref> in which he combined verismo with a certain bel canto.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=225|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[File:Leo_Slezak,_Tosca,_E_lucevan_le_stelle.ogg|left|thumb|''[[E lucevan le stelle]]'', aria from ''[[Tosca]]'' (1900), by Giacomo Puccini]] Otros exponentes fueron: [[Franco Alfano]] (''[[Risurrezione|Resurrection]]'', 1904),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=35|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[Alberto Franchetti]] (''[[Cristoforo Colombo (opera)|Christopher Columbus]]'', 1892; ''Germany'', 1902),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=380|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[Franco Leoni]] (''The Oracle'', 1905; ''Francesca da Rimini'', 1914),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=30|ref=DOII}}</ref> [[Giacomo Orefice]] (''Chopin'', 1901; ''The Moses'', 1905)<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=194|ref=DOII}}</ref> and [[Antonio Smareglia]] (''Istrian Wedding'', 1895; ''The Moth'', 1897).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=402|ref=DOII}}</ref> [[File:Poster_for_Gustave_Charpentier's_Louise_by_G._Rochegrosse.jpg|thumb|242x242px|Poster for ''[[Louise (opera)|Louise]]'' (1900), by Gustave Charpentier]] Close to verismo, but with a more personal style is [[Ermanno Wolf-Ferrari]]. A student of Arrigo Boito, he was an inconstant composer, who seemed not to find his own style. He made veristic works, as well as serious and comic operas, among which stand out: ''Cenerentola'' (1900), ''[[Le donne curiose]]'' (1903), ''I quatro rusteghi'' (1906), ''[[Susanna's Secret|Il segreto di Susanna]]'' (1909) and ''[[L'amore medico]]'' (1913), which have not lasted in the operatic repertoire.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=255|ref=Atl}}</ref>
In the 1910s this style evolved towards a so-called post-Verismo, characterized by the strong influence of the writer [[Gabriele D'Annunzio]] and a stronger link to [[Italian nationalism]].<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=255|ref=DOII}}</ref> Prominent in this current were [[Italo Montemezzi]] and [[Riccardo Zandonai]]. The former, a largely self-taught composer and conductor, had great success with his first two operas, ''Giovanni Gallurese'' (1905) and ''[[L'amore dei tre re]]'' (1913). He later produced ''[[La nave]]'' (1918), ''La notte di Zoraima'' (1931) and ''[[L'incantesimo]]'' (1943).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=138|ref=DOII}}</ref> Zandonai attracted the attention of the publisher Ricordi with ''La coppa del re'' (1907), so he commissioned an opera, ''Il grillo del focolare'' (1908), based on a Dickens short story. His first great success was ''[[Francesca da Rimini (Zandonai)|Francesca da Rimini]]'' (1914), based on an episode of the ''[[Divine Comedy]]'' by [[Dante]]. He developed a more original style in ''Giulietta e Romeo'' (1922), which was followed by ''[[I cavalieri di Ekebù]]'' (1925). He left his last opera, ''[[Il bacio]]'' unfinished.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=573|ref=DOII}}</ref> Mention should also be made of [[Alfredo Casella]], a composer of cosmopolitan style who was also influenced by post-Romantic and impressionism, author of ''[[La donna serpente]]'' (1929).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2011|p=319}}</ref>
Outside Italy, the veristic influence is denoted in the work of the French [[Gustave Charpentier]]. He was a pupil of Massenet and, in 1887, won the Rome prize. It was in that city that he was infected by the verist atmosphere and composed his most famous opera, ''[[Louise (opera)|Louise]]'' (1900), the love story of two young people from [[Montmartre]], with libretto by [[Saint-Pol-Roux]].<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|pp=246–247|ref=Atl}}</ref> Also in France, [[Alfred Bruneau]] set to music several texts by Émile Zola, such as ''Le rêve'' (1891), ''L'Attaque du moulin'' (1893), ''[[Messidor (opera)|Messidor]]'' (1897), ''L'Ouragan'' (1901) and ''L'Enfant roi'' (1905).<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=257|ref=Atl}}</ref> [[Henry Février]] was the author of ''[[Monna Vanna (Février)|Monna Vanna]]'' (1909), on a text by [[Maurice Maeterlinck]], a semi-Viverist work of symbolist inspiration.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=361|ref=DOI}}</ref>
In Germany, [[Eugen d'Albert]] was a prolific composer, author of some twenty operas, including ''[[Die Abreise]]'' (''The Departure Journey'', 1898) and ''[[Tiefland (opera)|Tiefland]]'' (1903), the latter based on ''[[Lowland]]'' by [[Ángel Guimerá]].<ref>{{harvtxt|''Enciclopedia temática Argos. Bellas Artes II''|1986|p=162|ref=BAII}}</ref><ref name="ReferenceB"/>
The Czech [[Josef Bohuslav Foerster]] started in a veristic style with his first two operas ''Debora'' (1893) and ''Eva'' (1899). Later he was the author of ''Jessika'' (1905), based on Shakespeare's ''[[The Merchant of Venice]]'', as well as ''Nepřemožení'' (''The Invincibles'', 1919), ''Srdce'' (''The Heart'', 1923) and ''Bloud'' (''The Klutz'', 1936).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=377|ref=DOI}}</ref>
The Greek [[Spyridon Samaras]] was the author of operas in Italian in the veristic style: ''La martire'' (1894), ''La furia domata'' (1895), ''Rhea'' (1908).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=342|ref=DOII}}</ref>
=== Post-romanticism === {{Main|Post-romanticism}}
[[File:Strauss3.jpg|thumb|[[Richard Strauss]]]] Between the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries there was the post-Romanticism, which as its name suggests was an evolution of Romanticism based on more modern premises, but maintaining the same spirit that characterized that movement. The main influence of this style was Wagner, so it is sometimes also called post-Wagnerism.
Its main representative was [[Richard Strauss]], who was noted for his harmonic creativity and mastery of orchestration. Under Wagner's influence he started in opera with ''[[Guntram]]'' (1894) and ''[[Feuersnot]]'' (''The Need for Fire'', 1901), which failed. On the other hand, he enjoyed public favor with ''[[Salome (opera)|Salome]]'' (1905), based on the play of the same name by [[Oscar Wilde]], despite the scandal caused by the erotic charge of the plot. Since then he began a collaboration with the Austrian playwright [[Hugo von Hofmannsthal]], who wrote most of his librettos. Their first joint work was ''[[Elektra (opera)|Elektra]]'' (1909), which was followed by ''[[Der Rosenkavalier]]'' (''The Knight of the Rose'', 1911), ''[[Ariadne auf Naxos]]'' (1912), ''[[Die Frau ohne Schatten]]'' (''The Woman Without a Shadow'', 1919), ''[[The Egyptian Helen|Die ägyptische Helena]]'' (''The Egyptian Helen'', 1928) and ''[[Arabella]]'' (1933). He later counted on [[Stefan Zweig]] for ''[[Die schweigsame Frau]]'' (''The Silent Woman'', 1935) and on [[Joseph Gregor]] for ''[[Friedenstag]]'' (''Day of Peace'', 1938), [[Daphne (opera)|''Daphne'']] (1938) and ''[[Danae's Love|Die Liebe der Danae]]'' (''Danae's Love'', 1940). His last opera was ''[[Capriccio (opera)|Capriccio]]'' (1942).<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|pp=234–235|ref=Atl}}</ref> In these works he introduced a new harmonic language that crossed traditional tonal boundaries, with passionate sounds that could include screams and other guttural sounds, thus anticipating later [[expressionism]].<ref>{{harvtxt|Azcárate Ristori|Pérez Sánchez|Ramírez Domínguez|1983|p=933}}</ref> [[File:Aino_Ackté_as_Salome.jpg|left|thumb|Soprano Aino Ackté in the opera ''[[Salome (opera)|Salome]]'' (1905), by [[Richard Strauss]]]] Other exponents were [[Engelbert Humperdinck (composer)|Engelbert Humperdinck]], [[Alexander von Zemlinsky]] and [[Hans Pfitzner]]. Humperdinck was a gifted student, who in 1881 met Wagner, who took him on as an assistant for the production of ''Parsifal''. However, his production was oriented towards a simpler line, as in ''[[Hansel and Gretel (opera)|Hänsel und Gretel]]'' (1893), his first and most successful opera, based on the fairy tale by the [[Brothers Grimm]]. He chose the same authors for his next project, ''Die sieben Geißlein'' (''The Seven Children'', 1898), which was not so successful. After several more failures, he succeeded with ''[[Königskinder]]'' (''The King's Children'', 1910), more Wagnerian in style.<ref name="Atlas-2011am" /> Zemlinsky devoted himself to opera as both composer and conductor. His first success was ''[[Es war einmal]]'' (''Once upon a time'', 1899), which he performed [[Gustav Mahler]] at the Vienna Hofoper. In 1906 he composed ''[[Der Traumgörge]]'' (''Görge the Dreamer''), which was not premiered until forty years later. It was followed by his two greatest hits, ''[[Eine florentinische Tragödie]]'' (''A Florentine Tragedy'', 1917) and ''[[Der Zwerg]]'' (''The Dwarf'', 1922), both one-acts. In 1932, ''[[Der Kreidekreis]]'' (''The Chalk Circle'') was banned by the [[Nazis]], after which he went into exile in the United States, where he died in anonymity.<ref name="Atlas-2011ar">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=305|ref=Atl}}</ref> Pfitzner was influenced by Schumann and Wagner, noticeable in his early operas: ''[[Der arme Heinrich]]'' (''Poor Heinrich'', 1893) and ''Die Rose vom Liebesgarten'' (''The Rose in the Garden of Love'', 1900). His greatest success was ''[[Palestrina (opera)|Palestrina]]'' (1917), a major work on the position of the artist in society. His next works, ''Von deutscher Seele'' (''Of the German Soul'', 1921) and ''Das dunkle Reich'' (''The Dark Kingdom'', 1929), expressed his ideas about a "pure" German music.<ref name="Atlas-2011as">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=293|ref=Atl}}</ref> [[File:Evening_prayer.ogg|thumb|''Evening Prayer'', from the opera ''[[Hansel and Gretel (opera)|Hänsel und Gretel]]'' (1893), by [[Engelbert Humperdinck (composer)|Engelbert Humperdinck]]]] The Austrian [[Erich Wolfgang Korngold]] was considered a child prodigy, and aroused the admiration of Mahler, Puccini and Strauss. His first operas were ''Der Ring des Polykrates'' (''The Ring of Polycrates'', 1916), ''[[Violanta]]'' (1916) and ''Die Tote Stadt'' (''The Dead City'', 1920), of late romanticism. ''[[Das Wunder der Heliane]]'' (''The Miracle of Heliane'', 1927) was a work of a certain eroticism with a score conceived on an epic scale that creates great difficulty for the performers. With the establishment of the [[Anschluss]] in 1938, he emigrated to the United States, where he composed film scores and won two Oscars.<ref name="Atlas-2011at">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=291|ref=Atl}}</ref>
In the Germanic field it is also worth mentioning: [[Wilhelm Kienzl]] (''Der Evangelimann'' [''The Evangelist''], 1895),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|pp=538–539|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[Max von Schillings]] (''Moloch'', 1900; ''Mona Lisa'', 1915),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=362|ref=DOII}}</ref> [[Siegfried Wagner]] —son of Richard Wagner— (''Der Bärenhäuter'' [''The Bearskin''], 1899; ''Der Kobold'' [''The Goblin''], 1904; ''Der Schmied von Marienburg'' [''The Blacksmith of Marienburg''], 1923)<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=540|ref=DOII}}</ref> and [[Hugo Wolf]] (''[[Der Corregidor]]'', 1895).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=561|ref=DOII}}</ref>
The British [[Rutland Boughton]] attempted to establish an "English-style Wagnerism", with operas such as ''The Immortal Hour'' (1922), ''Alkestis'' (1922), ''[[The Famous Tragedy of the Queen of Cornwall#Adaptation|The Queen of Cornwall]]'' (1924) and ''The Lily Maid'' (1934).<ref name="Alier-2007o" /> Similarly, [[Joseph Holbrooke]] sought to transfer to [[Celtic mythology]] the Wagnerian universe, through the trilogy ''The Cauldron of Annwyn'' (1912–1929), consisting of ''The Children of Don'' (1912), ''Dylan, Son of the Wave'' (1914) and ''Bronwen'' (1929).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=484|ref=DOI}}</ref>
In France, [[Gabriel Fauré]] showed a clear Wagnerian influence in his opera ''[[Pénélope]]'' (1913).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=350|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[Jean Nouguès]] was the author of ''Quo Vadis?'' (1909), on [[Henryk Sienkiewicz]]'s novel, a work of an ambitious staging that included [[circus]] animals.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=177|ref=DOII}}</ref>
In Italy, [[Luigi Mancinelli]] showed a clearly Wagnerian style, although with a more cosmopolitan component, not as Germanized as that of other followers of the German composer. His two best works were ''Ero e Leandro'' (1897) and ''Paolo e Francesca'' (1907).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2011|p=289}}</ref>
=== Impressionism === {{Main|Impressionism in music}}
[[File:Mary_Garden_in_Debussy's_Pelléas_et_Mélisande_2.jpg|thumb|[[Mary Garden]] in a performance of ''[[Pelléas et Mélisande (opera)|Pelléas et Mélisande]]'' by [[Claude Debussy]] (1908)]] Like his [[Impressionism|pictorial homologue]], [[Musical impressionism|impressionism]] arose in France, with a desire to modernize musical conception, which developed between the end of the 19th century and the beginning of the 20th. As in painting, it was intended to capture the sensations that the artist produced the surrounding world, with a basis of reality, but interpreted subjectively.<ref>{{harvtxt|Comellas|2010|p=304}}</ref> Influenced by symbolist poetry ([[Verlaine]], [[Baudelaire]], [[Mallarmé]]), they also sought a new language confronting both the formal purism of classicism and the passionate violence of Romanticism. To this end, [[Claude Debussy]], its main representative, resorted to a scale of tones not used until then, whole tones and complex intervals from the ninth onwards, as well as intervals of parallel fourths and fifths.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Diccionario de Música''|2003|p=147|ref=Lar}}</ref>
Claude Debussy began several operatic projects that he left unfinished-a couple on stories by [[Edgar Allan Poe]] and one on [[Rodrigo Díaz de Vivar|Cid]] entitled ''[[Rodrigue et Chimène]]''—until he found a project that fascinated him: ''[[Pelléas et Mélisande]]'' (1902), on a work by [[Maurice Maeterlinck]]. It was his only opera, but a masterpiece. Despite its simple plot – a love rivalry between two brothers and a young girl – Debussy created a profound and moving work, evocative and suggestive, that is still novel today.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|pp=244–245|ref=Atl}}</ref> With this work he initiated the genre of ''literary opera'' (''Literaturoper''), a type of opera based on literary texts respected in their integrity which, while they can be reduced, cannot be altered in their essence, and which are declaimed in the style of the Russian ''opéra dialogué'' and presented in transcomposed form.<ref>{{harvtxt|Menéndez Torrellas|2013|p=400}}</ref> [[File:Enrico_Caruso_tenor.jpg|left|thumb|233x233px|[[Enrico Caruso]], one of the most famous tenors of all times]] In France, [[Maurice Ravel]] and [[Paul Dukas]] also stood out. Ravel was a convinced anti-Wagnerian, who eagerly searched for his own style. He was very meticulous and nonconformist in his work, so he continually revised his works, which explains his scarce production. With a Basque mother, he felt great attraction for Spanish culture, which is evident in his first opera, ''L'heure espagnole'' (''La hora española'', 1911), a one-act comic work, with sound effects of machines and clocks. His next opera was ''L'enfant et les sortilèges'' (1925), with a libretto by [[Colette]].<ref name="Atlas-2011au">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=296|ref=Atl}}</ref> Dukas initially accused Wagnerian influence, as denoted in ''Horn et Riemenhild'' (1892) and ''L'arbre de science'' (1899), which he left unfinished. He completed only one opera, ''Ariane et Barbe-bleu'' (1907), based on the text by Maurice Maeterlinck, where he mixed Wagnerian chromaticism with the pentatonic scale used by Debussy.<ref name="Atlas-2011ap" />
The Italian [[Ottorino Respighi]] attempted to combine impressionism with traditional music, especially Baroque.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Diccionario de Música''|2003|p=239|ref=Lar}}</ref> His first two operas were of comic genre: ''Re Enzo'' (1905) and ''Semirâma'' (1910). He subsequently produced ''Belfagor'' (1923), ''La campana sommersa'' (1927), ''La Fiamma'' (1934) and ''Lucrezia'' (1937).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=292|ref=DOII}}</ref>
British [[Frederick Delius]] approached impressionism departing from Wagnerian influence. He only turned to opera early in his career: ''[[Koanga]]'' (1904), ''[[A Village Romeo and Juliet]]'' (1907), ''[[Fennimore and Gerda]]'' (1919).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=280|ref=DOI}}</ref>
The Swiss [[Ernest Bloch]] brought together the influence of Debussy with that of Richard Strauss. He produced a single opera, ''[[Macbeth (Bloch)|Macbeth]]'' (1910). In 1916 he emigrated to the United States, where he began an opera that he left unfinished, ''Jezebel''<ref name="Alier-2007w" />
=== Singers of the end of the century === At the turn of the century they stood out vocally: [[Mattia Battistini]], Italian baritone with a high-pitched, crystalline voice, adjusted to what was called ''baritenor'' at the time; [[Enrico Caruso]], Italian tenor, one of the most famous of all time, with a flexible tone that allowed him a great variety of roles; [[Fyodor Chaliapin]], Russian bass who performed both the repertoire of his country and Rossini, Bellini and Verdi; [[Emmy Destinn]], Czech soprano who excelled in roles by Puccini and Strauss; [[Geraldine Farrar]], American soprano with great stage presence; the same nationality had the soprano [[Mary Garden]], who excelled in the leading role of Debussy's ''Pelléas et Mélisande''; [[Hipólito Lázaro]], Spanish tenor of the Verist repertoire; [[Victor Maurel]], French baritone admired by Verdi, who gave him several roles; [[Nellie Melba]], Australian soprano of great virtuosity; [[Claudia Muzio]], Italian soprano who excelled in tragic roles, mainly in Verist operas; [[Elisabeth Schumann]], German soprano of high and delicate voice, interpreter of Mozart, Wagner and Strauss; [[Francisco Viñas]], Spanish tenor of wide register who could sing both lyric and heroic tenor; and [[Giovanni Zenatello]], Italian tenor of great facility for the highest register.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|pp=258–259|ref=Atl}}</ref>
== 20th century == {{Main|20th-century classical music|Modernism (music)|Contemporary classical music}}
[[File:Sydney_Opera_House_Sails_edit02.jpg|thumb|250x250px|[[Sydney Opera House]], opened in 1973, designed by Danish architect [[Jørn Utzon]]]] The 20th century saw a great revolution in music, motivated by the profound political and social changes that took place during the century. The transforming, experimental and renovating interest of the artistic avant-gardes was translated into a new musical language, at the same time that a technical renovation took place, motivated by the appearance of new technologies, such as electronic music. All this resulted in new compositional methods and new sound ranges, which were adapted to the new musical movements that were happening in the course of the century.<ref>{{harvtxt|Azcárate Ristori|Pérez Sánchez|Ramírez Domínguez|1983|p=932}}</ref> The new music composed in this century broke radically with the past and sought a new language, breaking the scheme of traditional musical discourse: if necessary, harmony, melody and tonality were broken. Many of these innovations caused bewilderment, especially [[atonality]], in a public accustomed to a hierarchy of notes where a fundamental note dominated; in atonality, each note has equal relevance to the others. For this reason, contemporary music has not enjoyed great public success and has often been confined to a closed circle of intellectuals.<ref>{{harvtxt|Comellas|2010|pp=299–300}}</ref>
Opera in the twentieth century maintained the previous repertoire, which continued to be successfully performed in the best theaters and auditoriums of the world, while, at the production level, although there was a copious and excellent production, the innovations produced in this field did not enjoy great success among the majority public. The composers' eagerness to experiment provoked harsh criticism and controversy, if not even censorship or political persecution: in the [[Soviet Union]], [[Stalin]] forced [[Shostakovich]] to adhere to the regime's cultural guidelines after attending a performance of ''[[Lady Macbeth of Mtsensk (opera)|Lady Macbeth of Mtsensk]]'' in 1936; in [[Nazi Germany]], avant-garde composers were classified as ''[[degenerate music]]ians'', and many had to go into exile.<ref name="ReferenceK">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=262|ref=Atl}}</ref><ref name="Atlas-2011av">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=290|ref=Atl}}</ref> During this century, there was even experimentation with the traditional operatic repertoire through avant-garde productions, more modern scenographies and contemporary adaptations of classical operas, as was the trend in the 1920s at the [[Kroll Opera]] in Berlin or as carried out by [[Wieland Wagner]] —grandson of the composer— at the [[Bayreuth Festival]].<ref name="Atlas-2011aw">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=263|ref=Atl}}</ref> On the other hand, in this period the fascination with opera singers continued and even increased, sometimes becoming highly popular phenomena, such as the so-called [[The Three Tenors|Tres Tenores]] ([[Luciano Pavarotti]], [[José Carreras]] and [[Plácido Domingo]]).<ref name="Atlas-2011aw" /> It is worth noting that the technical advances that allowed voice recording popularized this genre domestically: [[Enrico Caruso]], for example, was one of the singers who was most lavish in recording his performances, thus increasing his popularity.<ref group="note">The first partial opera recording on [[gramophone]] was a selection of Caruso's hits, recorded in Milan in 1902, while the first recorded opera was Verdi's ''Il trovatore'', recorded between 1903 and 1906. —{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=434.|ref=DOI}}</ref> Other media that contributed to the diffusion of opera were radio, cinema and television.<ref name="ReferenceL">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=264|ref=Atl}}</ref><ref>In 1920 the first complete opera was broadcast on radio,''[[Parsifal]]'' by [[Richard Wagner]], from the [[Teatro Colón]] in Buenos Aires. —{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=274|ref=DOII}}</ref> in 1951, the first television opera, ''[[Amahl and the Night Visitors]]'', by [[Gian Carlo Menotti]], was composed.<ref name="Alier 2011 372">{{harvtxt|Alier|2011|p=372}}</ref>
This century saw numerous novelties in the field of scenography: from verismo emerged a more sober and realistic trend in stage representation, whose pioneers were [[André Antoine]], founder of the [[Théâtre Antoine|Théâtre Libre]] in Paris, and [[Otto Brahm]], at the head of the Freie Bühne in Berlin. [[Max Reinhardt]], director of the Deutsches Theater, introduced numerous innovations, such as three-dimensional sets and the use of lighting to create atmosphere. Another innovator was [[Alfred Roller]], who teamed up with [[Gustav Mahler]], conductor at the Vienna Hofoper, both of whom strove to integrate the stage performance with the musical drama as a unified whole.<ref name="Atlas-2011ax">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=221|ref=Atl}}</ref>
At the beginning of this century emerged the [[chamber opera]], a minor modality of the genre composed of one-act works, with few characters and a reduced orchestra, a format similar to the género chico of the Spanish zarzuela. Examples would be ''L'heure espagnole'' by [[Maurice Ravel|Ravel]] (1911), ''Arlecchino'' by [[Ferruccio Busoni|Busoni]] (1917) and ''[[Histoire du soldat]]'' by Stravinski (1918).<ref name="DicMusica-2003a" /> In Vienna, the Kammeroper, dedicated to this genre, was founded in 1953.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=184|ref=DOI}}</ref> The English composer [[Benjamin Britten]] went so far as to found his own chamber opera orchestra, the English Opera Group. On the other hand, the traditional operetta genre evolved into the musical, a revamped genre that relied more on popular music and dance, in centers such as the West End theaters in London or the Broadway theaters in New York.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|pp=264–265|ref=Atl}}</ref><ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=434.|ref=DOI}}</ref> Other media that contributed to the diffusion of opera were radio, cinema and television.<ref name="ReferenceL"/><ref group="note">In 1920 the first complete opera was broadcast on radio,''[[Parsifal]]'' by [[Richard Wagner]], from the [[Teatro Colón]] in Buenos Aires. —{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=274|ref=DOII}}</ref> in 1951, the first television opera, ''[[Amahl and the Night Visitors]]'', by [[Gian Carlo Menotti]], was composed.<ref name="Alier 2011 372"/>
Another phenomenon of relevance in this century was the proliferation of opera festivals, which counted on the example of the [[Bayreuth Festival]] (1876). In 1901 the Munich Festival appeared, followed by the [[Savonlinna Opera Festival|Savonlinna]] (1912), Verona (1913), [[Salzburg Festival|Salzburg]] (1920), [[Maggio Musicale Fiorentino|Florence]] (Maggio Musicale Fiorentino, 1933), [[Glyndebourne Festival|Glyndebourne]] (1934), [[Ravinia Festival|Ravinia]] (1936), [[Edinburgh Festival|Edinburgh]] (1947), [[Aldeburgh Festival|Aldeburgh]] (1948), [[Festival International d'Art Lyrique d'Aix-en-Provence|Aix-en-Provence]] (1948), [[Santander International Festival|Santander]] (1952), Spoleto (1958), [[Tanglewood Festival|Tanglewood]] (1970) and Peralada (1987), among the most famous.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=360|ref=DOI}}</ref>
In an article published by Opera Holland Park, the opera director [[Ella Marchment]] explained that for every hour of a performance, an opera director will complete 150 hours of preparation and rehearsals; or 2.5 hours of work for every minute of onstage time.<ref>{{Cite web |date=7 June 2022 |title=Spotlight on Ella Marchment |url=https://operahollandpark.com/news/spotlight-on-ella-marchment/ |access-date=2022-06-19 |website=Opera Holland Park |language=en-GB}}</ref>
=== Expressionism === {{Main|Expressionism}}
[[File:Tiresias.jpg|thumb|Performance of ''[[The Breasts of Tiresias|Les mamelles de Tirésias]]'' by [[Francis Poulenc]] (2001)]] The [[Expressionism#Opera|expressionism]] emerged at the beginning of the century as a style fundamentally concerned with the inner expressiveness of the individual, in its psychological deepening, as opposed to the dominant naturalism at the turn of the century, which in opera gave the verismo. In this current, interpersonal relationships, emotionality and the psychic states of the characters were especially valued. On a literary level, its referents were [[Franz Kafka]], [[James Joyce]] and the German playwrights [[Ernst Toller]], [[Frank Wedekind]] and [[Georg Kaiser]]. For expressionist authors, art was a form of expression, not entertainment, so they were more concerned with the message they wanted to convey than with style or musical or plot device.<ref name="Atlas-2011ax" />
In France, this style was practiced by some composers who formed a group called [[Les Six]] ([[Francis Poulenc]], [[Darius Milhaud]], [[Arthur Honegger]], [[Germaine Tailleferre]], [[Georges Auric]] and [[Louis Durey]]), interested in musical avant-garde and popular rhythms, with an informal and spontaneous style. Poulenc, author of his own librettos, stood out for his lyrical melodies. His first opera was ''Les mamelles de Tirésias'' (''The breasts of Tiresias'', 1947), on a text by [[Guillaume Apollinaire]]. It was followed by ''Dialogues des Carmélites'' (1957), based on a work by [[Georges Bernanos]].<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=294|ref=Atl}}</ref> In ''La voix humaine'' (''The human voice'', 1959) he presented an opera with only a soprano speaking on the telephone for forty minutes, with libretto by [[Jean Cocteau]].<ref>{{harvtxt|Comellas|2010|p=318}}</ref> Milhaud was a pupil of Paul Dukas and Vincent d'Indy. He produced major historical works, such as ''[[Christophe Colomb]]'' (1930), ''Bolívar'' (1950) and ''[[David (Milhaud)|David]]'' (1954), as well as minor works: ''Les Malheurs d'Orphée'' (''The Evils of Orpheus'', 1926), ''[[Le pauvre matelot]]'' (''The Poor Sailor'', 1927) and ''[[La mère coupable]]'' (''The Guilty Mother'', 1966).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=126|ref=DOII}}</ref> Honegger began in the operatic field with ''Philippa'' (1903), which was followed by ''[[Judith]]'' (1925), ''Antigone'' (1927) and the operettas ''[[Les Aventures du roi Pausole]]'' (1930), ''La Belle de Moudon'' (1931) and ''Les Petites Cardinales'' (1937). Also in 1937 he composed jointly with [[Jacques Ibert]] ''L'Aiglon'', his last opera.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=486|ref=DOI}}</ref> Linked to this group was [[Erik Satie]], best known for his symphonic and piano compositions, who composed several operettas: ''Geneviève de Brabant'' (1899), ''Pousse l'amour'' (1905), ''Le Piège de Méduse'' (1913).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=354|ref=DOII}}</ref> Also worth mentioning are [[Jacques Ibert]] (''Angélique'', 1927; ''Le Roi d'Yvetot'', 1930).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2011|p=333}}</ref> [[File:Bertolt_Brecht,_Kurt_Weill,_Opera_za_tri_groše,_Drama_SNG_v_Ljubljani_(2).jpg|left|thumb|250x250px|Performance of ''[[The Threepenny Opera|Die Dreigroschenoper]]'' (''The Threepenny Opera''), by [[Kurt Weill]] (1961)]] In Germany, [[Franz Schreker]] began in post-romanticism, but later opted for a style close to expressionism. His first opera was ''[[Der ferne Klang]]'' (''The Far Sound'', 1910), with which he achieved great success. It was followed by ''[[Die Gezeichneten]]'' (''The Marked Ones'', 1918), an opera of great complexity that required an orchestra of 120 musicians, with a somber and tortured theme, fully immersed in the depressing spirit of the postwar period. In ''Der Schatzgräber'' (''The Treasure Hunter'', 1920) he also showed a theme centered on loneliness, despair and sexual desire. His last works, ''Christophorus'' (1931) and ''Der Schmied von Gent'' (''The Blacksmith of Ghent'', 1932), were sabotaged by the Nazis, who considered him a ''degenerate musician'', with the accentuated motif of being Jewish.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|pp=298–299|ref=Atl}}</ref>
[[Paul Hindemith]] started in expressionism with three one-act operas (''Mörder, Hoffnung der Frauen'' [''Murder, Hope of Women''], 1919; ''Das Nusch-Nuschi'', 1920; ''Sancta Sussana'', 1921), but later evolved into a neo-baroque style with use of polyphony. In that sense, ''[[Cardillac]]'' (1926) was a transitional work, while in ''Mathis der Maler'' (''Matthias the Painter'', 1928) he combined medieval influences with German folklore and counterpoint techniques. After being considered a ''degenerate musician'' by the Nazis, he went into exile in Switzerland and the United States. ''Die Harmonie der Welt'' (''The Harmony of the World'', 1957) was the culmination of his neo-baroque style.<ref name="Atlas-2011av" />
[[Kurt Weill]] was a representative of the so-called [[New Objectivity]] (''Neue Sachlichkeit''), a movement that in a certain way reacted against expressionist subjectivism, although it used many of its resources, while having points in common with neoclassicism and assimilating new musical forms such as [[jazz]], [[tango music|tango]] or [[cabaret]].<ref>{{harvtxt|Menéndez Torrellas|2013|p=441}}</ref> He sought in his works a type of musical theater that featured the best artists and professionals, from playwrights to dancers. His first operas were ''[[Der Protagonist]]'' (1926) and ''[[Der Zar lässt sich photographieren]]'' (''The Tsar takes a photograph of himself'', 1928). From then on he formed a fruitful partnership with the playwright [[Bertolt Brecht]], which produced numerous successes: ''[[The Threepenny Opera|Die Dreigroschenoper]]'' (''The Threepenny Opera'', 1928), ''Happy End'' (''Happy Ending'', 1929), ''[[Aufstieg und Fall der Stadt Mahagonny]]'' (''Rise and Fall of the City of Mahagonny'', 1929) and ''[[Der Jasager]]'' (''The Yes-Sayer'', 1930). His greatest success was ''[[The Threepenny Opera]]'', based on ''[[The Beggar's Opera]]'' by [[John Gay]] and [[Johann Christoph Pepusch]]. In 1933 he premiered ''[[Der Silbersee]]'' (''The Silver Lake''), with a libretto by Georg Kaiser. Shortly thereafter he went into exile in the United States, where he made several musicals.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=304|ref=Atl}}</ref>
[[Erwin Schulhoff]] composed in 1928 his opera ''[[Flammen (Schulhoff)|Flammen]]'' (''Flames''), a work of a fantastic nature, in which the author abandoned the [[Aristotelian Unities|Aristotelian theatrical rules]] in force until then in theater and opera for the sake of a new concept of staging, which understood theater as a game, a show, a fantasy overflowing reality. [[Berthold Goldschmidt]], a pupil of Schrecker, was director of the [[Darmstadt]] Opera. In 1930 he adapted [[Fernand Crommelynck]]'s ''Der Gewaltige Hanrei'' (''The Mighty Hanrei''), premiered in 1932, although his Jewishness caused it to be immediately withdrawn, so he emigrated to the UK.<ref name="Alier-2007ac" />
=== Dodecaphony === {{Main|Twelve-tone technique}}
[[File:Krenek_Jonny-spielt-auf_Titel.jpg|thumb|Handbill for the opera ''[[Jonny spielt auf]]'' (1927), by [[Ernst Krenek]]]] The spirit of renewal at the turn of the century, which led all the arts to a break with the past and to seek a new creative impulse, led the Austrian composer [[Arnold Schönberg]] to create a system where all notes have the same value and harmony is replaced by the progression of tones. In [[dodecaphonism]], the twelve notes of the scale (the main and intermediate notes) are used, but breaking the hierarchy between them.<ref>{{harvtxt|Comellas|2010|p=326}}</ref> Stylistically, dodecaphonism was strongly linked to expressionism.<ref>{{harvtxt|Menéndez Torrellas|2013|p=454}}</ref> Schönberg composed two operas in this context: ''[[Moses and Aaron|Moses und Aron]]'' (composed from 1926 and unfinished) and ''[[From Today to Tomorrow|Von Heute auf Morgen]]'' (''From Today to Tomorrow'', 1930).<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=298|ref=Atl}}</ref> But undoubtedly, the great opera of atonalism was ''[[Wozzeck]]'' (1925), by [[Alban Berg]], based on the play by [[Georg Büchner]], a romantic opera in terms of thematic, but of complex musical structure, where he experimented with all available musical resources from classicism to the avant-garde, from tonal to atonal, from recitative to music, from popular music to sophisticated music of dissonant counterpoint. A work of strong psychological expression, it united musical atonalism with argumental expressionism.<ref>{{harvtxt|Menéndez Torrellas|2013|pp=451–457}}</ref> His second opera, ''[[Lulu (opera)|Lulu]]'' (1929–1935), based on two dramas by [[Frank Wedekind]], was closer to dodecaphonism, and applied Wagnerian transcomposition.<ref>{{harvtxt|Menéndez Torrellas|2013|p=450}}</ref> The work remained incomplete and was completed by [[Friedrich Cerha]] in 1979.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|pp=266–269|ref=Atl}}</ref>
In 1927, [[Ernst Krenek]] premiered his opera ''[[Jonny spielt auf]]'' (''Jonny begins to play''), which achieved a remarkable success and was the most performed opera of the time. Greatly influenced by jazz, Krenek experimented with the main musical trends of the time: neo-romanticism, neoclassicism, atonality, dodecaphonism and other styles. In 1934 he composed ''[[Karl V]]'' with [[Serialism|serialist]] technique, which included filmed and [[pantomime]] scenes. With the advent of Nazism he emigrated to the United States, where he composed ''Der goldene Bock'' (''The Golden Fleece'', 1964) and ''Der Zauberspiegel'' (''The Magic Mirror'', 1966).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=554|ref=DOI}}</ref>
[[Viktor Ullmann]] was a pupil of Schönberg and, although he did not use atonality, his work employed the chromaticism of the modern Vienna School. His first opera was ''Der Sturz des Antichrist'' (''The Fall of the Antichrist'', 1935).<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|pp=302–303|ref=Atl}}</ref> Being Jewish, he was interned in the [[Theresienstadt concentration camp]] ([[Terezín]]), where he composed his opera ''[[The Emperor of Atlantis|Der Kaiser Von Atlantis]]'' (''The Emperor of Atlantis'', 1944). However, before its release it was banned by the [[SS]], who found some similarity between the protagonist and the figure of [[Adolf Hitler|Hitler]], and the author was sent to the [[Auschwitz]] camp for extermination.<ref name="Atlas-2011aw" /> The same fate befell the Czech [[Hans Krása]], author of the children's opera ''[[Brundibár]]'' (1941).<ref>{{harvtxt|Menéndez Torrellas|2013|p=466}}</ref>
Another field of experimentation was microtonalism, in which [[microtone]]s, musical intervals smaller than a [[semitone]], were used. Prominent in this current was the Czech [[Alois Hába]], author of the opera ''Matka'' (''The Mother'', 1931), which because of its ineffectiveness has been scarcely performed.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=455|ref=DOI}}</ref>
=== Neoclassicism === {{Main|Neoclassicism (music)}}
[[File:Igor_Stravinsky_LOC_32392u.jpg|thumb|218x218px|[[Igor Stravinsky|Ígor Stravinski]]]] The neoclassicism was a return to the musical models of eighteenth-century classicism, characterized by restraint, balance and formal clarity. It developed especially in the interwar period (1920s and 1930s). Its models were basically the classicists, but also recovered baroque forms, as well as various expressive options such as dissonance.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Diccionario de Música''|2003|p=199|ref=Lar}}</ref> In general, more objective and defined musical forms were sought, with a more contrasted and diaphanous timbre, repetitive rhythms – with frequent use of ''[[ostinato]]'' — and a more diatonic harmony, far from Wagnerian chromaticism.<ref>{{harvtxt|Menéndez Torrellas|2013|p=434}}</ref>
One of the countries where this style was most prevalent was the [[Soviet Union]]. Its main representative, [[Igor Stravinsky]], was close to expressionism, although he can sometimes be described as neoclassical, but more than anything else he was an unclassifiable genius with a surprising palette of sounds of great variety; he himself never wanted to be associated with any particular style. In his beginnings, still influenced by Russian folklore, and with a certain influence of French impressionism, he enjoyed great success with his ballets, represented by the [[Ballets Russes]] of [[Sergey Diaghilev]]. After them, he began a new stage of a more sober musicality, reducing instrumental resources, which is evident in his first opera, ''[[The Nightingale (opera)|Le rossignol]]'' (''The Nightingale'', 1914), based on a tale by [[Hans Christian Andersen]], which combined neo-romantic and orientalist influences. His next stage work was ''[[Mavra]]'' (1922), a tribute to the Russian school of traditional opera. A more ambitious project was ''[[Oedipus Rex (opera)|Œdipus Rex]]'' (1927), with a libretto by [[Jean Cocteau]] translated into Latin, in neoclassical style. After these works, all short, his only full-length opera was ''The Rake's Progress'' (''The Rake's Progress'', 1951), based on a series of etchings by [[William Hogarth]] that he saw at the [[Art Institute of Chicago]], which were dramatized by the poet [[Wystan Hugh Auden]].<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|pp=282–283|ref=Atl}}</ref> [[File:Lady_Macbeth_of_the_Mtsensk_District.jpg|left|thumb|Performance of ''[[Lady Macbeth of Mtsensk (opera)|Lady Macbeth of Mtsensk]]'' by [[Dmitri Shostakovich]], [[Teatro Comunale di Bologna|Teatro Comunale]] of [[Bologna]] (2014)]] [[Dmitri Shostakovich]] lived all his life under the censorship magnifying glass of the Soviet regime, but he achieved some of the best compositions of the century. His first opera was ''[[The Nose (opera)|Nos]]'' (''The Nose'', 1928), a critique of the reign of [[Nicholas I of Russia|Nicolas I]]. His masterpiece was ''[[Lady Macbeth of Mtsensk (opera)|Ledi Mákbet Mtsénskogo Uyezda]]'' (''Lady Macbeth of Mtsensk'', 1936), on a play by [[Nikolai Leskov]], which enjoyed great success outside his country, but was disliked by the Soviet authorities for its dissonant style. A revised version with the title ''Katerina Ismailova'' was premiered in Moscow in 1963.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=299|ref=Atl}}</ref>
[[Sergey Prokofiev]], a pupil of Rimsky-Korsakov, combined traditional music with some experimental features. He composed his first opera, ''Maddalena'' (1911) at the age of twenty. During a tour of the United States, the Chicago Opera commissioned him an opera, ''The Love of the Three Oranges'' (''The Love of the Three Oranges'', 1919), based on a text by [[Carlo Gozzi]], a comic work of [[Surrealism|surrealist]] tone. He lived a few years in Paris, time in which he made ''Ógnenny angel'' (''The Angel of Fire'', 1923). After returning to his country in 1933, he tackled ''[[War and Peace (opera)|Vojna i Mir]]'' (''War and Peace'', 1946), by [[Lev Tolstoy]], which was not well received by the Soviet authorities, who branded him as unpatriotic. To congratulate himself with them he composed ''[[The Story of a Real Man|Póvest ''or nastoyáschem cheloveke'']]'' (''The Story of a Real Man'', 1948), more adept to the regime.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|pp=294–295|ref=Atl}}</ref> [[File:Sergei_Prokofiev_02.jpg|thumb|[[Sergei Prokofiev|Serguéi Prokófiev]]]] [[Sergey Rakhmaninov]] was influenced by Tchaikovsky. He devoted himself mainly to symphonic and piano music, but tackled opera in works such as ''Aleko'' (1893), ''Skupój rýtsar'' (''The Miserly Gentleman'', 1906) and ''Francesca da Rimini'' (1907).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=277|ref=DOII}}</ref>
The Swedish-born British [[Gustav Holst|Gustav Theodor Holst]] premiered in 1916 ''[[Savitri (opera)|Savitri]]'', a chamber opera based on the Indian ''[[Mahābhārata]]'', in which he employed [[bitonality]]. He subsequently composed ''[[The Perfect Fool]]'' (1923), ''[[At the Boar's Head]]'' (1925) and ''The Tale of the Wandering Scholar'' (1934).<ref name="Alier-2007f" />
[[Ralph Vaughan Williams]] was the author in 1924 of a ''ballad opera'', ''Hugh the Drover'' (''Hugh the Drover''). In 1929 he premiered ''[[Sir John in Love]]'', about the Shakespearian character of [[Falstaff]]. After the operetta ''[[The Poisoned Kiss]]'' (''The Poisoned Kiss''), he composed ''[[Riders to the Sea]]'' (''Riders to the Sea''), of veristic and Wagnerian affiliation.
