His last work was ''[[The Pilgrim's Progress (opera)|The Pilgrim's Progress]]'' (''The Pilgrim's Progress'', 1951).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=512|ref=DOII}}</ref> [[File:Martinu_1943.jpg|left|thumb|254x254px|[[Bohuslav Martinů]]]] The German [[Carl Orff]] was the author of the operas ''Der Mond'' (''The Moon'', 1939), ''[[Die Kluge]]'' (''The Cunning One'', 1943), ''Antigonae'' (1949) and ''Oedipus der Tyrann'' (''Oedipus the King'', 1959).<ref>{{harvtxt|''Dictionary of Music''|2003|p=208|ref=Lar}}</ref> His cantata ''Carmina Burana'' (1937), based on medieval [[Goliardo|goliardic]] poems, although not an opera, is often performed in opera houses.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=197|ref=DOI}}</ref>
In Italy, [[Gian Francesco Malipiero]] developed a style which, despite its modernity, denoted the weight of Italian musical tradition, especially Monteverdi and Vivaldi. He was the author of ''L'Orfeide'' (1925), ''Giulio Cesare'' (1936), ''I caprici di Callot'' (1942), ''Il figliuol prodigo'' (1953) and ''Il capitan Spavento'' (1963).<ref name="DOII77">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=77.|ref=DOII}}</ref> [[Ildebrando Pizzetti]] also combined modernity and tradition, influenced by Renaissance and Baroque music. After several youthful works, in 1915 he premiered ''Fedra'', based on a play by [[Gabriele D'Annunzio]]. It was followed by operas such as ''Debora e Jaele'' (1922), ''Fra Gherardo'' (1928) and ''Lo straniero'' (1930). In his following works he gave predominance to the [[arioso]]: ''Orseolo'' (1935), ''Vanna Lupa'' (1950) and ''Cagliostro'' (1953). One of his greatest successes was ''Assassino nella cattedrale'' (1958), on a play by [[T. S. Eliot]].<ref name="Alier-2007r" /> [[Ferruccio Busoni]], based in Germany, evolved from a post-Romantic influenced by [[Robert Schumann|Schumann]], [[Johannes Brahms|Brahms]] and [[Felix Mendelssohn|Mendelssohn]] to a ''young classicism'' – in his words – that gradually assumed contemporary novelties. He was the author of ''Die Brautwahl'' (''The Bride's Choice'', 1912), ''Arlecchino'' (1917), ''[[Turandot (Busoni)|Turandot]]'' (1917) and ''[[Doktor Faust]]'' (1925); the latter, incomplete, was completed by his disciple [[Philipp Jarnach]].<ref>{{harvtxt|''Dictionary of Music''|2003|pp=58–59|ref=Lar}}</ref> [[Licinio Refice]], who was a priest, made operas on religious themes: ''Santa Cecilia'' (1934) and ''[[Margherita da Cortona]]'' (1938).<ref name="DOII285">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=285.|ref=DOII}}</ref>
The Czech [[Bohuslav Martinů]] was an eclectic musician who brought together neoclassicism with a certain neo-baroque tendency, impressionism and American popular music ([[jazz]] and [[gospel music|gospel]]). Settled in Paris, he produced works such as ''Le Soldat et la danseuse'' (''The Soldier and the Dancer'', 1927), ''Les Trois Souhaits'' (''The Three Wishes'', 1929) and ''Hry or Marii'' (''The Miracles of Mary'', 1934). He later composed two operas for radio: ''[[Comedy on the Bridge|Veselohra na mostě]]'' (''Comedy on the Bridge'', 1935) and ''Julietta'' (1937). His last works were ''Ariane'' (1958) and ''Řecké pašije'' (''The Greek Passion'', 1959).<ref name="Atlas-2011ay">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=292|ref=Atl}}</ref>
Other exponents were: [[Werner Egk]] (''[[Die Zaubergeige]]'' [''The Magic Fiddle''], 1935; ''[[Irische Legende]]'' [''Irish Legend''], 1954; ''[[Der Revisor]]'' [''The Revisor''], 1957),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=322|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[Dmitri Kabalevsky]] (''Breugnon Tails'', 1938),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|pp=526–527|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[Albert Roussel]] (''[[Padmâvatî]]'', 1923),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2011|p=332}}</ref> [[Othmar Schoeck]] (''Penthesilea'', 1927),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=368|ref=DOII}}</ref> [[Heinrich Sutermeister]] (''Die schwarze Spinne'' [''The Black Spider''], 1936; ''Romeo und Julia'', 1940; ''Die Zauberinsel'' [''The Magic Island''], 1942; ''Raskolnikoff'', 1948)<ref name="Alier-2007s" /> and [[Rudolf Wagner-Régeny]] (''Der Günstling'', 1935; ''Die Bürger von Calais'' [''The Burghers of Calais''], 1939).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=542|ref=DOII}}</ref>
=== Post World War II: tradition and avant-garde === [[File:Copenhagen_Opera_House_2014_04.jpg|thumb|300x300px|[[Copenhagen Opera House]], opened in 2005]] After [[World War II]], opera continued with its split, perhaps more accentuated, between the neoclassical tradition and the musical avant-gardes inherited from impressionism, expressionism and atonalism, which gave new musical styles such as [[concrete music]], [[integral serialism]], [[aleatoric music]], [[minimalism]], etc. In general, the new musical languages have been misunderstood by the public, which has remained anchored in the traditional repertoire.<ref>{{harvtxt|Azcárate Ristori|Pérez Sánchez|Ramírez Domínguez|1983|pp=938–941}}</ref>
The immediate postwar period was a difficult period for opera, not only because of the destruction caused during the war, but also because of the pessimism and skepticism of the intellectual elites, who came to question the viability of the operatic genre, which they considered obsolete. In those early years, modest productions predominated, along with experimental forms such as the so-called "anti-opera", based on the [[deconstruction]] of the traditional resources of the genre. Other authors, such as [[John Cage]], tried a type of scenic music without plot and, sometimes, even without text, in works such as ''Living Room Music'' (1940) or ''Water Walk'' (1959). [[György Ligeti]] created a type of ''phonetic theater'' based on noises and onomatopoeias (''Nouvelles Aventures'', 1966).<ref>{{harvtxt|Menéndez Torrellas|2013|pp=475–476}}</ref>
In the 1960s, opera was making a resurgence and composers embarked on more ambitious and larger-scale works. Alongside productions of a more traditional character, anti-opera with experimental and aleatoric elements, without any narrative character, followed; examples would be [[Dieter Schnebel]]'s ''Glossolalie'' (1961), [[Henri Pousseur]]'s ''Votre Faust'' (1969) and [[Mauricio Kagel]]'s ''Staatstheater'' (1970). As a reaction, between the 1970s and 1980s the ''anti-anti-opera'' emerged, which recovered the narrative text and the traditional resources of opera, although with a contemporary language and a certain ironic and satirical component; some exponents would be: ''[[We Come to the River]]'' by [[Hans Werner Henze]] (1976), ''[[Le Grand Macabre]]'' by György Ligeti (1978), ''[[Jakob Lenz (opera)|Jakob Lenz]]'' by [[Wolfgang Rihm]] (1979) and ''[[Un re in ascolto]]'' by [[Luciano Berio]] (1984).<ref>{{harvtxt|Menéndez Torrellas|2013|pp=487–488}}</ref>
==== United Kingdom ==== [[File:Benjamin_Britten,_London_Records_1968_publicity_photo_for_Wikipedia_crop.jpg|left|thumb|[[Benjamin Britten]]]] In this period, one of the most prolific opera composers was [[Benjamin Britten]]. The most recurrent themes in his works were loneliness and pain, his major existential concerns, as deoned in his first work, ''[[Peter Grimes]]'' (1945). He then ventured into chamber opera, as in his works ''[[The Rape of Lucretia (opera)|The Rape of Lucretia]]'' (1946) and ''[[Albert Herring]]'' (1947). For this he founded the English Opera Group orchestra and the [[Aldeburgh Festival]] ([[Sussex]]). In 1951, the Royal Opera proposed him to compose a second part of ''[[Peter Grimes]]'', which resulted in ''[[Billy Budd (opera)|Billy Budd]]'', based on a text by [[Herman Melville]]. For the coronation of [[Elizabeth II of the United Kingdom|Elizabeth II]] he composed ''[[Gloriana (opera)|Gloriana]]'' (1953). He dealt with psychological drama with ''The Turn of the Screw'' (1954), based on a play by [[Henry James]]. In 1960 he drew on Shakespeare for ''[[A Midsummer Night's Dream]]''. After some religiously inspired works, ''[[Owen Wingrave]]'' (1971) focused on [[pacifism]], one of Britten's preoccupations, whose premiere was on television. His last work was ''[[Death in Venice (opera)|Death in Venice]]'' (1973), on the novel by [[Thomas Mann]]. He was the partner of the tenor [[Peter Pears]], with whom he collaborated in numerous works.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|pp=270–271|ref=Atl}}</ref> [[File:A_Midsummer_Night's_Dream_at_BMF_(2016).jpg|thumb|Performance of ''[[A Midsummer Night's Dream (opera)|A Midsummer Night's Dream]]'' by [[Benjamin Britten]], Peking Music Festival (2016)]] [[William Walton]] was influenced by [[Igor Stravinsky|Stravinsky]], [[Jean Sibelius|Sibelius]] and [[jazz]]. In 1947 he was commissioned to write an opera for the reopening of the Royal Opera House in London and, after six years of work, he presented ''[[Troilus and Cressida (opera)|Troilus and Cressida]]'', whose premiere in 1954 was not as successful as expected. In 1967 he premiered ''[[The Bear (opera)|The Bear]]'', a one-act comedy that parodied the vocal excesses of traditional opera.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=303|ref=Atl}}</ref>
[[Michael Tippett]] was inspired by Mozart's ''[[Die Zauberflöte]]'' for his first opera, ''[[The Midsummer Marriage]]'' ''(''1955). His next projects were all different in theme and structure: ''[[King Priam]]'' (1962), ''[[The Knot Garden]]'' (1970), ''[[The Ice Break]]'' (1977) and ''[[New Year (opera)|New Year]]'' (1989). In the latter he introduced some elements of [[rap]].<ref name="Atlas-2011az">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=302|ref=Atl}}</ref>
[[Harrison Birtwistle]] achieved great success with his first opera, ''[[Punch and Judy (opera)|Punch and Judy]]'' (1968), which was notable for its lack of direct narration, being a story told from several points of view. He repeated the same formula in ''The Mask of Orpheus'' (1984). Other works of his were ''Yan Tan Tethera'' (1986), ''Gawain'' (1991), ''The Second Mrs. Kong'' (1994) and ''[[The Last Supper (opera)|The Last Supper]]'' (2000).<ref name="Atlas-2011ba">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=285|ref=Atl}}</ref>
[[Mark-Anthony Turnage]] developed an innovative work, with great jazz influence and a taste for percussion. In ''Greek'' (1988) he required from the singers a vocal declamation that should sound as if it were not opera. In 1997 he adapted [[H. G. Wells]]'s ''The Country of the Blind'' and, in 2002, a work by [[Sean O'Casey]], ''[[The Silver Tassie (opera)|The Silver Tassie]]''.<ref name="Atlas-2011az" />
Notable in minimalism was [[Michael Nyman]], author of ''The Man who mistook his Wife for a Hat'' (''The Man who mistook his Wife for a Hat'', 1986) and ''Facing Goya'' (2000).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=183|ref=DOII}}</ref>
Other exponents were: [[Lennox Berkeley]] (''Ruth'', 1956; ''The Castaway'', 1967),<ref name="Alier-2007ad">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=119|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[Arthur Bliss]] (''The Olympians'', 1949; ''Tobias and the Angel'', 1960),<ref name="Alier-2007w" /> [[Alan Bush]] (''Wat Tyler'', 1950; ''The Man of Blackmoor'', 1955; ''The Sugar Reapers'', 1964),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=173|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[Peter Maxwell Davies]] (''[[Taverner (opera)|Taverner]]'', 1962; ''The Lighthouse'', 1980),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|pp=104–105|ref=DOII}}</ref> [[Thea Musgrave]] (''Mary, Queen of Scots'', 1977; ''A Christmas Carol'', 1978)<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=153|ref=DOII}}</ref> and [[John Tavener]] (''St. Mary of Egypt'', 1992). More recently, [[Thomas Adès]] (''[[Powder Her Face]]'', 1995; ''[[The Tempest (opera)|The Tempest]]'', 2004),<ref>{{cite web |title=Thomas Adès |website=[[Discogs]] |url=https://www.discogs.com/en/artist/926336-Thomas-Ad%C3%A8s}}</ref> [[Judith Weir]] (''A Night at the Chinese Opera'', 1987)<ref>{{cite web |title=Judith Weir |url=https://www.wisemusicclassical.com/composer/1689/judith-weir/}}</ref> and [[George Benjamin (composer)|George Benjamin]] (''[[Written on Skin]]'', 2012).<ref>{{cite web |title=Written on Skin – review |website=[[TheGuardian.com]] |date=8 July 2012 |url=https://www.theguardian.com/music/2012/jul/08/written-on-skin-review}}</ref>
==== France and Italy ==== [[File:Olivier_Messiaen_(1986).jpg|thumb|[[Olivier Messiaen]]]] In France, one of the most outstanding musicians of the period was [[Olivier Messiaen]]. A student of [[Paul Dukas]], he integrated in his style diverse influences, from classical to serialism and oriental music. He composed only one opera, ''Saint-François d'Assise'' (''Saint-François d'Assise'', 1983), a highly staged work, which included three Martenot waves.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|pp=292–293|ref=Atl}}</ref>
[[Emmanuel Bondeville]] was director of the Opéra-Comique and the Paris Opera. He was the author of ''Madame Bovary'' (1951), based on the play by [[Gustave Flaubert]], and ''Antoine et Cléopâtre'' (''Antony and Cleopatra'', 1974), based on Shakespeare.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=142|ref=DOI}}</ref>
[[Philippe Fénelon]] was a disciple of Messiaen, author of operas such as ''Le chevalier imaginaire'' (1992), ''Salammbô'' (1998) and ''Faust'' (2007).<ref name="Alier-2007h" /> [[File:Scene_from_the_opera_Salvatore_Giuliano_by_Lorenzo_Ferrero.JPG|left|thumb|Representación de ''[[Salvatore Giuliano (opera)|Salvatore Giuliano]]'' de [[Lorenzo Ferrero]], Staatstheater Kassel (1996)]] In Italy, [[Luigi Dallapiccola]] framed himself in serialism, with a strong influence of Schönberg, as is evident in his first opera, ''[[Volo di notte]]'' (''Night Flight'', 1940), based on a work by [[Antoine de Saint-Exupéry]]. In 1949 he premiered ''[[Il prigioniero]]'', in which he used three rows of twelve tones that symbolically connected each element of the opera. It was followed by ''Job'' (1950) and ''Ulisse'' (1968), which were not as successful.<ref name="Atlas-2011ap" />
[[Luigi Nono]] started in serialism and then moved on to electronic music. He was the author of ''[[Intolleranza 1960]]'' (1961), which was followed by ''[[Al gran sole carico d'amore]]'' (1975) and ''Prometeo. Tragedia dell'ascolto'' (1984).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|pp=175–176|ref=DOII}}</ref>
[[Luciano Berio]], a disciple of Dallapiccola, evolved from neoclassicism towards integral serialism. He composed some operas, such as ''Opera'' (''Opera'', 1970), ''La vera storia'' (''The true story'', 1982), ''Un re in ascolto'' (''A king listens'', 1984) — the last two with librettos by [[Italo Calvino]] — ''Outis'' (1996) and ''Cronaca del luogo'' (''Chronicle of the place'', 1999). He also composed a new finale for the opera ''[[Turandot (Puccini)|Turandot]]'' by [[Giacomo Puccini]], replacing [[Franco Alfano]]'s, which he premiered in 2002.<ref name="Alier-2007ad" />
Other exponents were: [[Franco Mannino]] (''Vivì'', 1957; ''Il diavolo in giardino'', 1963; ''Il ritratto di Dorian Gray'', 1982),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=80|ref=DOII}}</ref> [[Jacopo Napoli]] (''Miseria e nobiltà'', 1945; ''Massaniello'', 1953),<ref name="Alier-2007u" /> Goffredo Petrassi (''Il cordovano'', 1949; ''Morte dell'aria'', 1960),<ref name="Alier-2007p" /> [[Renzo Rossellini (composer)|Renzo Rossellini]] (''La guerra'', 1956; ''Il vortice'', 1958; ''Uno sguardo dal ponte'', 1961)<ref name="Alier-2007q" /> and [[Nino Rota]] (''Il cappello di paglia di Firenze'', 1944).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=321|ref=DOII}}</ref> More recently, it is worth mentioning: [[Lorenzo Ferrero]] (''[[Salvatore Giuliano (opera)|Salvatore Giuliano]]'', 1986; ''[[La Conquista (opera)|La Conquista]]'', 2005; ''[[Risorgimento! (opera)|Risorgimento!]]'', 2011)<ref>{{cite web |title=Lorenzo Ferrero |url=http://www.ciamcreators.org/en/partners/lorenzo-ferrero/}}</ref> and [[Salvatore Sciarrino]] (''[[Luci mie traditrici]]'', 1998).<ref>{{cite web |title=Salvatore Sciarrino |url=http://www.ricordi.com/Compositori/Dettaglio.asp?IdCompositore=314| archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070614111816/http://www.ricordi.com/Compositori/Dettaglio.asp?IdCompositore=314 | archive-date=2007-06-14 }}</ref>
==== Germany and Austria ==== [[File:St._März_2005.jpg|thumb|[[Karlheinz Stockhausen]]]] In Germany, [[Karlheinz Stockhausen]] was one of the most prominent representatives of serialism and electronic music. He tackled one of the most complex operatic projects, ''Licht: Die Sieben Tage der Woche'' (''Light: The Seven Days of the Week''), a cycle of seven operas composed between 1977 and 2003.<ref name="Atlas-2011ao" /> It was premiered in its entirety in 2011, although each part had already been premiered separately. Stockhausen's pretension was to realize a "cosmic theater" that would unite music and religion, in search of a transcendental vision of the human being.<ref>{{harvtxt|Menéndez Torrellas|2013|p=503}}</ref>
[[Bernd Alois Zimmermann]] was an avant-garde musician who was influenced by Schönberg's dodecaphonism and composers such as Stravinsky, Honegger, Poulenc and Milhaud. In his work he brought together acoustic and electronic sounds, and used recording as an additional resource. He composed only one opera, ''[[The Soldiers|Die Soldaten]]'' (''The Soldiers'', 1965), which was considered one of the best German operas of the second half of the century. It was an innovative work, featuring a five-level stage on which up to three scenes could be performed simultaneously.<ref name="Atlas-2011ar" />
Hans Werner Henze brought together in his work expressionism, serialism, dodecaphonism, neoclassicism and popular music such as jazz. After his first full-length opera, ''[[Boulevard Solitude]]'' (1952), he moved to Italy, where his style became more sensual and exuberant, in works such as ''[[The Deer King|König Hirsch]]'' (''The Deer King'', 1956), ''[[Der Prinz von Homburg (opera)|Der Prinz von Homburg]]'' (1960), ''[[Elegy for Young Lovers|Elegie für junge Liebende]]'' (''Elegy for Young Lovers'', 1961), ''[[Der junge Lord]]'' (''The Young Lord'', 1965) and ''Die Bassariden'' (''The Bacchantes'', 1962). His following works expressed his leftist political commitment: ''[[We Come to the River]]'' (1976), ''[[Die englische Katze]]'' (1983). In ''[[L'Upupa und der Triumph der Sohnesliebe]]'' (''The Hoopoe and the Triumph of Filial Love'', 2003) he also wrote the libretto.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=289|ref=Atl}}</ref>
[[Wolfgang Rihm]] was a pupil of Stockhausen, although he sought to move beyond experimental languages in search of greater expressiveness. Influenced by [[Antonin Artaud]]'s theater of cruelty'','' he sought a greater communion between the musical performance and the audience, through a more textured orchestra, nuanced vocality and lyrical, dynamic music. He composed six operas'':'' Faust und Yorick ''(1976),'' [[Jakob Lenz (opera)|Jakob Lenz]] ''(1978),'' Die Hamletmaschine ''(1986),'' Oedipus ''(1987),'' Die Eroberung von Mexico ''(''The Conquest of Mexico'', 1992) and'' Séraphin ''(1994).<ref name="Atlas-2011au" />''
[[Wolfgang Fortner]] was the author of two operas based on works by [[Federico García Lorca]]: ''Bluthochzeit'' (''Blood Wedding'', 1958) and ''In seinem Garten lebt Don Perlimplín mit Belisa'' (''Don Perlimplín con Belisa en su jardín'', 1962). He also composed the opera buffa ''Corinna'' (1958) and ''Elisabeth Tudor'' (1972).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=378|ref=DOI}}</ref>
Other German composers were: [[Boris Blacher]] (''Fürstin Tarakanowa'', 1945; ''Die Flut'' [''The Flood''], 1947; ''Preußisches Märchen'' [''Prussian Fairy Tale''], 1949),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=131|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[Paul Dessau]] (''[[Die Verurteilung des Lukullus]]'' [''The Damnation of Lucullus''], 1951; ''Puntila'', 1966; ''Einstein'', 1974),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=285|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[Giselher Klebe]] (''Die Räuber'' [''The Bandits''], 1957; ''Figaro lässt sich scheiden'' [''Figaro gets divorced''], 1963),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|pp=542–543|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[Helmut Lachenmann]] (''Das Mädchen mit den Schwefelhölzern'' [''The Matchgirl''], 1996)<ref>{{harvtxt|Menéndez Torrellas|2013|p=501}}</ref> and [[Aribert Reimann]] (''[[Ein Traumspiel]]'' [''Phantasmagoria''], 1965; ''[[Lear (opera)|Lear]]'', 1978; ''[[Bernarda Albas Haus]]'' [''The House of Bernarda Alba''], 2000).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=287|ref=DOII}}</ref>
In Austria, [[Gottfried von Einem]] was influenced by Stravinsky, Prokofiev and jazz. He made his debut with ''[[Dantons Tod]]'' (''The Death of Danton'', 1947), on a text by [[Georg Büchner]], and ''[[Der Prozeß (opera)|Der Prozeß]]'' (''The Trial'', 1953), on the work of [[Franz Kafka]]. In 1971 he premiered ''Der Besuch der alten Dame'' (''The Visit of the Old Lady''), based on the play by [[Friedrich Dürrenmatt]]. His last opera was ''Kabale und Liebe'' (1976), based on the play by [[Friedrich Schiller]] that Verdi used for ''Luisa Miller''.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=323|ref=DOI}}</ref>
==== United States ==== [[File:Samuel_Barber.jpg|thumb|[[Samuel Barber]]]] In the United States, [[Samuel Barber]] excelled as a melodist, with a conservative style sometimes described as post-romantic. He collaborated on occasions with [[Gian Carlo Menotti]], who wrote the libretto for his first and most famous opera, ''[[Vanessa (opera)|Vanessa]]'' (1957), based on a play by [[Isak Dinesen]], for which he won a [[Pulitzer Prize]]. More grandiloquent was ''[[Antony and Cleopatra (1966 opera)|Antony and Cleopatra]]'' (1966), commissioned for the Metropolitan Opera.<ref name="Atlas-2011bb">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=284|ref=Atl}}</ref> For his part, the Italian-American Menotti was a precocious talent, having already written two operas by the age of thirteen. Influenced by Italian verismo, his work is notable for its melodramatism: ''[[Amelia Goes to the Ball]]'' (''Amelia Goes to the Ball'', 1937), ''[[The Medium]]'' (''The Medium'', 1946), ''[[The Telephone, or L'Amour à trois|The Telephone]]'' (1947), ''[[The Consul]]'' (1950), ''[[Amahl and the Night Visitors]]'' (''Amahl and the Night Visitors'', 1951), ''[[The Saint of Bleecker Street]]'' (1954).<ref name="Atlas-2011ay" />
[[Leonard Bernstein]] was a composer and conductor, author of numerous musicals and film scores. His first opera was ''[[Trouble in Tahiti]]'' (1951), which mixed the traditional operatic genre with pop music. In 1956 he premiered his comic operetta ''[[Candide (operetta)|Candide]]'', which he was not very satisfied with and tinkered with for thirty years.<ref name="Atlas-2011ba" />
Carlisle Floyd composed two operas during his student period: ''Slow Dusk'' (1949) and ''The Fugitives'' (1951). With ''[[Susannah]]'' (1955), which combined a certain post-romanticism with elements of American folklore —especially [[Quaker]] hymns— he obtained a great success and excellent reviews. His next works were not as successful: ''[[Wuthering Heights]]'' (''Wuthering Heights'', 1958), based on the work of [[Emily Brontë]]; and ''[[Of Mice and Men (opera)|Of Mice and Men]]'' (''Of Mice and Men'', 1969), based on a text by [[John Steinbeck]].<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|pp=286–287|ref=Atl}}</ref>
[[John Adams (composer)|John Adams]] was described as a minimalist, although in his music one finds neo-Romantic echoes and elements of popular music, such as [[jazz]], [[ragtime]], [[Swing (jazz)|swing]], pop and rock. His first opera was ''[[Nixon in China]]'' (1987), followed by ''[[The Death of Klinghoffer]]'' (1991), the operas-oratorios ''[[El Niño (oratorio)|El Niño]]'' (2000) and ''[[The Gospel According to the Other Mary]]'' (2012) and other stage works.<ref name="Atlas-2011bb" />
Philip Glass was one of the most prominent representatives of minimalism, a style that, as opposed to the dense structures used at the time in Europe, sought to elaborate music with few elements, using for example repetitive patterns in different speed, duration and volume, in staggered or superimposed alterations. He was also influenced by Eastern —especially Indian— and North African music. In 1967 he formed the Philip Glass Ensemble. His first opera, ''[[Einstein on the Beach]]'' (''Einstein on the Beach'', 1976), catapulted him to fame. It was then followed by ''[[Satyagraha (opera)|Satyagraha]]'' (1980) and ''[[Akhnaten (opera)|Akhnaten]]'' (1984). After his opera ''The Voyage'' (1992) he made a trilogy based on films by [[Jean Cocteau]]: ''[[Orpheus (film)|Orphée]]'' (1993), ''La Belle et la Bête'' (1994) and ''[[Les enfants terribles]]'' (1996). In 1998 he released a digital opera in 3-D, ''Monsters of Grace''.<ref name="Atlas-2011bc">{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|p=288|ref=Atl}}</ref> Another exponent of minimalism was [[Steve Reich]] (''Three Tales'', 2002).<ref>{{harvtxt|Menéndez Torrellas|2013|p=488}}</ref>
Mention should also be made of: [[Mark Adamo]] (''[[Little Women (opera)|Little Women]]'' [''Little Women''], 1998; ''[[Lysistrata (opera)|Lysistrata]]'', 2005),<ref name="Alier-2007l" /> [[George Antheil]] (''Volpone'', 1953; ''The Brothers'', 1954; ''The Wish'', 1955),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=52|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[Marc Blitzstein]] (''[[Regina (Blitzstein)|Regina]]'', 1949; ''Idiots First'', 1963),<ref name="Alier-2007w" /> [[William Bolcom]] (''Casino Paradise'', 1990; ''A View from the Bridge'', 1999)<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=139|ref=DOI}}</ref> and [[Aaron Copland]] (''[[The Tender Land]]'', 1954).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=243|ref=DOI}}</ref>
==== Spain and Latin America ==== [[File:Teatro_Real_(Madrid)_03.jpg|thumb|[[Teatro Real]] (Madrid)]] Spain entered modernity in the second half of the century, after leaving behind musical nationalism and the virtual disappearance of zarzuela. [[Xavier Montsalvatge]] stood out for his skillful use of instrumentation, with a certain influence of Olivier Messiaen. He was the author of ''El gato con botas'' (1947) and ''Una voce in off'' (1961).<ref>{{harvtxt|''Diccionario de Música''|2003|p=191|ref=Lar}}</ref> [[Roberto Gerhard]] was a student of Schönberg and the first Spanish composer to use dodecaphonism. He was also influenced by Falla.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Diccionario de Música''|2003|p=127|ref=Lar}}</ref> He was the author of the opera ''[[The Duenna]]'' (1951).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=408|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[Luis de Pablo]], self-taught, departed from serialism towards aleatoric music, while practicing electronic music. He was the author of the operas ''Kiú'' (1982), ''El viajero indiscreto'' (1988) and ''La madre invita a comer'' (1992).<ref>{{harvtxt|''Diccionario de Música''|2003|p=212|ref=Lar}}</ref> [[Cristóbal Halffter]] developed his work starting from serialism and dodecaphonism. He was the author of the operas ''Don Quixote'' (2000) and ''Lazaro'' (2007).<ref>{{harvtxt|''Diccionario de Música''|2003|p=138|ref=Lar}}</ref> Mention should also be made of: [[Salvador Bacarisse]] (''El tesoro de Boabdil'', 1958),<ref>{{harvtxt|''Diccionario de Música''|2003|p=24|ref=Lar}}</ref> [[Xavier Benguerel]] (''Llibre vermell'', 1988),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=114|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[José María Cano]] (''[[Luna (opera)|Moon]]'', 1998), [[Francisco Escudero García de Goizueta|Francisco Escudero]] (''Gernika'', 1987),<ref>{{harvtxt|''Diccionario de Música''|2003|p=110|ref=Lar}}</ref> [[Josep Mestres Quadreny]] (''El Ganxo'', 1959; ''Cap de Mirar'', 1991),<ref>{{harvtxt|''Diccionario de Música''|2003|p=185|ref=Lar}}</ref> [[Joaquín Nin-Culmell]] (''La Celestina'', 1965), [[Matilde Salvador]] (''Vinatea'', 1974),<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=340|ref=DOII}}</ref> [[Tomás Marco]] (''Selene'', 1974),<ref name="Alier-2011b">{{harvtxt|Alier|2011|p=336}}</ref> [[Amando Blanquer Ponsoda|Amando Blanquer]] (''El triomf de Tirant'', 1992),<ref name="Alier-2011b" /> [[Carles Santos]] (''Asdrúbila'', 1992; ''Ricardo y Elena'', 2000)<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2011|p=280}}</ref> and [[Joan Guinjoan]] (''Gaudí'', 2004).<ref>{{harvtxt|''Diccionario de Música''|2003|p=136|ref=Lar}}</ref> [[File:Fotografía_del_compositor_argentino_Alberto_Ginastera.jpg|left|thumb|223x223px|[[Alberto Ginastera]]]] Latin America entered into full modernity at this time. In [[Argentina]], [[Alberto Ginastera]] composed in a neo-expressionist style heir to serialism, as denoted in his operas ''Don Rodrigo'' (1964), ''[[Bomarzo (Ginastera)|Bomarzo]]'' (1967) and ''[[Beatrix Cenci]]'' (1971). ''[[Bomarzo]]'' was banned in Argentina for its scenes of sex and violence.<ref name="Atlas-2011bc" /> [[Mauricio Kagel]] moved between neoclassicism and aleatoric music, with an eclectic, anti-conventional style, in which a sense of humor and irony stand out. In ''Sur Scène'' (1962), considered an ''anti-opera'', he made a rupturist exercise with a [[deconstruction]] of traditional opera. He repeated with ''Staatstheater'' (1970), an experimental opera without libretto or plot, with recorded orchestral music, a ballet without dance and a choir of sixty voices singing solo. In ''Aus Deutschland'' (1981) he presented Schubert and Goethe singing a black spiritual.<ref name="Atlas-2011aw" /><ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|pp=290–291|ref=Atl}}</ref> Other Argentine authors were: [[Juan José Castro]] (''La zapatera prodigiosa'', 1943; ''Bodas de sangre'', 1953)<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|pp=59–60|ref=DOI}}</ref> and [[Astor Piazzolla]] (''[[María de Buenos Aires]]'', 1968, opera-tango).<ref>{{cite web |title=María de Buenos Aires |url=http://www.piazzolla.org/works2/maria.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150924073019/http://www.piazzolla.org/works2/maria.html |archive-date=2015-09-24 }}</ref> Already in the 21st century it is worth mentioning [[Osvaldo Golijov]] (''[[Ainadamar]]'', 2003).<ref>{{cite web |title=Ainadamar (2003) |url=http://www.osvaldogolijov.com/wd7.htm |access-date=17 June 2022 |archive-date=26 October 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081026103211/http://www.osvaldogolijov.com/wd7.htm |url-status=dead }}</ref>
In Brazil, [[Heitor Villa-Lobos]], self-taught, studied the musical forms of the native tribes of his country, which influenced his music. During a stay in Paris he assimilated the novelties of the musical avant-garde, especially those of Erik Satie and Darius Milhaud. He was the author of ''Magdalena'' (1948), ''Yerma'' (1955) and ''A Menina das Nuvens'' (''The Girl of the Clouds'', 1957).<ref>{{harvtxt|''Diccionario de Música''|2003|p=295|ref=Lar}}</ref>
In Chile it is worth mentioning [[Juan Orrego-Salas]], author of ''El retablo del rey pobre'' (1952). [[Ecuador]]'s exponent was [[Luis Humberto Salgado]] (''Cumandá'', 1940; ''El centurión'', 1959–1961; ''Eunice'', 1956–1962). In Mexico, it is worth mentioning [[Carlos Chávez]] (''Pánfilo y Laurita'', later renamed ''The visitors'', 1957).<ref name="Larousse-1990b" />
==== Other countries ==== [[File:Театр_оперы_и_балета_2017_1.jpg|thumb|[[Astana Opera|Astana Opera House]] ([[Kazakhstan]]), opened in 2013]] In the [[Soviet Union]] (Russia since 1991), music enjoyed state protectionism, although under the stylistic and argumentative guidelines imposed by the regime, generally linked to a certain post-Romantic nationalism.<ref name="ReferenceM">{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=328|ref=DOII}}</ref> [[Tikhon Khrennikov]] excelled as a symphonic musician and was the author of several operas, including ''Frol Skobeiev'' (1950) and ''Mat'' (''The Mother'', 1957), based on a novel by [[Maksim Gorky]].<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=538|ref=DOI}}</ref> [[Fikret Amirov]] composed ballets, symphonic and concert music and some operas, most notably ''Syevil'' (1953), which he recomposed three times until he premiered the definitive version in 1980.<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=44|ref=DOI}}</ref> Other exponents were: [[Vissarion Shebalin]] (''Ukroshcheniye stroptivoy'' [''The Taming of the Shrew''], 1957; ''Solntse nad stepyu'' [''The Sun Above the Steppe''], 1958) and [[Rodion Shchedrin]] (''Oratoriya Lenina'' [''Lenin Oratorio''], 1972; ''[[Lolita (opera)|Lolita]]'', 1992).<ref name="ReferenceM"/>
In [[Hungary]], [[György Ligeti]] developed an eclectic oeuvre in which each of his compositions was independent of the rest, although a certain [[Neo-Dadaist|neodadaist]] tendency was discernible. He composed only one opera, ''[[Le Grand Macabre]]'' (1976), with a somewhat chaotic score that included fragments alluding to other composers, as well as various heterodox sounds, both guttural and from instruments such as horns and whistles.<ref name="Atlas-2011at" />
In [[Finland]], [[Aulis Sallinen]] moved in a traditional tonal language, with a great sense of rapport between music and theater. His first opera, ''Ratsumies'' (''The Rider'', 1975), was a great success, so the Finnish National Opera commissioned another work, ''Punainen viiva'' (''The Red Line'', 1978). The next commission was from the [[BBC]] and the Royal Opera House in London, ''Kunningas lähtee Ranskaan'' (''The King Marches to France'', 1983). Other works of his were ''Kullervo'' (1988), ''Palatsi'' (''The Palace'', 1995, chamber opera) and ''King Lear'' (2000).<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|pp=296–297|ref=Atl}}</ref> [[Einojuhani Rautavaara]] was mostly devoted to symphonic music, but made a foray into opera with ''Apollo v. Marsyas'' (1973), after which he conceived a trilogy in which he elaborated text and music: ''Sammon Ryosto'' (''The Myth of Sampo'', 1982), ''Thomas'' (1986) and ''Vincent'' (1990).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=282|ref=DOII}}</ref> More recently noted composer [[Kaija Saariaho]] (''[[L'amour de loin]]'' [''Love from afar''], 2000; ''Only the Sounds Remains'' [''Only the Sounds Remain''], 2015).<ref>{{harvtxt|Menéndez Torrellas|2013|p=504}}</ref>
Polish [[Krzysztof Penderecki]] was a violin virtuoso as well as a composer, with an avant-garde style heir to atonalism. He composed four operas, of difficult vocal interpretation due to the orchestral density —with [[glissando]]s and [[vibrato]]s— and the guttural sounds integrated in his compositions: ''[[The Devils of Loudun]]'' (1969), ''Paradise Lost'' (1978), ''[[The Black Mask|Die Schwarze Maske]]'' (1986) and ''[[Ubu Rex]]'' (1991).<ref name="Atlas-2011as" />
Slovak [[Eugen Suchoň]] showed some influence of [[Leoš Janáček]], in operas such as ''Krútňava'' (''The Whirlpool'', 1949) and ''Svätopluk'' (1960).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=430|ref=DOII}}</ref>
In Sweden, worth mentioning in these years are: [[Karl-Birger Blomdahl]] (''Aniara'', 1959)<ref name="Alier-2007w" /> and [[Hilding Rosenberg]] (''Hus med dubbel ingäng'' [''House with Two Doors''], 1970).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=315|ref=DOII}}</ref>
In [[Belgium]], [[Philippe Boesmans]] denoted the influence of [[Henri Pousseur]], as seen in ''Reigen'' (1993) and ''Wintermärchen'' (''Winter's Tale'', 1999).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=136|ref=DOI}}</ref>
In Israel, [[Marc Lavry]] composed the first opera in [[Hebrew language|Hebrew]]: ''Dan ha-shomer'' (''Dan the guard'', 1945). Subsequent works include [[Menachem Avidom]] (''Aleksandrah'', 1961).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|pp=503–504|ref=DOI}}</ref>
John Haddock was the author of the first Australiana opera: ''Madelin Lee'' (2004).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=77|ref=DOI}}</ref>
The US-based Chinese [[Tan Dun]] was noted for his music for film and events such as the [[2008 Beijing Olympics]], and was the author of several operas: ''[[Marco Polo (opera)|Marco Polo]]'' (1996), ''[[Tea: A Mirror of Soul]]'' (2002) and ''[[The First Emperor]]'' (2006).<ref>{{cite web |title=Compositions |url=http://tandun.com/compositions/ |access-date=17 June 2022 |archive-date=26 June 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200626214605/http://tandun.com/compositions/ |url-status=dead }}</ref>
In [[Thailand]], the composer [[Somtow Papinian Sucharitkul]], grand-nephew of King [[Rama VI]], was the promoter of the establishment of opera in the country and author of the first national operas: ''Madana'' (1999), ''Mae Naak'' (2001), ''Ayodhya'' (2006).<ref>{{harvtxt|Alier|2007|p=|ref=DOII}}</ref>
=== Singers of the 20th century === [[File:Luciano_Pavarotti_2004.jpg|thumb|250x250px|[[Luciano Pavarotti]], one of the most outstanding tenors of the 20th century]] [[File:Maria_Callas_1958.jpg|thumb|235x235px|[[Maria Callas]], one of the most outstanding sopranos of the 20th century]] Among the most outstanding opera singers of the 20th century it is worth mentioning:
* [[Victoria de los Ángeles]], Spanish soprano of great stage presence; * [[Janet Baker]], English mezzo-soprano interpreter of traditional works and numerous works by Britten and Walton; * [[Cecilia Bartoli]], Italian mezzo-soprano specialist in Handel, Mozart and Rossini; * [[Teresa Berganza]], Spanish mezzo-soprano described by [[Herbert von Karajan]] as the "best mezzo-soprano in the world"; * [[Carlo Bergonzi (tenor)|Carlo Bergonzi]], Italian tenor nicknamed the "tenor of tenors", ideal in Verdian roles; * [[Jussi Björling]], Swedish lyric tenor who excelled in the Italian repertoire; * [[Montserrat Caballé]], Spanish soprano of international renown, interpreter of ''bel canto'' and minor genres such as zarzuela; * [[Maria Callas]], Greek soprano, one of the most famous divas of all times, who combined dramatic intensity and technical precision; * [[Piero Cappuccilli]], Italian baritone interpreter of Verdi; * [[José Carreras]], Spanish tenor, formed the group [[The Three Tenors|''Los Tres Tenores'']] with Domingo and Pavarotti; * [[Boris Christoff]], Bulgarian bass considered the best in the role of ''[[Boris Godunov (opera)|Boris Godunov]]'', as well as in operas by Verdi; * [[Plácido Domingo]], Spanish tenor with a wide variety of roles, the one who has recorded the most records in history; * [[Dietrich Fischer-Dieskau]], German baritone and conductor, much in demand in operatic recitals; * [[Kirsten Flagstad]], Norwegian soprano who performed several Wagnerian roles; * [[Juan Diego Flórez]], Peruvian light tenor specialist in Rossini and Donizetti; * [[Nicolai Gedda]], Swedish tenor who mastered several languages, which allowed him a great variety of roles; * [[Beniamino Gigli]], Italian tenor who succeeded Caruso at the Metropolitan in New York, with a sweet and light voice; * [[Tito Gobbi]], Italian baritone of great stage presence; * [[Elisabeth Grümmer]], German soprano of pure voice and wide register; * [[Marilyn Horne]], American mezzo-soprano who performed Händel and ''bel canto'' works; * [[Hans Hotter]], German bass-baritone of the Wagnerian repertoire; * [[Maria Jeritza]], Czech soprano who triumphed in the interwar period; * [[Alfredo Kraus]], Spanish light lyric tenor, mainly Italian and French repertoire; * [[Lotte Lehmann]], German soprano interpreter of Wagner and Strauss; * [[Christa Ludwig]], German mezzo-soprano, one of Karajan's favorites; * [[Giovanni Martinelli]], Italian tenor of elevated tessitura, performed at the Metropolitan for thirty-three years; * [[Lauritz Melchior]], Danish tenor, the most famous ''[[heldentenor]]'' Wagnerian of the century; * [[Birgit Nilsson]], Swedish soprano with a wide and powerful voice, ideal for Wagnerian registers; * [[Jessye Norman]], American soprano with a wide repertoire; * [[Luciano Pavarotti]], Italian tenor of great vocal gifts, one of the most famous and charismatic of recent times; * [[Ezio Pinza]], Italian bass who performed several seasons at the Metropolitan, as well as in musicals and films; * [[Rosa Ponselle]], American soprano with a deep, soaring voice; * [[Leontyne Price]], American soprano who excelled in Verdian roles; * [[Friedrich Schorr]], Hungarian bass-baritone with a Wagnerian register; * [[Ernestine Schumann-Heink]], Austrian contralto with a wide register; * [[Elisabeth Schwarzkopf]], German soprano who excelled in Mozart roles; * [[Léopold Simoneau]], Canadian tenor specializing in Mozart; [[Mariano Stabile]], Italian baritone who excelled in the role of [[Falstaff]], which he performed 1200 times; * [[Giuseppe Di Stefano]], Italian tenor who formed an artistic couple with Maria Callas; * [[Conchita Supervía]], Spanish mezzo-soprano who excelled in Rossini roles; * [[Joan Sutherland]], Australian soprano capable of great vocal prowess, for which she excelled in ''bel canto''; * [[Renata Tebaldi]], Italian soprano, one of the stars of the Metropolitan Opera along with Maria Callas, with whom she rivaled; * [[Bryn Terfel]], Welsh baritone with a repertoire ranging from Mozart to Wagner; * [[Jon Vickers]], Canadian heroic tenor who excelled in both acting and singing; * [[Leonard Warren]], American dramatic baritone, the only one in his time to reach the high C; * and [[Fritz Wunderlich]], German lyric tenor with a powerful voice.<ref>{{harvtxt|''Atlas ilustrado de la ópera''|2011|pp=308–313|ref=Atl}}</ref>

Bellas Artes II |publisher=Argos Vergara |year=1986 |isbn=84-7017-442-8 |location=Barcelona |language=spanish |ref=BAII}} * {{Cite book |title=Historia de la cultura occidental |publisher=Labor |year=1966 |location=Barcelona |language=spanish |ref=Occ}} * {{Cite book |last=Menéndez Torrellas |first=Gabriel |title=Historia de la ópera |publisher=Akal |year=2013 |isbn=978-84-460-3186-4 |language=spanish}} * {{Cite book |last1=Oliva |first1=César |title=Historia básica del arte escénico |last2=Torres Monreal |first2=Francisco |publisher=Cátedra |year=2005 |isbn=84-376-0916-X |location=Madrid |language=spanish}} * {{Cite book |last=Souriau |first=Étienne |url=https://archive.org/details/diccionarioakald0000sour |title=Diccionario Akal de Estética |publisher=Akal |year=1998 |isbn=84-460-0832-7 |location=Madrid |language=spanish}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Historical commune in the western suburbs of Paris}}[[File:Lycee Paul-Langevin Suresnes3.jpg|thumb|Suresnes motto inscribed on the facade of the [[Paul Langevin]] high school: “No one leaves Surenne who often does not return there.”<ref group="note">“''Surenne''” is the old spelling of the town of Suresnes.</ref>]] The '''history of [[Suresnes]]''' ([[Hauts-de-Seine]]), a commune in the western suburbs of [[Paris]], is closely linked to its unique geographical position between the [[Seine]] and Mont Valérien, one of the highest points in the Paris conurbation. Its economic activities have historically developed in line with this environment, from fishing on the river to vine-growing on the hillsides, the automotive and aeronautics industries along the [[Seine]], and, since the creation of the [[La Défense]] business center in the neighboring towns of [[Courbevoie]] and [[Puteaux]], the headquarters of major corporations. [[File:Suresnes map.svg|left|thumb|Location of Suresnes in the inner suburbs of Paris.]] A simple [[Carolingian dynasty|Carolingian]] villa first mentioned in the 9th century, Suresnes remained a small outlying village until the 19th century. Not connected to the main roads leading to the capital, it was almost self-sufficient, even though it suffered several destructions during the [[Middle Ages]] and [[Modern era|Modern Era]]. However, as the hillsides became covered with vineyards, the town's reputation grew, and writers celebrated the wine of [[Suresnes]]. Establishing a religious pilgrimage to Mont Valérien in the 16th century also contributed to the development of the town's economy, as devotees had to walk the village paths to the [[Calvary (monument)|Calvary]], and [[cabaret]]s were set up in Suresnes to entertain them. In the 17th and 18th centuries, numerous bourgeois and aristocratic residences with vast gardens grew up around the historic center, where wealthy Parisians came to relax. In 1593, one of them even made his property available to host [[Estates General of 1593|negotiation conferences]] between [[Catholic Church|Catholics]] and [[Protestantism|Protestants]], which helped end the [[French Wars of Religion|Wars of Religion]]. [[File:Suresnes schématique.svg|thumb|Diagram of Suresnes.]] From the mid-19th century onwards, the face of Suresnes changed radically, with the arrival of the train, tramway, and navigation services on the Seine: the town was now within easy reach of Parisians, who flocked to the [[guinguette]]s lining its quays, often after attending the races at the nearby [[Longchamp Racecourse|Longchamp racecourse]]. The [[Industrialisation|development of the industry]] from the 1890s onwards led to the installation of numerous factories, gradually eradicating the properties of the Ancien Régime: Suresnes was now an industrial town with a rapidly growing working-class population. The election of [[Henri Sellier]] as mayor of Suresnes in 1919 led to considerable urban change: a promoter of workers' housing and driven by hygienic ideas, the mayor increased the number of public services (schools, medical dispensaries, etc.) and housing projects, with his [[Garden City of Suresnes|garden city]] considered a model of its kind. However, he was dismissed by [[Vichy]] in 1941. During the [[German military administration in occupied France during World War II|Occupation]], a thousand resistance fighters were executed by the Nazis in the [[Fort Mont-Valérien|Mont-Valérien fortress]], a military building that had replaced the calvary in the mid-19th century. The second half of the 20th century saw a new urban metamorphosis, as factories [[Deindustrialization|disappeared]] and were gradually replaced by residential buildings and service and high-tech companies.
== Geology == Suresnes historian René Sordes describes the town as situated “on a small alluvial plateau, sheltered from flooding, at the foot of Mont Valérien, on the banks of the tranquil Seine, opposite the beautiful [[Forêt de Rouvray|Rouvray forest]], of which all that remains today is our [[Bois de Boulogne]].”<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|p=18}}</ref> {{multiple image | image1 = Plaque rue des Carrières Suresnes 2-3.jpg | direction = horizontal | width1 = 255 | alt1 = Color photograph of a street name plaque. | image2 = Plaque rue de la Tuilerie Suresnes.jpg | alt2 = Color photograph of a street name plaque. | width2 = 227 | caption2 = | footer = Street plaques for ''Rue des Carrières'' and ''Rue de la Tuilerie'', recalling their historical activities related to the geology of [[Suresnes]]. }} Suresnes is located on the “Gennevilliers peninsula,” a [[meander]] of the [[Seine]] 15 km long and 5 km wide. Until the 19th century, this meant that Suresnes and the surrounding towns were geographically isolated. Bordered by the hills of [[Montretout]], [[Garches]], and [[La Celle-Saint-Cloud]], the commune is dominated by Mont Valérien, a 162-metre [[Inliers and outliers (geology)|high butte]] that bears witness to major deposits eroded during the [[Quaternary]] period. The mountain is located on the last spur of the [[Beauce, France|Beauce]] plateau, on its northeastern edge.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Maleyx|Dupouey|2000|p=6}}</ref> The [[Lutetian]] period also saw the formation of coarse-grained limestone in the region, which was used in the construction of [[Notre-Dame de Paris|Notre-Dame de Paris Cathedral]], the [[Louvre]], and some of the houses in [[Suresnes]]. During the same period, when many [[lagoon]]s were spreading out, seawater evaporated, forming [[gypsum]], giving Suresnes large gypsum quarries. Toponymy has preserved this memory, as evidenced by the presence of a “''rue des Carrières''.” Until the 19th century, plaster furnaces were located in the “Fécheray” area,<ref group="note">Formerly “''Feucheret'',” derived from the word “fern.”</ref><ref name=":39">{{Harvtxt|Société Historique de Suresnes|1968|p=21}}</ref> which is said to have been used in the construction of the [[Palace of Versailles|Château de Versailles]] and the [[Château de Bagatelle]]. During the Sannois period, the sea covered these lagoons, and green clays ([[marl]]s) were deposited: they were later used to make roof tiles, as evidenced by the surviving “''rue de la Tuilerie''.” In the 16th century, sculptor [[Girolamo della Robbia]] used these clays to create artistic coverings for the [[Château de Madrid]], built in the Bois de Boulogne.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|pp=13–15}}</ref><ref>{{Harvtxt|Hebert|Noël|1995|pp=7, 41}}</ref>
== Prehistory and Antiquity == There is evidence of human presence on the [[Gennevilliers]] peninsula during the [[Neolithic]] period. Archaeological remains dating from this period have been found in Suresnes, including a carved and polished axe at the Croix-du-Roy crossroads, blades at the foot of the [[Suresnes American Cemetery and Memorial|American cemetery]], and a deer horn used as a pick at Fécheray.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|p=19}}</ref><ref name=":78">{{Harvtxt|Hebert|Noël|1995|p=70}}</ref>
During [[Protohistory]], the Mont Valérien site was considered important by the Gallic [[Parisii (Gaul)|Parisii]] people, with the Gennevilliers peninsula forming part of their domain.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|p=20}}</ref> Gallic druids may have worshipped here.<ref name=":78"/> After the [[Gallic Wars|Roman conquest]], the mountain undoubtedly became imperial property.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|p=21}}</ref> The creation of a land register under Emperor [[Diocletian]], and the absence of any new names or divisions of the estate, suggest that during the [[Roman Gaul|Gallo-Roman]] period, and indeed until the 6th century, this territory remained unchanged.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|p=22}}</ref> Proof that Gallo-Romans once resided within the perimeter of present-day Suresnes is provided by the exhumation of a tomb on what is now Place Henri-IV, containing a classical-type lamp. Pieces of pottery and glass, as well as two coins (representing [[Hadrian]]), were also unearthed by diggers in a garden on ''rue Pierre-Dupont'', while a coin representing [[Faustina the Younger|Faustina Augusta]] was found during work on ''rue des Carrières''. However, until the end of Antiquity, this area was of minor importance, and it was the site of the neighboring town of [[Nanterre]] which was considered the spiritual center of the peninsula.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|pp=23–24}}</ref>
The name Mont Valérien (''Mons Valerianus'' or ''Mons Valeriani'' in the earliest mentions) is said to derive from [[Valerian (emperor)|Valerian]], a Roman emperor who had a villa there,<ref name=":79">{{Harvtxt|Hebert|Noël|1995|p=71}}</ref> although there is no certainty as to the origin of this term, which may also have a military origin.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=La vocation religieuse du mont Valérien |language=fr |trans-title=The religious vocation of Mont Valérien |url=https://www.mont-valerien.fr/ressources-historiques/lorigine-du-mont-valerien/vocation-religieuse/ |website=mont-valerien.fr |access-date=October 3, 2018}}</ref>
== Early and Middle Middle Ages ==
=== First mention of Suresnes === [[File:Abbaye de Saint-Germain-des-Prés 140131 1.jpg|thumb|Church of the [[Saint-Germain-des-Prés (abbey)|Abbey of Saint-Germain-des-Prés]], owner of the lands of Suresnes for almost a millennium.]] In October 1885, during the construction of a section of the railway line from [[Moulineaux]] to [[Suresnes]], earth workers unearthed fifteen plaster coffins with funerary furnishings, part of an ancient [[Franks|Frankish]]-Pagan cemetery. Around 1900, at the same time as the above-mentioned find in rue Pierre-Dupont, two coffins were discovered, probably dating from the end of the 5th century. According to René Sordes, it is therefore possible that Mont Valérien was once occupied by a Frankish military post designed to keep watch over the Seine valley.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|p=25}}</ref><ref name=":49">{{Harvtxt|Prévost|1989|p=11}}</ref><ref name=":79"/>
To appease them, King [[Clovis I|Clovis]] and his successors tended to give the other Frankish chieftains estates far from the capital, reserving those around Paris for the less turbulent ecclesiastics who restored a certain temporal order. The estate of [[Rueil-Malmaison]] and its outbuildings (on which Clovis had had a church built at [[Genevieve|Saint Geneviève]]'s request, and where she was initially buried), on which the [[Gennevilliers]] peninsula certainly depended, was thus donated on March 27, 875 by King [[Charles the Bald|Charles II the Bald]] to the [[Basilica of Saint-Denis|Abbey of Saint-Denis]]. However, the troubles caused by Norman incursions and the weakness of the abbots in the 9th and 10th centuries undermined the authority of the religious institution over these lands and led to usurpations. In the 10th century, what remained of the estate was given to the powerful [[Saint-Germain-des-Prés (abbey)|abbey of Saint-Germain-des-Prés]]. The territory on which Suresnes is located was therefore subject to the great Parisian abbeys even before its birth. Each of the villages in the western part of Paris, however, had a modest and essentially rural dimension, with Nanterre retaining only relative supremacy over them.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|pp=26–28}}</ref>
For René Sordes, it was at the turn of the 9th and 10th centuries that Suresnes “entered the history books.” Indeed, in 884, [[Carloman II|Carloman]] donated vast estates to the abbey of Saint-Leufroy ([[Normandy]]), which his grandfather, Charles le Chauve, had already promised them. This donation includes “Surisna [Suresnes] in its entirety,”<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|p=29}}</ref> the first historical mention of the village. However, continued Viking invasions at the end of the century led the monks to flee to the Parisian abbey of Saint-Germain-des-Prés. After being admitted to their community, the abbey of Saint-Leufroy ''de facto'' disappeared, rendering Carloman's donation moot. [[Robert I of France|Robert]], the brother of Count [[Odo of France|Eudes]] of Paris, then obtained from King [[Charles the Simple|Charles III the Simple]] (who owed him his crown) the charter of May 14, 918, which, repeating the terms of Carloman's donation, formalized the transfer of the estate to Saint-Germain-des-Prés.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|p=30}}</ref><ref name=":49"/><ref>{{Harvtxt|Hebert|Noël|1995|p=22}}</ref>
=== Sources de Suresnes === [[File:Plaque avenue de la Fontaine du Tertre Suresnes.jpg|thumb|Plaque on ''Avenue de la Fontaine-du-Tertre'', in homage to the old spring.]] According to local historians, the word “''Surisnas'', ''Surisnae'', or ''Surisna''” is of Celtic [[hydronym]] origin, i.e. derived from the word “source.” Indeed, numerous springs were gushing out of Mont Valérien, both on the Suresnes side and on the [[Nanterre]] side. As the Gauls worshipped the forces of nature, associated with deities, it's conceivable that they worshipped the goddess Surisna here. As [[Christianization]] did not completely eradicate Gallic saints from the countryside, substitutions were often made, with the Christian saint taking the place of the Gallic god, as probably happened to Surisna.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|p=32}}</ref><ref name=":50">{{Harvtxt|Prévost|1989|p=8}}</ref> [[File:Pierre Puvis de Chavannes - Saint Genevieve as a child in prayer - Google Art Project.jpg|left|thumb|Painting by [[Pierre Puvis de Chavannes]], ''L'enfance de Sainte Geneviève'' (between 1874 and 1876), depicting the saint as a child on Mont Valérien, in homage to the legend that she grazed her sheep there.<ref group="note">The misconception that Genevieve was a shepherdess was in fact developed by 15th century iconography. In reality, she came from an aristocratic family.</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Sot|first=Michel|year=2020|title=Geneviève au secours de Paris|journal=L'Histoire|language=fr|trans-title=Genevieve to the rescue of Paris|volume=469|pages=24–25|url=https://www.lhistoire.fr/genevi%C3%A8ve-au-secours-de-paris|access-date=February 14, 2025}}</ref>]] At the end of the 19th century, there were still several springs in Suresnes, the best known of which was the Fontaine du Tertre, destroyed in 1907.<ref name=":51">{{Harvtxt|Prévost|1989|p=9}}</ref> The nearby ''Avenue de la Fontaine-du-Tertre'' is a reminder of this fountain. The fountain was said to have curative powers for the eyes, in connection with a legend according to which [[Genevieve|Saint Geneviève]] came to Mont Valérien to give her sheep a drink and restored her mother's sight thanks to the water from the well serving the family home.<ref group="note">A well recalling this story still exists today but is located in Nanterre, on another side of Mont Valérien.</ref> Much later, in 1131, when Parisians were suffering from “[[Ergotism|''mal des ardents'']],” an inflammation of the skin, only the procession of the saint's shrine could cure the sick. Pilgrims would later flock to the Tertre fountain to have their eyes cured and still do several centuries later.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|p=33}}</ref>
=== Domaine de Suresnes === In [[Gaulish]] times, only lumberjacks would have frequented the marshy banks of this area, which gradually dried out. The Romans may then have cleared the land and planted [[vine]]s. The higher parts of Mont Valérien were used for livestock farming. Soldiers and settlers must have moved in later, and a small hamlet developed between the 7th and 10th centuries, by which time its boundaries were probably similar to those drawn up in the 17th century: to the north, towards [[Puteaux]], the ''rue des Bas-Rogers'' separated the [[Saint-Germain-des-Prés]] estate from that of Saint-Denis; to the west, several paths (''des Chênes'', ''des Traineaux'' and the ''sente des Bartoux'') delimited the territory, passing over the grounds of the [[Normandy American Cemetery and Memorial|American cemetery]], the ''sentier de la Motte'', the boulevard du Maréchal-de-Lattre-de-Tassigny, the ''rue des Bons-Raisins'' and the ''Chemin du Syndicat-des-Cultivateurs'', the ''sente du Val d'Or'' marking the boundary of the [[Saint-Germain-des-Prés]] estate, and to the south the ''Chemin du Val d'Or'' separating [[Suresnes]] from [[Saint-Cloud]]. However, there is little information on the evolution of the estate.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|p=34}}</ref><ref name=":80">{{Harvtxt|Hebert|Noël|1995|p=103}}</ref> The terms ''Val d'Or'', ''Vaux d'Or'', or sometimes ''Veaux d'Or'' are commonly used, meaning a rich, fertile valley.<ref name=":40">{{Harvtxt|Société Historique de Suresnes|1968|p=47}}</ref> [[File:Vigne municipale de Suresnes 10.jpg|thumb|Suresnes vineyards in contemporary times: they currently extend over only one hectare, almost all of the former wine-growing land having been urbanized since the 19th century.]] The large estate of [[La Celle-Saint-Cloud|La-Celle-Saint-Cloud]] (567 hectares in the 9th century) had a major influence on the neighboring estate of Suresnes (300 hectares). Also owned by [[Saint-Germain-des-Prés]], it was home to some 320 inhabitants, vineyards, arable land, and acres of woodland (as evidenced by the name of ''Rue des Chênes''). In [[Suresnes]], the hillsides were covered with vines (as were many of the hills and [[plateau]]s in the Paris region),<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|last=Hubin|first=Florence|year=2018|title=Suresnes entretient le souvenir du vin des rois|language=fr|trans-title=Suresnes keeps alive the memory of the wine of kings|url=https://www.leparisien.fr/hauts-de-seine-92/suresnes-entretient-le-souvenir-du-vin-des-rois-16-08-2018-7854813.php|website=Le Parisien|access-date=October 3, 2018}}</ref> while a fifth of the territory was planted with a variety of crops. It is likely that in 918, Suresnes was under the tutelage of the neighboring domain in terms of farming and administration.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|pp=38–39}}</ref> The vintages of [[Beaujolais]] and [[Burgundy]] were reserved for aristocratic tables, while those of the Île-de-France region, including Suresnes, were prized by the people of Paris.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|p=49}}</ref><ref name=":80"/>
To avoid the [[Seine]] overflowing its banks and the marshy odors of the river, the center of [[Suresnes]] would have been located on a small plateau formed by the river's ancient [[Alluvium|alluvial]] deposits, on land corresponding to today's ''Scheurer-Kestner Alley'', bounded by ''rue Émile-Zola'', ''rue du Puits-d'Amour'', ''rue de la Huchette'' and ''rue Étienne-Dolet''. The chapel, then the village church, certainly stood on this site, surrounded by simple houses. In the 12th century, the abbots would also have erected buildings on the banks of the Seine to collect [[tithe]]s, house the mayor and his staff, and supervise the management of the estate. The seigniorial house was located not far from the current bridge, on the eastern side. With a slow-growing population of just a few hundred, Suresnes was also home to many craftsmen. The later development of Suresnes took place along a north-south axis parallel to the Seine: ''rue d'en Haut'' (now ''rue Émile-Zola'' and ''rue de Verdun'', leading to [[Courbevoie]] and on to [[Paris]]) and ''rue d'en Bas'' (now ''rue de Saint-Cloud'', ''rue des Bourets'' and ''rue Ledru-Rollin'', which led to [[Puteaux]] to the north and [[Saint-Cloud]] to the south), as well as various paths, some of which have been preserved in contemporary roads.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|pp=50–51}}</ref> The marshy lands along the Seine were then drained once and for all,<ref name=":50"/><ref>{{Harvtxt|Hebert|Noël|1995|p=7}}</ref> and the clearing of the land around Suresnes was also completed. In addition to vines, Suresnois winegrowers also raise poultry, cattle, and pigs.<ref name=":81">{{Harvtxt|Hebert|Noël|1995|p=104}}</ref>
=== Saint-Leufroy church === [[File:Vitraux église Cœur-Immaculé-de-Marie, Suresnes, 9.jpg|left|thumb|231x231px|Stained glass window in the Church of the Immaculate Heart of Mary depicting the founding of the first church in [[Suresnes]].]] Before [[Charles the Bald|Charles le Chauve]]'s donation, the parish of [[Nanterre Cathedral|Saint-Maurice de Nanterre]] served the faithful of Suresnes, but it was a long and arduous journey. The abbots of Saint-Germain therefore asked their bishop for permission to build a chapel in Suresnes, in accordance with an 803 [[capitulary]]. The chapel was built around 910. An agreement signed in 1070 between the abbot of Saint-Germain and bishop Geoffroy de Boulogne unreservedly recognized that Suresnes belonged to the abbey, and no longer to the episcopal authority. Although the neighboring village of [[Puteaux]] had belonged to the Abbey of Saint-Denis since 875, it had no church, which led its faithful to come and pray in Suresnes, forming the parish of Suresnes-Puteaux, where the Abbey of Saint-Germain also collected tithes. It wasn't until 1509 that Puteaux was able to build a chapel, and only in 1717 that it was able to constitute a parish independent that of Suresnes, although certain practices and marks of superiority remained imposed on Puteaux.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|pp=40–42}}</ref> [[File:Plaque promenade Saint-Leufroy Suresnes.jpg|thumb|Plaque on the Saint-Leufroy promenade, in the district where the old church was located.]] There are no architectural references to the 10th-century chapel. It is possible that the passage under the authority of Saint-Germain-des-Prés led to the construction of a larger building and that, in the 12th century, the chapel became a church in the [[Gothic architecture|Gothic style]]. It was damaged during the [[Hundred Years' War]] and destroyed in 1590 by the [[Huguenots]] during the [[French Wars of Religion|Wars of Religion]], but certain architectural elements were preserved, giving the renovated building a heterogeneous style. The church was demolished in 1905-1906.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|pp=43–44}}</ref><ref>{{Harvtxt|Hebert|Noël|1995|pp=16–17}}</ref> [[File:Chapelle Saint-Leufroy Suresnes.jpg|left|thumb|The Saint-Leufroy chapel, whose name recalls the thousand-year-old church of Suresnes destroyed in 1906.]] On January 22, 1222, the church received a portion of [[Leutfridus|Saint Leufroy]]'s ashes, the relics leading to the building being named after the saint.<ref group="note">In particular a rib, later replaced, after its disappearance and thanks to a donation from the abbey, by a leg.</ref><ref name=":75">{{Harvtxt|Prasteau|1985|p=105}}</ref> A fire at the end of the 16th century damaged the relics, and the shrine (which had been modified several times) was finally sold during the [[French Revolution]] to a woodcutter for use as a rabbit cage. Discarded, the relics were picked up by a devotee who later gave them to the parish priest of Suresnes, although no further mention was made of them thereafter. Although a small bone was preserved by ecclesiastics, its authenticity is doubtful. After the church's demolition in 1906, its successor changed its name to ''Église du Cœur-Immaculé-de-Marie de Suresnes'', although St. Leufroy remains theoretically the patron saint of Suresnes, and a small chapel erected in 1947 on rue du Chemin-Vert is dedicated to him.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|pp=45–46}}</ref><ref>{{Harvtxt|Prévost|1989|pp=14, 17}}</ref>
In 1977, students from the Paul-Langevin High School carried out excavations on the site of the former Saint-Leufroy church (now the Suresnes 2 shopping center), revealing that it was originally a rectangular chapel, to which an [[Apse chapel|apsidal chapel]] was later added, creating a Gothic church, “flanked by a flamboyant aisle and chevet,” which was rebuilt after the [[Hundred Years' War]]. This church was to resemble Notre-Dame-de-Pitié in [[Puteaux]], with almost identical apses.<ref name=":82">{{Harvtxt|Hebert|Noël|1995|p=6}}</ref>
=== Early developments === The first mayor of Suresnes whose name has been preserved is “Gui,” who appeared in court in 1161 for insubordination to the abbey. The judicial text, which confirms that the village was administered by a mayor and his subordinates, all of whom came under the jurisdiction of the abbey, mentions the existence of several small civil servants and craftsmen, who, in addition to the winegrowers, made up the population of Suresnes at the time.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|p=52}}</ref> In France, however, the mayor was considered inefficient, and the public prosecutor was born, appointed from among the notables, approved by the seigneur, and entrusted with some thankless maintenance and management tasks in the service of the community.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|p=53}}</ref>
The evolution of property rights and freedoms in the 13th century remained advantageous for the abbots: the estate still belonged to them, but personalities from outside Suresnes could acquire shares and dispose of them, provided they paid a substantial fee. Several Parisian bourgeois thus acquired the Suresnes vineyards, to make good investments and own modest or comfortable country houses near [[Paris]]. Their land was cultivated by laborers, often from [[Brittany]], [[Normandy]], or [[Picardy]]. The emancipation of the [[Serfdom|serfs]] on the estates surrounding Suresnes during this same century suggests that the village had already done away with serfdom for some time. Also in the 13th century, the prior of [[La Celle-Saint-Cloud]] still retained certain prerogatives over Suresnes, including receiving royalties owed by his winegrowers.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|pp=55–56, 59}}</ref>
From the 13th to the 16th century, certain knights bore the title “''de Suresnes''” (''Sorenis'' or ''Syrenis''), lending credence to the hypothesis that the monastery may have ceded them a portion of territory and that “''seigneurs de Suresnes''” may have existed. In reality, these were only modest individuals to whom certain lands may have been temporarily given as a reward for protection or legal aid, but who never used this title other than to specify their identity. There were also fiefs in Suresnes, a word which at the time did not designate noble property as it would later, but land owned by commoners, even servile personalities.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|pp=61–63}}</ref>
== Late Middle Ages == From the 14th to the 19th century, Suresnes remained largely unchanged. By the end of the [[Middle Ages]], however, the built-up area had expanded: the church district was joined by the “[[Banalité|four banal]]” district,<ref group="note">With the ''Rue des Cheaudeaux'', now ''Rue Étienne-Dolet'', and the ''Place des Courqueux''.</ref> ''Rue d'En-Bas''<ref group="note">Current ''Rue des Bourets''.</ref> and ''Rue d'En-Haut''.<ref group="note">Formerly ''Rue du Moutier'' — an old form of the word “monastery” — then ''Rue de l’Église'' and currently ''Rue Émile-Zola''.</ref><ref name=":41">{{Harvtxt|Société Historique de Suresnes|1968|p=19}}</ref> Bourgeois country houses for Parisian officials and magistrates were built around the village, sometimes including large gardens where these notables came to enjoy the peace of Suresnes while looking after their property. [[Urbanization]] was limited, however, and the push north towards [[Puteaux]] was not yet on the agenda.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|p=65}}</ref>
The [[Hundred Years' War]] disrupted the tranquility of Suresnes. In 1346, King [[Edward III of England]] set foot in France and his troops pillaged the area around Paris.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|pp=70–71}}</ref> No sources mention Suresnes in these destructions, but it's likely that these events had repercussions on the town. Later, the English king [[Henry VI of England|Henry VI]] confiscated a number of Suresnes lands and gave them to his secretary Étienne Bruneau, until the [[Battle of Formigny|victory of Formigny]] in 1450.<ref name=":83">{{Harvtxt|Hebert|Noël|1995|p=24}}</ref>
Because of the destruction suffered by [[La Celle-Saint-Cloud]] during the war, the Abbey of Saint-Germain installed a provost-monk in Suresnes to manage the “provostry of La-Celle-Suresnes.” The administrative center of the estate thus moved to Suresnes, in premises that the abbey owned on the banks of the [[Seine]]. At the time, Suresnes was linked spiritually to [[Puteaux]] (parish of Saint-Leufroy) and administratively to La Celle-Saint-Cloud.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|p=73}}</ref>
Financially, its inhabitants remained destitute and bore crushing burdens, with the syndic trying in vain over the following centuries to challenge the religious tax. However, in a decree issued in September 1406, [[Charles VI of France|Charles VI]], King of France, eased these burdens, noting the “such degree of poverty and indigence” of the Suresnois.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|pp=74–75}}</ref> Other sources from the late Middle Ages mention various controversies and conflicts linked to taxation and the use of the communal oven.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|pp=76–80}}</ref> The end of the Hundred Years' War was synonymous with ruin, pillage, and wasteland in the Paris region, with René Sordes noting that “Suresnes, surrounded as it was, could not be spared.”<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|p=80}}</ref> Once the war was over, the village returned to its wine-growing vocation.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|p=82}}</ref> The winegrowers also devoted themselves to growing cereals and fodder (wheat, oats, and hay), useful for paying royalties and various taxes.<ref name=":81"/>
=== 15th and 16th centuries === [[File:King Henry IV of France.jpg|thumb|King [[Henry IV of France|Henry IV]], who legend associates with Suresnes: he is said to have come there because he was attracted to its wine and to the young [[Gabrielle d'Estrées]], who became his mistress.]] At the end of the 15th century, the abbey of Saint-Germain found itself in a difficult position, with its administration undergoing numerous crises, which affected the management of the Suresnes provost's office, even leading to usurpation of posts.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|pp=85–92}}</ref> On June 8, 1575, the [[Benedictines]] of the Abbey finally took control of the [[Provost (religion)|provostry]], putting an end to what had been a priory for some time.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|pp=95–96}}</ref> Suresnes had 350 inhabitants in 1460, rising to 500 by the end of the century.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Hebert|Noël|1995|p=115}}</ref>
During the [[French Wars of Religion|Wars of Religion]], the abbots of [[Saint-Germain-des-Prés (abbey)|Saint-Germain-des-Prés]] paid little heed to the miserable conditions of the Suresnes winegrowers, who were victims of looting in particular, and the village had to defend itself on its own.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|p=99}}</ref> In 1569, a protective wall was finally built, with seven gates<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|pp=100–101}}</ref> and three [[postern]]s.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Prévost|1989|p=29}}</ref> Once peace had returned, these walls - which had become useless - were sometimes moved or destroyed: the ''Puteaux gate'' was demolished in 1788, the ''Dessus-l'Eau gate'' in 1818, and the last gate, towards [[Saint-Cloud]], in 1906.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|p=102}}</ref> Although vineyards still dominated in Suresnes, [[Onobrychis|sainfoin]], and peas were also beginning to be grown, as were cherry trees (as evidenced by the name of ''rue de la Cerisaie'')<ref name=":42">{{Harvtxt|Société Historique de Suresnes|1968|p=13}}</ref> and apple trees on the northern plateau.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Hebert|Noël|1995|p=105}}</ref> A text from 1710 also indicates that barley was being grown in Suresnes, in response to demand that may have come from Parisian breweries.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Hebert|Noël|1995|pp=106–107}}</ref> [[File:Vitraux église Cœur-Immaculé-de-Marie, Suresnes, 4.jpg|left|thumb|Stained glass window in the church of the Immaculate Heart of Mary depicting the Suresnes conferences.]] On October 14, 1590, clashes broke out in Suresnes. [[Jean de Biencourt de Poutrincourt et de Saint-Just|Jean de Poutrincourt]], a ''[[Catholic League (French)|Ligueur]]'', had come to the village to supply wine to the army of the Sieur du Maine. Henri de Navarre (the future [[Henry IV of France|Henri IV]]) sent a large force to Suresnes, forcing Poutrincourt's soldiers to take refuge in the church, where fighting ensued and the church was burnt to the ground.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|pp=102–103}}</ref> Suresnes, a small village able to house many people, was chosen after the conflict to host a [[Estates General of 1593|peace conference]]. This took place between the end of April and the end of May 1593, under military guard and presided over by the Archbishop of Bourges. On May 8, Henri IV wrote to the Duke of Mayenne: "I have resolved to move away from Paris because of the Conference and to go to [[Senlis]] and [[Compiègne]] during the Conference, which is being held in the village of Surèsne.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Prévost|1989|p=32}}</ref> It prepares Henri's abjuration to Protestantism. For René Sordes, it was “the only event in history to bear the name” of Suresnes.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|pp=104–107}}</ref> The conference took place on a property between ''Rue de la Seine'' and ''Rue de Saint-Cloud'', the site of the future Château de la Source, where the Coty factory was built in the early 20th century.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|p=164}}</ref><ref name=":83"/><ref name=":59">{{Harvtxt|Collectif |1994|p=376}}</ref> In Paris, ''Rue de Surène'' may have been named in tribute to this event.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|p=106}}</ref> [[File:Suresnes Cœur-Immaculé-de-Marie 450.jpg|thumb|Stained glass window in the Church of the Immaculate Heart of Mary depicting [[Henry IV of France|Henry IV]] abjuring [[Protestantism]].]] A local legend has it that Henri IV owned property in Suresnes and liked to come here to rest, linking the memory of the king to love and wine; he is also said to have climbed Mont Valérien to meet a hermit, who predicted his tragic death. These stories are unfounded but persist in the collective memory. As proof of this, in 1816, at the start of the [[Bourbon Restoration in France|Restoration]], ''Place de la Croix'' was renamed ''Place Henri-IV'', while the neighboring ''Quai'' was renamed ''Quai de Bourbon''.<ref group="note">Became ''Quai Gallieni'' in 1916, after having been ''Quai Royal'' in 1846, ''Quai Impérial'' in 1855 and ''Quai de Suresnes'' in 1873.</ref><ref name=":39"/> A parallel legend has it that [[Gabrielle d'Estrées]] also lived in Suresnes, secretly meeting King Henri around the “old well of love,” although there is no documentary evidence of this.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|pp=168–169, 188}}</ref> [[File:Plaque avenue des Conférences de Suresnes.jpg|left|thumb|Plaque on the ''Avenue des Conférences-de-Suresnes'', named in 1926<ref name=":83"/> in homage to the negotiation conferences that contributed to the conclusion of peace between [[Catholic Church|Catholics]] and [[Protestantism|Protestants]].]] The existence of this well is attested as far back as the 15th century; it was a meeting place for young boys and girls, who drew water and took oaths of love.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Société Historique de Suresnes|1968|p=41}}</ref> Members of the ''d'Estrées'' family did own a house in Suresnes, but only in the 18th century.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|p=345}}</ref> In tribute to this history, a guinguette was renamed “''À la Belle Gabrielle''” in the 19th century, featuring a fresco by Paul Chocat in the dining room, depicting the king wooing the young woman.<ref name=":83"/><ref name=":53">{{Harvtxt|Prévost|1989|pp=32–33}}</ref> ''Avenue de la Belle-Gabrielle'', for its part, recalls the memory of this establishment, now destroyed, which played on this episode to attract customers, as evidenced by a poster preserved in the ''Musée de Suresnes'', featuring a young girl and captioned as follows: “He loves me because he takes me to dinner at ''La Belle Gabrielle''.”<ref>{{Harvtxt|Prasteau|1985|pp=108, 112–113}}</ref>
On July 8, 1524, [[François Vatable]] was appointed parish priest of Suresnes. He inaugurated the writing of baptismal and marriage registers in French. In 1530, he was appointed professor of Hebrew by [[Francis I of France|François I]], then superior of the Abbey of Bellozane in 1543, before he died in 1547.<ref>{{Harvtxt|Prévost|1989|p=28}}</ref> In the 16th century, Suresnes saw little development, even though a growing number of Parisian personalities, attracted by its pastoral atmosphere, the charm of the [[Seine]], and proximity to Paris, acquired property here, including vineyards.<ref name=":13">{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|p=107}}</ref>
== 17th century ==
=== Wealthy estates and famous owners === [[File:Lionne-fresne.jpg|thumb|Minister [[Hugues de Lionne]] was a landowner in Suresnes.]] In terms of urban layout, Suresnes has always been structured around two roads running parallel to the [[Seine]], delimiting three groups of houses: The first to the south, with the church and the seigneurial house, the second with the communal oven, the presbytery and the school, and the third to the north; this historic Suresnes largely disappeared with the industrial and real estate construction of the 19th and 20th centuries (for example, the property of Étienne de Sain, attorney to the Parliament, which was located at 24, rue de Verdun, near the town gate, and which was demolished after the [[World War II|Second World War]] to make way for modern buildings).<ref>{{Harvtxt|Sordes|1965|p=163}}</ref> Surrounding the modest winegrowers' village was a belt of bourgeois and aristocratic residences.
